unit.name: Sterile Bulk Hold Vessel
unit.cfj: Contain sterile bulk drug product solution under controlled aseptic conditions pending fill-finish

meta.method: Ulwick ODI (Importance × Satisfaction → Opportunity), needs scoring v2

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that microbial contamination in the held solution remains undetected by the available evidence
rows.plain: Make sure the available test information is good enough to reveal germs in a stored solution. Otherwise, contaminated liquid could be treated as safe.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected contamination is uniquely dangerous because a contaminated batch could proceed to fill-finish; central to disposition integrity with catastrophic downstream harm.
rows.sat_rat: The compendial sterility test's low sensitivity to low-level or non-culturable contamination means undetected contamination remains a chronic, only partially mitigated risk. Sterility testing and endotoxin results take days, so undetected contamination is judged retrospectively rather than in real time during the hold. Interpreting sparse sterility and bioburden evidence demands scarce microbiological judgement, and false negatives are a well-known limitation of the compendial sterility test. Sampling and microbial testing consume real resources yet only sample a tiny fraction of the bulk volume, leaving statistical blind spots.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a contamination excursion remains undetected before transfer to fill-finish
rows.plain: Make it less likely that a contamination event goes unnoticed before the product is moved to the filling and finishing area. This helps stop contaminated material from moving forward.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected contamination excursion before fill-finish transfer risks releasing contaminated product; central to safe disposition with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Alarmed pressure, filter and environmental monitoring catch many excursions, but slow microbial confirmation leaves a real gap before fill-finish. Excursion recognition relies on environmental and monitoring signals that may lag the actual event by hours or the full test turnaround. Distinguishing a true excursion signal before transfer requires experienced review of trended monitoring data. Continuous monitoring instrumentation is installed but adds modest ongoing calibration and alarm-handling cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that microbial contamination enters the bulk solution during holding
rows.plain: Prevent germs from getting into the bulk liquid while it is being held before the next step. This helps keep the liquid safe and clean until it is used.
rows.imp_rat: Preventing microbial ingress during holding is the central purpose of aseptic containment; if contamination enters, the batch is lost and patient safety is compromised.
rows.sat_rat: Mature closed-system and barrier practices make contamination during holding uncommon, though rare breaches still occur and drive the residual risk that keeps this short of solved. Closed sterile systems and validated hold procedures act continuously, so time is rarely the binding factor in preventing ingress. Maintaining aseptic integrity depends heavily on trained operators following strict aseptic technique, which remains a demanding, vigilance-intensive practice. Single-use closed systems, sterilizing-grade filters and cleanroom infrastructure impose substantial ongoing cost to keep contamination out.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of failing to detect evidence of lost aseptic control before disposition of the held bulk
rows.plain: Make sure there is enough evidence to spot a loss of protection from germs before deciding what to do with the held product. Missing that evidence could lead to releasing a contaminated batch.
rows.imp_rat: Failing to detect lost aseptic control before disposition risks releasing compromised bulk; highly relevant to safe handoff with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Detection before disposition is only partly assured because some indicators (viable counts) lag, leaving a recurring gap where compromised bulk could pass review. Evidence of lost aseptic control often surfaces only after sterility/EM results return, sometimes near or after the disposition point. Recognizing subtle indicators of compromised aseptic control before disposition requires experienced QA judgement that is not uniformly available. The monitoring and testing needed to catch such evidence is already funded, so cost is not the primary constraint.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of viable microbial contamination of the bulk solution during holding
rows.plain: Keep germs from growing in or getting into the stored liquid before it is used. This helps keep the product safe and suitable for people.
rows.imp_rat: Viable microbial contamination during holding is the core failure mode the aseptic hold exists to prevent, with catastrophic patient-safety stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Validated closed holds and sterile filtration make viable contamination rare, but the lag between event and confirmation limits certainty during the hold itself. Viable contamination is only confirmed after incubation of sterility/bioburden samples, so detection is inherently delayed relative to onset. Interpreting microbial data and maintaining aseptic technique demand microbiology expertise that is standard in QC but concentrated in few staff. Sterility testing, media, incubation and closed-system integrity are established recurring costs that are well absorbed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial contamination of the bulk solution during aseptic holding
rows.plain: Prevent germs from getting into the large amount of medicine while it is being held in a clean environment. This helps ensure the medicine remains safe and usable.
rows.imp_rat: Near-identical to item 1: microbial contamination during aseptic holding directly defeats the CFJ and carries catastrophic sterility/patient consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Closed sterile vessels, validated aseptic connections and EM programs make contamination during holding uncommon under GMP, but residual risk from breaches and human factors persists. Aseptic hold durations are governed by validated hold-time studies that give the overseer a defined window, though monitoring cadence can lag real-time contamination onset. Maintaining aseptic integrity relies heavily on trained operators and QA oversight, so the outcome remains dependent on scarce expertise and vigilant discipline. Sterility assurance is resource-intensive (validated closed vessels, cleanroom classification, environmental monitoring) but these costs are routine and budgeted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of escaping tank contents crossing the containment boundary
rows.plain: Keep leaking tank contents inside the designated spill-control area. This helps stop the leak from spreading to people, equipment, or other areas.
rows.imp_rat: Contents escaping the containment boundary is the ultimate failure of a storage tank's core job of safely holding material; an uncontrolled release causes catastrophic environmental, safety, and compliance harm, so both relevance and severity are maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Mature layered containment (primary shell plus bund, gaskets, and periodic inspection) makes gross releases uncommon, though seep-through and slow weld/seam failures still occur. Leak detection and secondary containment inspections are routine but a slow leak can persist between rounds before being caught. Bunds, dikes and liners are engineered by specialists but their daily monitoring relies on operator vigilance and periodic integrity testing. Secondary containment and interstitial monitoring add capital and maintenance cost but are standard and budgeted in most facilities.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a structural breach releasing bulk material from the intermediate bulk container during active service
rows.plain: Prevent the large container from breaking open and releasing its contents while it is being used. This helps protect people and prevent spills.
rows.imp_rat: A structural breach releasing bulk material during active service is the most direct failure of the IBC's containment CFJ, with catastrophic spill, safety, and loss consequences—both proxies maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Type-tested, rated IBCs with defined service limits very seldom breach structurally during normal active service. Pre-use integrity checks are a brief standard step before active service. Recognizing a compromised container relies on routine inspection skill. A structural release spills the full contents, but qualified containers make this rare.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sterile vessel boundary integrity is lost during holding
rows.plain: Make sure the vessel holding the sterile product stays sealed while the product is being held. A damaged seal could allow contamination to enter.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of sterile boundary integrity during holding is a direct containment failure central to the CFJ, with severe sterility loss consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-hold tests and closed designs give good assurance, but undetected slow leaks or seal failures still occasionally compromise the boundary. Integrity is maintained passively once the vessel is sealed, so elapsed time seldom threatens the boundary directly. Correct assembly, gasket seating and connection verification rely on skilled technicians and disciplined procedures. Validated seals, filters and pressure-hold test equipment carry meaningful capital and consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a deviation is classified as having no batch impact despite evidence of compromised sterility or quality
rows.plain: Make sure a problem is not labeled as harmless to the batch when there is evidence that sterility or product quality may have been harmed. This helps ensure affected batches receive the right review.
rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying a sterility-compromising deviation as no-impact could release unsafe product; highly relevant to disposition with catastrophic consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Standard deviation systems reduce but do not eliminate the risk of a compromised batch being wrongly deemed non-impacting, a recurring and serious failure mode. Deviation classification happens within defined timelines but rushing under fill-finish schedule pressure can truncate proper impact assessment. Correctly judging batch impact of a sterility/quality signal demands seasoned QA reviewers, and misclassification traces to judgement gaps. Investigation resources exist but a wrongly closed 'no impact' deviation risks a costly downstream loss, so the exposure is significant.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting containment as intact when the aseptic boundary is compromised
rows.plain: Make sure the system is not wrongly accepted as sealed and protected when its sterile boundary has actually been damaged. This prevents a hidden loss of protection from being overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting containment as intact when compromised is a false-pass that can release unsafe product; relevant with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Physical integrity tests have known sensitivity gaps for microbial ingress, so intact-when-compromised acceptance still happens. Integrity testing is reasonably fast to run, so timing is not the limiting factor. Correlating an integrity-test pass to true aseptic-boundary intactness requires specialist interpretation prone to error. Accepting a compromised boundary as intact risks batch loss and contamination costs far exceeding the test cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of approving an IBC that cannot maintain the required sterile boundary during filling, storage, or discharge
rows.plain: Approve only large containers that can keep their contents protected from germs while being filled, stored, and emptied. This helps prevent contamination during the whole process.
rows.imp_rat: Approving an IBC that cannot hold the sterile boundary across filling, storage, and discharge undermines the containment job at every stage; an undetected breach can contaminate large volumes of product and cause recalls, making both proxies very high.
rows.sat_rat: Established URS-and-supplier-audit practice catches most gross boundary risks, but latent gasket, valve, and seal weaknesses still slip through into approval, leaving recurring gaps. Sterile-boundary verification during acquisition relies on document review and supplier qualification that add lead time but are routinely scheduled into procurement cycles. Judging whether an IBC design will hold a sterile boundary through filling, storage, and discharge demands scarce sterile-processing engineering expertise that is unevenly available on procurement teams. The cost of pre-award integrity assessment is modest relative to a contamination event, though re-qualification of rejected units adds spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of container pressure exceeding safe limits during purging
rows.plain: Prevent the pressure inside a container from becoming dangerously high while its remaining contents are removed. This helps avoid damage or injury.
rows.imp_rat: Pressure exceeding safe limits during purging is central to safe containment (relevance high) and can cause catastrophic rupture, injury, or release (severity max), making it a top-tier error for IBCs.
rows.sat_rat: Purging is a less standardized operation, so pressure excursions occur more often than in routine fill/discharge. Purging with pressure monitoring adds a controlled but tolerable time step. Safe purging demands trained personnel who understand pressure limits and inert gas handling. Relief valves and gauges are affordable but overpressure events risk expensive damage and downtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sterile-boundary integrity loss remains unrecognized during bulk hold
rows.plain: A loss of the protective seal around the germ-free product should be noticed while the product is being held. This helps prevent contaminated product from moving forward unnoticed.
rows.imp_rat: Unrecognized boundary integrity loss risks proceeding with a contaminated batch; highly relevant to detection with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Passive breaches during hold often go unrecognized until sampling or review, a recognized weak spot in current practice. Continuous monitoring is possible but integrity loss can persist between periodic checks. Recognizing subtle boundary integrity loss during hold demands expert interpretation and is not easily automated. Continuous pressure-hold or leak monitoring adds instrumentation cost that many holds do not fully implement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a sterile-boundary breach at the discharge connection during flow adjustment
rows.plain: Keep the connection at the outlet sealed while adjusting the flow. This prevents a break in the protected, germ-free area and reduces the chance of contamination.
rows.imp_rat: A sterile-boundary breach at discharge directly corrupts the containment job during transfer; in sterile/aseptic contexts the consequences are severe product loss and patient-safety risk.
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic and single-use connectors help, but flow-adjustment manipulation at the discharge remains a recurring sterile-boundary risk. Maintaining aseptic connection during flow adjustment slows the discharge setup. Preserving a sterile boundary at the discharge while adjusting flow demands high aseptic technique. A sterility breach can condemn an entire batch, a very high-value loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminant ingress into the sterile transfer path while IBC discharge is interrupted
rows.plain: Keep dirt or germs from getting into the sterile material path if the flow of material stops. This helps protect the material while the interruption is handled.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminant ingress into a sterile transfer path during interrupted discharge is highly central to the containment job (high relevance) and can spoil an entire sterile batch (severity very high).
rows.sat_rat: Interrupted sterile transfers are a known contamination weak point where standard closures leave real exposure risk. Maintaining sterile closure during an interruption requires slow, careful capping steps. Preserving asepsis at a paused transfer demands high aseptic technique and specialist training. Closed and single-use systems that prevent ingress are expensive and not universally deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterile bulk exposure to a non-aseptic flow path during transfer or hold
rows.plain: Keep the sterile liquid from coming into contact with a pathway that is not kept sterile during transfer or waiting, so it is not contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to a non-aseptic flow path during transfer or hold is a direct contamination breach of the containment job with severe batch-loss and safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Mature closed-system and CIP/SIP practice makes intact aseptic paths the norm, though rare connection or manipulation failures still occur. Closed transfer setups add setup time but do not by themselves guarantee an aseptic path is maintained throughout the hold. Preventing exposure to a non-aseptic flow path depends heavily on operator aseptic technique and rigorous SOP adherence, so residual human-error risk persists. Single-use closed systems and steam-sterilized transfer lines are costly but standard, and a breach means loss of an entire high-value bulk batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sterile-boundary integrity loss remains undetected during readiness verification
rows.plain: Make it less likely that a loss of the germ-free barrier goes unnoticed during the check that a work area is ready. This matters because an unnoticed breach could allow contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected boundary loss at readiness verification could start a hold in a compromised state; relevant to the job with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-use integrity testing catches gross losses but subtle breaches can pass verification undetected. Readiness verification includes integrity tests but only at a point in time, not continuously. Detecting boundary integrity loss depends on the sensitivity of the test method and skilled interpretation. Pressure-decay/integrity test equipment is standard but not universally sensitive to small breaches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an intervention introduces microbial contamination into the bulk solution during holding
rows.plain: Prevent hands-on work from bringing germs into the bulk liquid while it is being held, so the liquid stays sterile.
rows.imp_rat: Interventions introducing contamination are a well-known aseptic risk central to hold integrity, with severe batch-loss consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Human interventions remain the leading contamination source despite training, so residual failure rates persist. Aseptic interventions are quick but any intervention pauses normal operation and adds monitoring overhead. Preventing intervention-borne contamination depends almost entirely on rigorous gowning and aseptic technique by trained personnel, the classic weak point. Extensive gowning, training and qualification programs are a substantial recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an uncontrolled material release from residual container or line pressure during connection
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that trapped pressure forces material out of a container or pipe when it is being connected. This helps prevent spills, injuries, and exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Uncontrolled release from residual pressure during connection is central to safe material transfer and can cause chemical exposure or injury, giving high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Vent and pressure-relief procedures exist but residual pressure at connection still causes recurring uncontrolled releases when steps are skipped. Depressurizing or venting before connection adds a preparatory step. Confirming residual line and container pressure is relieved requires operator diligence. An uncontrolled release wastes product and triggers cleanup and safety response.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of aseptic interventions introducing contaminants into the vessel during hold
rows.plain: Reduce how often people must reach into or otherwise interfere with the vessel during a waiting period in a way that could introduce contamination. This matters to keep the product sterile.
rows.imp_rat: Aseptic interventions introducing contaminants are a direct ingress route central to hold integrity with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Intervention minimization and aseptic technique reduce but do not eliminate contamination introduced during manipulations. Interventions are minimized but each adds unplanned time when they occur. Every aseptic intervention during hold is a highly technique-dependent contamination opportunity requiring expert execution. Interventions consume gowning, supplies, and potentially product if they go wrong.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time a vessel-boundary breach remains undetected during hold
rows.plain: Shorten the time that a leak or opening in the vessel’s protective boundary remains unnoticed while the product is being held. This matters to limit the chance of contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Time a boundary breach stays undetected drives contamination exposure; relevant to detection speed with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Breaches can go undetected for extended periods, so the outcome window of exposure is poorly bounded today. Detection latency for a breach depends on monitoring frequency and can be long between checks. Faster detection depends on skilled monitoring or instrumentation rather than luck. Continuous monitoring to shorten detection time adds instrumentation cost not always deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial contamination entering the sterile bulk during transfer into the hold vessel
rows.plain: As the germ-free product is moved into its holding container, as few germs as possible should enter it. This helps keep the stored product safe from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering during transfer into the vessel is a primary ingress route central to aseptic containment, carrying catastrophic batch-loss stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Validated sterile filtration into the vessel is a well-established, highly effective barrier, so contamination on transfer-in is uncommon. Sterilizing filtration and pre-sterilized transfer assemblies fit within normal transfer windows without notable delay. Correct filter integrity handling and aseptic connections require trained operators, leaving skill-dependent failure modes. Sterilizing-grade filters and single-use manifolds are recurring costs justified against catastrophic batch contamination loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sterilization status for the vessel or aseptic product path is expired or unverified at bulk receipt
rows.plain: Avoid receiving bulk product when the vessel or the path used to handle sterile product is no longer confirmed as treated and ready. Current, verified status helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Expired/unverified sterilization status at receipt means holding in a possibly nonsterile vessel; central to the job with catastrophic consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Sterilization hold-time limits and pre-use verification are mature controls, making expired/unverified status at receipt rare. Sterilization expiry dating gives a clear validated window that is tracked ahead of receipt. Verifying sterilization status is a routine checklist task requiring modest skill. Tracking and re-sterilization costs are routine and predictable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of starting transfer before confirming aseptic readiness of the vessel and product-contact path
rows.plain: Avoid moving the sterile product into the vessel until the vessel and the path it will travel through have been confirmed clean and ready. This helps keep the product free from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Starting transfer before confirming aseptic readiness invites contamination at the outset; highly relevant to the job with severe batch-loss consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Standard pre-transfer checklists and batch-record gates catch most premature starts, but manual sign-off leaves recurring gaps. Readiness confirmation before transfer adds a defined checklist step that is scheduled into the sequence. Confirming aseptic readiness relies on operator/QA judgement interpreting status indications, which is error-prone without automation. Verification labor is modest relative to the loss from starting into a non-ready vessel.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that vessel boundary seals or closures lose integrity during holding
rows.plain: Keep the vessel’s seals and covers intact while its contents are being held. This helps stop germs or other contaminants from getting inside.
rows.imp_rat: Seal/closure integrity loss is a primary boundary-breach mechanism central to containment, with severe sterility loss consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-hold and integrity testing plus qualified closures make boundary seal integrity generally well controlled. Seal integrity is confirmed before and sometimes after the hold, not continuously, so a loss may go unrecognized for a time. Assembly and torque of closures require trained technique but is well-proceduralized. Qualified gaskets, seals and integrity tests add moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of aseptic boundary breaches during bulk holding
rows.plain: Reduce the number of times the barrier keeping a bulk product clean is broken while the product is being held. This helps prevent contamination before the product is filled.
rows.imp_rat: Aseptic boundary breaches directly defeat the containment job; reducing their number is highly relevant with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Modern closed and single-use configurations reduce breach frequency substantially, though interventions and connections still create recurring breach points. Breaches are logged as they occur but the overseer's awareness depends on reporting latency from the floor. Preventing boundary breaches rests on operator aseptic discipline and supervision, a persistent skill-dependent exposure. Closed-system design and single-use assemblies reduce breach opportunities at a manageable capital cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the acceptance criteria classify inadequate aseptic containment as qualified
rows.plain: Make sure the pass-or-fail rules do not wrongly approve equipment that fails to keep germs out. This matters because weak rules could let unsafe equipment be accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance criteria qualifying inadequate containment is a systemic gap that could pass unsafe conditions; relevant with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Guidance-driven criteria (media fill, integrity limits) exist but false-qualification from loosely set thresholds remains a recurring, consequential failure. Setting acceptance criteria takes review cycles but does not chronically delay the qualification effort. Defining criteria that discriminate adequate from inadequate containment demands scarce microbiological and sterile-process judgement that is unevenly available. A wrongly-lenient criterion imposes enormous downstream cost through contamination recalls, so the stakes are high even if criteria-setting itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a tank interlock fails to establish a safe state under a hazardous condition
rows.plain: When a tank enters a dangerous condition, its safety controls should put it into a safe state. This helps limit harm to people and equipment.
rows.imp_rat: An interlock failing to establish a safe state under a hazardous condition removes the last line of protection central to safe operation (relevance 9); the result is an uncontrolled hazardous event (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Standard functional-safety practice (IEC substantially reduces interlock failures, yet real recurring gaps remain from proof-test errors, misconfiguration and untested failure modes. Interlock verification and SIL testing extend commissioning schedules but are scheduled activities. Functional safety validation demands specialized instrumentation and safety engineers who are not always available on site. SIS design, testing and documentation carry meaningful cost but are justified against hazard consequences.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial or particulate contamination being introduced onto product-contact surfaces during fitting
rows.plain: Fitting a valve or connector should not put germs or tiny particles onto surfaces that touch the product. Keeping these surfaces clean helps protect the product.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced onto product-contact surfaces during fitting directly defeats the IBC's job of holding product safely; when it occurs, contaminated batches can cause recalls, product loss, or consumer harm, making consequences severe.
rows.sat_rat: Contamination during fitting is a recognized risk that current handling practices only partially control. Careful fitting under controlled conditions slows the assembly step. Fitting valves and closures without contaminating surfaces requires trained aseptic technique. Controlled environments and single-use fittings add recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting an IBC with latent vessel or component defects
rows.plain: The selected large container should not have hidden defects in the container or its parts. This helps prevent leaks, failures, or problems during use.
rows.imp_rat: Selecting an IBC with latent vessel or component defects undermines containment reliability from the outset; consequences include in-service failures, spills, and injury, so both relevance and severity are high.
rows.sat_rat: Standard visual and documentary acceptance misses subsurface and latent defects, making this a chronic, poorly mitigated exposure. Detecting latent vessel or component defects at acquisition is limited to whatever inspection time is allotted, which rarely reveals hidden flaws quickly. Finding latent weld, wall, or component defects needs NDT expertise that few receiving teams have on hand. Thorough latent-defect detection (e.g., NDT, pressure testing) is costly and usually skipped, so failures surface expensively later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the sampling plan misses a localized or intermittent containment breach
rows.plain: Make sure the checks can find a small leak or a leak that happens only sometimes. This helps prevent a containment problem from going unnoticed.
rows.imp_rat: A sampling plan missing localized/intermittent breaches leaves contamination undetected; relevant to detection with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Discrete sampling inherently misses localized or intermittent breaches, making this a persistent blind spot. Sampling schedules are fixed and do not add meaningful delay. Designing a sampling plan that catches localized or intermittent breaches requires strong statistical and process expertise that is scarce. Sampling is inexpensive relative to the missed-breach consequences it is meant to catch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of transfer connections with compromised aseptic integrity
rows.plain: Keep the connection between parts of the transfer system sealed and free from contamination. This protects the cleanliness of the product while it is being moved.
rows.imp_rat: Compromised transfer-connection integrity is a leading aseptic breach mechanism, highly relevant to the job with severe contamination consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Genderless sterile connectors and tube welders greatly reduce compromised connections, though technique-driven failures still occur at a nonzero rate. Making and verifying connections takes time but is bounded within the transfer step. Aseptic connector integrity depends strongly on operator technique, a well-known source of variability. Sterile connectors and single-use assemblies are a recurring consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of nonsterile material entering through a connected vessel path during holding
rows.plain: Prevent material that still contains germs from entering through a connected vessel while the product is being held. This helps keep the product protected from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Nonsterile material entering through a connected path is a direct ingress route during hold, central to containment with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-system design, sterile connectors and tube welding are mature and substantially reduce ingress through connected paths. Connection integrity is verified at setup rather than continuously, so a compromised path may persist undetected for the hold duration. Correctly configuring and verifying closed transfer connections depends heavily on trained operator technique. Single-use closed connectors and sterile welding add material cost but are now standard spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that viable contamination remains on product-contact surfaces after sanitation or sterilization
rows.plain: Make sure germs are removed from surfaces that touch the product after cleaning or sterilizing.
rows.imp_rat: Residual viable contamination surviving sanitation/sterilization is a core failure of the cleaning step central to reuse; releasing such a container into service risks batch adulteration and patient harm, so severity is high.
rows.sat_rat: Sterilization validation and sterility assurance are mature, but residual viable contamination in shadowed zones remains a genuine recurring failure mode. Post-sanitation verification via swabbing or rinse sampling adds lab turnaround delay before release. Sampling and interpreting microbial results requires QC expertise that not all sites have on-hand. Repeat sanitation and lab testing on failures consume resources but are manageable.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of a contamination excursion before recognition
rows.plain: Recognize a contamination problem as soon as possible. This limits how long the problem can continue before someone responds.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of a contamination excursion before recognition drives exposure extent; relevant to limiting harm with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Because confirmation is slow, excursions often persist for the whole hold before being recognized, a poorly served pain. Recognition of a contamination excursion is gated by multi-day microbial test turnaround, so excursion duration before recognition is long. Faster recognition depends on expert trend interpretation of surrogate signals which is scarce. Rapid microbial methods exist but are costly and not universally deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of container pressure moving outside safe limits during discharge
rows.plain: Keep the pressure inside the container within safe limits while measured amounts are emptied from it.
rows.imp_rat: Pressure moving outside safe limits during discharge directly threatens the containment job (high relevance) and can cause vessel failure, spills, or operator harm (near-catastrophic severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established venting practice keeps most discharges within limits, but transients still push pressure out of range at times. Pressure-controlled discharge is routine and does not materially slow operations. Operators need to balance vent and discharge pressure, requiring moderate competence. Standard relief and vent fittings are cheap compared with the cost of a pressure failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that aseptic-boundary deterioration remains undetected between scheduled verification points
rows.plain: Make sure a weakening of the clean barrier is noticed even between planned checks. This helps identify a loss of protection as soon as it happens.
rows.imp_rat: Boundary deterioration undetected between verification points risks a live breach; relevant to detection with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Between scheduled verifications, gradual boundary deterioration commonly goes undetected. The interval between verification points is the very gap here, but the issue is coverage not turnaround. Judging deterioration between checks requires trend expertise that is not routinely applied. Continuous monitoring to close the gap is costly, so most rely on periodic checks.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of nonsterile gas entering through the vessel gas path during hold
rows.plain: Lower the chance that unclean gas enters through the vessel’s gas line while the contents are being held. This helps keep the medicine free from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Nonsterile gas ingress through the vent/gas path is a direct contamination route central to aseptic containment with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Hydrophobic sterilizing vent filters are standard and effective, though blockage or wetting can compromise the gas barrier occasionally. Sterile vent filters are installed once and operate passively without adding hold time. Little ongoing skill is needed beyond correct filter installation and periodic integrity checks. Vent filters are inexpensive consumables relative to batch value.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of tank pressure exceeding the upper process limit during use
rows.plain: Prevent the pressure inside the tank from rising above the highest safe operating level. This helps protect the tank, the product, and nearby people.
rows.imp_rat: Overpressure exceeding the upper process limit is central to safe tank operation (relevance 9); when it occurs the consequences are catastrophic rupture, explosion, and loss of life (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Layered protection (relief devices plus high-pressure interlocks) is a mature, code-mandated practice that makes uncontrolled overpressure a rare event. Pressure relief valves and rupture discs act instantly on overpressure, so time-to-mitigate is essentially immediate once the setpoint is reached. Sizing and setting relief devices is a codified engineering task (ASME, API), removing most operator judgement from the runtime protection. PSVs, rupture discs and interlocks are standard, inexpensive fixtures relative to the catastrophic loss they prevent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of viable microbial contamination remaining on the container's interior surfaces before filling
rows.plain: Remove living germs from the inside surfaces of the container before it is filled. This helps keep the material placed inside from becoming contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Viable microbial contamination on interior surfaces before filling directly defeats the IBC's job of safely containing product; for pharmaceutical/sterile bulk this can render an entire batch adulterated and unsafe, so both centrality and consequences are near-catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP/SIP with bioburden monitoring is mature standard practice, yet dead legs and complex geometries leave recurring residual-contamination risk. Automated CIP/SIP cycles are time-defined and routine, adding predictable but non-trivial duration to preparation. Validated cleaning recipes reduce operator judgement, though setup and cycle verification still need trained technicians. Cleaning agents, water, and energy for validated cycles are recurring costs but well-budgeted in this industry.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that tank-wall degradation remains undetected until integrity is lost
rows.plain: Find signs that the tank wall is weakening before it develops a hole or loses its ability to contain the contents. This helps prevent leaks and possible exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected wall degradation until integrity is lost strikes at the core containment job of the tank (relevance 8); sudden failure yields catastrophic release and injury (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Point-based gauging leaves gaps between measurement locations, so localized degradation can and does slip through until a leak forms. UT thickness mapping and periodic gauging campaigns take significant downtime but are schedulable during planned outages. Reliable detection depends heavily on certified NDT technicians whose probe placement and interpretation drive whether thinning is caught. Full-coverage inspection and instrumentation are costly, so practice often samples rather than covers the whole wall.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of connecting the IBC to the wrong transfer path in the sterile fill-finish area
rows.plain: Prevent the large material container from being connected to the wrong route for moving its contents. This helps ensure the material goes to the correct place without creating a safety or product-mixing problem.
rows.imp_rat: Connecting to the wrong transfer path in sterile fill-finish is highly central to correct, safe product handling and can cause cross-contamination or batch loss, making both relevance and severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Line-clearance procedures, labelling and keyed/unique fittings reduce wrong-path errors, yet look-alike lines in fill-finish still produce occasional mix-ups. Verifying the correct transfer path adds line-clearance and cross-check time but does not dominate the setup schedule. Correct connection currently leans heavily on trained operators following batch records and second-person verification in the sterile suite. A misconnection is costly through rejected batch and investigation, but the connection hardware itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that a containment-boundary breach remains undetected during holding
rows.plain: Find any break in the barrier protecting the product as quickly as possible while it is being held. This limits the time contamination could get inside.
rows.imp_rat: Duration a containment breach stays undetected drives contamination extent; relevant to detection with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure monitoring gives some continuous signal, shortening but not eliminating undetected breach time. Boundary breaches are found at scheduled integrity checks, so undetected duration equals the interval between checks. Detection uses standardized integrity tests requiring modest skill. Continuous integrity monitoring is available but adds instrument cost, so periodic checks dominate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial contamination entering the sterile solution during discharge to fill-finish
rows.plain: Prevent germs from getting into the sterile liquid while it is moved into the filling process. This protects the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during discharge to fill-finish is a direct ingress route at handoff, central to maintaining sterility with severe patient-safety impact.
rows.sat_rat: Closed discharge practice is mature and reliable, though the handoff to fill-finish introduces a residual contamination opportunity. Discharge through closed sterile connections proceeds within routine timelines. Aseptic disconnection and connection at fill-finish handoff still rely on operator technique under classified conditions. Single-use discharge assemblies and RTP ports add cost but are standard and cheaper than a lost fill lot.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a loaded IBC containment breach caused by transit movement
rows.plain: Make sure a large loaded container cannot be damaged or opened by movement during transport. This protects the contents from spilling or being exposed.
rows.imp_rat: A transit-induced containment breach defeats the core containment job of the IBC entirely; consequences include material loss, spill exposure, and potential worker harm, making both proxies very high.
rows.sat_rat: Certified UN-rated IBCs with tested closures reliably maintain containment through normal transit vibration and movement. Securing and inspecting closures before transit is a quick standard step. Proper lid and valve closure verification is routine and low-skill for trained handlers. A transit breach spills product and incurs cleanup, but robust closures rarely fail.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a leak remaining undetected at a container joint, closure, or seal
rows.plain: Make sure leaks at a container connection, cap, or seal are found during testing. This prevents a damaged container from being accepted as intact.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected leak at a joint, closure, or seal strikes at the container's central containment function; when it goes unnoticed the result is product loss, spread of hazardous contents, and possible environmental or safety incidents.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-decay and helium leak testing are mature and sensitive, catching most joint/seal leaks though slow micro-leaks can escape detection. Leak/pressure-decay testing takes measurable time but is a standard step. Automated leak testers reduce dependence on operator interpretation. Leak-test equipment is a standard capital item with low per-test cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that product-contact surfaces are contaminated before sterile bulk enters the vessel
rows.plain: Keep surfaces that touch the product clean before clean bulk product is put into the vessel. Contamination at that point could affect the product.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminated product-contact surfaces before bulk enters defeat aseptic containment from the start; central with severe sterility consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP/SIP with clean-hold time limits reliably delivers sterile contact surfaces before bulk entry. SIP/CIP cycles and hold-time-before-use limits are built into schedules but add turnaround time. Verifying contact-surface sterility depends on validated cycles more than daily operator skill, reducing judgement burden. Cleaning validation and SIP utilities are significant fixed costs already absorbed as standard practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical tank-shell or weld deterioration remains undetected during inspection
rows.plain: Make sure serious damage or weakening of the tank walls or joined seams is found during inspection. Finding it early helps prevent leaks or failure.
rows.imp_rat: Critical shell or weld deterioration missed during inspection is central to verifying containment integrity (relevance 8); an undetected weld defect can rupture catastrophically under pressure (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Mature codified weld inspection methods catch most consequential defects, though subsurface and root flaws still occasionally escape. Weld and shell inspection during a turnaround is time-consuming, especially with surface prep and access setup. Detecting weld deterioration relies on skilled RT/UT/PAUT operators whose judgement governs flaw sizing and acceptance. Advanced weld NDT and access scaffolding are expensive but broadly budgeted for critical vessels.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that inaccurate vessel indications conceal an actual excursion during holding
rows.plain: Prevent incorrect readings from hiding a real problem with the vessel while material is being held. Accurate readings are needed to notice a harmful change in time.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate indications concealing a real excursion undermines detection and can allow harm to progress undetected; relevant to monitoring with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Single-sensor reliance means an inaccurate indication can silently conceal a real excursion until periodic calibration or cross-checks reveal it. Calibration cycles occur on schedules, so a drifting sensor can mislead for the interval between checks. Recognizing that an instrument is lying rather than the process failing demands experienced judgement. Redundant sensors and frequent calibration add cost that many setups only partially deploy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sampling or material addition compromises aseptic integrity during holding
rows.plain: Prevent taking samples or adding material from breaking the clean conditions while the solution is being held. These actions must be done without exposing the solution to contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Sampling/material addition compromising aseptic integrity is a common breach route central to hold integrity with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Closed sampling devices help, but every intervention remains a recognized contamination opportunity that technique alone cannot fully eliminate. Sampling and additions are brief but each introduces a discrete window of exposure risk. Aseptic sampling technique is highly skill-dependent and a frequent source of operator-induced contamination. Closed sampling systems and aseptic connectors add cost but reduce risk when used.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of containers holding incompatible substances being placed in the same segregation area during active use
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that containers holding substances that should not be stored together are placed in the same area. This helps prevent dangerous reactions or mix-ups.
rows.imp_rat: Placing incompatible substances in the same segregation area is important to safe storage (relevance solid) and can trigger dangerous reactions, fire, or toxic release (severity very high).
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility charts and zoning exist but manual placement in busy areas still lets incompatible IBCs end up adjacent. Checking compatibility before placement takes time that congested storage often shortcuts. Correct segregation relies heavily on operator knowledge of chemical compatibility and matrices. Segregation zoning uses existing space and signage at low direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that specified product-contact construction, closures, or discharge features fail sterile fill-finish constraints
rows.plain: Make sure the container's product-touching parts, closures, and outlets meet the needs of sterile filling. This helps keep the product protected and uncontaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Specified construction, closures, or discharge features failing sterile fill-finish constraints is central to whether the IBC can perform its sterile containment job, and failure risks batch contamination, keeping both proxies elevated.
rows.sat_rat: Practice exists for common configurations, yet novel discharge or closure features regularly reveal sterility-constraint failures only after selection, so gaps recur. Verifying that contact surfaces, closures, and discharge features meet sterile fill-finish constraints extends the specification-approval timeline noticeably. It takes specialized aseptic-design knowledge to judge whether closure and valve geometries satisfy cleanability and sterility constraints, and that expertise is thin. Getting it wrong forces costly redesign or rejection, but the assessment itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that microbiological evidence is invalid or unrepresentative of the held solution
rows.plain: Make sure the germ-related evidence is valid and truly represents the solution that was held. Weak or unrepresentative evidence should not be used to decide whether the batch is ready.
rows.imp_rat: Invalid or unrepresentative microbiological evidence can mask contamination or force false rejects; relevant to disposition with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Small sample volumes and sampling variability mean evidence is often only marginally representative of the bulk. Validity of microbiological evidence is only confirmed after test completion, adding delay to any assurance. Judging whether a sample is representative demands scarce microbiological and sampling expertise. Method suitability and validation studies are costly but one-time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the fitted valve, closure cap, or seal material is incompatible with the intended contents or sterilization conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the valve, cap, and sealing material will work safely with what they contain and with the heat or other treatment used to sterilize them. Incompatible parts could fail or contaminate the contents.
rows.imp_rat: An incompatible valve, cap, or seal material directly breaches the containment and integrity the IBC exists to provide, risking product contamination or failure; both relevance and severity are high, especially for pharma/sterile use.
rows.sat_rat: Material compatibility datasheets are widely available and reliable, though sterilization-condition mismatches occasionally slip through. Checking material specs against contents adds minimal time. Selecting compatible valve/seal materials requires chemical and thermal compatibility knowledge. Approved-material sourcing costs little more than defaults.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of bulk material escaping from the IBC during transport
rows.plain: Prevent the material from leaking or spilling out of the large container while it is being moved. This protects the material, people, and surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Bulk material escaping during transport is a direct containment failure of the IBC's core job, causing loss, environmental exposure, and safety hazards—both proxies very high.
rows.sat_rat: Mature UN/DOT-certified containment design and pre-shipment checks make in-transit escape uncommon when procedures are followed. Closure and inspection routines before transport are quick and well-integrated into loading workflows. Standard valve-locking and closure procedures are simple and widely trained, requiring modest skill. Certified valves, seals and UN-rated IBCs are commodity items with predictable cost.

rows.source_job: Manage tank pressure and vent gases
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that tank pressure moves outside safe operating limits during filling, storage, or withdrawal
rows.plain: Keep the pressure inside the tank within safe limits while it is being filled, stored, or emptied. This helps prevent damage, leaks, or other unsafe conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Pressure moving outside safe limits during filling/storage/withdrawal can rupture the tank—directly central to safe storage and catastrophic in consequence, driving high relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Passive PVRVs and interlocks are a mature, redundant safeguard so pressure excursions are rare, though vent fouling or blocked breathers remain a residual failure mode. Pressure/vacuum relief valves and breather vents act instantly during filling and withdrawal without operator delay. Correct valve sizing and setpoints require engineering expertise but day-to-day protection is passive and needs little operator skill. Relief devices and nitrogen blanketing add cost but are standard installed equipment with predictable maintenance spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of secondary containment failing to retain the required spill volume
rows.plain: Make sure the backup container can hold the full amount of liquid that might spill. This helps prevent a spill from spreading.
rows.imp_rat: Secondary containment failing to retain spill volume is a critical last-line defense; its relevance is high because containment is central to the job, and severity is severe since a breach turns a contained spill into an uncontrolled release.
rows.sat_rat: Codes like SPCC and API mandate containment sizing and there is mature inspection practice, but cracked liners, settled berms, and undersized drains still slip through periodic checks. Secondary containment inspection and hydrostatic or visual checks add days to preparation but are routinely scheduled into commissioning timelines. Verifying berm or dike capacity against required spill volume requires engineering judgement and code familiarity that is available but not universally applied on site. Liner and dike volume verification is inexpensive relative to a real spill, so cost rarely blocks the work.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a hazardous atmosphere remaining in the tank after purging
rows.plain: Make sure no dangerous vapors or gases are left in the tank after it is cleared out. This protects people who open or enter the tank and prevents unsafe conditions.
rows.imp_rat: A hazardous atmosphere remaining after purging directly defeats the tank's safe-processing job (relevance 8); the consequence is ignition or asphyxiation during subsequent work, causing severe injury (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Continuous multi-gas monitoring and lock-out permits make residual hazardous atmospheres uncommon under mature practice, though stratified pockets still occasionally slip through. Purging and gas-testing cycles add hours to the disposal timeline but are well-established sequential steps. Confined-space entry standards and gas-testing protocols exist but demand a competent, certified attendant whose judgement remains the failure point. Purge gases, monitors and standby crews add modest cost that is routinely budgeted for.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a faulty overfill safeguard is incorrectly judged functional during testing
rows.plain: Make sure a faulty protection device is not mistakenly judged to be working during a test. This helps ensure it will protect against too much material being added.
rows.imp_rat: A faulty overfill safeguard incorrectly judged functional creates a false sense of safety; relevant to preventing overflow, and severe because an actual overfill can cause a large uncontrolled release.
rows.sat_rat: Bench simulation often verifies the sensor but not the full actuated response, so a device can be judged functional while a stuck valve or bypassed logic remains hidden. Functional overfill-device testing is a brief scheduled bench or in-situ check, so time is rarely the constraint. Correctly proving a high-level alarm or automatic shutoff requires a technician who understands the full trip loop, and shortcuts by less-trained staff cause false passes. Testing overfill safeguards is inexpensive relative to a spill, so cost is not the binding issue.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of breaching the sterile product-contact boundary while connecting the IBC to transfer equipment
rows.plain: Keep the clean path that touches the product sealed while connecting the large container to the transfer equipment. This prevents germs or other contamination from getting into the product.
rows.imp_rat: Breaching the sterile boundary while connecting to transfer equipment is a direct, common failure point in the containment job; an ingress at connection can contaminate the whole transfer, so severity is high.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile-connection technology exists and is maturing, but breaches of the sterile boundary during connection still occur regularly. Aseptic connections take deliberate time and cannot be rushed without risk. Manual aseptic connection technique is highly operator-dependent and a frequent source of error absent single-use connectors. Single-use sterile connectors reduce risk but add per-batch consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing microbial contamination into the sterile bulk during deviation response
rows.plain: Avoid bringing germs into the sterile product while responding to a problem with the batch. This helps protect the product while the issue is being investigated or corrected.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contamination during deviation response is a real risk during interventions; relevant to hold integrity with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation responses are ad hoc and a known source of secondary contamination despite SOPs. Deviation response is unplanned and its aseptic handling extends timelines unpredictably. Responding to a deviation without adding contamination demands the highest aseptic judgement under pressure. Poor deviation handling can waste the entire bulk, a major cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the rate of microbial proliferation in the bulk solution during holding
rows.plain: Keep germs from multiplying in the mixed liquid while it is waiting. This helps keep the medicine safe and usable.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial proliferation rate is the growth side of contamination; controlling it is central to maintaining sterile-equivalent conditions with severe consequences if it occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cold-hold conditions plus enforced hold-time limits reliably keep bioburden growth low, with excursions being the main residual risk. Temperature control acts continuously to suppress growth, so timing is well handled by the control loop. Once setpoints are established, little operator skill is needed to keep proliferation suppressed beyond monitoring. Refrigeration/jacket utilities and validated hold-time limits consume energy and constrain scheduling but are routine.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an out-of-limit vessel condition remains undetected during holding
rows.plain: Make sure any unsafe condition in a holding tank is noticed while the material is being held. This allows workers to correct the problem before the material is affected.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected out-of-limit vessel condition can allow degradation or contamination to progress; relevant to monitoring with serious quality consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Alarmed monitoring catches most excursions, yet gaps between manual observations and unalarmed parameters leave a real chance of an undetected condition. Continuous or frequent monitoring shortens the window an out-of-limit condition can persist unseen. Detecting subtle out-of-limit conditions still leans on operator attention and interpretation of alarms and trends. Instrumentation, alarms and data historians represent notable but standard investment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of seal or closure failures at the vessel boundary during sterile bulk hold
rows.plain: Reduce how often a seal or closure fails at the vessel wall while sterile material is being held. This helps keep outside contamination from reaching the material.
rows.imp_rat: Seal/closure failures at the boundary directly cause containment loss; central to the job with severe sterility consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Qualified seals and closures perform reliably during hold, with failures being infrequent but not eliminated. Seal integrity is set at assembly and not a per-shift time burden. Proper gasket/seal selection and torqueing require trained maintenance and assembly staff. Seals and closures are low-cost, but a failure risks the whole hold batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a loaded IBC tipping during transit
rows.plain: Prevent the loaded large container from tipping over while it is being moved. This protects the container, its contents, and people nearby.
rows.imp_rat: A loaded IBC tipping during transit is central to safe transport and can cause catastrophic spills and personnel injury, so severity is near-maximal even if occurrence is intermittent.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized pallet bases, low centers of gravity, and strapping make tipping in transit uncommon under normal handling. Securing pallets and forklift handling are fast, established practices. Safe forklift and load-securing operation requires certified but common operator skill. A tip loses the whole load and container, but proper handling keeps this rare.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of product or process fluid leaking from the IBC connection during transfer
rows.plain: Prevent the product or processing liquid from leaking where the container is connected to the transfer equipment. This avoids spills, product loss, and exposure to the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Leaking at the connection during transfer is central to the fluid-handling job and, when it occurs, causes product loss, exposure, and downtime, giving it high relevance and strong severity.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized sanitary connections leak-test well, though improper torque or worn gaskets still cause occasional transfer leaks. Verifying connection integrity before transfer adds little time. Standardized fittings and gaskets reduce the skill needed for leak-free connections. Quality seals and fittings are inexpensive and routine.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminants entering through a fill port, vent, or discharge point while the container is in service
rows.plain: Keep dirt, germs, and other unwanted material from getting into the container through its openings while it is being used. This helps protect what is inside.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminants entering through fill port, vent, or discharge while in service is core to keeping contents pure (high relevance) and directly ruins product integrity (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard closures and vent filters cover most in-service contamination paths but lapses still allow ingress. Fitting caps, filters and closed connections adds modest setup time. Keeping ports and vents protected needs consistent operator discipline more than deep expertise. Vent filters and port caps are inexpensive consumables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sanitized or sterilized product-contact surfaces become recontaminated before filling
rows.plain: Keep surfaces that were cleaned or made germ-free from becoming contaminated before the product is put into its container. This helps protect the product from germs and other unwanted material.
rows.imp_rat: Recontamination of already-sanitized surfaces before filling nullifies the sterilization effort and is central to maintaining the sterile boundary; the downstream consequence of a contaminated fill is serious, keeping both proxies high.
rows.sat_rat: Recontamination between sterilization and fill is a well-known weak point where breaches still occur despite closed-system and capping practice. Maintaining hold-time integrity between sterilization and filling constrains scheduling and can force delays. Aseptic handling to preserve sterility demands high operator discipline and technique. Sterile barriers, caps, and controlled environments add cost but are established line items.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that available compatibility evidence does not represent the intended product concentration, temperature, contact period, cleaning, or sterilization exposures
rows.plain: Make sure the available test information matches the product’s actual strength, temperature, contact time, cleaning, and heat or chemical treatment. This helps confirm that the material will remain safe and suitable in real use.
rows.imp_rat: Compatibility evidence that fails to represent actual exposures undermines confidence that the container will safely hold contents—central to the job—and misjudgment can cause silent failures, so both proxies are high.
rows.sat_rat: Available compatibility evidence frequently fails to match the actual concentration, temperature, and exposure conditions, a poorly closed gap today. Locating and reviewing representative compatibility data can be slow and iterative. Judging whether existing evidence covers actual exposure conditions demands specialized technical assessment. Commissioning bespoke compatibility studies is expensive, so teams often rely on generic data.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an unsafe pressure excursion in the transfer path when IBC discharge is interrupted
rows.plain: Prevent a dangerous pressure increase in the material path if the flow from a large container suddenly stops.
rows.imp_rat: An unsafe pressure excursion when discharge is interrupted risks rupture and violent release, so severity is high; it is relevant to reliable, safe discharge but is one of several transfer-phase hazards.
rows.sat_rat: Relief valves and interlocks mitigate this, but transfer-path pressure surges on abrupt discharge interruption remain an inconsistently controlled hazard. Managing an interrupted discharge safely can stall the transfer while pressure is bled. Anticipating pressure excursion on interruption requires process and safety understanding. A pressure excursion can damage lines or product, incurring repair and loss cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unplanned vessel interventions that compromise aseptic control during holding
rows.plain: Reduce unexpected work on process containers that could let germs enter while materials are being held. This helps keep the contents clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned interventions compromising aseptic control are a direct contamination risk central to hold integrity with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures constrain interventions but unplanned ones remain an inherent aseptic risk that standard practice only partially mitigates. Unplanned interventions consume schedule and their aseptic re-qualification adds delay. Executing interventions without compromising aseptic control is highly technique-dependent and a known error source. Each intervention carries requalification and potential material-risk cost that is significant.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that integrity-affecting transit damage or defects remain undetected during arrival inspection
rows.plain: Make sure damage or defects caused during transport are found when the item arrives, especially damage that could affect its protective condition. This helps prevent damaged items from being used unnoticed.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected integrity-affecting transit damage at arrival inspection is central to guaranteeing containment before use; missing it leads to in-service leaks or failures with significant spill and safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Established arrival-inspection practice catches most visible transit damage, but subtle integrity-affecting defects can go undetected without deeper testing. Arrival inspection is a scheduled receiving step, so detecting transit damage does not add unexpected delay. Recognizing integrity-affecting transit damage requires trained inspectors, though basic dent-and-leak checks are within typical receiving competence. Visual arrival inspection is low-cost and routinely resourced.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the container's cleaned interior surfaces becoming recontaminated between cleaning and filling
rows.plain: Keep the inside of the container clean after it has been cleaned and until it is filled. This helps prevent unwanted germs or other dirt from getting inside.
rows.imp_rat: Recontamination between cleaning and filling defeats the cleaning step essential to safe reuse; a compromised interior entering a fill can adulterate product, so both centrality and consequences are high.
rows.sat_rat: Capping and clean-hold-time controls are standard but breaches during handling and storage remain a recurring gap. Clean-hold time limits force filling within a window, occasionally creating rework delays. Protecting cleaned surfaces relies on operator care and correct capping, a modest skill demand. Protective covers and controlled storage add minor recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of combined failure scenarios involving vessel seals, connections, vents, sampling points, or transfers excluded from the assessment
rows.plain: Make sure the safety review considers failures that could happen together at seals, connections, air outlets, sampling points, or transfer points. This helps prevent important combined risks from being overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Excluded combined failure scenarios (seals/vents/sampling) leave unassessed contamination routes; relevant with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Combined and interaction failure scenarios are commonly excluded, undermining assessment completeness. Analyzing combined scenarios adds analysis time but is not the main delay. Reasoning about combined seal/connection/vent/transfer failures demands sophisticated systems expertise that is scarce. Combinatorial analysis is low-cost yet omissions are expensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that held bulk is released before all required disposition conditions are satisfied
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that stored product is released before every required condition for making the release has been met. This helps prevent unsuitable product from reaching the next stage or customers.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing held bulk before disposition conditions are met risks unsafe product proceeding; highly relevant to controlled handoff with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Formal release/disposition checklists and QA sign-off make premature release uncommon, one of the better-controlled failure modes. Release-gate checks are built into batch record review and add defined but predictable time before handoff. Verifying all disposition conditions relies on QA release discipline that is well-trained and procedurally supported. The review effort is a routine, absorbed cost relative to the value of preventing premature release.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a crack, puncture, or weld failure remains after repair
rows.plain: Make sure no crack, hole, or failed joint is left after the repair. This helps prevent the tank from leaking or becoming unsafe.
rows.imp_rat: A crack, puncture, or weld failure remaining after repair reintroduces a direct breach path in the tank shell; highly relevant to containment and severe since repairs are meant to certify structural integrity.
rows.sat_rat: Codes like API NDE of repairs and volumetric methods catch most flaws, though tight cracks and root defects sometimes evade single-method inspection. Post-repair NDE such as radiography or ultrasonics adds inspection time but is a well-established step in weld repair workflows. Reliable detection of residual crack or weld defects depends heavily on certified NDE technicians and their interpretation, a persistent skill dependency. Qualified welders plus multiple NDE passes are costly but standard practice for pressure and containment repairs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that bulk solution enters the vessel before validated aseptic readiness is confirmed
rows.plain: Do not let the bulk product enter the container until the required germ-free conditions have been confirmed. This helps prevent contamination before filling begins.
rows.imp_rat: Bulk entering before validated readiness is confirmed risks contamination from the start; central to the job with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Line-clearance and readiness verification before charging bulk are well-established, making premature charging uncommon. Readiness confirmation is a defined pre-transfer gate that fits normal scheduling. Confirming validated aseptic readiness follows standard checklists requiring moderate trained oversight. The verification is low-cost relative to the loss it prevents.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of hold initiations with vessel interfaces outside the required aseptic state
rows.plain: Start a hold only when the vessel connections are in the required germ-free condition. This helps prevent contamination while work is paused or reviewed.
rows.imp_rat: Hold initiations with interfaces outside required aseptic state introduce contamination at the start; central with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-use checks and SIP/sanitization make most hold initiations start in the required aseptic state, with occasional setup misses. Interface state is verified during setup, adding some pre-hold time but not delaying holding recognition. Correctly achieving the required aseptic state at each interface leans on trained assembly technique. Sterilization/sanitization of interfaces before hold is a routine cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting a liner incompatible with the container or intended fill material
rows.plain: Choose a liner that works safely with the container and the material being put into it. The wrong liner could damage the container or affect its contents.
rows.imp_rat: Selecting an incompatible liner directly undermines the container's core job of safely holding and preserving bulk material; the error can cause leaks, contamination, or product loss, making both relevance and severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor compatibility guides and approved-liner lists exist, but mismatches still occur when selections deviate from standard SKUs. Consulting compatibility charts before liner selection is quick. Correct liner selection depends on materials knowledge that varies by operator and site. Wrong-liner scrap and rework are costly but avoidable with reference data.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of the sterile bulk holding interval lacking interpretable aseptic-condition evidence
rows.plain: Increase the amount of time during which there is clear, understandable evidence that the held sterile product stayed free from contamination. Gaps in this evidence make it harder to know whether the product remained safe.
rows.imp_rat: Portions of the hold interval lacking interpretable aseptic evidence create blind spots undermining disposition; relevant with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Physical-condition evidence is fairly continuous while microbial evidence is sparse, leaving interpretable gaps for sterility status. Continuous parameter monitoring covers most of the interval in real time, but microbial coverage is intermittent. Interpreting whether coverage is adequate takes trained judgement. Data historians and monitoring add cost but are standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an uncontrolled release of contained material during sampling
rows.plain: Prevent the material from escaping unexpectedly while a sample is being taken. This helps protect people, the surroundings, and the material itself.
rows.imp_rat: An uncontrolled release during sampling breaches containment and exposes personnel to the contained material; while sampling is a discrete task, the consequences of exposure are serious.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop and septum samplers reduce release, but grab sampling in practice still leaks material with notable frequency. Closed sampling devices add setup time versus a simple open draw. Operating sample valves without release depends on careful technique. Spilled sample material and exposure cleanup add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an aseptic-containment failure remains undetected during a qualification run
rows.plain: Detect any failure that lets germs escape from or enter the sealed work area during an approval test. This ensures the test does not falsely show that the area is protecting the product.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected aseptic-containment failure during qualification undermines validation confidence; relevant to establishing the hold with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous containment monitoring during runs is imperfect, so an in-run failure can slip past detection. Qualification runs are time-bounded, and detection latency within a run is not primarily a delay problem. Detecting a failure mid-run leans on experienced monitoring and interpretation that is not uniformly present. Undetected failure passing through qualification is very costly later, though monitoring itself adds modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration that leakage or deformation progresses unobserved between periodic checks
rows.plain: Keep leaks or changes in shape from going unnoticed for long periods between checks. This helps problems get found before they become more serious.
rows.imp_rat: Leakage or deformation progressing unobserved between checks directly undermines the container's core containment and integrity job, with escalating severity if a slow failure becomes a large release.
rows.sat_rat: Reliance on scheduled walkarounds rather than continuous monitoring leaves a chronic unobserved window between checks. Periodic manual inspection means leaks or bulging can progress for hours or shifts before detection. Spotting early deformation or seep on rounds depends on inspector attentiveness. Undetected leakage between checks accumulates material loss and cleanup expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of credible contamination pathways not addressed by the acceptance criteria
