rows.plain: Reduce the amount of medicine lost because it sticks to or reacts with nearby surfaces while it is being held in clean conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Active-ingredient loss via contact-material interactions affects potency; relevant to quality with significant consequences though not sterility-critical.
rows.sat_rat: Active-ingredient loss to surfaces (e.g., adsorption) is a known, partially quantified risk that standard recovery studies address but do not fully eliminate. Assays for active loss via adsorption/absorption require method development time before hold decisions. Distinguishing material-driven active loss from other losses needs analytical expertise that is not always available. Recovery and adsorption studies add material and assay costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of product temperature outside its required range during hold
rows.plain: Product should spend less time outside its required temperature range while it is being held. This helps protect the product’s quality.
rows.imp_rat: Product temperature out of range drives degradation/growth; relevant to condition control with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Jacketed vessels with feedback control keep temperature excursions brief in normal operation. Duration outside range is minimized by responsive jacketed temperature control. Automated temperature control reduces operator dependence. Cooling/heating utilities are standard operating costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of vessel pressure outside its required range during hold
rows.plain: Shorten the time that the pressure inside the container is outside its required range while the product is being held. This helps keep the product in the intended condition.
rows.imp_rat: Pressure out of range can threaten the aseptic barrier; relevant to condition control with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Vessel pressure control is mature and keeps excursions brief and rare. Pressure control responds quickly to keep out-of-range duration short. Automated pressure regulation reduces operator involvement. Pressure control uses routine gas utilities.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residue from another product contacting the sterile bulk during transfer
rows.plain: Prevent leftover material from a different product from touching the germ-free product while it is being moved. This helps avoid mixing products or contaminating the product.
rows.imp_rat: Residue from another product contacting sterile bulk is cross-contamination affecting safety; relevant with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning validation and single-use paths make cross-product residue contact uncommon. Prevention is via cleaning validation, done before transfer, not during. Depends on validated CIP rather than daily skill. Cleaning validation and dedicated/single-use paths add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of cleaning residue remaining on product-contact surfaces before the next use
rows.plain: Remove as much cleaning residue as possible from surfaces that touch the product before using the container again, so the product is not contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning residue remaining on product-contact surfaces can cross-contaminate the next batch, tying directly to reuse cleanliness; the consequence is product quality impact and possible cross-contamination, warranting moderately high scores.
rows.sat_rat: Riboflavin/swab/ATP verification and validated cycles catch most residue, but geometry-hidden films and variable soils still cause occasional failures requiring re-clean. CIP/COP wash cycles are time-standardized and run largely unattended, so residual-residue removal adds little duration to the maintenance flow. Validated wash recipes and swab-test protocols mean operators follow set programs rather than exercising scarce judgement. Water, detergent and energy per wash cycle are modest and well understood, though rework washes add some avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of stored pressure, electrical energy, or agitator motion being released during tank disconnection
rows.plain: Prevent trapped pressure, stored electricity, or movement of the mixer from being released while the tank is disconnected, so workers are not hurt.
rows.imp_rat: Release of stored pressure, electrical, or agitator energy during disconnection is a maintenance-phase hazard, somewhat peripheral to core processing (relevance 6); when it occurs it causes severe crush, burn, or electrocution injuries (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: LOTO is a mature, widely enforced practice that reliably prevents most uncontrolled energy release, with residual failures tied to overlooked sources. Energy-isolation steps add time but are compact compared to the overall disconnection effort. Lock-out/tag-out is a codified competency, though correctly identifying all stored-energy sources still needs a trained eye. Isolation hardware and blinding materials are cheap relative to the job.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between emergence of critical hold-parameter drift and operator recognition
rows.plain: Shorten the time between an important product condition starting to drift and the operator noticing it. This allows problems to be corrected sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Time between parameter drift and operator recognition drives condition-control harm; relevant to detection with moderate-serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Recognition often waits for threshold alarms, so early drift frequently goes unnoticed until it is well developed. The delay between drift onset and operator recognition is central and depends on monitoring frequency. Spotting early drift in trends requires attentive, experienced operators rather than waiting for hard alarms. Trend-alarming and continuous data reduce recognition delay where the investment is made.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of gaps in critical hold-parameter observations during aseptic holding
rows.plain: Shorten the gaps in recording important conditions while the product is being held in a germ-free environment. This makes it easier to notice problems before they grow.
rows.imp_rat: Gaps in critical-parameter observations create blind windows; relevant to monitoring completeness with moderate-serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Manual or periodic observation leaves recurring coverage gaps, though continuous systems close them where deployed. Observation gaps are inherently durations of missing coverage, the direct time loss here. Manual observation regimes depend on staff diligence to avoid missed rounds. Continuous monitoring eliminates gaps but at instrumentation cost not always incurred.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a vessel condition excursion invalidates the validated holding allowance
rows.plain: Prevent changes in the container’s condition from making the approved waiting time unreliable. This helps ensure the product remains safe and acceptable while it is being held.
rows.imp_rat: A vessel excursion invalidating the holding allowance affects disposition; relevant to controlled hold with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: The logic linking excursions to hold validity is often applied inconsistently and after the fact. Linking an excursion to hold-allowance invalidation is a review-time activity, not real-time. Judging whether an excursion voids the validated hold requires QA/technical expertise. Conservative invalidation may waste bulk unnecessarily, a cost tradeoff.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that monitoring values are altered before the sterility assessment
rows.plain: Prevent recorded monitoring results from being changed before the sterility check. This helps ensure the decision is based on trustworthy information.
rows.imp_rat: Altered monitoring values before sterility assessment corrupts the disposition basis and integrity/compliance; relevant with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Modern audit trails and access controls make unauthorized alteration of monitoring values rare where deployed. Electronic capture is immediate, so little delay exists before assessment. Audit-trailed systems reduce reliance on operator vigilance to preserve values. Validated data-integrity systems are a significant but established investment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that foundation bearing capacity is below the fully loaded tank requirement
rows.plain: The floor support should be strong enough to hold the tank when it is full and fully equipped. A weak support could crack, sink, or fail.
rows.imp_rat: Inadequate foundation bearing capacity is a precondition for safe loaded operation but is a one-time siting concern (relevance 6); if it fails the tank tips or ruptures with severe consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Mature codified practice (API B, geotechnical standards) makes bearing-capacity failures uncommon where proper investigation is done, though undetected subsurface variation still causes occasional settlement problems. Geotechnical investigation and foundation design add weeks to project schedules but are routinely planned into preparation timelines. Requires licensed geotechnical and structural engineers, whose expertise is standard on tank projects of any scale. Soil borings, load calculations and foundation engineering are established budgeted line items, not surprise costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of routing the sterile solution to the wrong fill-finish destination during discharge
rows.plain: Make sure the sterile solution is sent to the correct destination for final filling and packaging. This prevents the product from going to the wrong place.
rows.imp_rat: Routing to the wrong fill-finish destination is a mix-up affecting product identity/traceability; relevant with serious consequences though not sterility per se.
rows.sat_rat: Line verification and labeling reduce misrouting, but multi-destination facilities still see occasional errors. Routing setup is part of transfer prep and does not add notable time. Correct valve/line lineup depends on operator verification and can be misrouted. Misrouting can waste a batch, but hardware for correct routing is standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incorrect critical-condition limits are applied to the held batch
rows.plain: Make sure the correct limits for important conditions are used when checking the held batch. This helps identify whether the batch is still within its approved requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect critical-condition limits applied to the batch can pass unacceptable states; relevant with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Master records generally carry correct limits, though manual application can introduce errors. Limit assignment is a setup activity, not time-pressured. Applying correct critical-condition limits needs technical product knowledge. Wrong limits risk batch quality but control cost is low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of vessel batches assigned an incorrect validated holding limit
rows.plain: Reduce the number of batches assigned the wrong approved maximum time for being held in a container. This helps prevent products from being held longer than allowed.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect validated holding limit assigned risks holding beyond safe duration; relevant to controlled hold with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Master batch records typically fix the correct limit, but manual assignment errors do occur. Assigning the holding limit is a one-time setup step per batch. Selecting the correct validated hold limit requires knowing product-specific data. An incorrect limit can waste bulk or risk quality, but the control itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of aseptic vessel storage beyond the approved pre-use limit
rows.plain: Use the sterile vessel before the approved storage time runs out. This helps prevent the vessel from being stored too long before use.
rows.imp_rat: Aseptic vessel storage beyond pre-use limit risks starting with lapsed sterility; relevant with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Expiry dating on prepared vessels makes exceeding the pre-use limit relatively rare when tracking is followed. Pre-use storage limits are calendar-tracked so overruns are usually caught before use. Compliance relies on routine tracking rather than specialized skill. Expired vessel prep forces re-sterilization, an avoidable but bounded cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that correcting one hold parameter drives another parameter outside acceptable limits
rows.plain: When fixing one holding condition, avoid pushing another condition outside its allowed range. This keeps the material within safe and approved conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Correcting one parameter driving another out of limit causes cascading excursions; relevant to condition control with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Coupled-parameter interactions are managed case-by-case and correction sometimes overshoots. Correcting one parameter and watching another takes real-time attention. Balancing coupled parameters (e.g., temp affecting pH/DO) demands considerable process expertise. Overcorrection can spoil a batch, a cost risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that vessel intervention destabilizes an in-limit hold parameter
rows.plain: Avoid making unnecessary changes to the vessel while a holding condition is within its allowed range. This reduces the chance that the condition becomes unstable.
rows.imp_rat: An intervention destabilizing an in-limit parameter can create new excursions; relevant to condition control with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Interventions on complex vessels not uncommonly perturb otherwise stable parameters, a recognized human-factor risk. Interventions are brief, so time is not the main loss when one destabilizes an in-limit parameter. Avoiding collateral disturbance during an intervention depends heavily on operator finesse and system knowledge. A destabilized parameter can waste batch value, but the intervention itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required vessel parameters lacking current verified status during holding
rows.plain: Reduce the share of required container settings that do not have a current, confirmed status while the contents are being held. This helps ensure the container is being monitored with reliable information.
rows.imp_rat: Required parameters lacking current verified status weakens the assurance basis; relevant with moderate-serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Some parameters routinely lack a current verified status between checks, leaving partial visibility of true vessel state. Verified status lapses as time passes between confirmations of each parameter. Keeping all parameters currently verified demands disciplined tracking across many data points. Verification effort and systems add ongoing overhead.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that held-solution investigation samples are compromised after collection
rows.plain: Keep samples taken during an investigation of a held solution from being damaged, contaminated, or mixed up after collection. Reliable samples are needed to find out what happened.
rows.imp_rat: Compromised investigation samples after collection distort conclusions; relevant to disposition accuracy with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Post-collection compromise of investigation samples still occurs, though holding procedures reduce it. Timely processing of investigation samples is needed, and delays degrade them. Preserving sample integrity post-collection depends on trained handling. Cold-chain and controlled storage add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that stored material loses sterility during withdrawal
rows.plain: Material that must remain free of germs should stay clean and protected while it is removed from storage. Losing that protection could make it unsafe or unusable.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of sterility during withdrawal applies only to sterile-storage contexts, limiting general relevance, but severity is high where a sterile product becomes unusable or hazardous to end users.
rows.sat_rat: Even with SIP and closed transfer, breaks in aseptic technique during connection make loss of sterility a persistent, hard-to-eliminate risk in the applicable sector. Sterile withdrawal requires careful aseptic setup that slows the operation. Maintaining sterility during withdrawal is highly technique-dependent and demands trained aseptic operators. Sterile filters, single-use assemblies and steam-in-place add material and utility cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing an incorrect additive into the tank
rows.plain: The person should make sure the correct substance is added to the tank. Adding the wrong one could affect the process or damage the equipment.
rows.imp_rat: An incorrect additive corrupts the formulation the tank is processing, moderately central to the job (relevance 6); it usually results in an off-spec or reactive batch requiring disposal (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-enforced dispensing and barcode checks reduce errors where present, but manual additive handling still leaves a persistent error gap. Additive verification adds minimal time when scanning is used but slows manual weigh-and-charge steps. Correct additive selection leans on operator diligence and recipe-following where no automated dispensing exists. Verification tools are cheap, but a wrong additive can ruin a batch and waste material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of identity conflicts among the held product, vessel, and batch record
rows.plain: Reduce the number of disagreements between the identity of the held product, its container, and its production record. This helps ensure everyone can tell exactly which product and batch is being held.
rows.imp_rat: Identity conflicts among product/vessel/record threaten traceability and correct disposition; relevant with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Batch record reconciliation catches most conflicts, though manual transcription creates residual discrepancies. Reconciling identities is a documentation-review activity done at defined points. Detecting conflicts among product, vessel, and record needs attentive review. Reconciliation labor is modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the operator's available-volume estimate and the actual available volume during dispensing
rows.plain: Make the estimated amount of product available during filling match the actual amount as closely as possible. This helps prevent the process from running short or being overfilled.
rows.imp_rat: A wrong available-volume estimate during dispensing causes overfill or spillage, moderately central to the fill/dispense role; consequences are meaningful but usually recoverable, so both are mid-to-high.
rows.sat_rat: Sight gauges and level markings give rough guidance, but the estimate frequently deviates from actual, causing recurring headroom surprises. Checking available volume with sight gauges or level marks is quick but adds a verification step. Estimating remaining headroom from a sight tube or dipstick relies on operator reading and interpretation. Misjudging available volume risks overflow spills or underfilling, wasting material or time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual contents being released while removing the IBC from service
rows.plain: Prevent leftover material from coming out when the large container is taken out of use. This helps avoid spills and unwanted contact with the material.
rows.imp_rat: Residual contents released while removing an IBC from service is an end-of-life handling error, somewhat peripheral to the primary containment job, but the spill of hazardous/pharmaceutical residue creates real exposure and cleanup consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Established triple-rinse and emptying protocols catch most residual release risk, but pooled residues in valves, sumps and dead legs still occasionally slip through, leaving recurring gaps. Decommissioning cleaning and residue removal follow scheduled procedures that add time but rarely bottleneck the disposal stage. Trained decommissioning technicians and standard emptying/rinsing SOPs reduce the finesse required to remove contents before service exit. Cleaning solvents, PPE and wash-out water impose modest recurring cost that facilities routinely budget for.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in critical hold conditions across the sterile bulk solution
rows.plain: Keep important storage conditions as steady as possible throughout the sterile liquid. Large changes could affect its quality.
rows.imp_rat: Variation in hold conditions across the solution affects uniformity and quality; moderately relevant with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Designed agitation and jacketing give reasonable uniformity, but stratification and localized gradients in large vessels remain a recurring concern. Achieving uniformity is fast where agitation is designed in, but slow to correct where mixing is marginal. Setting adequate agitation and interpreting spatial variation requires process understanding. Agitators, jackets and mixing studies add engineering cost to reduce spatial variation.

rows.source_job: Position container at point of use
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination of container connection surfaces during positioning
rows.plain: Avoid contaminating the surfaces where containers will be connected while placing them. This helps keep the product and connection clean.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of connection surfaces during positioning affects transfer integrity (moderate-high relevance) and can introduce contaminants into product, causing quality loss (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Connection surfaces are frequently exposed during positioning and get contaminated without protective caps in place. Wiping and protecting connection surfaces during positioning takes brief but often-skipped effort. Keeping connection surfaces clean while maneuvering depends on careful operator handling. Caps and wipes are cheap, so failures cost cleaning and rejected connections rather than hardware.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration the affected hold parameter remains outside its acceptable limit after response initiation
rows.plain: Shorten the time that the affected process condition stays outside its acceptable range after the response begins. This helps return the process to a safe, acceptable state sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Duration a parameter stays out of limit after response drives cumulative harm; relevant to recovery with moderate-serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Restoration is generally achieved but the time to fully return within limits varies with response quality and system inertia. This need is squarely about shortening the post-response duration outside limits, which control systems address directly. Effective, quick restoration still depends on the responder choosing the right corrective magnitude. Automated corrective control is cost-efficient where present but absent in manual setups.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of bulk solution quality degradation before controlled conditions are restored
rows.plain: Limit how much the stored liquid loses quality before normal controlled conditions are brought back. This helps protect the liquid while a problem is being corrected.
rows.imp_rat: Quality degradation before conditions are restored reduces product acceptability; relevant with moderate-serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Prompt control usually limits degradation, but the extent depends on excursion severity and product sensitivity. Degradation accrues with the time conditions stay off, so quick restoration limits it. Judging degradation impact requires product and process knowledge. Quality degradation can mean lost batch value, a significant cost driver.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous residues while recovering components from the retired container
rows.plain: Protect the technician from harmful leftover material while removing usable parts from the retired container. The parts should be recovered without exposing the person to the residue.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to hazardous residues during retirement is a serious safety consequence, but it occurs at end-of-life recovery rather than the container's core operational job, so relevance is moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-cleaning before recovery helps, but hidden residues in fittings and crevices still expose technicians during salvage more often than desired. Component recovery from a retired IBC adds handling time proportional to how carefully residues are managed. Avoiding residue exposure while salvaging valves and fittings relies on technician care and correct PPE selection. PPE and decon supplies for recovery work are a recurring but manageable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of temperature variation throughout the stored contents
rows.plain: Keep the temperature of all the stored material as even as possible. This helps protect the material from becoming too hot or too cold in different areas.
rows.imp_rat: Temperature variation across stored contents affects product stability for temperature-sensitive materials and is fairly central to preservation, with meaningful but generally manageable severity.
rows.sat_rat: Insulated or jacketed IBCs and temperature-controlled rooms help, but internal stratification and cold spots persist without active circulation. Bringing stored contents to uniform temperature via jackets or rooms takes considerable dwell time. Managing heat-up and hold profiles for viscous or stratifying material takes process knowledge. Heating or cooling jackets and conditioned storage consume significant energy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of bulk material lost through leaks during dispensing
rows.plain: Prevent bulk material from leaking out while measured amounts are being poured. This saves material and keeps the work area clean.
rows.imp_rat: Bulk material lost through leaks during dispensing directly relates to containment integrity, but the consequence is mainly material/yield loss rather than safety catastrophe unless the material is hazardous, so severity is moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Standard bottom-discharge valves with dust caps and integral seals reliably contain dispensing in most service, with intermittent seal-wear leaks remaining. Leaks at dispense valves are usually caught quickly once flow starts, so lost time is modest. Proper gasket seating and valve operation depend on operator care, but the task is routine for trained staff. Mature valve and coupling seals limit material loss, though occasional weeping still wastes product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between detection of an unacceptable tank condition and onset of quenching
rows.plain: Start cooling or stopping the tank contents as soon as an unsafe condition is found. This helps limit the effects of the problem.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between detecting an unacceptable condition and quenching affects how fast an event is arrested, moderately central to safe operation (relevance 6); slow response lets an excursion escalate to serious harm (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Where safety-instrumented quench systems exist they respond consistently, but many installations still rely on slower operator intervention. Automated quench/emergency-cooling interlocks trigger within seconds of a detected excursion, but manual-response designs still lag. Emergency response depends on operator recognition and correct action where automated quench is absent, demanding trained vigilance. Quench systems and emergency cooling add capital cost but are justified for reactive processes.

rows.source_job: Store idle containers under protective conditions
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of corrosion or seal degradation caused by storage-environment exposure
rows.plain: Protect stored containers from conditions that cause rust or damage their seals. Good protection helps them remain usable while they are not in use.
rows.imp_rat: Corrosion or seal degradation from storage exposure undermines containment integrity (moderate-high relevance) and can lead to leaks over time, though usually detectable before catastrophic failure (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Storage environments are often uncontrolled and seal condition is judged subjectively, so degradation-driven failures still recur. Corrosion and seal degradation develop slowly, so time pressure is not the felt loss during maintenance inspection. Detecting early corrosion or seal fatigue relies on inspector experience that varies across facilities. Controlled indoor storage and periodic seal replacement are affordable but not always funded consistently.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of contact-material particles introduced into the sterile bulk solution during aseptic hold
rows.plain: Reduce the number of particles that break off from contacting materials and enter the product while it is being held. This helps keep the product clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Particulate introduction from contact materials affects product quality/patient safety via particulates; moderately relevant with significant consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Sub-visible and visible particle controls per USP are mature and widely deployed, making contributed particulate a well-managed risk. Particulate testing is routine and fast within established release timelines. Standardized particulate counting methods reduce reliance on specialized skill. Particle testing is inexpensive relative to full characterization.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an uncontrolled release of stored pressure during testing
rows.plain: Prevent the stored pressure from escaping suddenly during the test. This protects people nearby from being hurt.
rows.imp_rat: Uncontrolled release of stored pressure during testing is confined to the test/maintenance phase rather than core operation, but a pressure burst can seriously injure technicians, giving high severity against moderate relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Regulated test pressure with relief devices is mature engineering practice, making uncontrolled release uncommon but not impossible. Controlled pressurization and venting procedures extend test time but are predictable. Pressure testing follows established protocols requiring competent but not rare technicians. Relief valves and controlled test rigs are standard, moderate-cost equipment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the final fill level deviates from the required range
rows.plain: Keep the amount of material in the filled container within the required range. This ensures each container has neither too much nor too little.
rows.imp_rat: Final fill level deviating from the required range is central to the container's fill accuracy and can cause overpressure, spillage, or under-delivery; both proxies are solidly high.
rows.sat_rat: Weigh-cell and level-sensor fill control reliably lands the final level within range in most standard operations. Automated cutoff at target level avoids slow manual topping to the required range. Level-controlled filling reduces reliance on operator watching the fill. Hitting the fill range avoids overfill spillage and underfill rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation of the dispensed quantity from the predetermined target
rows.plain: Make the amount released from each container as close as possible to the intended amount. This keeps the product amount consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation of dispensed quantity from target is core to the container's dispensing function and directly affects downstream batch accuracy; both relevance and severity are substantial though often correctable.
rows.sat_rat: Flow meters and weigh-scale dosing are mature and consistently land near the predetermined target with small residual error. Metered or load-cell dispensing hits target quickly without prolonged manual topping. Automated cutoff reduces reliance on operator judgement for hitting the target quantity. Accurate dispense minimizes give-away and rework from off-target quantities.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that quality disposition remains incomplete at the scheduled fill-finish handoff
rows.plain: Complete all required quality decisions before the scheduled handoff to filling and finishing. This prevents the next step from starting with unfinished approvals.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete disposition at handoff risks proceeding without release confidence; relevant to controlled handoff but consequence often a delay.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduling and review coordination is partial, so disposition remaining incomplete at handoff is a common recurring gap. Disposition completion frequently races the scheduled handoff, and incomplete-at-handoff situations recur. Timely disposition depends on experienced reviewers being available, a scarce-attention constraint under load. Late disposition delays fill-finish and can idle expensive downstream capacity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of transferring contaminants to clean stainless steel IBC surfaces while establishing grounding and bonding connections
rows.plain: Keep dirt or other unwanted material from getting onto the clean metal surfaces of the container while making safe electrical connections. This protects the cleanliness of the container and helps prevent sparks.
rows.imp_rat: Transferring contaminants during grounding/bonding is a real cleanliness concern but is a narrower procedural step; severity is moderate since it affects surface cleanliness rather than immediate containment failure.
rows.sat_rat: Establishing metal-to-metal bonding contact inherently risks touching cleaned surfaces, and current practice relies on careful handling rather than a design that prevents it. Wiping or re-cleaning a contaminated bonding contact point adds only minor rework time. Avoiding contact contamination during clamping depends on operator gowning discipline and aseptic technique that varies by person. The consumables (wipes, sanitizer) are cheap, but a contamination event can scrap product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the suspended tank moving uncontrollably during removal
rows.plain: Prevent the hanging tank from swinging or moving unexpectedly while it is being removed. This helps protect people and nearby equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Uncontrolled movement of a suspended tank during removal is a decommissioning/rigging event peripheral to processing (relevance 4); a dropped tank causes fatal crush injuries and major damage (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Standard rigging practice controls most lifts, but shifting centre of gravity from residual contents and awkward geometry still cause uncontrolled movement. Rigging and lift planning add setup time before the tank can be moved. Safe suspended-load control depends on qualified riggers and a lift plan, a genuinely skill-intensive task. Cranes, spreader bars and certified rigging represent significant mobilized cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a sterile-boundary breach places the bulk solution on quality hold before handoff
rows.plain: Avoid having the main liquid put on hold because something may have broken the clean, germ-free boundary before it is handed over. This matters because the liquid may need extra checks or could be unusable.
rows.imp_rat: A boundary breach placing bulk on hold is the correct protective outcome; relevant but consequence is production delay rather than catastrophic release.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation procedures reliably trigger holds when a breach is recognized, but unrecognized or under-reported breaches leave a residual gap. Placing bulk on quality hold after a breach is procedurally prompt once the breach is reported, but reporting can lag. Deciding that a breach warrants quality hold requires QA judgement that is generally codified but still interpretation-dependent. Quality holds consume schedule and material value, but the control action itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation of stored material quality attributes from approved limits
rows.plain: Keep the stored material’s quality as close as possible to its approved limits over time. This matters because the material may become unsuitable if its quality changes too much.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation of stored material quality attributes from approved limits matters to storage integrity but is more about product preservation than containment safety, so both relevance and severity are moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Blanketing, temperature control and scheduled sampling cover most cases but slow degradation, stratification and contamination still cause recurring out-of-limit events. Quality checks depend on periodic sampling and lab turnaround, so deviations can develop between tests before being noticed. Interpreting quality drift and root causes needs trained QC/process staff, making detection dependent on scarce expertise. Off-spec material, rework or disposal represents significant loss when limits are breached during holding.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician injury from uncontrolled shifting or collapse of container sections during dismantling
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that sections of a container will shift suddenly or fall while a technician takes it apart. This matters because falling or moving sections could injure the technician.
rows.imp_rat: Uncontrolled shifting or collapse during dismantling is a peripheral end-of-life activity relative to the IBC's core containment job, but the consequence—technician injury—is severe. Moderate relevance, high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Standard dismantling practice reduces collapse events, but heavy stainless sections still shift unexpectedly at a nontrivial rate. Bracing and controlled sectioning add time to dismantling but are generally built into the task. Preventing uncontrolled shifting or collapse depends on rigging and cutting-sequence judgement that experienced technicians handle better than novices. Supports, slings and lifting aids are affordable and commonly available.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that product-contact surfaces become contaminated during damage inspection
rows.plain: Avoid getting dirt or germs onto surfaces that touch the product while checking for damage. This helps keep the product safe.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of product-contact surfaces during damage inspection is a narrower handling-step risk; when it occurs it compromises purity for the next fill, a serious but recoverable consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Standard controlled-inspection practice keeps contact surfaces clean in most cases, but improvised or rushed inspections still cause occasional contamination. Protecting contact surfaces during damage inspection follows established handling procedures that add little time. Avoiding contamination while inspecting requires trained personnel using proper gowning and handling technique, and that attention is not always present at receiving. Clean handling consumables and controlled inspection areas add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that supplier claims about stainless steel IBC suitability for sterile fill-finish cannot be substantiated
rows.plain: Make sure claims that a large stainless-steel container is suitable for making sterile products can be supported by reliable evidence. This helps prevent choosing equipment that is not fit for the job.
rows.imp_rat: Unsubstantiated supplier claims about sterile suitability is largely a qualification/documentation gap somewhat peripheral to the physical containment job, though unverified suitability can lead to compliance findings and quality risk of moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier claims are frequently taken partly on trust because full substantiating evidence is inconsistently provided, leaving a real residual risk of unverified suitability. Substantiating supplier suitability claims requires requesting and reviewing validation dossiers, which slows the award decision. Critically evaluating a vendor's sterile fill-finish claims demands auditor-level expertise to spot unsupported or overstated assertions. Independent verification or third-party testing to confirm claims adds meaningful cost that buyers often try to avoid.

rows.stk: Waste Management Technician or Environmental Health and Safety Officer
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incompatible residual materials being combined during disposal staging
rows.plain: Avoid mixing leftover materials that should not be combined while they are being prepared for disposal. This helps prevent dangerous reactions or other problems.
rows.imp_rat: Combining incompatible residual materials during disposal staging risks dangerous reactions, but this occurs at disposal rather than the core containment job, so relevance is limited while severity is high.
rows.sat_rat: Segregation rules and compatibility charts are standard, but commingling of incompatible residuals during staging still happens when labeling or knowledge is lacking. Segregated staging adds minor handling time before consolidation or pickup. Recognizing chemical incompatibilities at staging requires hazmat knowledge that is not always present at the disposal floor. Separate containment for incompatible streams costs modestly more in space and containers.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of associating the sample with the wrong intermediate bulk container or batch
rows.plain: Avoid linking a sample to the wrong large container or production batch. This helps ensure that test results are tied to the right material.
rows.imp_rat: Misassociating a sample with the wrong IBC or batch is an information-integrity error that can lead to wrong release decisions and recalls; less central to physical containment but severe in regulated settings.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode or ID labeling reduces mix-ups, but manual sample-to-batch association still produces recurring traceability errors. Labeling and logging the sample at draw is a quick manual step. Correct sample-to-container association depends on operator attention and clerical discipline. A mislabeled sample can condemn or misrelease a batch, a costly error.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of product pH deviation from its required range during hold
rows.plain: Keep the product's acidity level within the required range while it is waiting. This helps preserve the product's quality and safety.
rows.imp_rat: pH deviation affects product stability and integrity; relevant to condition control with moderate-serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: pH is usually stable during hold but probe drift makes deviation detection less reliable than temperature. pH drift during hold develops slowly, giving some time to respond. Online pH probes drift and require skilled calibration/interpretation. pH excursions can spoil a batch; probes are inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hazardous residues make the retired container unsafe for recipient reuse
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that dangerous leftover material makes a retired container unsafe for someone else to use. This helps protect the person receiving the container.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous residues making a retired container unsafe for reuse endangers downstream recipients, but reuse safety is an end-of-life concern peripheral to the core job, though severity is high.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning validation for reused containers is well established in pharma, yet residue carryover making a unit unsafe for recipients still slips through occasionally. Decontamination verification before releasing a container for reuse adds a testing/hold step. Judging whether residues render a unit unsafe for reuse needs analytical or cleaning-validation competence. Verification cleaning and sampling add cost, sometimes exceeding a used container's value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product-contact surface obscured by residual product soil before sanitation or sterilization
rows.plain: Leave as little product residue as possible covering surfaces that touch the product before they are cleaned or sterilized. This helps the cleaning and sterilizing work properly.
rows.imp_rat: Product soil obscuring surfaces before sanitation reduces the effectiveness of cleaning, which is central to reuse; the consequence is inadequate decontamination leading to contamination, moderately severe.
rows.sat_rat: Visual and riboflavin coverage checks help, but residual soil obscuring surfaces before sanitation remains inconsistently controlled. Pre-clean soil inspection adds a minor step before sanitation. Judging visible soil coverage relies on operator inspection and can be subjective. Pre-rinse and inspection add small incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of drug product nonuniformity within the vessel before fill-finish begins
rows.plain: Keep the product evenly mixed throughout the tank before filling starts. An uneven mixture can make the finished containers differ from one another.
rows.imp_rat: Nonuniformity before fill-finish affects dose consistency; moderately relevant to product quality with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Validated mixing reliably achieves uniformity for most products, with rare issues for sensitive formulations. Adequate pre-fill mixing takes defined time that is scheduled. Mixing is largely automated per validated recipes, low ongoing skill. Mixing energy is a trivial cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of dissolved oxygen outside the product-specific range during hold
rows.plain: Keep the amount of oxygen dissolved in the product within the required range while it is being held. This helps keep the product in the right condition.
rows.imp_rat: Dissolved oxygen out of range affects oxidative product stability; relevant to condition control with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Nitrogen overlay reliably holds DO for products that need it, though DO monitoring is not always continuous. DO changes gradually so response time is usually adequate. Overlay gas control is largely automated, low skill demand. Inert gas overlay is a modest utility cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of vessel-pressure deviation from allowable limits during transfer
rows.plain: Keep the pressure inside the tank within the allowed limits while material is moved into it. Stable pressure helps the transfer happen safely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Vessel-pressure deviation during transfer can stress the boundary; relevant with moderate-serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer pressure stays within limits reliably under normal automated control. Pressure during transfer is actively controlled in real time. Automated regulation minimizes operator judgement. Standard instrumentation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterile bulk exceeding the allowable vessel fill level during transfer
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that too much germ-free product liquid is put into a vessel during transfer. This helps prevent the vessel from being overfilled.
rows.imp_rat: Exceeding allowable fill level during transfer risks overflow/breach; relevant to safe operation with moderate-serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: High-level alarms and load cells reliably prevent overfill in most vessels. Level control during fill does not materially slow transfer. Level instrumentation reduces reliance on operator watching, lowering skill demand. Level sensors/load cells are standard low-cost instrumentation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incoming bulk volume exceeds verified available vessel capacity
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the incoming product liquid is more than the vessel can safely hold. This helps prevent overfilling during transfer.
rows.imp_rat: Incoming volume exceeding verified capacity risks overflow/breach; relevant to safe transfer with moderate-serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-record capacity checks reliably prevent volume mismatch, though manual entry errors occur rarely. Capacity verification is a quick pre-transfer check. Comparing incoming volume to capacity is simple arithmetic supported by records. No significant cost beyond planning effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in evidence confirming containment-boundary integrity during holding
rows.plain: Make it clearer whether the sealed area is still intact while materials are being held. This helps people know that outside contamination cannot get in.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in integrity evidence weakens confident disposition; moderately relevant to decision quality with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-decay and similar tests give reasonably unambiguous boundary integrity evidence, though indirect for microbial ingress. Integrity confirmation results are available quickly for pressure-based tests. Interpreting integrity evidence requires modest, well-proceduralized skill. Integrity test equipment is a modest standard cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of stored material exceeding approved temperature limits during holding
rows.plain: Keep stored material from becoming hotter than the allowed limit while it is being held. This helps protect the material and keep it within approved conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Exceeding approved temperature limits during holding degrades material and can raise pressure risk, but is a controllable environmental parameter rather than direct containment, so relevance and severity are moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Jacketed tanks with automated control and alarms keep temperature within limits well, with residual failures from sensor drift or utility loss. Temperature control loops respond continuously so excursions during holding are corrected promptly. Setpoint control is largely automated, needing little skill once tuned, though troubleshooting jacket/coil faults needs technicians. Heating/cooling utilities and insulation carry ongoing energy cost but are routine and budgeted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the tank is decommissioned while assigned to an active controlled activity
rows.plain: Make sure the tank is not taken out of service while it is still assigned to an ongoing controlled activity. This prevents the activity from being disrupted or handled incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Decommissioning a tank while assigned to an active controlled activity is a process-control lapse rather than a core containment function, giving moderate relevance, but consequences can be severe if the activity depends on the tank.
rows.sat_rat: Isolation permits, lock-out/tag-out and management-of-change procedures are mature at regulated sites, but record gaps in older or contractor-run facilities still cause occasional mistaken retirements. Verifying active-service status before decommissioning adds a check that is usually quick when tank registers and permits are current. It relies on operators knowing which tanks are still tied to controlled activities, a judgement that can slip in complex facilities. Decommissioning an in-service tank causes expensive process disruption, so the incentive to avoid it is strong.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between recorded and actual fill or dispense volumes
rows.plain: Make the recorded amounts filled or dispensed match the amounts that were actually used as closely as possible. This keeps usage and production records accurate.
rows.imp_rat: A discrepancy between recorded and actual fill volumes compromises inventory and batch records; it is relevant to reliable dispensing but often an administrative/traceability issue with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Weigh-based reconciliation and batch records are standard and largely accurate, though manual entry and rounding still create recurring discrepancies. Automated data capture removes manual logging delays at fill or dispense. Digital recording lessens dependence on operator transcription discipline. Reconciliation discrepancies drive inventory losses and audit cost when records diverge.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical tank functions operating outside approved acceptance limits during verification
rows.plain: Make sure important tank functions stay within their approved limits while the tank installation is being checked. This confirms the tank is working safely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Critical functions operating outside approved acceptance limits during verification is a qualification check moderately tied to readiness (relevance 6); missing it releases a marginally compliant tank to service (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Formal commissioning checklists and acceptance protocols catch most out-of-limit functions, though incomplete test coverage and rushed handovers still let some through. Acceptance verification against limits is a defined commissioning step that adds bounded time. Requires commissioning engineers who understand acceptance criteria, a skill that is common but not trivial. Verification testing consumes labor and utilities but prevents costlier downstream failures.

rows.stk: Waste Management Technician or Environmental Health and Safety Officer
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that IBC remnants are routed to a disposal pathway not approved for their residual contents
rows.plain: Make sure leftover material from a large reusable container goes to a disposal method approved for what remains inside it. This helps prevent unsafe handling or improper disposal.
rows.imp_rat: Routing IBC remnants to an unapproved disposal pathway is an end-of-life compliance error, peripheral to the operational job, but severity is high due to regulatory and environmental harm from residual contents.
rows.sat_rat: Waste manifesting and profiling systems are mature, so approved-pathway routing is usually correct, though residue misclassification still occasionally sends remnants down the wrong route. Waste-stream classification and routing add administrative time before pickup. Correct pathway selection requires waste-characterization knowledge that trained EHS staff generally provide. Approved hazardous-waste disposal pathways are considerably more expensive than general routes, driving occasional misrouting to save money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the repaired area fails containment acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make sure the repaired area keeps unwanted material contained and passes the required checks. A failed repair could allow leaks or contamination.
rows.imp_rat: A repaired area failing containment acceptance criteria is a repair-specific concern, less central to routine operation (relevance 5); if the repair fails in service a breach and release results (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Codified repair-and-retest workflows make acceptance failures uncommon though not rare enough to be trivial. Post-repair testing and re-inspection extend the outage but are a standard closeout step. Repair quality and its verification lean on qualified welders and inspectors following welding procedure specifications. Rework when a repair fails acceptance is expensive, but qualified procedures keep failure rates modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of excursion recurrence after vessel conditions appear restored
rows.plain: Prevent a problem from returning after the vessel seems to be back to normal. This helps ensure the correction lasts.
rows.imp_rat: Excursion recurrence after apparent restoration indicates unresolved root cause; relevant to reliable recovery with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Conditions that appear restored sometimes drift out again when the underlying cause was not addressed. Recurrence extends total off-condition time but the need is about the recurrence itself. Preventing recurrence requires correctly diagnosing root cause, a skilled task. Repeat excursions consume resources and risk product each time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting aseptic compliance determinations for the same held batch
rows.plain: Make sure people do not reach different conclusions about whether the same held batch was kept free from contamination. This provides one clear basis for deciding what to do with the batch.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting compliance determinations create disposition confusion; moderately relevant with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Governance forums reduce but do not eliminate conflicting determinations on the same batch, a recurring friction. Reconciling conflicting compliance determinations adds review cycles and delays disposition. Conflicts arise from differing expert interpretations, so resolution is heavily judgement-dependent. Rework and extended holds from conflicting determinations carry schedule and material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of the container's interior surfaces that remain wet before filling
rows.plain: Make sure the inside of the container is as dry as required before filling it.
rows.imp_rat: Wet interior surfaces before filling can dilute or contaminate product (moderate relevance) but consequences are usually a quality deviation rather than catastrophic loss (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Air-blow drying and drain-dry holds are standard, but confirming complete interior dryness in complex geometries is inconsistent and residual wet spots persist. Drying residual moisture with air purge or dwell time adds a real block of waiting before filling can start. Judging when surfaces are adequately dry relies on operator experience unless instrumented. Drying consumes energy and clean dry air, and re-drying wastes utilities.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of residual pharmaceutical material or cleaning chemicals released into the surrounding area during dismantling
rows.plain: Limit the amount of leftover medicine or cleaning chemicals that escape into the surrounding area while a container is taken apart. This helps protect people and the work area.
rows.imp_rat: Release of residual pharmaceutical or cleaning chemicals during dismantling threatens environment and personnel, yet it is a peripheral disposal-phase error rather than the container's central containment task; severity is notable.
rows.sat_rat: Bunded cutting areas and pre-rinsing limit release, but cutting into residue-bearing sections still spreads material into the work area intermittently. Containing releases during dismantling adds setup time for drip trays and capture that crews usually accept. Minimizing spread during cutting depends on careful technique rather than specialized expertise. Spill containment, absorbents and captured-liquid disposal add ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discharge-pressure deviation from allowable limits during transfer to fill-finish
rows.plain: Keep the pressure used to move the product into the filling process within its allowed limits. This helps the transfer happen safely and as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Discharge-pressure deviation during transfer mainly affects transfer control; relevant with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled discharge pressure is reliably held within limits with standard equipment. Discharge pressure is regulated in real time during the transfer. Pump/pressure control is automated, low skill demand. Pressure regulation hardware is standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of product agitation beyond approved limits during hold
rows.plain: Keep the product from being mixed or shaken more than the approved limits while it is being held. This helps preserve the product’s required condition.
rows.imp_rat: Agitation beyond approved limits can degrade sensitive product; relevant to quality with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-controlled agitation reliably stays within limits during hold. Agitation is set per recipe and does not add time. Programmed agitation reduces operator judgement. Trivial energy cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of sterile bulk temperature deviation from allowable limits during transfer
rows.plain: Keep the temperature of the clean product within its allowed range while it is being moved. This helps protect the product from being damaged or made unsafe.
rows.imp_rat: Bulk temperature deviation during transfer affects product quality briefly; relevant with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Short controlled transfers keep temperature within limits reliably. Transfer is brief so temperature excursion exposure time is inherently limited. Little operator skill needed if lines are conditioned. Jacketed or short transfer lines add minor cost.

rows.source_job: Agitate contained material to maintain uniformity
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of bulk material degradation caused by excessive agitation
rows.plain: Avoid damaging or spoiling the stored material by stirring it too aggressively. The material should stay evenly mixed without being harmed.
rows.imp_rat: Bulk material degradation from excessive agitation harms product quality, but agitation is more a handling/transfer concern than the container's central containment function, so relevance is moderate while severity is meaningful.
rows.sat_rat: Low-shear pumps and defined mixing protocols keep most product within spec, though variability in handling still causes occasional degradation. Gentle handling and controlled agitation may slow processing slightly but is generally accommodated. Judging acceptable agitation and pump speed for shear-sensitive material demands operator experience. Degraded material is scrapped, so avoiding over-agitation protects product value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of material temperature excursions outside the required process range
rows.plain: Keep the material from staying outside the required temperature range for long. This helps protect the material and keep the process consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Temperature excursions outside the required process range affect product quality, moderately central to the processing job (relevance 6); consequences are typically scrapped batches rather than safety harm (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop temperature control is mature and widely deployed, keeping most excursions short, though large thermal masses and upsets still cause lingering deviations. PID temperature control with jacket/coil heating and cooling corrects excursions fairly quickly, though thermal lag on large volumes prolongs them. Tuned control loops handle routine regulation with little operator input, but tuning and disturbance recovery still need process knowledge. Heating/cooling utilities and control instrumentation are standard operating costs, with energy waste during excursions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting a tank setpoint outside approved batch limits
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that a tank’s target setting will be chosen outside the approved limits for the batch. Staying within those limits helps keep the batch safe and acceptable.
rows.imp_rat: Selecting a setpoint outside approved batch limits is moderately relevant to correct tank processing, and severity is meaningful since an out-of-limit setpoint can compromise the batch, but the ranking caps it below higher-priority contamination errors.
rows.sat_rat: Range-checked recipe systems and DCS limit-clamping are mature and widely used, making out-of-limit setpoints uncommon where deployed. Recipe-driven setpoints load automatically in modern control systems, saving entry time versus manual configuration. Electronic recipe management removes most of the judgement in choosing setpoints, though manual-entry systems still depend on the operator. Recipe management software has a cost but prevents expensive out-of-spec batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of process material escaping through the tank boundary during use
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of production material that leaks out of the tank while it is being used. This prevents waste and helps protect people and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Process material escaping through the tank boundary is relevant to containment and carries notable severity through material loss and exposure, yet it ranks below the top-batch items and stays capped at 5.5.
rows.sat_rat: Proven sealing and gasketing practice contains most material, though seal degradation produces recurring minor losses. Leaks are usually found during rounds or by alarms rather than instantly, so some loss accrues before detection. Sealing technology (gaskets, mechanical seals) is standardized, requiring modest maintenance skill rather than expert judgement. Good gaskets and seal designs limit fugitive losses, but slow weeps and seal wear still waste material over time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of repair debris or residue left on the IBC after frame or pallet-base repair
rows.plain: Leave as little repair dust, debris, or residue as possible on the large container after fixing its frame or base. This helps keep the repaired container clean and ready for use.
rows.imp_rat: Repair debris left after frame or pallet-base repair is a maintenance-adjacent concern (moderate relevance) that can contaminate later loads and cause rejection (moderate-to-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice is inspect-and-clean after repair, yet metal filings or weld spatter migrating to product areas is an inconsistently controlled residual risk. Post-repair cleanup of debris is a quick manual step that rarely extends the repair timeline much. Removing grinding/welding debris is low-skill housekeeping that most technicians perform routinely. Cleanup consumables are trivial, but missed debris causing later contamination carries disproportionate cost.

rows.source_job: Flush contaminated container with neutralising agent
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of foreign residue introduced into the IBC by the neutralising agent
rows.plain: Introduce as little unwanted residue as possible into the large container along with the neutralizing chemical. This helps prevent the container from becoming contaminated while it is being cleaned.
rows.imp_rat: Foreign residue from the neutralising agent is a narrower, step-specific contamination path; it matters to purity but is less central than general cleaning, and consequences are contained batch contamination rather than catastrophic failure.
rows.sat_rat: Residual neutraliser is a recognised risk managed by post-rinse testing, but conductivity/pH checks are not universally applied so introduced foreign residue sometimes slips through. A neutralisation-then-rinse sequence adds a discrete extra step but does not dominate maintenance time. Correct agent selection and dosing depends on operator knowledge of prior contents, so some finesse is required. The neutralising agent and its rinse water are extra consumables, adding modest material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the condemned IBC remains capable of holding materials after disablement
rows.plain: Make sure a rejected large container cannot hold materials after it has been taken out of use. This prevents it from being used again by mistake.
rows.imp_rat: A condemned IBC still capable of holding materials after disablement is an end-of-life concern peripheral to the primary containment job, but severe if the unit re-enters service and fails, causing spills or contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Physical destruction reliably prevents reuse when performed, though incomplete disablement occasionally leaves a unit that still appears serviceable. Physically disabling a condemned IBC so it can no longer hold material is a fast, decisive cutting or crushing step. Rendering a container unusable requires little specialized skill once the disable method is defined. Cutting or crushing equipment is a modest one-time or shared cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of container damage from excessive test pressure
rows.plain: Prevent the container from being damaged by using too much pressure during the leak check. This helps confirm that it is sealed without harming it.
rows.imp_rat: Container damage from excessive test pressure occurs during testing rather than routine service and mainly harms the asset; unless it fails violently the consequence is equipment loss, so relevance and severity are moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Defined test limits with regulators reliably prevent overpressure damage, with rare failures from gauge error or procedure lapses. Ramp-controlled pressurization adds modest, predictable test duration. Pressure limits are set by procedure and gauges, reducing reliance on operator judgement. Pressure regulators and calibrated gauges are inexpensive standard tooling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of discovering incompatible IBC and transfer-equipment interfaces during connection
rows.plain: Prevent the connection points on the storage container and transfer equipment from being found incompatible only when the connection is attempted. Compatible connections help material move safely and smoothly.
rows.imp_rat: Discovering incompatible IBC/transfer interfaces at connection time delays operations and can force improvised risky workarounds; relevant to reliable transfer but usually caught before harm occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized tri-clamp and sanitary fittings plus pre-job kit checks catch most mismatches, yet non-standard or legacy interfaces still surprise crews at the point of connection. Discovering an interface mismatch at connection time stops the job while adapters or alternatives are sourced. Recognizing incompatible fittings before forcing them needs experienced eyes but is not deep expertise. Mismatch causes idle equipment and sometimes emergency adapter purchases, a real but bounded cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that microbial contamination remains on product-contact surfaces after tank sanitization
rows.plain: Make sure germs are removed from all surfaces that touch the product after the tank is sanitized. This helps keep the product safe.
rows.imp_rat: Residual microbial contamination after sanitization directly threatens product integrity (severity), and is fairly central to clean tank processing (relevance), but the batch ranking holds it at or below 5.5.
rows.sat_rat: Validated sanitization plus micro monitoring reliably removes contamination, though occasional biofilm survives in dead legs. CIP/SIP cycles and swab/rinse verification are time-defined and integrated into turnaround schedules. Validated cleaning recipes reduce operator judgement, though sampling technique still affects results. Chemicals, water and validation testing carry recurring cost but are budgeted operating expense.

rows.source_job: Label container with contents and status
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of conflicting identification or status markings being displayed on the same container
rows.plain: A container should be as unlikely as possible to show conflicting information about what is inside it or what condition it is in. Clear markings help people handle and use it correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting identification/status markings is an information-clarity error that can cause misuse of container contents; moderately consequential but not central to physical containment.
rows.sat_rat: Stale or conflicting markings routinely persist on reused IBCs because there is no enforced single-source-of-status practice. Removing or updating old placards and status tags takes visible time that is often skipped. Judging which markings are current relies on operator vigilance rather than a systemic control. Labels and tags are cheap, so the failure is procedural rather than resource-driven.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that combined toxicological effects of multiple leachables are omitted from the safety evaluation
rows.plain: Make sure the safety review considers the combined effects of all harmful substances that could come from the materials, not just each substance separately.
rows.imp_rat: Ignoring combined leachable toxicological effects can understate patient risk; severity is real for safety, but it is a tox-evaluation input peripheral to the hold job.
rows.sat_rat: Combined effects are routinely omitted because methods are immature, making this a persistent weak spot. Mixture assessment adds little schedule time when performed. Evaluating combined toxicological effects is scientifically difficult and depends on advanced toxicology judgement rarely available. Mixture assessment is low direct cost but methodologically limited.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of bulk solution contamination during excursion intervention
rows.plain: Avoid contaminating the main solution while taking action to deal with a contamination or other unusual event.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during excursion intervention directly threatens sterility of the held bulk; severity is high given potential batch loss, though ranked below the batch above.
rows.sat_rat: Interventions under excursion conditions are a recognized elevated contamination risk that technique only partly controls. Intervention windows are brief but each is an exposure moment. Maintaining asepsis during an active excursion intervention is highly skill- and pressure-dependent. Contamination during intervention can destroy the batch, a severe cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of residual bulk material released from the container or transfer connections during purging
rows.plain: As little leftover material as possible should escape from the container or its connecting pipes during emptying. This helps reduce waste, spills, and exposure to the material.
rows.imp_rat: Residual bulk material released during purging is primarily a waste and minor-exposure issue moderately relevant to service; consequences are yield loss and localized cleanup rather than major harm.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-transfer and purge fittings help but incidental release at connections during purge remains a common recurring gap. Purging and blowdown cycles add setup and dwell time before the line is clear. Correctly sequencing purge valves and confirming a clear line takes trained judgement. Released residue during purge is lost product plus cleanup and effluent cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that microbial contamination enters the bulk product during discharge
rows.plain: The worker should prevent germs from getting into the product as it leaves the holding vessel. This keeps the released product clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial contamination entering bulk during discharge directly defeats sterility at delivery; severity high, held at the batch ceiling by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Closed aseptic transfer makes discharge contamination uncommon but connection breaches remain a residual risk. Discharge is a bounded window of contamination exposure. Aseptic discharge connection is skill-dependent. Closed transfer systems add cost but protect the batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an aseptic-boundary defect remains undetected before hold initiation
rows.plain: The worker should find any damage or opening in the vessel’s protective seal before starting to hold the product. This prevents an unsafe vessel from being used.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected aseptic-boundary defect before hold initiation allows contaminated holding of the batch; high severity, relevance capped in this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Integrity testing catches gross defects reliably but fine or intermittent defects can go undetected before hold. Defect detection is a pre-hold check window. Detecting subtle boundary defects requires skilled testing and interpretation. Sensitive integrity testing adds cost but avoids catastrophic loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual cleaning agent or prior-product material remaining at bulk introduction
rows.plain: The worker should make sure no leftover cleaning liquid or material from an earlier product remains when the new product is added. This prevents unwanted chemicals or product mixing into it.
rows.imp_rat: Residual cleaning agent or prior product at introduction contaminates the bulk chemically and microbially; severity high, held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning with rinse sampling makes residual agent or prior product at introduction uncommon. Cleaning verification is a scheduled pre-use step. Validated CIP reduces reliance on operator skill for residue removal. Cleaning agents, water and validation carry ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required microbial lethality is not achieved across the vessel boundary during preparation
rows.plain: The worker should make sure the preparation step kills the required amount of germs throughout the vessel’s protective boundary. This helps ensure the vessel is safe to use for holding the product.
rows.imp_rat: Failure to achieve required microbial lethality across the vessel boundary means the hold begins non-sterile; severity high, held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validated sterilization with parametric release makes lethality failures across the boundary rare when cycles run correctly. Sterilization is a defined cycle with fixed duration. Validated SIP/autoclave cycles remove most operator judgement from lethality assurance. Steam, energy and cycle validation are standard recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an aseptic vessel boundary breach before bulk introduction
rows.plain: The worker should prevent any break or opening in the vessel’s protective seal before the product is added. This keeps germs and other unwanted material out.
rows.imp_rat: An aseptic boundary breach before bulk introduction predisposes the whole hold to contamination; severity high, held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-use integrity checks make pre-introduction breaches uncommon, but latent defects can still exist. Boundary integrity before introduction is verified in a discrete pre-check. Correct assembly and connection integrity depend on skilled technicians. Integrity test equipment and rework cost when breaches are found.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that microbial contamination is introduced into the bulk product during receipt
rows.plain: The worker should prevent germs from getting into the product as it enters the holding vessel. This keeps the product clean from the start of holding.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial contamination introduced during receipt seeds the entire hold with non-sterility; severity high, held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Closed aseptic receipt limits contamination, but connection and transfer steps remain a recognized ingress risk. Receipt is a bounded exposure window. Aseptic transfer connection at receipt is skill-dependent. Closed transfer systems add cost but protect against ingress.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an unauthorized bulk product or batch enters the hold vessel
rows.plain: The worker should prevent unapproved product or an unapproved batch from entering the holding vessel. This ensures that only the intended, authorized product is received.
rows.imp_rat: An unauthorized bulk/batch entering the vessel corrupts identity and can compromise release, but capped here; severity is high because a wrong-product mixup is serious yet this ranks below batch-1 items.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode/label reconciliation and line-clearance practices make unauthorized batch entry rare in disciplined operations. Verification at introduction is quick and does not gate on time. Batch and material verification is procedural, needing checks more than expertise. Wrong-product entry would waste an entire batch, a large but preventable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the held bulk solution falls outside defined potency or purity specifications
rows.plain: Keep the held liquid at the required strength and cleanliness. This helps ensure the final medicine works as intended and is safe to use.
rows.imp_rat: Held bulk falling outside potency/purity specs directly harms product quality; severity high, but ordering caps this item within batch 2.
