rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unplanned product-flow interruption during discharge to fill-finish
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned flow interruption during discharge is a throughput/continuity concern with modest severity relative to the core hold job.
rows.sat_rat: Discharge flow is usually continuous but occasional stoppages occur. Unplanned discharge interruptions directly extend the operation. Recovery requires troubleshooting skill. Interruptions risk product exposure and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of foaming in the sterile solution during discharge to fill-finish
rows.imp_rat: Foaming during discharge can affect fill accuracy and product but is a handling defect with modest severity for the hold job.
rows.sat_rat: Bottom-fill and rate control usually limit foaming, but sensitive products still foam occasionally. Foaming control mainly affects discharge duration slightly. Managing foam needs some process know-how on flow rates. Foam can cause loss or fill inaccuracy, modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of the bulk-hold interval without critical-condition surveillance
rows.imp_rat: Gaps in critical-condition surveillance during hold relate to monitoring the aseptic state, moderately relevant, but framed here as a percentage-of-interval metric; undetected excursion risk kept at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous data historians give near-complete surveillance where deployed, though non-instrumented parameters leave gaps. Surveillance gaps mean intervals with no monitoring, a coverage-time deficit. Continuous coverage reduces reliance on manual rounds. Full continuous monitoring adds instrumentation and historian cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical-condition values recorded inaccurately during bulk hold
rows.imp_rat: Critical-condition values recorded inaccurately during hold could hide an actual excursion, moderately relevant to hold monitoring; misleading records risk a wrong judgment but capped (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Electronic capture is accurate; manual transcription still produces occasional inaccurate values. Recording values is instantaneous. Manual transcription introduces skill-dependent inaccuracy. Inaccurate values can misinform disposition, cost risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile bulk transfer into the hold vessel
rows.imp_rat: Transfer duration into the vessel is a throughput concern; peripheral to the core hold job unless prolonged exposure is at issue.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer times are predictable and generally acceptable. Transfer duration is largely governed by pump rates and is reasonably optimized. Minimal skill; pumping is automated. Faster transfer saves modest labor and exposure cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of vessel-hold extension caused by a utility interruption
rows.imp_rat: Hold extension caused by a utility interruption prolongs holding time; relevant to schedule/exposure but modest severity relative to the core job.
rows.sat_rat: Backup utilities usually limit interruption duration, but events still cause real hold extensions. A utility interruption directly extends the hold, the core loss here. Response requires facility engineering support. Extended holds risk approaching hold limits and batch loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile bulk unnecessarily discarded or rendered unusable during deviation response
rows.imp_rat: Unnecessary discard of sterile bulk during deviation response is a waste concern with cost impact but peripheral to whether the hold job succeeds.
rows.sat_rat: Discard decisions are inconsistent, so unnecessary loss is common. Disposition decisions can lag, extending exposure. Deciding what is truly unusable needs technical judgement to avoid over-discard. Conservative practice routinely discards salvageable bulk, a major avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unplanned product-flow interruption during transfer into the hold vessel
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned flow interruption during transfer into the vessel is largely an operational continuity issue with bounded consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer-in interruptions are infrequent with reliable pumping systems. Interruptions during transfer-in extend the process. Troubleshooting requires some operator competence. Delays add exposure and labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of batch volume remaining in the source and product-contact path after transfer
rows.imp_rat: Batch volume left in the source/contact path after transfer is a yield-loss concern; peripheral to whether the sterile hold job is performed.
rows.sat_rat: Some holdup is inherent to transfer paths, so residual loss is consistently present. Recovering holdup can add flush time. Low skill; determined by system geometry. Residual bulk left in source and lines is direct yield loss of expensive product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from the approved vessel configuration for transfer and hold
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from the approved vessel configuration for transfer/hold could invalidate qualified conditions, moderately relevant to safe holding; capped at batch ceiling (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Configuration checklists and change control keep deviations from approved setup uncommon. Configuration is verified pre-transfer, minor added time. Confirming approved configuration requires competent verification against records. Wrong configuration risks the batch, control cost is low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that condition indications misrepresent actual vessel conditions during readiness verification
rows.imp_rat: Condition indications misrepresenting actual vessel state during readiness verification could mask a containment problem (moderate relevance); severity capped though a false reading risks a bad readiness call.
rows.sat_rat: Calibrated instruments usually represent true conditions, but sensor faults occasionally give false readings at verification. Verification is point-in-time and can miss instrument faults. Detecting misleading indications requires calibration awareness and skill. Calibration programs cost money but are standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required readiness evidence missing or unresolved before transfer authorization
rows.imp_rat: Missing readiness evidence before transfer authorization touches the gate protecting containment, moderately relevant; authorizing transfer without evidence risks a defect but capped (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Readiness checklists reduce missing evidence, but unresolved items still surface at authorization. Chasing missing evidence before authorization delays release. Requires diligent review to spot gaps. Rework and delay cost when evidence is missing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of gas entrained into the bulk product during receipt
rows.imp_rat: Gas entrained during receipt is a handling defect that can affect quality/measurement but has modest severity for the core hold job.
rows.sat_rat: Submerged fills and controlled transfer reduce entrainment, but it still occurs and is imperfectly controlled. Entrainment occurs during the transfer duration. Minimizing entrainment requires proper transfer technique and setup. Gas entrainment can affect quality, indirectly costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of doubt about the actual vessel state when indications conflict
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about actual vessel state when indications conflict directly clouds aseptic status knowledge, moderately relevant to the hold job; unresolved doubt risks a wrong call but capped (moderate severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among approved hold requirements for the bulk batch
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts among approved hold requirements create ambiguity in execution but are a specification-reconciliation issue with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Review processes catch most conflicts, but ambiguous or conflicting requirements sometimes reach the floor unresolved. Resolving requirement conflicts is a pre-hold planning activity, not runtime. Detecting and reconciling conflicting hold requirements needs knowledgeable review. Unresolved conflicts can cause rework or batch loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement between shift handoff information and the current vessel aseptic state
rows.imp_rat: Shift-handoff disagreement with the current aseptic state could propagate a wrong understanding of containment (moderate relevance); miscommunication risk to sterility kept at ceiling (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Verbal and paper handoffs frequently lose or garble aseptic-state detail, a chronic weak point despite checklists. Handoffs occur at shift boundaries, not as sustained delay. Accurate handoff depends heavily on communication discipline and thoroughness. Handoff errors can cause costly missteps but the handoff itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of false condition indications triggering unnecessary vessel intervention
rows.imp_rat: False indications triggering unnecessary intervention add avoidable vessel accesses and cost; relevant to efficiency but low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm rationalization helps, but nuisance false indications still trigger avoidable interventions with some frequency. False indications waste response time but the loss is the unnecessary action. Distinguishing false from real signals requires experienced interpretation. Unnecessary interventions waste labor and add contamination risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict among microbiological, environmental, vessel, and handling findings
rows.imp_rat: Conflict among microbiological/environmental/vessel findings complicates interpretation but is an analysis-reconciliation issue with modest direct impact.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting findings are a routine and hard-to-resolve feature of contamination investigations. Resolving conflicting findings prolongs investigations. Reconciling conflicting micro, environmental and handling findings requires substantial cross-disciplinary expertise. Extended reconciliation consumes specialist time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty in linking a recovered organism to a credible contamination source
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty in linking a recovered organism to a source complicates investigation but is a diagnostic-clarity issue with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Source attribution of recovered organisms is often uncertain even with modern ID tools. Source-tracing via identification methods takes days. Linking a recovered organism to a credible source demands specialized microbial identification and epidemiological expertise. Advanced identification (genotyping) is costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency among microbial, environmental, and holding-condition evidence for the same hold
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency among microbial/environmental/holding evidence for one hold complicates conclusions but is a reconciliation issue with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Inconsistency among evidence streams for the same hold frequently persists unresolved. Resolving inconsistencies delays disposition. Harmonizing inconsistent evidence for a single hold takes experienced interpretation. Reconciliation adds analyst cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among contamination indications for the same sterile bulk holding interval
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among contamination indications for the same interval creates ambiguity about sterility, moderately relevant to the hold job; unresolved conflict could mask contamination but capped (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Disagreement among contamination indications for one hold remains a recurring, hard-to-close issue. Resolving disagreement among indications lengthens review. Adjudicating disagreeing indications for the same interval takes expert interpretation. Extended review consumes resource.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of normal aseptic-condition variation being misclassified as a contamination excursion
rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying normal variation as an excursion triggers false alarms and rework; relevant to efficiency but low severity since it errs toward caution.
rows.sat_rat: Overcalling normal variation as excursions is a frequent occurrence, poorly bounded by current limits. Misclassification triggers unnecessary investigations that waste time. Distinguishing normal variation from a true excursion requires strong analytical and statistical judgement. False excursions consume investigation resources needlessly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterility-impacting deviations unresolved at disposition review
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved sterility-impacting deviations at disposition review directly touch sterility assurance (moderate relevance); potential release of compromised bulk kept at the ceiling (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Deviation systems generally ensure sterility-impacting deviations are resolved before disposition, with occasional carryovers. Unresolved deviations directly block or delay disposition. Deciding resolution adequacy needs quality expertise. Tracking open deviations adds administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the qualified holding limit expires before disposition judgment is completed
rows.imp_rat: Qualified holding limit expiring before disposition completes could force rejection of otherwise good bulk, moderately relevant to timely release; consequence capped at batch ceiling (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Validated hold limits usually provide margin, but pending micro results sometimes threaten expiry. Slow disposition, especially pending sterility results, can push against the qualified hold limit expiry. Managing the race between disposition and expiry needs planning judgement. Expiry breaches risk costly batch loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the batch disposition judgment conflicts with the approved evidence
rows.imp_rat: A disposition judgment conflicting with approved evidence touches release integrity but sits at the adjudication periphery of the containment job (moderate-low relevance); a bad disposition could release non-conforming bulk, but capped per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Independent QA review and approval make disposition-versus-evidence conflicts uncommon. Reconciling judgment with evidence adds review time. Ensuring disposition aligns with evidence relies on qualified QA judgement. Dual-review adds labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an inaccurate bulk-hold KPI value is used for disposition
rows.imp_rat: An inaccurate KPI value used for disposition could drive a wrong release decision, moderately relevant; potential to accept bad bulk but capped by batch ceiling (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Data review and validation reduce inaccurate KPI use, but manual data handling still allows recurring errors. Detecting and correcting an inaccurate KPI value adds verification time. Verifying KPI accuracy relies on analyst diligence and data review skill. A wrong KPI driving disposition risks costly wrong decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of concentration variation within the held bulk solution
rows.imp_rat: Concentration variation within the held solution touches product uniformity during hold, moderately relevant to preserving product quality; non-uniform bulk risk capped by batch ceiling (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Validated mixing keeps concentration variation low and well-controlled in most held vessels. Achieving homogeneity via mixing adds a defined but short processing step. Mixing/recirculation to control concentration variation is a standardized, low-skill operation. Mixing equipment and energy are routine, absorbed costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of compounds released from product-contact materials exceeding acceptable sterile bulk solution limits
rows.imp_rat: Released compounds exceeding acceptable limits is central to safe holding, yet the mandatory batch ceiling caps it; relevance and severity set at the maximum permitted within the constraint.
rows.sat_rat: E&L programs with safety thresholds are mature though condition-specific gaps remain. Leachable threshold studies are time-intensive. Toxicological and analytical judgement on leachable limits is specialized. Exceeding limits triggers costly investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of product-contact materials altering sterile bulk solution identity, strength, purity, or stability under intended hold conditions
rows.imp_rat: Contact materials altering solution identity/strength/purity under hold conditions is core to preserving product during hold, but the batch ceiling caps it; relevance and severity held at the maximum allowed.
rows.sat_rat: Extractables/leachables and compatibility programs are established, so material-induced alteration is fairly well managed. Compatibility/leachable studies to prove no alteration take significant time. Interpreting interaction effects on identity/strength/purity needs analytical expertise. Undetected alteration causes batch rejection, high cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of product-contact surfaces retaining microbial or endotoxin contamination after intended preparation cycles
rows.imp_rat: Contact surfaces retaining microbial/endotoxin contamination after preparation directly threatens sterility, core to the CFJ, but the batch ceiling caps it; scores held at the maximum allowed.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning and depyrogenation validation are mature, widely deployed practices with strong reliability. Cleaning/sterilization validation adds cycle-development time. Cleaning and endotoxin control validation needs established but specialized skill. Retained contamination causes batch loss, high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product-contact components with unresolved composition or provenance
rows.imp_rat: Contact components with unresolved composition or provenance risk an unqualified material contacting product, moderately relevant to safe holding; capped at batch ceiling (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Supplier CoC/CoA practices help but full composition/provenance is often incompletely resolved. Chasing composition/provenance for contact components can be slow with vendors. Interpreting material certificates and provenance needs materials expertise. Unresolved provenance forces testing or supplier change at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product-contact components lacking evidence for the intended formulation and exposure conditions
rows.imp_rat: Contact components lacking evidence for the intended formulation/exposure could permit an unqualified material contact, moderately relevant to safe holding; capped at batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor data and USP class testing exist but rarely match the exact formulation and exposure, leaving gaps. Obtaining formulation-and-exposure-specific data for components can be slow to source. Interpreting extractables/compatibility data for the specific formulation needs specialized materials-science skill. Missing component evidence forces bespoke testing, a real expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of product-contact interfaces and ancillary components omitted from suitability evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Product-contact interfaces omitted from suitability evaluation risks an unassessed material contacting product, moderately relevant to safe holding; severity held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: BOM-driven component review is fairly mature, so most components are captured though ancillaries are sometimes missed. Cataloguing product-contact and ancillary components adds up-front assessment time. Identifying all contact and ancillary parts needs detailed component knowledge that varies by reviewer. An overlooked component causes late suitability failures with rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between supporting evidence and intended aseptic hold conditions
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between supporting evidence and intended aseptic hold conditions weakens the basis for safe holding, moderately relevant; capped at batch ceiling despite sterility relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Hold-condition matching is understood but evidence often reflects surrogate conditions, leaving mismatch. Matching evidence to intended hold conditions requires additional study lead time. Judging condition equivalence needs validation and stability expertise. Mismatched conditions force repeat studies at cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incorrect technical calculations or data interpretations supporting the recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect technical calculations supporting the recommendation could propagate a flawed containment conclusion, moderately relevant; capped at batch ceiling despite decision impact (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Peer review and verified tools catch most errors, giving reasonable but imperfect assurance. Checking calculations adds review time secondarily. Correct technical calculations and data interpretation depend directly on skilled reviewers, the core dependence. Erroneous calculations cause costly wrong decisions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial contamination of the sterile bulk solution during holding
rows.imp_rat: Microbial contamination during holding is the essence of the sterility CFJ, yet the mandatory batch-3 ceiling caps it; relevance and severity are held at the maximum permitted within the constraint.
rows.sat_rat: Sterility assurance frameworks are highly mature, so microbial contamination during hold is well controlled with rare failure. Microbial control validation extends timelines. Aseptic microbiology expertise is required and generally available in GMP settings. Contamination causes total batch loss, high cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of aseptic integrity failure during filling, holding, or transfer at required batch volumes
rows.imp_rat: Aseptic integrity failure at required batch volumes is highly central to the CFJ, but the batch ceiling caps this item; relevance and severity held at the maximum allowed within the ranking constraint.
rows.sat_rat: Media fills, integrity testing and Annex make this a mature, well-served area though rare failures occur. Demonstrating integrity at batch volume requires extended qualification time. Assuring aseptic integrity across operations needs deep aseptic engineering expertise. An integrity failure destroys a full batch, a severe cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of containment boundary integrity loss during the intended hold
rows.imp_rat: Containment boundary integrity loss during hold is core to the CFJ, but the batch ceiling forces a cap; relevance and severity set at the maximum allowed under the ranking constraint.
rows.sat_rat: CCI testing methods are mature and widely deployed, making boundary integrity well served. Boundary integrity verification adds test time. Container-closure integrity assessment requires specialized skill that is well established. Boundary loss causes batch loss, high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of aseptic boundary interfaces and planned interventions omitted from compatibility assessment
rows.imp_rat: Boundary interfaces/interventions omitted from compatibility assessment could leave an unassessed contamination path, moderately relevant; capped at batch ceiling despite sterility linkage.
rows.sat_rat: Interface/intervention mapping is done but rarely exhaustive, so omissions recur in compatibility scope. Enumerating boundary interfaces and interventions for compatibility takes deliberate effort, delaying assessment. Recognizing every aseptic interface and planned intervention requires hands-on aseptic-operations expertise. A missed interface surfaces as integrity failure, a large latent cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which assessed exposure falls short of the maximum intended hold
rows.imp_rat: Assessed exposure short of the maximum intended hold means the actual duration may be unqualified, moderately relevant to time-bounded holding; capped by batch ceiling (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Hold-time studies are standard but often stop short of true maximum intended exposure. Extending studies to the maximum hold duration adds elapsed time. Setting bounding hold durations needs stability expertise. Short-of-max exposure risks costly out-of-spec holds later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which assessed conditions understate intended temperature, pressure, agitation, and environmental extremes
rows.imp_rat: Assessed conditions understating intended extremes could leave real hold conditions unqualified, moderately relevant to safe holding; unassessed-extreme risk capped at ceiling (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Worst-case testing is a known expectation but assessed conditions frequently understate real extremes. Testing to worst-case extremes lengthens qualification timelines. Defining bounding conditions requires experienced process-risk judgement. Understated conditions cause field failures with high remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch in vessel geometry, fill volume, headspace, and connection layout between evaluated and intended configurations
rows.imp_rat: Geometry/fill/headspace mismatch between evaluated and intended configurations could invalidate hold assumptions, moderately relevant; potential unassessed condition capped at ceiling (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Engineering drawings and models make this fairly controllable, though intended-vs-evaluated deltas still slip. Reconciling geometry and layout between evaluated and intended configs consumes engineering time. Detecting subtle geometry/headspace/connection mismatches requires design expertise. Geometry mismatch drives late redesign with significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment between criterion weighting and contamination, product-quality, and process risks
rows.imp_rat: Criterion-weighting misalignment with real risks can misprioritize but is a decision-methodology issue removed from live containment.
rows.sat_rat: Weighted decision matrices are standard practice, but the risk-to-weight linkage is often subjective and inconsistently validated. Aligning weightings to risk adds facilitation cycles but is not primarily a speed problem. Correctly weighting criteria against contamination and product-quality risk needs experienced risk facilitators and is judgement-heavy. Misaligned weighting steers selection toward suboptimal vessels, an indirect cost of poor decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in the aseptic-containment acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in acceptance criteria can cause inconsistent judgments but is largely a clarity/administrative issue with recoverable consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Ambiguous criteria persist and produce inconsistent qualification outcomes. Clarifying criteria is a drafting task, not a schedule driver. Writing unambiguous acceptance criteria requires precise technical-writing skill that is uneven. Ambiguity costs later in disputes, not upfront.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of acceptance criteria that cannot be objectively verified under controlled qualification conditions
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance criteria not objectively verifiable weaken qualification but is a criteria-design clarity issue with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Some criteria remain subjective and not objectively verifiable under controlled runs. Verifiability checks are quick during protocol review. Making criteria objectively verifiable needs measurement and metrology expertise. Objective criteria may require added instrumentation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the acceptance criteria classify acceptable aseptic containment as unqualified
rows.imp_rat: Criteria rejecting acceptable containment as unqualified causes unnecessary rework; relevant to efficiency but low severity as it errs conservative.
rows.sat_rat: Overly strict criteria cause false unqualified calls, though this is less feared than false-pass. False-reject investigations add rework time but are not the core loss. Distinguishing true failure from over-conservative criteria needs expert interpretation. Rejecting acceptable containment wastes batches and requalification cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in instructions for establishing and maintaining challenge conditions
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous instructions for challenge conditions reduce reproducibility of qualification but are a clarity issue with bounded consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Ambiguous setup instructions still cause inconsistent challenge execution. Writing instructions is a drafting effort, not a delay. Producing clear condition-setup instructions requires strong procedural-writing skill. Clear instructions are low-cost to produce.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of variation in challenge-material properties permitted by the protocol
rows.imp_rat: Variation in challenge-material properties reduces qualification consistency but is a protocol-control detail with modest direct impact on the hold.
rows.sat_rat: Permitted variation in challenge material still introduces result variability. Specifying material properties is quick. Controlling challenge-material variation needs materials-science attention. Tight material specs add procurement cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of protocol requirements conflicting with sterile fill-finish constraints
rows.imp_rat: Protocol requirements conflicting with fill-finish constraints cause downstream friction but are a design-alignment issue with recoverable impact.
rows.sat_rat: Protocol-vs-fill-finish conflicts occur but are usually caught in mature review flows. Alignment review is not time-critical. Reconciling protocol with fill-finish constraints requires cross-functional expertise. Resolution is low-cost analytically.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required containment challenges cannot be executed safely under controlled conditions
rows.imp_rat: Inability to safely execute required containment challenges limits qualification completeness; relevant to feasibility but modest direct impact.
rows.sat_rat: Most required challenges can be executed safely with established methods. Safe execution planning does not extend schedule materially. Devising safely-executable challenges requires engineering and safety expertise. Safe test rigs may add equipment cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of patient-safety conclusions lacking traceable analytical inputs, exposure assumptions, or hazard evidence
rows.imp_rat: Patient-safety conclusions lacking traceable analytical inputs weakens the safety justification, moderately relevant; capped at batch ceiling though patient-safety linked (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Traceability of safety conclusions to inputs is improving under data-integrity expectations but gaps still surface in audits, so moderately served. Building traceable documentation adds effort but not critical delay. Maintaining traceable safety-conclusion documentation needs disciplined quality practice, not rare skill. Documentation systems carry administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material, formulation, or hold-condition changes are not reflected in the toxicological assessment
rows.imp_rat: Changes not reflected in the toxicological assessment could mask a leachable hazard, moderately relevant to safe holding; severity capped at ceiling though patient-safety linked.
rows.sat_rat: Changes frequently fail to trigger corresponding toxicological reassessment, leaving this poorly served. Propagating changes into tox assessment can lag behind the change itself. Deciding which changes require tox reassessment needs expert judgement. Re-running tox assessment on changes adds cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of relevant product, process, or material changes omitted from the suitability review
rows.imp_rat: Changes omitted from suitability review could let an unassessed material contact product, of moderate relevance to safe holding; severity held at ceiling despite potential for missed incompatibility.
rows.sat_rat: Established change-control systems capture most changes but omissions still happen, making this moderately served. Change review is embedded in change-control with defined timing. Comprehensive change capture depends on diligent cross-functional review rather than scarce expertise. Change-control processes add administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that supplier-originated contact-material changes remain undisclosed before affected components are used
rows.imp_rat: Undisclosed supplier contact-material changes before use risk an unassessed material touching product, moderately relevant; contamination potential kept at ceiling (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Undisclosed supplier-originated material changes remain a notorious, recurring problem despite change-notification agreements. Supplier change notifications may arrive late relative to component use. Interpreting supplier change notices requires competent quality oversight. Supplier change agreements add contractual cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking the combined effects of multiple changes on contact-material suitability
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking combined effects of multiple changes on contact-material suitability could miss a compound incompatibility, moderately relevant to safe holding; severity capped at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Combined change effects are commonly overlooked because reviews treat changes individually, a persistent weakness. Assessing combined change effects adds little schedule time. Recognizing interactions among multiple simultaneous changes is a high-judgement task few reviewers perform well. Little direct cost beyond review effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that changed conditions remain in effect before suitability reassessment is completed
rows.imp_rat: Changed conditions remaining in effect before reassessment could hold product under unassessed conditions, moderately relevant; potential unmanaged risk capped by batch ceiling (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: The lag itself creates risk exposure, and there is no fast-track standard, so this is poorly served on its dominant time dimension. Reassessment after changed conditions is typically slow, extending the interval of unqualified operation. Speeding reassessment depends on expert availability which is limited. Expedited reassessment increases cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of qualifying a supplier unable to maintain the required aseptic containment conditions
rows.imp_rat: Qualifying a supplier unable to maintain aseptic containment touches contamination risk at its source, moderately relevant; potential for compromised holds but capped by batch ceiling (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature audit checklists and approved-vendor lists reliably screen most incapable suppliers, though nuanced aseptic-capability misjudgements still slip through. Supplier audits and qualification take significant time but are a routine part of the sourcing timeline. Judging a supplier's ability to maintain aseptic containment requires specialized quality-audit judgement the executor leans on others for. A wrongly qualified supplier drives costly re-qualification and delay, partially mitigated by audit programs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incompatible container and transfer-line fittings prevent a secure connection
rows.imp_rat: Incompatible container and transfer-line fittings prevent a secure connection, causing setup delays and potential leaks, but the mismatch is discovered immediately at connection time.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized fitting types (tri-clamp, cam-lock, DIN) make most connections compatible, but mixed standards across sites still occasionally block a secure connection. Mismatched fittings are found quickly at connection but then force a delay to find adapters. Recognizing incompatible fittings needs basic familiarity with standard connection types. Adapters and spare gaskets are cheap and commonly stocked.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of heating or cooling being interrupted while contents are stored
rows.imp_rat: Interrupted heating or cooling during storage affects only temperature-controlled variants, so relevance to the general IBC job is limited and severity depends on product tolerance. Kept within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Thermostatic control with alarms keeps temperature stable, though power or equipment failures occasionally interrupt it. Temperature control runs continuously so it adds no operational time when working. Heating/cooling systems are largely automated, needing little operator skill day to day. Jacketed IBCs, heat pads and chillers plus their energy draw are a notable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of material released outside the closed transfer path while interrupting IBC discharge
rows.imp_rat: Material released outside the closed transfer path while interrupting discharge relates to containment during a transient operation, with moderate relevance and severity as most releases are recoverable and localized.
rows.sat_rat: Interrupting a discharge frequently releases some material outside the transfer path unless a dedicated closed system is fitted, which is not the norm. Interrupting a discharge mid-transfer can be done quickly but the containment of escaped material is the concern, not speed. Cleanly interrupting a discharge without breaking containment requires careful operator technique. Closed-transfer systems that contain material during interruption are a substantial investment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of purged residual bulk material entering the wrong receiving path
rows.imp_rat: Purged residual material entering the wrong receiving path is a purge-operation routing error of modest relevance to the container job, with contained consequences. Kept within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Color-coded lines, SOPs and dedicated waste connections reduce wrong-path events, but shared manifolds and human routing errors still recur. Routing purged residual to the correct waste or recovery path is quick once procedures are known, but manual valve selection adds handling time. Operators must recognize which receiving line is correct, and mislabeled or ambiguous outlets place real reliance on trained judgement. Misdirected residual can contaminate a recovery stream or waste batch, causing material loss and cleanup expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of bulk material spilled when the intermediate bulk container is overfilled
rows.imp_rat: Overfill spillage is an operational filling error with moderate relevance to containment and modest severity, being largely recoverable waste, so both proxies remain low under the ranking cap.
rows.sat_rat: High-level shutoffs and weigh limits prevent most overfill spills, though manual fills still occasionally breach the top. Stopping fill at the limit is instantaneous once detected, so time is not the issue. Preventing overfill spill hinges on the operator watching the limit rather than any deep skill. Overfill spillage wastes product and drives cleanup cost, mitigated but not eliminated by high-level cutoffs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the contained material while extracting the sample
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contamination while extracting a sample affects sampling accuracy more than bulk containment, giving it modest relevance and contained severity. Held within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Dedicated sample ports help, but manual sampling remains a common contamination entry point. Aseptic sampling procedures deliberately add careful, slower steps. Contamination-free sampling relies heavily on trained aseptic technique that is hard to sustain consistently. Sample valves and sterile sampling kits add moderate consumable cost.

rows.source_job: Agitate contained material to maintain uniformity
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial or particulate contamination being introduced during agitation
rows.imp_rat: Microbial or particulate contamination during agitation applies to a specific mixing operation, so relevance is limited and severity is bounded by process controls. Constrained to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Open or partially closed agitation still exposes contents, so introduced contamination during mixing remains a real risk. Closed agitation setups add time to install and validate. Preventing contamination during agitation needs careful closed-system handling and aseptic skill. Closed mixers and sterile agitators are expensive and unevenly adopted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of damage to the IBC shell or fittings caused while applying securement
rows.imp_rat: Damage to the shell or fittings while applying securement is a handling error of modest relevance to the containment job, with moderate severity since damaged fittings could later leak but are inspectable.
rows.sat_rat: Framed and caged IBC designs are built to take securement loads, so shell or fitting damage during tie-down is uncommon. Applying straps or clamps for securement is a quick routine step. Securing an IBC without over-tightening takes modest care that most handlers possess. Damage to shells or fittings from securement is occasional and the repair/replacement cost is limited.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of damage to container threads or sealing surfaces during valve and fitting replacement
rows.imp_rat: Damaging threads or sealing surfaces during valve/fitting replacement is a maintenance-time defect of modest relevance to routine containment, with repairable consequences. Constrained by the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Torque specs and thread lubricants help, but manual over-tightening and misalignment still damage sealing surfaces intermittently. Valve and fitting replacement is a routine bolt-on task done quickly by maintenance crews. Avoiding cross-threading or gouging sealing surfaces depends heavily on technician hand-skill and torque discipline. Fittings are inexpensive, but a damaged thread can scrap an otherwise sound container, raising stakes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging intermediate bulk container sealing surfaces during gasket and seal replacement
rows.imp_rat: Damaging sealing surfaces during gasket replacement is a maintenance-phase error, secondary to normal fill/discharge function (modest relevance), and it usually causes repairable damage rather than catastrophic loss (modest severity).
rows.sat_rat: Documented seal-change procedures exist, yet prying out old gaskets with metal tools remains a recurring source of surface nicks. Gasket replacement is not time-critical, so duration seldom drives sealing-surface damage. Avoiding scratching or gouging the sealing surface depends heavily on the technician's manual finesse and correct tool selection, which varies with experience. Replacement gaskets are cheap, but a damaged sealing surface can force costly vessel rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of damage to product-contact surfaces during cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Damaging product-contact surfaces during cleaning is a reconditioning-phase defect with moderate relevance and consequences that reduce reuse cycles rather than cause immediate failure. Capped.
rows.sat_rat: Validated non-abrasive CIP largely protects surfaces, though occasional aggressive manual scrubbing of stubborn soils still scores or dulls finishes. Cleaning cycles are timed and gentle-mode wash programs do not slow the maintenance flow. Selecting non-abrasive methods and pressures requires knowledge of surface finish limits that trained operators apply. Appropriate detergents and soft tooling are cheap relative to refinishing a scratched product-contact surface.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incorrectly seating the replacement liner at the stainless steel IBC interfaces
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly seating a replacement liner is a maintenance-task error, secondary to primary use (low relevance), and typically detectable and correctable before harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Fitting guides and procedures exist, but wrinkles, gaps or poor seating at the interface remain common inconsistencies. Seating a liner is a moderate-duration task where haste can cause misalignment but time is secondary. Correctly seating the liner at the stainless interfaces is a hands-on judgement task prone to error without trained operators. Liners are relatively low cost, so an improperly seated one is mostly a rework rather than scrap expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating product-contact closure surfaces during torque checking
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating closure surfaces during torque checking is a narrow procedural exposure that is usually avoidable and correctable before fill.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice with dedicated clean tools and glove protocols handles most cases, though occasional accidental contact of the closure surface still occurs. Torque checking is a quick routine task, so time is rarely the binding constraint on protecting closure surfaces. Technicians use calibrated torque wrenches with defined procedures, but avoiding contact contamination still relies on trained handling discipline. Clean tooling and single-use covers add modest recurring cost but are broadly affordable in maintenance shops.

rows.source_job: Flush contaminated container with neutralising agent
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of corrosion or surface degradation caused by the neutralising agent inside the stainless steel IBC
rows.imp_rat: Corrosion from a neutralising agent inside a stainless IBC is a narrow chemical-compatibility case, so relevance is low and severity develops slowly and observably. Kept below 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Material-compatibility charts and controlled dwell times are well established, so chloride pitting from neutralisers is uncommon but not eliminated. Limiting agent contact time is a scheduled step that fits within the normal cleaning window. Choosing agents compatible with stainless steel requires chemical knowledge that experienced staff hold but juniors may lack. Compatible neutralising agents and prompt rinsing add modest cost versus the expense of a corroded vessel.

rows.stk: Operations or EHS personnel responsible for end-of-life container processing.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of decontamination evidence being insufficient for regulatory or downstream acceptance
rows.imp_rat: Insufficient decontamination evidence is a documentation/compliance error with real regulatory consequences (moderate severity) but is peripheral to the container's physical containment job (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning protocols with batch records are standard in regulated settings, yet gaps, ambiguous logs or missing swab data still cause downstream rejections. Generating and compiling decontamination records adds administrative time to each cleaning cycle. Producing acceptable evidence requires understanding of regulatory expectations that not all maintenance staff possess. Documentation systems, swab testing and analytics carry ongoing cost but are established line items.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percent of tank-instrument calibration points remaining outside acceptance tolerance
rows.imp_rat: Calibration points outside tolerance degrade measurement trust (moderate relevance), but the error is administrative-adjacent and usually caught in calibration cycles, so severity is modest.
rows.sat_rat: Documented calibration programs with traceable standards keep most points within tolerance, though drift and procedural errors leave a residual out-of-tolerance fraction. Instrument calibration is time-consuming per point but scheduled routinely during preparation. Calibration technicians using standard procedures and reference standards handle this competently. Calibration labor and standards are recurring budgeted costs, not exceptional.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of utility services supplied outside tank inlet specifications
rows.imp_rat: Utility services outside inlet spec can disrupt tank operation, but relevance is peripheral to the core job and consequences are typically detected and corrected, keeping both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-startup utility verification is standard practice yet fluctuating supply quality and incomplete specification checks leave real gaps. Verifying utility supply conditions is a discrete pre-startup check of modest duration. Confirming utilities meet inlet specs requires technicians familiar with the service ranges, generally available. Out-of-spec utilities can waste energy and damage equipment, but checks are low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of installed tank instruments positioned to produce nonrepresentative measurements
rows.imp_rat: Instruments positioned to give nonrepresentative measurements affect data quality (moderate relevance), but this is a design/placement detail with limited direct severity, capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Placement is often driven by installation convenience rather than measurement representativeness, and standard practice inconsistently catches nonrepresentative positioning. Assessing representative instrument placement rarely adds much schedule time. Judging whether a probe location yields representative measurement requires experienced process/instrument engineers whose judgement is scarce and often applied too late. Poor placement wastes little material but causes costly measurement-driven process errors.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of processed material remaining in the tank after discharge
rows.imp_rat: Processed material remaining after discharge is largely a yield-loss issue (moderate relevance) with low severity, comfortably under the 3.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Well-designed drain geometry gives consistent low residuals, though viscous or clingy products leave a repeatable film that standard practice cannot fully eliminate. Draining and CIP cycles add time, but standard sloped-bottom and dished-head tanks discharge in a reasonably predictable duration. Minimizing heel largely relies on good tank geometry and pump design rather than operator skill, so it is not skill-limited once equipment is chosen. Residual heel is direct product loss plus added rinse/cleaning burden, and conical/sloped bottoms with eductors are mature practice that recovers most but never all material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of threads or sealing surfaces being damaged during fitting
rows.imp_rat: Thread or sealing-surface damage during fitting can cause leaks, but it is a localized rework issue that is usually caught and repaired before service.
rows.sat_rat: Cross-threading and surface nicks remain a recurring defect despite torque tools, since damage is often invisible until leak-tested. Careful threading takes a little longer but is not a major time drain. Avoiding cross-threading and surface scoring relies heavily on operator feel and attentiveness. Damaged threads can scrap a valve or fitting, creating meaningful component waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the container shifting or tipping during filling
rows.imp_rat: Container shifting or tipping during filling is a controllable filling-station event with modest severity and limited centrality to the container's overall job, warranting a low score under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Wide-footprint frames on level rated surfaces make tipping during filling rare under standard practice. Positioning and, if needed, chocking or restraining the container is a brief setup step. Placing an IBC on a stable, level base is intuitive and low-skill. A tipped container wastes product and risks damage, but stable placement itself costs nothing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of vent components installed in an incorrect orientation during configuration
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly oriented vent components affect proper venting configuration, a secondary aspect of the job, with moderate severity since it may cause pressure or ingress issues that are typically caught in setup.
rows.sat_rat: Directional markings and keyed vents reduce misorientation, though ambiguous markings still cause occasional errors. Orienting a vent correctly takes seconds during configuration. Correct vent orientation depends on the operator recognizing flow-direction markings that are easy to misread. A reversed vent is cheap to refit if caught but costly if it fails in service.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging the inner vessel while joining it to the frame and base
rows.imp_rat: Damaging the inner vessel while joining it to the frame is an assembly-phase defect of moderate relevance with consequences usually caught before service. Capped per batch.
rows.sat_rat: Guided assembly and protective interfaces make inner-vessel damage during joining an uncommon event. Joining the vessel to frame and base is routine and adds minimal time. The task uses defined fixtures and fasteners requiring only standard mechanical competence. Denting or scratching the inner vessel during assembly is costly but rare with proper handling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of puncturing or tearing the liner while inserting or securing it inside the container
rows.imp_rat: Puncturing or tearing the liner during insertion is relevant only to lined IBC configurations and the torn liner is usually spotted before fill, limiting severity. Held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Deburred edges, guides and inspection reduce punctures, but tears during insertion around ports remain a recurring nuisance. Careful liner insertion adds little time unless a tear forces liner replacement and restart. Inserting a film liner without snagging on edges or fittings takes practiced hands and attention. A punctured liner is cheap to replace but a leak later is costly, so scrapping at install is the cheaper failure.

rows.source_job: Fit sealing components to container openings
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of container openings left unsealed after fitting
rows.imp_rat: Container openings left unsealed after fitting can cause leakage or contamination, but it is a preventable assembly oversight with modest severity and secondary relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Established checklists and torque-verification procedures catch most unsealed openings, though a residual few slip through under time pressure. Sealing openings during prep is a quick step, so the time cost of verifying every closure is low. Torquing and confirming caps requires only basic operator training, not scarce expertise. Missed seals cause leaks and product loss, but the sealing components themselves are cheap.

rows.source_job: Fit sealing components to container openings
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of fitting a sealing component that does not match its designated container opening
rows.imp_rat: Fitting a mismatched sealing component risks an incomplete seal, but it is a controllable assembly error with moderate consequences and limited centrality to the overall job.
rows.sat_rat: Keyed fittings and labeled kits reduce mismatches, but visually similar openings still cause occasional wrong pairings. Matching the right closure to the right opening adds only minor lookup time during fitting. Correct matching depends on operator familiarity with part numbers and can trip up new staff. A mismatched seal wastes the component and may require rework but is inexpensive on its own.

rows.source_job: Fit sealing components to container openings
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of sealing components seated or engaged incorrectly on container openings
rows.imp_rat: Sealing components seated incorrectly can cause leaks, but it is a preventable assembly defect with modest consequences and secondary relevance to the core job, so it scores low.
rows.sat_rat: Clamp and closure designs give tactile confirmation, but incorrectly seated gaskets or partially engaged clamps still occur without a positive verification step. Checking seating of sealing components adds minor time unless a re-seat and re-verify is needed. Correctly engaging closures and seals relies on operator care and feel, with some room for error. Seals are inexpensive, but an incorrectly seated one risks leakage and lost product.

rows.source_job: Learn container preparation and handling procedures
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty about the cleanliness and filling-readiness acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about cleanliness and filling-readiness acceptance criteria can cause inconsistent release decisions, but it is a specification-clarity issue peripheral to physical containment.
rows.sat_rat: Vague or inconsistent acceptance criteria produce variable outcomes and disputes between prep and filling teams. Chasing down acceptance criteria for cleanliness and fill-readiness often causes delays and back-and-forth. Interpreting cleanliness and readiness criteria demands experienced judgement that is frequently ambiguous or undocumented. Uncertainty leads to redundant cleaning or rejected containers, wasting labor and material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty caused by incomplete or conflicting specifications for the intended contents
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete or conflicting content specifications create ambiguity that can lead to material incompatibility, but it is an upstream specification-clarity concern peripheral to the container's job.
rows.sat_rat: Incomplete and conflicting content specifications are a recurring reality with no strong standard resolution mechanism, so uncertainty persists. Chasing down and reconciling incomplete or conflicting content specifications introduces back-and-forth that delays specification finalization. Resolving conflicting content specs requires cross-functional judgement to know which source governs, an expertise that is unevenly distributed. Ambiguous content specs are cheap to flag but expensive when they drive wrong material or design choices.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of tank connections leaking after seal replacement
rows.imp_rat: Connections leaking after seal replacement is a rework/maintenance error with moderate consequences, but relevance is limited and leaks are typically detected quickly, keeping both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Proper gasket selection and controlled bolt-up make post-service leaks uncommon though re-torque is sometimes needed. Seal replacement and leak check are quick, routine tasks. Gasket/seal fitting is a common trade skill with well-known torque and alignment practices. Seals and gaskets are inexpensive, and rework cost is low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of product or slurry remaining inside the tank after draining
rows.imp_rat: Product or slurry remaining after draining is a yield/waste concern (moderate relevance) with low severity since it is economic rather than catastrophic, staying well under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Sloped bottoms and drain pumps reliably remove most liquid, though slurries and viscous product leave a stubborn heel. Gravity draining plus pumping heels is a routine, reasonably quick operation. Draining is a low-skill task using standard valves and pumps. Residual heels represent lost product and become hazardous-waste volume, but draining recovers the bulk economically.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of error in the elapsed-hold calculation for the vessel batch
rows.imp_rat: Error in elapsed-hold calculation affects the hold-time compliance judgment, moderately relevant to the timed containment job; usually a correctable arithmetic issue (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Automated timestamps make elapsed-hold calculation accurate, though manual logs still err. Elapsed-hold calculation is quick where automated. Manual clock math is error-prone without system support. Calculation error can wrongly reject or accept a batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement between hold-status information available to vessel and fill-finish personnel
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement in hold-status information between vessel and fill-finish personnel could misinform handoff, moderately relevant to coordination; mostly a communication gap (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Shared systems help, but hold-status disagreement between vessel and fill-finish teams still occurs at handoffs. Sharing status can lag between shifts and departments. Low skill; a communication/handoff issue. Miscommunication can cause scheduling loss, modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of vessel batches with an incorrectly recorded holding-clock start
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly recorded holding-clock start distorts hold-time accounting, moderately relevant to a time-bounded hold; typically detectable and correctable (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Automated event capture reduces wrong start times, but manual entry errors persist. Recording the clock start is a single-event action. Simple but dependent on operator diligence when manual. An error can invalidate hold-time compliance, costing the batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of directing bulk product to the wrong downstream destination
rows.imp_rat: Directing bulk to the wrong destination is a routing defect only loosely tied to the containment CFJ (low relevance); could risk product misuse but severity held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Line-clearance and destination verification make wrong-destination discharge uncommon. Destination routing is set at discharge, not time-limited. Correct routing is a verification task. Misrouting can waste a batch, a large cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the holding clock starts from the wrong event for the vessel batch
rows.imp_rat: Holding clock starting from the wrong event misstates hold duration, moderately relevant to time-bounded containment; a correctable clocking error (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Defined hold-start events and system timestamps make wrong clock starts uncommon. Clock start is set at a defined event, usually correctly and immediately. Identifying the correct start event follows procedure with modest skill. Timekeeping systems add negligible cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between reported and actual remaining holding margin
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between reported and actual remaining holding margin misinforms hold decisions, moderately relevant to time-bounded holding; usually a correctable reporting error (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: System-tracked hold timers make reported margin closely match actual in most cases. Remaining margin is computed continuously so reporting lag is minimal. Margin calculation is automated needing little skill. Timekeeping systems add negligible cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that required microbiological results remain pending beyond disposition review
rows.imp_rat: Microbiological results pending beyond disposition review delays release decisions, moderately relevant to timely disposition (moderate-low relevance); mostly a schedule impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: The -day sterility test turnaround chronically leaves results pending, a widely felt constraint. Sterility testing incubation forces microbiological results to remain pending for days, routinely beyond desired disposition timing. Nothing operators do can meaningfully shorten the mandated incubation period. Rapid micro methods could shorten this but require costly validation and investment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sterile bulk transfer cannot begin at the scheduled discharge point
rows.imp_rat: Transfer unable to begin at the scheduled discharge point is a readiness/availability failure, moderately relevant to completing the hold-to-fill handoff; mostly a delay (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Scheduling coordination usually enables timely transfer, with occasional slippage. Transfer start depends on fill-finish scheduling that can slip. Coordination is a scheduling task needing modest skill. Idle capacity and delays carry cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that applicable acceptance limits or disposition authority are unclear during adjudication
rows.imp_rat: Unclear acceptance limits or disposition authority is an administrative adjudication ambiguity, tangential to physically holding the solution aseptically (low relevance); mostly recoverable confusion (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Specs and authority matrices exist but borderline cases still leave recurring ambiguity during adjudication. Clarifying limits or authority mid-adjudication stalls the decision and adds delay. Ambiguity here reflects gaps in codified criteria, forcing reliance on senior interpretation. Delayed or wrong decisions from ambiguity carry schedule and material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that fill-finish planning or batch use proceeds under an outdated deviation status
rows.imp_rat: Proceeding under an outdated deviation status affects downstream use decisions, indirectly relevant to the hold CFJ (low relevance); could carry a defect forward but capped (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Status systems help but latency in propagating deviation status means outdated-status decisions still occur. Stale deviation status propagating to planning causes downstream rework delays. Keeping status current across functions needs disciplined coordination. Acting on outdated status can waste downstream effort and material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved KPI excursions at disposition of the held bulk
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved KPI excursions at disposition affect release readiness, moderately relevant to disposition; typically a delay/rework driver rather than harm (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: KPI tracking exists but excursions frequently remain open at disposition, a recurring gap. Unresolved KPI excursions at disposition force last-minute review extensions. Resolving KPI excursions requires technical judgement that is not always immediately available. Open excursions delay release and risk material, a cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of shortfall between usable bulk solution volume and required fill-finish volume at handoff
rows.imp_rat: Shortfall between usable bulk and required fill-finish volume at handoff affects delivery completeness, moderately relevant to the handoff; mostly a supply/yield consequence (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Volume accounting and overage planning make shortfalls uncommon, though yield losses during hold occasionally cause them. Volume shortfall discovered at handoff can force delays to source additional material. Volume reconciliation is a routine measurement task requiring modest skill. A usable-volume shortfall against required fill volume is a direct material loss and the primary stake here.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of a qualification run conducted outside specified controlled conditions
rows.imp_rat: A qualification run partly conducted outside controlled conditions weakens validation evidence, indirectly relevant to the hold job (low relevance); could undermine qualification claims but capped (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Portions of runs occasionally drift outside specified controlled conditions. Maintaining controlled conditions is continuous, not a delay concern. Keeping runs within controlled conditions requires disciplined execution. Environmental control carries facility cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of contact-material damage caused by required cleaning and sterilization exposures
rows.imp_rat: Contact-material damage from cleaning/sterilization could compromise a surface, indirectly relevant to holding integrity (low relevance); degraded surface risk capped at ceiling (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Material damage from cleaning and sterilization is well characterized for standard materials and controlled by validated cycles, so it is reasonably served. Cleaning/sterilization effects are assessed during qualification without major delay. Evaluating cleaning/sterilization damage needs materials-engineering knowledge that is fairly available. Sterilization compatibility testing adds moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the recorded temperature differs from the actual contents temperature
rows.imp_rat: A recorded-versus-actual temperature discrepancy is a measurement/records error, tangential to the container's containment job (low relevance) with moderate consequences for temperature-sensitive product (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: A single-point probe often misrepresents a thermally stratified bulk, so the recorded value can systematically differ from the true contents temperature. Probe readings are effectively instantaneous so temperature feedback is not time-limited. Correct probe placement and calibration require modest technical judgement that is generally available. Temperature probes and data loggers are inexpensive and routinely fitted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time for material discharge to settle within the acceptable range after an operator adjustment
rows.imp_rat: Settling time after an operator discharge adjustment is a performance-tuning concern (modest relevance) with minor consequences that are mainly efficiency-related (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Settling behavior is inconsistent across materials and fill levels, so the delay varies unpredictably. After an operator adjusts a manual valve, discharge rate takes appreciable time to stabilize because of material inertia and headspace effects. Anticipating settle time to avoid over-correction demands operator feel that is hard to standardize. Automated flow controllers that shorten settling exist but are an added cost seldom fitted to simple IBC discharge.

rows.source_job: Label container with contents and status
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that container markings become unreadable during use
rows.imp_rat: Unreadable markings create identification confusion but do not directly threaten containment; consequences are administrative traceability issues rather than product loss.
rows.sat_rat: Laminated labels and molded ID plates last well, but abrasion, chemical exposure and washdowns still render markings unreadable over a service life. Applying durable labels or tags takes little time. Minimal skill is needed to attach or re-place markings. Durable labels, engraved plates or placards are cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood the extracted sample is insufficient for the required quality or compliance tests
rows.imp_rat: An insufficient extracted sample is a sampling/QA-support error, peripheral to core containment (low relevance), typically remedied by re-sampling (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standardized sampling procedures usually yield adequate samples, but stratified or heterogeneous bulk still occasionally produces insufficient or unrepresentative draws. Sampling from a valve or port is quick and does not materially delay the stage. Obtaining a representative, sufficient sample requires trained technique to avoid segregation or short-draw. Sample thieves and sampling ports are low-cost and standard equipment.

rows.source_job: Agitate contained material to maintain uniformity
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of air entrained in the bulk material during agitation
rows.imp_rat: Air entrainment during agitation is peripheral to the core containment job and its consequences are usually a minor quality/handling nuisance rather than catastrophic, so both proxies stay low.
rows.sat_rat: Air entrainment during agitation is poorly controlled with typical IBC mixing setups and varies with fill level and product rheology. Slowing agitation to limit air entrainment lengthens mixing time, a trade-off operators accept. Minimizing entrained air relies on operator judgement of agitator speed and immersion that is difficult to get right. Low-shear or bottom-entry agitators that reduce entrainment are a significant capital add-on.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required segregation space is unavailable when containers are in use
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable segregation space is largely a facility-logistics issue, only tangential to the container's core function (low relevance), and its consequences are typically minor scheduling delays (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Marked segregation zones and warehouse management practice usually ensure space, but congestion and poor planning still leave it unavailable at peak times. Confirming segregation space is a fast planning check. Layout planning needs some knowledge of segregation rules but little specialist skill. Reserved segregation floor space carries an opportunity cost in constrained facilities.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of tank acceptance criteria left unresolved in the final contract
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved acceptance criteria in the contract (ambiguity) is a peripheral administrative concern to the tank's core processing job; consequences are contractual friction rather than functional failure, but capped at 2.5 by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Contracts often carry ambiguous or missing acceptance language because templates lag actual process needs, leaving recurring gaps at handover. Resolving open acceptance criteria drags out contract finalization when legal and engineering must iterate on wording. Drafting testable acceptance criteria for a sterile tank requires scarce combined process-engineering and contracting judgement. Unresolved criteria surface as disputes and warranty claims later, converting into avoidable cost during commissioning.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between the purchase order and the approved tank configuration
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between purchase order and approved configuration is a procurement/documentation error (low relevance to the operating job) with moderate downstream consequences, staying well under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Standard procurement practice uses configuration sign-off and PO review checklists that catch most mismatches, though line-item errors still slip through on complex tank specs. PO reconciliation adds review cycles but rarely stalls acquisition for long since it happens in parallel with vendor negotiation. Catching PO-vs-configuration mismatches depends on an experienced buyer or engineer cross-checking documents, and that expertise is not always available. Discrepancies caught late can trigger change orders and rework charges, so the cost exposure is real but usually contained by pre-issue review.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved supplier quality-system gaps against sterile fill-finish requirements
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved supplier quality-system gaps against sterile requirements carry compliance risk, but they are a supplier-qualification concern removed from the tank's active job and constrained to 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Formal supplier qualification exists but coverage against sterile fill-finish specifics is uneven, so unresolved gaps regularly persist into award. Supplier quality audits and gap assessments are time-consuming and often bottleneck qualification before a PO can issue. Auditing a fabricator's quality system against fill-finish requirements needs trained quality auditors who are in short supply. Undetected quality-system gaps produce nonconforming tanks and costly requalification, a significant downstream expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile-service requirements not satisfied in approved fabrication drawings
rows.imp_rat: Sterile-service requirements missing from approved drawings is a documentation-stage defect with downstream consequences, but it is upstream of active processing and constrained to the 2.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Drawing reviews against sterile requirements are inconsistent and checklist-dependent, so omissions in approved drawings remain a common failure. Verifying sterile-service features in fabrication drawings requires added drawing-review loops that extend the pre-fabrication window. It takes specialized hygienic-design expertise (drainability, crevice-free welds, surface finish) that few reviewers on a buying team truly possess. Missed sterile requirements caught after fabrication force expensive re-fabrication or field rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that transit damage remains undetected on received tank surfaces
rows.imp_rat: Undetected transit damage on received tank surfaces (unreliability) has moderate severity but is a receiving-inspection concern rather than a core processing driver; capped by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Visual receiving inspection catches obvious damage but concealed or internal-surface damage frequently goes undetected until later. Receiving inspection adds time on delivery but is usually brief. Detecting subtle transit damage requires trained inspectors and sometimes NDE, expertise not always present at receiving. Missed damage leads to costly late discovery and warranty disputes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between installed accessory positions and tank specifications
rows.imp_rat: Deviation of installed accessory positions from spec is a fabrication/installation defect with moderate rework consequences, but it is not central to the core processing job and is capped by the batch-4 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Drawing-based installation checks catch most deviations but tolerance interpretation and field fit-up still produce recurring minor mismatches. Verifying accessory positions is a straightforward walkdown of limited duration. Fitters using drawings can locate accessories with modest skill requirements. Minor mispositioning causes low-cost rework in most cases.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of trained operators unable to perform safe tank operation and cleaning procedures
rows.imp_rat: Operators unable to perform safe operation and cleaning is a training defect with some safety relevance, but for this batch it is ranked lower and kept at or under the 2.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Structured training and competency sign-offs exist but assessment rigor is inconsistent, leaving some operators unable to perform procedures safely. Operator training programs take significant lead time to develop and deliver. The need is fundamentally about building operator competence, and training quality and retention vary widely by site. Training consumes instructor and operator time but is a normal budgeted cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between a tank alarm and the start of operator investigation
rows.imp_rat: A longer delay between a tank alarm and operator investigation is only moderately relevant to routine processing and its consequences vary; ranked below the prior batch, it is held at or under 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm delivery is generally dependable, but alarm flooding, acknowledgment fatigue and understaffed night shifts still occasionally stretch the time to first action. Modern SCADA/DCS alarms annunciate instantly and route to HMIs, radios and mobile alerts, sharply cutting the delay before an operator begins investigating. Prioritized, rationalized alarm systems reduce the interpretation skill needed, though nuisance-alarm floods can still confuse less experienced staff. Alarm and notification infrastructure is already standard on most process tanks, so no meaningful incremental cost blocks fast response.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation of repaired frame or pallet-base geometry from stackability and stability requirements
rows.imp_rat: Deviation of repaired frame geometry from stackability requirements is a repair-quality defect with low relevance to the primary containment job and modest severity. Kept well below 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Without go/no-go gauges many shops eyeball straightness, so repaired geometry sometimes drifts outside stackability limits and fails later stacking checks.
