Frame and pallet-base repair with jig setup and re-check takes meaningful time on the maintenance floor. Restoring stackable geometry to tolerance depends on skilled fitters and correct fixturing. Jigs, fixtures and rework consume real labour and material, and a mis-repaired frame may be scrapped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applying an incorrect calibration tolerance or range to an attached level, pressure, or temperature instrument
rows.imp_rat: Applying an incorrect calibration tolerance to an attached instrument is a peripheral administrative concern for the container's core containment job, and consequences are limited to measurement inaccuracy rather than batch loss.
rows.sat_rat: Documented calibration procedures and traceable standards make correct-range application usual, though transcription or wrong-spec errors occasionally slip through. Pulling the correct calibration range from procedures is a fast lookup within the calibration task. Applying the right tolerance requires metrology knowledge, but calibration procedures largely encode it for the technician. Reference standards and documentation are already resourced, so incremental cost is low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of container or closure damage during leak testing
rows.imp_rat: Container or closure damage during leak testing is a test-induced defect that is caught in the same process step, with limited downstream consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized pressure limits and calibrated gauges make test-induced damage uncommon, though manual over-pressurisation still occasionally deforms closures. Leak testing itself is quick and rarely the bottleneck in maintenance. Setting safe test pressures to avoid overstressing the container is procedural for trained testers. Test equipment is cheap, and damage from over-pressure is the main avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which repaired pallet feet or frame sections remain misaligned beyond permitted tolerances
rows.imp_rat: Misaligned repaired pallet feet or frame sections beyond tolerance is largely a refurbishment quality concern with limited severity and low centrality to the container's active job.
rows.sat_rat: Alignment is frequently checked by eye rather than gauge, so residual misalignment beyond tolerance recurs and only surfaces at stacking. Realigning pallet feet or frame sections adds setup and re-measure time to the repair. Achieving alignment within tolerance requires a skilled fitter's touch and reference to fixtures. Rework labour and possible scrap on repeated misalignment drive avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contaminants onto product-contact surfaces during identification marking restoration
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during identification-marking restoration is a peripheral, infrequent maintenance task relative to the containment job, though touching product-contact surfaces gives it slightly elevated but still capped severity.
rows.sat_rat: Restricting marking to exterior zones with approved inks is well established, so product-contact contamination from marking is fairly uncommon. Marking restoration is a brief task not gated by time. Using non-contaminating markers and keeping them off product zones is simple with minimal skill once specified. Approved food/pharma-grade marking materials cost slightly more but are minor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between the purchase order and the agreed tank quotation and specification
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between purchase order and agreed quotation are administrative/commercial errors with low relevance to the tank's functional job and generally minor, easily reconciled consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Established PO-verification and approval workflows in ERP systems make discrepancies fairly detectable, though manual spec matching still lets some slip through. PO-to-quote reconciliation is a discrete review step that adds little delay when done. Matching a PO line-by-line to the agreed quotation and spec is clerical work most procurement staff can handle. Undetected discrepancies drive rework and dispute costs, but standard procurement controls catch most.

rows.source_job: Compare tank configurations against installation requirements
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the selected tank cannot pass through the delivery route or fit the installation area
rows.imp_rat: A selected tank that cannot pass the delivery route or fit the site is a logistics/sizing error, peripheral to the operating job (low relevance) though costly to remedy when it happens (moderate severity). Bounded by the batch ceiling at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Dimensional drawings, delivery route surveys and 3D fit checks are mature practice that reliably prevents most access failures. Route and fit surveys are quick to arrange and rarely delay the acquisition when scheduled. Rigging and logistics specialists routinely perform transport-envelope checks with well-understood methods. A tank that cannot fit or transit incurs huge remediation costs, so the check pays for itself.

rows.source_job: Assess site suitability for tank installation
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that site load capacity is insufficient for the filled tank
rows.imp_rat: Insufficient site load capacity for a filled tank is a serious structural risk, but it is a pre-install site-assessment error rather than an operating-phase job element (modest relevance) with high stakes when realized (moderate severity). Held at 2.5 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Load calculations and foundation design reviews are well-codified civil engineering practice that reliably catches insufficient capacity. Structural load checks are a standard early engineering task that seldom delays acquisition. Structural engineers routinely compute filled-tank loads against foundation capacity using established methods. Foundation reinforcement caught early is far cheaper than post-delivery discovery.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of installed monitoring instruments producing readings outside tolerance
rows.imp_rat: Monitoring instruments reading outside tolerance affects oversight accuracy but is ancillary to physical storage (modest relevance) and usually correctable via recalibration (low severity). Capped at 2.5 per the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Factory calibration plus site loop checks catch most out-of-tolerance instruments, though field drift and installation effects still produce occasional bad readings. Loop checks and instrument verification are scheduled activities that consume commissioning time but are planned. Instrument techs performing verification are trained, though correct setup and reference standards require care. Faulty instruments cause rework and possible product loss, but verification itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of tank deviation from specified levelness tolerance
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from levelness tolerance affects install quality and gauging accuracy but is a commissioning error with limited centrality to storing material (modest relevance) and generally minor consequences (low severity). Capped at 2.5 by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Optical/laser leveling and documented tolerances make achieving levelness a well-controlled and verifiable outcome. Leveling with shims and survey instruments is a routine, reasonably quick step. Surveyors and millwrights using laser levels handle this with mature, well-understood technique. Grout, shims, and survey labor are modest costs within normal installation budgets.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of product residue or cleaning agent remaining on interior surfaces after cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Product residue or cleaning agent left on interior surfaces matters for changeover purity but is a periodic maintenance error rather than central to holding material (modest relevance), with consequences usually limited to a re-clean (low severity). Held at or below 2.5 per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mechanical and chemical cleaning plus swab/visual checks remove most residue, but corners, heating coils, and porous surfaces routinely retain traces. Tank cleaning and rinse verification is one of the longest maintenance activities, often days per tank. Achieving and confirming a clean interior depends on trained cleaning crews and confined-space discipline rather than automated assurance. Cleaning agents, waste disposal, and water use make residue removal materially expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of valve components reassembled incorrectly after service
rows.imp_rat: Valve components reassembled incorrectly after service could cause leaks, but it is a maintenance-task error peripheral to the tank shell's storage job (low relevance) though moderately serious if a valve fails (moderate severity). Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Manufacturer procedures and exploded diagrams help, but without functional bench testing, misassembly can persist undetected until operation. Valve reassembly is a modest-duration task and rarely the schedule driver. Correct reassembly hinges on the technician's familiarity with the specific valve and torque sequence, a classic manual-skill error source. Parts are inexpensive, though a misassembled valve failing in service can be costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of holidays or pinholes remaining in the renewed lining
rows.imp_rat: Holidays or pinholes in a renewed lining can seed corrosion, but this is a maintenance-cycle defect somewhat removed from the core containment job (modest relevance) with gradual rather than immediate consequences (moderate severity). Constrained to 2.5 by batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Spark testing is a mature, sensitive method that finds nearly all holidays, so residual pinholes are uncommon where testing is done to spec. High-voltage holiday testing of a new lining is fast once cure is complete, adding little to the reline schedule. Both applying a pinhole-free lining and running the holiday test properly demand skilled coating applicators and inspectors. Relining materials are costly, but the incremental holiday-test step is cheap insurance.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of water, sludge, or sediment remaining on the tank floor after draining
rows.imp_rat: Water, sludge, or sediment left on the floor after draining affects turnaround cleanliness but is a maintenance detail somewhat removed from core storage (modest relevance) with minor consequences (low severity). Constrained to 2.5 by the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Standard sumps, squeegeeing, and vacuuming clear most liquid, but low spots, pitting, and sludge under coils commonly retain residue affecting inspection. Draining, squeegeeing, and vacuuming residual water and sludge adds significant hands-on time to floor work. Getting the floor genuinely dry is manual grunt work needing diligence more than rare expertise. Sludge handling and disposal drive notable cost, especially for hazardous residues.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of residual contamination remaining above applicable limits at the former tank location
rows.imp_rat: Residual contamination above limits at a former tank location is an end-of-life decommissioning concern, peripheral to the ongoing storage job (low relevance), though the environmental/regulatory consequences can be moderately serious when it occurs. Capped by the batch ranking at or below 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Even with standard site-assessment protocols, undiscovered leaks and migrated contamination routinely leave residual levels above limits, forcing reopening of closures. Site remediation and confirmatory sampling to reach clean levels can drag on for months, especially with subsurface contamination. Achieving closure below limits demands experienced environmental consultants and regulator negotiation, which many operators lack in-house. Soil and groundwater remediation to applicable limits is frequently the single largest and least predictable disposal cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that specified dimensions, pallet base, or discharge geometry conflict with facility interfaces
rows.imp_rat: Dimension, pallet-base, or discharge-geometry conflicts with facility interfaces are logistics/fit issues surfaced at commissioning, peripheral to the container's containment function.
rows.sat_rat: Established dimensional-review practice reliably prevents most facility-interface conflicts, with occasional misses on discharge-height or docking specifics. Dimensional and interface checks are quick when facility drawings are available during specification. Comparing IBC footprint, pallet base, and discharge geometry to facility interfaces is straightforward for most facilities engineers. Catching interface conflicts before purchase is cheap and avoids costly retrofits or unusable units.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment among the frame, pallet base, and inner vessel in the assembled container
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment among frame, pallet base, and inner vessel is largely a build-quality nuisance with low relevance to containment and mild, correctable severity. Kept low under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized frames with keyed mounting points make gross misalignment uncommon, so this is well served in typical practice. Aligning frame, pallet and vessel is quick when using purpose-built jigs or pre-integrated units. Alignment is largely designed-in for standard IBCs, needing little operator judgement. Misalignment rarely wastes material because it is caught and corrected before filling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of wetted closure components omitted from the compatibility assessment
rows.imp_rat: Omitting wetted closure components from compatibility assessment is an upstream documentation gap (low relevance to daily use) that could later cause a seal failure but is usually caught (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Small gaskets, o-rings and vent parts are routinely missed from compatibility lists, making omissions a persistent gap. Adding every wetted closure component to the assessment is a modest documentation effort. Knowing which closure components are wetted and must be assessed requires component knowledge that is easily overlooked. An omitted component that later fails compatibility causes costly recalls or batch loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of the tank-port cross-section obstructed after flushing
rows.imp_rat: Tank-port cross-section obstruction after flushing can affect flow, giving some relevance, but this item ranks below the prior batch and is held so the mean stays at or under 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Standard flushing procedures exist but verification of residual obstruction is frequently visual and inconsistent, so partial blockage recurs and is a real recurring gap. Flushing cycles and inspection to confirm ports are clear can be time-consuming, often requiring repeat passes. Judging whether a port is adequately cleared often relies on operator experience rather than a hard measurable spec. Flushing consumes water, solvents and disposal capacity, and inefficient cycles waste those resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of external tank cleaning introducing contaminants into the sterile fill-finish area
rows.imp_rat: Contaminants introduced into a sterile fill-finish area during external cleaning can be severe, but this item sits below the prior batch in importance ordering; both proxies are held so the mean does not exceed 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Barriers and procedures help, but external cleaning near classified areas remains a recurring contamination-ingress risk. Controlled external cleaning near sterile zones requires careful sequencing that adds time. Preventing cross-contamination into fill-finish areas depends on trained personnel following gowning and zoning discipline. Containment, controlled cleaning agents and area protection add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing a consumable element with an incorrect material, size, or rating
rows.imp_rat: Installing a consumable with wrong material, size, or rating is a defect that can degrade performance, but for this batch it ranks lower and both proxies are kept so the mean stays at 2.5 or below.
rows.sat_rat: BOM controls and spec labeling reduce mismatches, but manual selection still allows occasional incorrect elements. Verifying consumable material/size/rating is a brief check when parts are staged correctly. Correct part selection relies on technician attention and readable specs, an error-prone manual step. Wrong-part installs are cheap individually but costly if they cause failure or rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of tank fasteners remaining outside specified torque after tightening
rows.imp_rat: Fasteners left outside specified torque is a mechanical defect that could affect integrity, but it ranks below the prior batch and both proxies are held so the mean does not exceed 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Calibrated torque tools and documented torque values are mature, widely deployed practice that keeps out-of-spec fasteners uncommon, though tool drift and human skips still cause occasional misses. Calibrated torque wrenches make tightening reasonably quick, though checking every fastener on a large tank adds appreciable time. Torque-to-spec is a well-documented routine that most maintenance technicians can execute with modest training using standard tools. Torque wrenches and periodic calibration are inexpensive relative to the cost of a leaking joint failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the return channel refuses the tank because contamination status is unverified
rows.imp_rat: A return-channel refusal over unverified contamination status is a logistics/reverse-flow error, peripheral to the tank doing its processing job, and held under 2.5 per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Cleanliness certificates are standard practice, but mismatched or unverified documentation still causes receivers to reject tanks at the gate. Verification paperwork must clear before the tank ships, adding coordination delay. Producing acceptable contamination verification needs someone who understands the receiver's acceptance criteria. A refused tank means wasted transport and rescheduling costs.

rows.source_job: Obtain regulatory approval for tank disposal
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that tank disposal begins before required regulatory approval
rows.imp_rat: Disposal before regulatory approval is an end-of-life compliance error with real regulatory consequences, but it is remote from the tank's active processing job and ranked below the prior batch, so it stays at the 2.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Permit-tracking and hold-point procedures usually catch premature starts, though informal disposals occasionally jump the gate. Waiting on regulatory approval is a known scheduling gate that adds delay. Navigating permits requires compliance expertise but is a defined administrative competency. Permit fees and hold time carry cost, but proceeding early risks far larger fines.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of bulk holds with a missing start or end entry
rows.imp_rat: Holds with a missing start or end entry is a records-completeness defect, tangential to actual containment (low relevance); a reconstructable documentation gap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Required-field batch records make missing start/end entries uncommon. Start/end entries are discrete quick actions. Low skill; a completeness discipline. Missing entries force investigation, indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required condition observations absent from the hold record
rows.imp_rat: Required condition observations absent from the hold record is a monitoring-records gap, tangential to the physical hold (low relevance); a documentation completeness issue (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Automated data logging captures required observations reliably; manual gaps remain where used. Recording observations is routine at defined intervals. Low skill; discipline of completing logs. Missing observations trigger review overhead.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of hold deviations or interruptions omitted from the execution record
rows.imp_rat: Hold deviations omitted from the execution record is a records-completeness defect, tangential to the physical hold (low relevance); a documentation gap not product harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Deviation logging is procedurally required but occasional omissions still occur under workload. Logging deviations happens during/after events, minor time. Requires operator diligence to capture all interruptions. Omitted deviations risk compliance findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of hold actions lacking attributable performer identification
rows.imp_rat: Hold actions lacking attributable performer identification is a records-attribution ambiguity, peripheral to the physical hold (low relevance); a documentation gap only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: E-signature and initials requirements make unattributed actions rare. Attribution is captured at time of action. Low skill; requires login/signature discipline. Missing attribution risks data-integrity findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between each hold event and its corresponding record entry
rows.imp_rat: Lag between a hold event and its record entry is a documentation-timeliness delay, tangential to the hold job (low relevance); a records-currency gap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Real-time electronic capture minimizes lag where used, but paper records lag notably. Lag between event and record entry is the direct concern and can be long with paper. Low skill; a discipline/timeliness issue. Delayed entries risk data integrity findings, indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of bulk holds lacking traceable linkage among inbound transfer, vessel hold, and discharge records
rows.imp_rat: Bulk holds lacking traceable linkage across receipt/hold/discharge records is a documentation-continuity ambiguity, peripheral to containment (low relevance); a traceability gap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Batch record structure usually links transfer-hold-discharge, though manual systems leave occasional gaps. Linking records is done at review, adding some effort. Requires disciplined record cross-referencing. Broken linkage can trigger investigations, cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of record corrections obscuring the original entry, rationale, or performer
rows.imp_rat: Record corrections obscuring the original entry is a data-integrity defect, tangential to actual containment (low relevance); an audit-trail concern not product harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Audit-trailed systems preserve original entries reliably; paper corrections occasionally obscure originals. Correction handling is a per-edit action. Requires knowledge of GDP correction rules. Obscured corrections cause audit findings, cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the complete hold record is unavailable when batch traceability must be demonstrated
rows.imp_rat: Complete hold record unavailable for traceability is a documentation-availability defect, peripheral to physically holding the bulk (low relevance); a traceability gap not product harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Controlled document/EBR systems make complete hold records reliably available at traceability demand. Retrieval time matters mainly at audit, not during hold. Low skill to retrieve; depends on system organization. Missing records risk batch rejection, high cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of hold-status data lost or altered before batch review
rows.imp_rat: Hold-status data lost or altered before review is a data-integrity defect, peripheral to physically holding the bulk (low relevance); a records-preservation gap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Validated data historians with audit trails reliably prevent loss or alteration before review. Data preservation is continuous, not a time burden. Low skill; system-controlled. Lost data forces batch rejection, high cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of validated holding allowance consumed before sterile bulk enters the vessel
rows.imp_rat: Validated holding allowance consumed before bulk enters the vessel is a schedule-efficiency waste, peripheral to containment (low relevance); absorbable timing consumption (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Practice varies on when the clock starts, so pre-entry consumption of allowance is inconsistently managed. Allowance consumed before bulk enters is pure lost hold time from prep and staging. Little skill; a scheduling/coordination matter. Consumed allowance shrinks usable window and risks batch loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of fill-finish unavailability after vessel transfer is complete
rows.imp_rat: Duration of fill-finish unavailability after transfer is a downstream scheduling concern, peripheral to the CFJ of maintaining aseptic containment (low relevance); consequences are absorbable delay (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Downstream scheduling misalignment periodically extends holds beyond plan. Fill-finish unavailability after transfer forces the vessel to hold longer, consuming the clock. Little skill involved; it is a scheduling dependency. Extended hold risks limit breach and lost bulk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement about the events that start and stop the vessel holding clock
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement about clock start/stop events is a definition ambiguity, tangential to physical containment (low relevance); reconcilable procedural matter (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: SOP definitions usually align personnel, but ambiguity about clock events still causes disagreement. Defining start/stop events is a procedural matter resolved before execution. Requires clear SOP definitions to avoid interpretive disagreement. Disputes can cause batch disposition delays.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of deviation records containing inaccurate or missing observations and response actions
rows.imp_rat: Deviation records with inaccurate/missing observations is a documentation-quality defect, peripheral to actual containment (low relevance); a records fidelity gap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Deviation records frequently contain gaps or inaccuracies in observations and actions. Deviation documentation is done post-event, adding effort. Complete, accurate deviation records require trained diligence. Inaccurate deviation records prolong investigations, cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between readiness records and the vessel state
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between readiness records and vessel state is a records-fidelity ambiguity, peripheral to the containment job (low relevance); usually correctable before transfer (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Manual record-keeping leaves recurring discrepancies between records and actual state. Reconciling records to vessel state is a review-time task. Spotting record-vs-state discrepancies needs attentive review. Low cost beyond labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt when holding allowance and fill-finish readiness indications conflict
rows.imp_rat: Doubt when holding allowance and readiness indications conflict is a scheduling ambiguity, peripheral to containment (low relevance); resolvable coordination matter (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of bulk receipt beyond the approved transfer window
rows.imp_rat: Receipt extending beyond the approved transfer window is a scheduling/timing delay, only loosely tied to containment (low relevance); typically an absorbable timing overrun (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Controlled transfer rates usually complete within window, though occasional flow issues extend it. The transfer window duration is the explicit time metric here. Transfer rate is largely equipment-set, needing little skill. Overlong transfers tie up equipment time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of bulk product lost through leakage or spillage during receipt
rows.imp_rat: Leakage or spillage during receipt is a yield-loss/waste concern, marginal to aseptic containment integrity itself (low relevance); economic loss rather than patient harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Closed connections keep leakage/spillage low, with occasional connection losses remaining. Leakage occurs during the transfer window, not as time loss. Proper connections limit spillage with modest skill. Product loss through leakage is a direct material cost the setup aims to minimize.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of additional vessel holding caused by downstream unavailability after release eligibility
rows.imp_rat: Extra holding from downstream unavailability after eligibility is a scheduling delay, peripheral to the containment CFJ (low relevance); an absorbable timing overrun (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Scheduling coordination limits it, but downstream bottlenecks still force additional holding with some regularity. Extra holding from downstream unavailability is a pure waiting-time loss. This depends on scheduling coordination rather than operator skill. Extended holding consumes utilities and risks approaching hold limits.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of releasable bulk product remaining in the vessel after discharge
rows.imp_rat: Releasable bulk remaining after discharge is a yield/waste issue, peripheral to aseptic containment (low relevance); economic loss without safety consequence (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Vessel geometry and drain design recover most product reliably, though some heel is routinely left behind. Residual recovery adds minor time at discharge end. Draining technique is straightforward. Product left in the vessel is direct yield loss, the central concern.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for required pre-hold results or approvals after the scheduled start
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for pre-hold results/approvals after scheduled start is a process-timing delay, marginal to the hold job (low relevance); mostly a schedule impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Approval workflows are established but result and sign-off delays routinely push start beyond schedule. Waiting for pre-hold results or approvals is a direct start-delay time loss. Little operator skill affects the wait; it depends on lab and QA turnaround. Idle equipment and staff during waits carry opportunity cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt about aseptic decision authority when directions conflict
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about aseptic decision authority when directions conflict is a governance ambiguity, peripheral to the hold job (low relevance); resolvable through escalation (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting holding deadlines governing the same vessel batch
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting holding deadlines for the same batch is a governance ambiguity, tangential to actual containment (low relevance); resolvable through clarification (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting holding deadlines occasionally arise from overlapping validated limits and manual tracking. Reconciling deadlines adds minor scheduling time. Managing multiple hold limits needs planning skill. Scheduling systems add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of remaining holding margin consumed by unplanned vessel retention
rows.imp_rat: Holding margin consumed by unplanned retention is a schedule-efficiency delay, peripheral to the containment CFJ (low relevance); absorbable timing consumption (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Retention overruns still occur from unplanned events, eroding margin. Unplanned retention extends holding time and defers throughput. Reducing unplanned retention is a scheduling discipline issue, not a skill gap. Consumed margin from unplanned retention wastes validated hold capacity and risks batch value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of related contamination signals not connected to the held-solution deviation
rows.imp_rat: Contamination signals not connected to the held-solution deviation is an investigation-linkage defect, tangential to physically maintaining containment (low relevance); a detection-completeness gap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Related signals are often not connected to a deviation because systems are siloed. Connecting related signals takes investigative time. Recognizing related contamination signals requires broad situational awareness. Trending tools add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in the acceptance criteria applied to the held solution's evidence
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in acceptance criteria for held-solution evidence is an interpretive documentation matter, distant from actual containment (low relevance); resolvable through clarification (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Sterility acceptance criteria are relatively well defined, leaving limited but real ambiguity. Ambiguity forces clarifying cycles that cost time. Interpreting ambiguous acceptance criteria falls to expert judgement. Clarification meetings consume resource.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from completion of required monitoring to evidence availability for sterility assessment
rows.imp_rat: Time from monitoring completion to evidence availability is a data-latency delay, tangential to the containment CFJ (low relevance); an absorbable timing gap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Automated data flow makes monitoring results promptly available, though manual entry can add lag. Once monitoring completes, electronic systems make results available for assessment quickly. Data transfer is automated, needing little skill. LIMS/historian integration is an established cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical holding records with unverifiable provenance or completeness
rows.imp_rat: Holding records with unverifiable provenance is a documentation-integrity defect, peripheral to actually containing the solution (low relevance); a traceability gap rather than product harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails and controlled records make unverifiable provenance of critical records fairly uncommon where electronic. Verifying provenance adds review time. Provenance checks are proceduralized needing modest skill. Data-integrity controls add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency among approved disposition criteria applied by reviewing parties
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency among reviewers' disposition criteria is a governance harmonization issue, distant from the containment CFJ (low relevance); consequences are reconcilable procedural variation (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Written acceptance criteria reduce but do not eliminate inconsistency across reviewers. Aligning reviewers' criteria can add discussion time. Consistent application of criteria depends on reviewer training and calibration. Governance and training add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of fill-finish interruption while sterile bulk remains in the vessel
rows.imp_rat: Fill-finish interruption while bulk remains in the vessel is a downstream schedule delay, peripheral to the hold CFJ (low relevance); absorbable timing impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Fill-finish interruptions occur intermittently from line issues, affecting hold outcomes. Fill-finish interruptions while bulk waits directly prolong idle vessel time. Reducing interruptions is an operations coordination matter, not skill. Interruptions waste line capacity and hold margin.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of validated holding margin consumed by quality disposition delay
rows.imp_rat: Validated holding margin consumed by disposition delay is a schedule-efficiency issue, peripheral to the hold CFJ (low relevance); absorbable timing consumption (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Disposition delays routinely eat into margin because of slow sterility confirmation. Disposition delay, driven by test turnaround, consumes hold margin. Speeding disposition is limited by test timelines, not staff skill. Margin consumed by QA delay is lost validated hold value and risks batch loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of validated holding margin consumed by upstream staging before vessel receipt
rows.imp_rat: Holding margin consumed by upstream staging before receipt is a schedule-efficiency waste, peripheral to containment (low relevance); absorbable timing consumption (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Staging overruns intermittently reduce margin depending on upstream timing. Upstream staging before receipt shortens available hold window. Managing staging is a coordination task, not skill-limited. Margin consumed by upstream staging is wasted validated capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of disagreement among quality, manufacturing, and technical parties over deviation impact
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among parties over deviation impact is a coordination delay, tangential to the containment CFJ (low relevance); resolvable through review (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Escalation forums help but disagreements among QA, manufacturing and technical parties persist and remain time-consuming. Cross-functional disagreement over deviation impact can drag on and directly delay disposition. Resolution depends on senior alignment and negotiation skill across functions. Prolonged disagreement consumes expert time and delays valuable throughput.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency among KPI records used for the same held bulk
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency among KPI records for the same bulk is a data-reconciliation ambiguity, tangential to containment (low relevance); correctable record discrepancy (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Data systems reduce inconsistency but multiple record sources still produce recurring mismatches for the same batch. Reconciling inconsistent KPI records adds time before a defensible disposition. Spotting inconsistencies across records requires careful reviewer attention. Reconciliation is labor cost, generally modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in disposition criteria for borderline KPI results
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in disposition criteria for borderline KPI results is an interpretive edge-case matter, tangential to containment (low relevance); addressable by criteria refinement (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Criteria cover clear cases well but borderline KPI ambiguity is a recurring, poorly resolved issue. Ambiguity in borderline KPI criteria stalls decisions while interpretation is sought. Borderline calls fall back on expert judgement because criteria are underspecified. Ambiguous criteria risk inconsistent, costly decisions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of rejecting conforming held bulk because disposition evidence is misinterpreted
rows.imp_rat: Rejecting conforming bulk due to misinterpreted evidence is a waste/false-reject issue, peripheral to the containment CFJ (low relevance); economic loss without safety harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Review and second-checks reduce but do not eliminate misinterpretation leading to false rejection. Re-evaluating misinterpreted evidence to avoid wrong rejection adds review cycles. Correct interpretation of disposition evidence depends on experienced reviewer judgement. Rejecting conforming bulk needlessly destroys valuable product, a direct loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from completion of required quality evaluations to the bulk disposition decision
rows.imp_rat: Time from quality evaluations to disposition decision is a process-latency delay, peripheral to the containment CFJ (low relevance); an absorbable schedule impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Defined disposition workflows shorten this interval, though queueing and approvals still add recurring delay. The gap from completed evaluations to the disposition decision reflects review queueing that varies. Prompt decisions depend on availability of authorized decision-makers. Decision delay idles downstream capacity, a schedule cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the bulk solution reaches the sterile bulk hold vessel after its scheduled arrival
rows.imp_rat: Bulk reaching the vessel after scheduled arrival is a timing/delay issue, peripheral to the containment CFJ (low relevance); an absorbable schedule impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Production scheduling makes on-time arrival usual, but upstream slippage still causes recurring late arrivals. Late bulk arrival directly delays the hold and downstream fill-finish schedule. Timely arrival depends on upstream coordination rather than overseer skill. Late arrival can compress hold windows and idle capacity, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time that the sterile bulk hold vessel remains unavailable beyond the scheduled bulk arrival
rows.imp_rat: Vessel remaining unavailable beyond scheduled arrival is a readiness/timing delay, tangential to actual containment (low relevance); a schedule impact only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Vessel scheduling and turnaround planning usually ensure availability, with occasional recurring conflicts. Vessel unavailability beyond scheduled arrival forces waiting that delays the hold. Vessel turnaround/cleaning is a routine, moderately skilled operation. Idle bulk waiting for a vessel risks schedule and material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between the approved bulk-hold schedule and fill-finish handoff schedule
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between bulk-hold and fill-finish handoff schedules is a scheduling-coordination ambiguity, peripheral to containment (low relevance); an addressable timing misalignment (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Scheduling tools help but hold-vs-handoff misalignment recurs given cross-unit dependencies. Mismatch between hold and handoff schedules directly creates waiting or rushing at the interface. Aligning schedules requires planning coordination skill across units. Schedule mismatch idles capacity or risks hold-limit breach, a cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the fill-finish receiving interface is unavailable at the scheduled handoff
rows.imp_rat: Fill-finish receiving interface unavailable at handoff is a downstream-readiness delay, tangential to containment (low relevance); a schedule/coordination impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Handoff coordination is generally reliable, though occasional interface unavailability still disrupts the scheduled transfer. An unavailable receiving interface at handoff delays transfer and can consume hold time. Interface readiness is a coordination task rather than a deep-skill dependency. Handoff delay idles both units, a scheduling cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of criterion revision caused by late discovery of sterile bulk process constraints
rows.imp_rat: Criterion revision from late discovery of process constraints is a rework/planning issue, tangential to the hold CFJ (low relevance); leads to rework not product harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Stage-gate reviews reduce late surprises but late constraint discovery still recurs. Late discovery of process constraints forces criterion rework, and the primary loss is the schedule churn this causes. Early constraint capture depends on experienced process input that is not always available up front. Rework consumes engineering resources but the felt loss is schedule slip.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical specification claims lacking defensible technical evidence
rows.imp_rat: Specification claims lacking defensible evidence is a documentation-support ambiguity, tangential to containment (low relevance); a defensibility gap only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Evidence review is standard but critical claims still sometimes rest on thin data. Securing defensible evidence for each critical claim extends timelines. Assessing evidentiary defensibility requires seasoned technical reviewers. Weakly supported claims trigger costly additional studies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable quality and regulatory expectations omitted from the recommendation rationale
rows.imp_rat: Regulatory expectations omitted from the recommendation rationale is a documentation-completeness defect, distant from the containment CFJ (low relevance); an addressable narrative gap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory rationale is standard in submissions, and coverage is generally solid though occasional gaps appear. Compiling the full set of applicable expectations into the rationale takes effort but delay is secondary. Comprehensive regulatory mapping depends on QA/RA specialists whose availability limits completeness. Omitted expectations trigger audit findings with costly remediation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of specification requirements lacking traceability to supporting evidence
rows.imp_rat: Specification requirements lacking evidence traceability is a documentation-support ambiguity, distant from the hold CFJ (low relevance); a defensibility gap only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Traceability matrices are common practice yet completeness varies, so untraced requirements recur. Tracing every spec requirement to evidence adds documentation time. Maintaining traceability matrices demands disciplined validation practice. Untraceable requirements cause rework and audit cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved contradictions among evidence sources supporting the recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved contradictions among evidence sources for the recommendation is an evidence-reconciliation ambiguity, distant from containment (low relevance); addressable in review (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Review processes catch many contradictions but some persist into the recommendation. Resolving evidence contradictions adds investigation time. Adjudicating conflicting sources requires senior technical judgement. Unresolved contradictions cause rework and delayed decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of supporting evidence with unverified origin, currency, or integrity
rows.imp_rat: Supporting evidence with unverified origin/currency/integrity is a documentation-provenance defect, distant from the hold CFJ (low relevance); a defensibility gap only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Data-integrity practices (ALCOA+) are established, so most evidence provenance is verifiable, though gaps remain. Verifying provenance and currency of evidence takes administrative time. Assessing data integrity requires data-governance literacy. Unverified evidence forces re-sourcing at cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in applying evaluation criteria across sterile bulk containment options
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency applying evaluation criteria across containment options is a governance-consistency issue, tangential to the hold job (low relevance); a review-process variation (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Structured comparison methods exist but consistency across options still varies by evaluator. Inconsistent application forces re-scoring loops that waste time secondarily. Uniform application across options depends on disciplined, experienced evaluators, the core skill gap here. Inconsistency leads to re-work and disputed decisions with modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material safety, quality, capacity, and lifecycle tradeoffs unexplained in the recommendation rationale
rows.imp_rat: Tradeoffs unexplained in the recommendation rationale is a documentation-completeness ambiguity, peripheral to containment (low relevance); a narrative gap only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Tradeoff documentation exists but depth is inconsistent, leaving decision rationale partially unsupported. Explaining tradeoffs thoroughly lengthens rationale writing but time is not the core loss. Articulating safety, quality, capacity and lifecycle tradeoffs coherently demands broad multidisciplinary judgement that is scarce. Unexplained tradeoffs lead to poor selections carrying lifecycle cost penalties.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of product-loss controls omitted from the containment recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Product-loss controls omitted from the recommendation is a completeness gap tied to yield, peripheral to aseptic containment (low relevance); economic-loss oriented (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Loss-control practices exist but are inconsistently embedded in containment recommendations. Adding product-loss controls to the recommendation adds design effort. Identifying appropriate loss controls needs process-design expertise. Omitted loss controls directly waste expensive bulk product, a clear cost impact.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required batch volumes lacking representative fit evidence
rows.imp_rat: Batch volumes lacking representative fit evidence is a documentation-support ambiguity, peripheral to containment (low relevance); a validation-evidence gap only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Scale/fit assessment is routine but representative evidence across all required volumes is often incomplete. Generating representative fit evidence across batch volumes takes study time. Designing volume-representative studies needs process-engineering competence. Lacking fit evidence risks costly rescoping of vessel sizing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of aseptic compatibility evidence not traceable to the evaluated configuration and hold conditions
rows.imp_rat: Compatibility evidence not traceable to the evaluated configuration is a documentation-traceability ambiguity, distant from actual holding (low relevance); a defensibility gap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Traceability discipline is improving but evidence-to-configuration mismatch remains a common weakness. Establishing traceability of compatibility evidence to the exact configuration is time-consuming. Judging whether evidence truly matches the evaluated config/hold conditions requires validation expertise. Non-traceable evidence forces repeat studies, wasting resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts between evaluation criteria and applicable quality and regulatory expectations
rows.imp_rat: Conflicts between evaluation criteria and regulatory expectations is a compliance-alignment documentation issue, distant from the hold job (low relevance); reconcilable before finalization (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory review is a routine gate, so major conflicts are usually caught, though subtle ones persist. Cross-checking criteria against the regulatory landscape adds review time but is not a delay-dominated need. Reconciling criteria with evolving GMP/Annex requires specialized regulatory knowledge in short supply. Conflicts caught late in inspection or audit are costly to remediate.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of evaluation criteria permitting inconsistent evaluator interpretation
rows.imp_rat: Criteria permitting inconsistent evaluator interpretation is a wording ambiguity, distant from the containment CFJ (low relevance); addressable by clearer criteria (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Rubric-based scoring exists but wording rigor varies, so inconsistent interpretation remains a recurring gap. Ambiguous criteria cause rework loops that cost time, but the root gain is reducing interpretation dependence. When criteria permit varied evaluator readings, correct scoring hinges on individual judgement, exactly the skill dependence this need targets. Inconsistent interpretation causes re-evaluation effort, a modest resource cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of evaluation criteria lacking defined evidence for claim verification
rows.imp_rat: Evaluation criteria lacking defined verification evidence is a methodological documentation gap, peripheral to the containment CFJ (low relevance); correctable review artifact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Requirements-traceability practices exist but are unevenly applied to evaluation criteria, leaving verification gaps. Defining verification evidence per criterion upfront slows criteria finalization somewhat. Knowing what constitutes defensible evidence for each claim requires validation and regulatory literacy that is not universal. Undefined evidence needs cause redundant testing later, wasting resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved criterion conflicts among quality, manufacturing, engineering, and validation stakeholders
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved criterion conflicts among stakeholders is a governance-alignment ambiguity, distant from the hold job (low relevance); resolvable through coordination (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Governance forums exist but genuine criterion alignment is often incomplete at decision time. Reconciling stakeholder criterion conflicts drags out decision timelines. Facilitating alignment among quality, manufacturing, engineering and validation requires strong cross-functional leadership, the scarce capability here. Unresolved conflicts cause rework and suboptimal decisions with indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved contradictions across supplier, facility, and quality information about vessel containment
rows.imp_rat: Contradictions among supplier/facility/quality info about containment is an evidence-reconciliation ambiguity, peripheral to the physical hold (low relevance); addressable in review (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Unresolved contradictions across information sources are a common friction point. Resolving contradictions across sources takes coordination time. Reconciling supplier/facility/quality data needs cross-functional expertise. Coordination is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of containment conclusions lacking traceable links to assessment criteria, assumptions, and supporting evidence
rows.imp_rat: Containment conclusions lacking traceable links to evidence is a documentation-traceability defect, peripheral to the physical hold (low relevance); a defensibility gap only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Conclusions sometimes lack full traceable links to underlying assumptions and evidence. Building traceability adds documentation time. Linking conclusions to criteria and evidence needs rigorous documentation discipline. Traceability effort is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting acceptance-criterion definitions across qualification documents
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting acceptance-criterion definitions across qualification documents is a documentation-consistency ambiguity, distant from the hold CFJ (low relevance); reconcilable editorial matter (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting criterion definitions across documents still occur despite review. Reconciling definitions across documents adds review time. Harmonizing criterion definitions needs careful editorial control. Reconciliation is low material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required protocol evidence lacking traceability to challenge conditions and acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Protocol evidence lacking traceability to challenge conditions is a documentation-traceability defect, distant from the hold CFJ (low relevance); a defensibility gap only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Protocol evidence sometimes lacks traceability to conditions and criteria. Cross-referencing evidence is documentation effort. Mapping evidence to challenge conditions and criteria needs meticulous discipline. Documentation labor is the main cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of foreseeable deviations and invalid-run conditions lacking predefined handling in the protocol
rows.imp_rat: Foreseeable deviations lacking predefined handling in the protocol is a study-design rework gap, tangential to containment (low relevance); leads to rework not product harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Foreseeable deviations often lack predefined handling, causing ad hoc decisions. Predefining deviation handling is upfront drafting, saving time later. Anticipating deviations and invalid-run conditions requires experienced foresight. Predefinition is low-cost preparation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification evidence lacking complete source-data traceability
rows.imp_rat: Qualification evidence lacking source-data traceability is a documentation-integrity defect, peripheral to actual containment (low relevance); affects data defensibility not the hold (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature data-integrity expectations mean source traceability is usually reasonably complete. Source-data traceability is a records task. Complete data lineage requires disciplined data-management practice. Data-integrity systems carry cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of protocol deviations lacking documented resolution before study disposition
rows.imp_rat: Protocol deviations lacking documented resolution before disposition is a study-closure rework gap, tangential to the hold CFJ (low relevance); leads to rework not product harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Formal deviation-management systems make undocumented resolutions relatively rare. Resolving deviations before disposition can delay closeout. Documented deviation resolution relies on QA competence that is well-established. Investigation and resolution consume labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required containment evidence lacking traceability to evaluated operating conditions
rows.imp_rat: Containment evidence lacking traceability to operating conditions is a documentation-traceability defect, distant from the hold CFJ (low relevance); affects defensibility not containment (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Some evidence lacks clear traceability to the conditions it represents. Evidence linkage is documentation work, not a bottleneck. Tying evidence to operating conditions requires careful record management. Traceability is a labor overhead.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of detected extractable compounds without defensible chemical identities
rows.imp_rat: Extractable compounds without defensible identities is an analytical-characterization ambiguity, peripheral to the hold job (low relevance); affects data defensibility not containment (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Many detected extractables remain unidentified, so defensible identity is often incomplete, leaving this poorly served. Resolving chemical identities of extractables is time-consuming and iterative. Assigning defensible structures to unknown peaks requires scarce expert spectroscopists. Advanced identification (high-res MS/NMR) is expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of background contaminants falsely attributed to vessel contact materials
rows.imp_rat: Background contaminants falsely attributed to contact materials is an attribution/analytical issue, tangential to the hold job (low relevance); affects investigation accuracy not containment (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: False attribution of background contaminants to the vessel persists despite standard blank practices, leaving a moderate gap. Ruling out background contaminants adds analytical iteration. Correct attribution requires skilled use of blanks and controls. Additional control runs add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of extractable compounds lost or transformed during specimen handling and preparation
rows.imp_rat: Loss/transformation of extractables during specimen handling is a lab-method fidelity defect, distant from containment (low relevance); impacts characterization data quality only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Extractable loss or transformation during specimen prep is a recognized, only partially mitigated error source. Minimizing prep losses requires careful, sometimes slower handling. Avoiding loss or transformation of extractables during prep demands skilled analysts. Careful prep adds modest labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of variation in measured extractables profiles across equivalent specimens and characterization runs
rows.imp_rat: Variation in extractables profiles across specimens is an analytical-reproducibility concern, peripheral to the hold job (low relevance); affects characterization confidence only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Extractables profile variability across runs is a recognized issue but managed acceptably by standardized protocols. Repeat characterization runs add time. Reducing extractables profile variability depends on analytical rigor typically available. Repeat characterization adds cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of reported extractables results lacking traceability to specimens, exposure conditions, or raw observations
rows.imp_rat: Extractables results lacking traceability to specimens/conditions is an analytical-documentation ambiguity, tangential to the hold job (low relevance); a data-defensibility gap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Data-integrity requirements and LIMS make extractables result traceability fairly reliable, so this is well served. Documenting result traceability is routine within reporting. Maintaining specimen-to-result traceability is a standard LIMS-supported skill. LIMS and documentation add ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of unresolved conflict among toxicological sources used to evaluate identified leachables
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflict among toxicological sources for leachables is an evidence-reconciliation ambiguity, peripheral to containment (low relevance); addressable in assessment (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Unresolved conflicts among tox sources persist because data are heterogeneous, leaving conclusions uncertain. Reconciling conflicting tox sources adds analysis time. Adjudicating among conflicting toxicological data requires senior toxicology judgement. Resolution effort is mostly labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of extraneous contamination misattributed to migration from vessel contact materials
rows.imp_rat: Extraneous contamination misattributed to material migration is an investigation-attribution issue, tangential to the containment CFJ (low relevance); affects root-cause accuracy not the hold (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Background contamination is frequently misattributed despite blanks and controls, a recurring nuisance only partly controlled. Distinguishing contamination from migration requires investigation time. Attributing signals correctly requires experienced analytical troubleshooting. Blank/control runs add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of leachable alteration during sample storage, handling, and preparation
rows.imp_rat: Leachable alteration during sample storage/handling is an analytical-integrity concern, peripheral to the physical hold CFJ (low relevance); affects measurement accuracy not the hold itself (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Leachable alteration during storage/prep is a known variability source only partly controlled by handling SOPs. Controlling storage-induced alteration requires timely sample handling. Preventing leachable alteration needs trained sample-handling discipline. Stabilized handling and cold storage add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of variation in leachable measurements across equivalent samples from the same hold condition
rows.imp_rat: Variation in leachable measurements across equivalent samples is an analytical-reproducibility issue, peripheral to the hold CFJ (low relevance); affects data confidence not containment (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Method validation controls leachable measurement variability reasonably well for validated methods, so this is moderately served. Replicate leachable testing adds analytical time. Controlling inter-sample variation requires solid method-validation skill that is fairly standard. Replicate testing increases cost per condition.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to resolve the identities of leachable signals needed for the safety assessment
rows.imp_rat: Time to resolve leachable signal identities is an analytical-workflow delay, tangential to the hold CFJ (low relevance); a schedule impact on assessment (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: The prolonged identification cycle is a chronic time pain with no fast standard solution, so this is poorly served. Resolving leachable signal identities is notoriously slow, often gating the safety assessment for weeks. Fast identity resolution depends on scarce expert spectroscopists and databases. Rapid identification via advanced instrumentation is costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of reported leachable results lacking traceable sample provenance or reviewable calculations
rows.imp_rat: Leachable results lacking traceable provenance is an analytical-documentation ambiguity, peripheral to containment (low relevance); affects data defensibility not the hold (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Leachable result provenance and reviewable calculations are reasonably well maintained under GMP data-integrity practice. Provenance documentation is embedded in reporting workflows. Capturing sample provenance and calculations is standard QC documentation skill. Documentation systems add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict among product, process, and material-compatibility evidence used for the recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Conflict among compatibility evidence for the recommendation is an evidence-reconciliation ambiguity, distant from actual containment (low relevance); addressable in review (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting evidence is common and reconciliation is inconsistent, making the recommendation's basis only partially reliable. Reconciling multi-domain evidence adds review time. Integrating conflicting product/process/material evidence into a recommendation is a high-expertise synthesis task. Mostly labor cost for cross-functional review.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of current product-contact components not traceable to material identities covered by suitability evidence
rows.imp_rat: Traceability of contact components to suitability evidence is a documentation-provenance ambiguity, peripheral to the physical hold job (low relevance); mostly a paperwork gap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Gaps in linking current components to suitability-covered identities occur, especially with complex supply chains, leaving a real gap. Tracing components to qualified material identities is a documentation task with modest time. Maintaining traceability needs disciplined but not rare quality-system skill. Traceability systems carry ongoing administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt that the scientific justification can withstand quality or regulatory scrutiny
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that the justification withstands scrutiny is a documentation-confidence ambiguity, peripheral to physically holding the bulk (low relevance); a defensibility concern only (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that allocated capital is insufficient for aseptic containment and bulk-holding requirements
rows.imp_rat: Insufficient allocated capital for containment requirements is a program-funding concern, distant from the operational hold CFJ (low relevance); an upstream planning matter (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Under-provisioned budgets recur predictably because early estimates rely on optimistic vendor figures rather than validated containment scope. Capital sizing estimates are prepared during the budgeting cycle but often lag the evolving aseptic-containment scope, causing late realization of shortfalls. Accurately scoping aseptic containment cost demands cross-functional engineering and quality expertise that the purchase executor rarely commands alone. Status quo capital estimates for aseptic vessels chronically omit containment complexity, so allocated capital frequently proves insufficient once true requirements surface.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required sterility and safety pre-fill checks omitted from the filling procedure
rows.imp_rat: Omitting pre-fill sterility/safety checks has real severity if a bad container is filled, but as a procedural-check error its relevance is moderate and it is capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists capture most required checks, but omissions from procedures remain a recurring compliance gap. Adding pre-fill checks to the procedure lengthens the documented sequence only slightly. Knowing which sterility and safety checks are required demands regulatory and process expertise. Omitted checks can lead to failed batches or safety incidents, very costly outcomes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of filling sequence steps with ambiguous order or completion criteria
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous filling sequence steps can cause operator errors, but this batch is ranked below prior items so relevance and severity are capped; the error is procedural and largely recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Standard procedures reduce ambiguity, but poorly specified order and completion criteria still cause inconsistent execution. Clarifying ambiguous steps is a one-time authoring effort that saves time later. Writing unambiguous order and completion criteria requires procedure-authoring skill that is often lacking. Ambiguous steps cause rework and errors that waste labor and material.

rows.source_job: Set fill-level indicator or measurement reference
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination onto sterile product-contact surfaces while setting the reference point
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating sterile product-contact surfaces during reference-point setting could compromise product (moderate severity), but the ranking caps its relevance to the core job here.
rows.sat_rat: Cleanroom controls, isolators and validated aseptic procedures give this a mature, largely repeatable outcome though occasional excursions still occur. Setting the reference point under aseptic conditions adds handling time, but established gowning and clean-line sequencing keep it bounded. Maintaining sterility during reference-point setting demands trained aseptic operators whose technique remains the main safeguard. Contamination events trigger costly batch loss and reprocessing, and the controls (HEPA, isolators) carry standing overhead.

rows.source_job: Set fill-level indicator or measurement reference
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of offset between the configured reference point and the validated container datum
rows.imp_rat: Offset between configured reference point and validated datum is a precision error with limited operational fallout, and the item is ranked below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Datum fixtures and calibration checks exist but offsets still slip through when references are ambiguous, leaving recurring gaps. Aligning the configured reference to the validated datum takes measurable setup time when tooling and fixtures must be checked. Correct offset control leans on the technician's measurement judgement and interpretation of the validated datum. An undetected offset scraps or reworks fills, so the residual error carries meaningful cost exposure.

rows.source_job: Set fill-level indicator or measurement reference
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of reference-position shift between calibration and filling
rows.imp_rat: Reference-position shift between calibration and filling is a measurement precision issue with limited consequences, and the item ranks below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Locking features and re-check steps mitigate shift but movement between stations still introduces occasional undetected drift. Re-verifying reference position between calibration and filling adds a check step that is usually quick when procedures are followed. Detecting drift depends on operator diligence and understanding of what can shift the datum during transit or setup. Position shift causes fill errors and rejected containers, making the failure moderately expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unplanned product-flow interruptions caused by pressure imbalance during filling or dispensing
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned flow interruptions from pressure imbalance cause delay but not lasting harm; the item ranks below prior batch so relevance and severity remain minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Vented caps and vacuum breakers are standard on IBCs and largely prevent pressure-driven flow stoppages when correctly used. Pressure-imbalance stalls interrupt filling or dispensing until vents are cleared or opened. Operators generally know to open vents, so the fix needs little specialized skill. Interruptions cost throughput time more than material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of product leakage from the joined fittings during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Leakage from joined fittings during transfer is a containment concern (moderate relevance) with a spill consequence that is usually contained quickly (low-moderate severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Quick-connect couplings and standardized fittings largely prevent transfer leaks, yet worn seals and mismatched connectors still cause occasional drips at the joint. Connecting and verifying fittings is fast for standard camlock or threaded couplings. Making a leak-free joint depends on the operator correctly seating and torquing the coupling. Leaks at joints waste product and demand cleanup, but fittings and seals are low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that contents remain outside the validated storage temperature range
rows.imp_rat: Contents drifting outside validated storage temperature can degrade product, giving real severity, and relevance to safe holding, but this batch ranks below prior items so it is held at the 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Jacketed or insulated IBCs with data loggers and alarms are established practice but gaps in monitoring coverage still let contents drift out of range. Temperature excursions may persist until noticed and corrected, and warm-up or re-cooling adds time. Maintaining validated temperature depends on facility HVAC and monitoring setup rather than operator skill. Excursions risk scrapping high-value contents and consume energy for jacket heating or cooling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of structural deformation of an IBC under stacked weight
rows.imp_rat: Structural deformation under stacked weight affects containment reliability, but its centrality and consequence are held to the 2.0 ceiling per the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: IBCs are engineered and stack-rated to defined loads, so deformation under compliant stacking is rare and largely predictable. Structural performance under stacking is a static property, so time is not relevant to this need. Stacking within rated limits requires only that handlers follow load ratings, a low-skill discipline. Robustly framed IBCs cost more but prevent expensive collapse and product loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of IBC closures or connection points damaged during stacking
rows.imp_rat: Closures or connection points damaged during stacking impair reuse, but the error is localized and repairable, keeping both proxies low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized IBC frames with recessed closures and stack-rating limits substantially protect connection points, so damage is fairly uncommon when limits are observed. Damaged closures found after stacking force rework and re-handling that add time. Proper stacking is routine handler practice requiring little specialized skill. Damaged valves and caps are replaced or scrapped, a real material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of container surfaces and connections obscured during integrity checks
rows.imp_rat: Obscured surfaces during integrity checks hampers inspection thoroughness (low relevance) and mildly raises the chance a defect is missed (low severity). Kept within the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Cage frames, wraps, insulation and stacked positions routinely block line of sight to seams and valves, so integrity checks remain partial and inconsistent. Visual and pressure-decay integrity checks add modest time, but obscured surfaces force repeat inspections that stretch the process. Spotting a concealed seam or fitting still relies heavily on an experienced inspector's judgement rather than a foolproof procedure. Missed integrity faults from obscured surfaces occasionally cause spills and rework, but the direct inspection cost itself is low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting container integrity assessments between operator checks
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting integrity assessments between operator checks introduce inconsistency, but the impact is process-level and recoverable, and the item sits low in the importance ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Visual integrity checks are subjective and lack standardized criteria, so different operators routinely reach conflicting conclusions. Reconciling conflicting integrity assessments between shifts delays release of the container. Integrity judgement varies with inspector experience, making consistent calls dependent on scarce skilled attention. Re-inspections and disputed holds consume labor but little material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of the transfer conducted outside the acceptable discharge range as IBC contents decline
rows.imp_rat: Transfer conducted outside the acceptable discharge range as contents decline is a flow-control variability issue (moderate relevance) with limited downstream impact (low severity). Constrained by the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Declining head pressure and vortexing near empty cause flow rate to drift outside the acceptable band, and manual throttling only partly controls it. Managing flow as the level drops can slow the tail of the transfer with throttling and monitoring. Keeping discharge within range as head pressure falls relies on operator adjustment and attentiveness. Out-of-range transfer wastes energy and risks off-spec dosing, but the incremental cost is limited.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of material-discharge surges during transfer to downstream equipment or receiving vessels
rows.imp_rat: Material-discharge surges during transfer are a handling nuisance with limited severity, and as a lower-ranked item both proxies are held at or below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Flow-control valves and metered pumps are common practice that dampens most surges, though sudden head or viscosity changes still cause occasional surges. Discharge surges may force operators to throttle flow and restart, adding transfer time. Controlling surge relies on operator feel for valve opening and pump ramp-up. Surges can overflow receiving vessels and waste product or damage equipment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unplanned material-discharge stoppages per IBC transfer
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned discharge stoppages disrupt transfer efficiency but are recoverable interruptions (moderate relevance, low severity) rather than catastrophic to the CFJ. Bounded by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Bottom-discharge valves and pumps are mature, but clogging, air locks, and residual settling still cause recurring mid-transfer stoppages. Each unplanned stoppage per transfer interrupts flow and requires restart, adding time. Restarting flow after a stoppage is routine operator work needing little skill. Stoppages cost throughput time more than material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of contents leakage through fill ports, vents, or discharge points while the container is in service
rows.imp_rat: Leakage through ports, vents, or discharge points is genuinely central to containment, but the ranking places it below the prior batch, so relevance is held low and severity moderate to keep the mean at the 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized valves, caps and vent designs make in-service leakage uncommon, but gasket wear and improperly seated caps still produce recurring seepage. Leak checks are routine and quick, adding little to the in-service handling time. Detecting weeping vents or ports needs some vigilance but no specialist skill. Leakage through ports or vents wastes product and creates cleanup and containment costs, though gaskets are cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of fill ports, vents, or discharge points left unsecured when the container enters service
rows.imp_rat: Unsecured fill ports, vents, or discharge points at service entry touches containment integrity, but ranked below the prior batch it is capped; severity held low as it is typically caught pre-dispatch.
