rows.sat_rat: Supplier qualification programs and audits provide mature practice, though gaps in evidence coverage still occur at authorization. Verifying supplier aseptic and regulatory evidence adds review time at authorization. Assessing aseptic quality and regulatory evidence requires quality-audit expertise the executor relies on QA for. Unverified supplier evidence risks costly rework or rejection later, but verification itself is modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the vessel cannot be accepted for commercial production because aseptic containment requirements are unmet
rows.imp_rat: Vessel non-acceptance for commercial production is central-sounding but ranked below batch 3; the ceiling caps it, so relevance and severity stay low here.
rows.sat_rat: Established URS and qualification protocols make outright commercial rejection uncommon when standard aseptic specs are enforced, though residual risk remains. Acceptance failures surface late in commissioning, adding long unplanned delays before commercial release. Judging whether a vessel will pass aseptic acceptance requires deep sterility-assurance expertise the executor must borrow from quality units. A rejected vessel represents a near-total loss of the committed capital plus rework spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of contamination, batch-rejection, and production-interruption exposure omitted from lifecycle costs
rows.imp_rat: Contamination/batch-rejection exposure omitted from lifecycle costs is a costing-completeness error, tangential to containment with financial consequence.
rows.sat_rat: These low-probability, high-impact costs are consistently and predictably excluded from status-quo models. Contamination and interruption exposures are quantified late, if ever, delaying complete lifecycle costing. Quantifying contamination and batch-rejection exposure requires risk and sterility expertise seldom applied in cost models. Contamination, batch-rejection and production-interruption exposures are frequently omitted from lifecycle costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the capital priority understates contamination and sterility-loss exposure during bulk holding
rows.imp_rat: Capital priority understating contamination exposure is a prioritization error, tangential to containment execution with financial-risk consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Capital priority routinely understates contamination exposure because it is difficult to quantify credibly. Reassessing priority against sterility exposure adds time when data is missing. Weighing contamination exposure into capital priority needs sterility-risk judgement beyond typical procurement skill. Understated sterility-loss exposure makes the case look cheaper than reality, misdirecting priority.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which the documented filling flow exceeds the limits specified for the contents or stainless steel IBC
rows.imp_rat: Documented fill flow exceeding limits is a specification error (low relevance to the container itself) with contained consequences, sitting near the bottom of importance.
rows.sat_rat: Flow limits are usually specified but the documentation does not always reflect the actual content or vessel constraints. Verifying documented flow limits is a quick review against specifications. Setting flow within content and IBC limits needs engineering knowledge of the materials involved. Excessive flow can damage the IBC or cause spills, incurring repair and loss costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the documented filling sequence conflicts with content or stainless steel IBC requirements
rows.imp_rat: A documented filling sequence conflicting with content/IBC requirements is a procedural-documentation error of low relevance to the physical job, with recoverable consequences, ranking very low.
rows.sat_rat: Documented procedures exist, but conflicts with specific content or stainless requirements are caught inconsistently. Checking the filling sequence against requirements is a documentation-review step of modest duration. Recognizing conflicts between sequence and content/IBC constraints requires process and product expertise. A conflicting sequence discovered late causes rework and possible batch loss.

rows.source_job: Set fill-level indicator or measurement reference
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of difference between the container orientation during reference setup and filling
rows.imp_rat: The orientation difference between reference setup and filling is a peripheral calibration nuance for IBCs; relevance is low because the container job largely succeeds regardless, and severity is minimal since consequences are easily corrected.
rows.sat_rat: Keyed frames and orientation marks make this fairly repeatable, though manual placement still allows occasional mismatches. Matching orientation between setup and filling is typically enforced by fixtures with little added time. Consistent orientation relies on the operator recognizing and reproducing the correct container attitude. Orientation mismatch spoils fills and wastes product, giving it a real but contained cost impact.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that the container fill level remains indeterminable during loading or dispensing
rows.imp_rat: An indeterminable fill level briefly is an unreliability nuisance of limited relevance to core containment, with low, recoverable severity.
rows.sat_rat: Load cells and level sensors exist but many IBCs in use still lack reliable inline indication, leaving level genuinely uncertain during operations. Indeterminable fill level forces operators to pause and verify by sight glass, weighing, or dipping, slowing loading and dispensing. Estimating level in opaque or translucent IBCs relies on operator experience and manual checks. Guesswork risks overfill spillage or short fills, wasting material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of harmless surface conditions misidentified as leakage during use
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying harmless surface conditions as leakage causes only nuisance false alarms (low relevance, low severity), not failure of the containment job. Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without clear diagnostic criteria, harmless surface conditions are frequently misjudged as leaks, so this remains poorly controlled. Misidentified surface conditions trigger unnecessary investigations and holds that waste time. Distinguishing condensation, spillage residue, or sweating from genuine leakage takes experienced judgement that many operators lack. False leak calls mainly cost inspection labor and needless quarantine.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of discharge interruption caused by blockage
rows.imp_rat: Discharge interruption from blockage causes a timing delay with limited consequence, peripheral to the core containment job.
rows.sat_rat: Cohesive and bridging materials block IBC outlets unpredictably, and manual clearing means the interruption duration varies widely. Clearing a discharge blockage can idle the operation for an extended and unpredictable period while operators intervene. Diagnosing and clearing a bridge or rathole demands experienced operator judgement and manual intervention. Flow aids like vibrators or aeration cones that shorten blockage downtime are an added expense.

rows.source_job: Position container at point of use
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of misalignment between the container inlet or outlet and the required process connection
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment between inlet/outlet and process connection is a peripheral fit issue with limited consequence, kept low by both proxies and the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized fitting geometries and docking aids make correct connection common, yet manual positioning still yields periodic misalignment. Aligning inlets and outlets to process connections is a brief positioning task. Achieving alignment often depends on the operator manually jockeying the IBC into position. Alignment guides and docking stations reduce misalignment but cost extra to install.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of tank options missing critical sterile-service specifications
rows.imp_rat: Tank options missing sterile-service specs create selection ambiguity, yet this is a pre-purchase information gap with modest downstream stakes; capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Bid-tabulation practice normalizes options but sterile-spec completeness across options is inconsistently verified, leaving recurring gaps. Confirming that every option carries its sterile-service spec adds comparison effort during bid evaluation. Detecting which options lack critical sterile specs requires detailed technical comparison skill across vendor quotes. Selecting an under-specified option leads to change costs, though usually smaller than full rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the processing tank being damaged during transport or rigging
rows.imp_rat: Tank damage during transport or rigging can be costly, yet within this ordering it is ranked below batch 4 and the mandatory ceiling limits both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Mature logistics practice with bracing, insurance, and qualified riggers keeps transport/rigging damage relatively uncommon. Transport and rigging planning is routine and rarely the pacing item in acquisition. Specialized riggers and heavy-haul carriers bring mature expertise, reducing dependence on the buyer's own skill. Transit damage is expensive when it occurs, but insurance and specialized packaging cap the exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between fabrication schedule slippage and its visibility to the project manager
rows.imp_rat: Delay in surfacing fabrication schedule slippage affects project visibility but is upstream and recoverable; ranked low and held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Progress reporting is inconsistent and often optimistic, so surprises at delivery remain common despite periodic status calls. Schedule slippage often becomes visible to the PM only at the next milestone review, so the reporting lag is chronically long. Extracting real fabrication status demands a diligent expediter who knows how to read a shop's true progress versus its claims. Late visibility forces expedited shipping and downstream schedule crashing, adding avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of submitting the tank warranty registration after the eligibility deadline
rows.imp_rat: Submitting warranty registration after the eligibility deadline forfeits coverage, a modest financial consequence far from core function; capped by the batch-5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard project closeout checklists and vendor-driven reminders make missed registration deadlines uncommon. Warranty registration is a short, well-bounded task usually completed near delivery without meaningful delay. Registration is clerical and requires almost no specialized skill. Missing the deadline forfeits warranty value, but the mitigation cost is negligible when tracked.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of process-media constituents and cleaning residues omitted from the compatibility assessment
rows.imp_rat: Omitting media constituents or cleaning residues from a compatibility assessment could allow corrosion, but this item sits low in the ordering and the ceiling holds relevance and severity minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility assessments routinely miss trace constituents and cleaning agents, making this a recurring and poorly controlled failure mode. Compiling a full media-and-cleaning-residue compatibility assessment is slow when data must be gathered from multiple process owners. Complete compatibility assessment requires materials and process-chemistry expertise that is scarce and easily overlooks minor constituents. Omitted constituents cause corrosion or contamination failures whose remediation cost is very high.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sterile-process requirements missing from the quotation request
rows.imp_rat: Missing sterile-process requirements from a quotation request affects procurement accuracy but is upstream and correctable; ranked below prior batch and capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: RFQ templates help but are frequently incomplete for sterile specifics, so critical requirements are commonly left out of the initial request. Building a complete RFQ takes time, and omissions found later force re-quoting rounds that add delay. Specifying all critical sterile-process requirements up front requires experienced specifiers who understand hygienic design end to end. Requirements missing from the RFQ resurface as scope changes with premium pricing after award.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between recorded and actual installation-space and access-route dimensions
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between recorded and actual installation-space dimensions can cause install rework, but it is a data-accuracy issue ranked below the prior batch and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Site surveys and 3D scans are increasingly standard and catch most issues, but reliance on stale drawings still produces mismatches. Field-verifying install-space and access-route dimensions takes coordinated site visits that can extend planning. Accurate as-built measurement and rigging-path assessment needs surveying and rigging know-how not always on the buying team. A dimensional mismatch discovered at delivery causes very costly rework, storage, or return of an oversized tank.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of as-built tank details differing from the installed configuration
rows.imp_rat: As-built details differing from installed configuration is a documentation-accuracy defect of low relevance to active processing and modest severity; kept well under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: As-built accuracy is a well-known chronic weakness where field changes routinely go unrecorded, leaving documentation that diverges from the installed configuration. Updating as-built documentation is often deferred to the end and squeezed by schedule pressure. Redlining and drawing updates require draftsmen and engineers whose time is often reprioritized to commissioning tasks. As-built updating is low direct cost but chronically underfunded relative to its downstream value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required supplier qualification evidence missing from the submission
rows.imp_rat: Missing supplier qualification evidence is procurement paperwork, peripheral to the tank actually storing contents; low relevance and modest severity keep it near the bottom of the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized supplier qualification questionnaires and audit checklists make missing evidence largely trackable, though gaps still recur. Supplier qualification packages take time to compile and follow up on. Assessing qualification evidence completeness requires trained supplier-quality assessors. Requalification and audit rework from missing evidence add avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of operators unable to demonstrate required tank procedures before release
rows.imp_rat: Operators unable to demonstrate procedures before release is a training/readiness gap, indirect to the tank's storage function; low relevance and modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Competency sign-off procedures exist, but demonstration is often reduced to paperwork, so genuine ability to perform procedures is inconsistently verified. Operator training and demonstration before release take scheduled time that is often compressed near start-up. This need is fundamentally about building operator competency, and quality of training and assessment varies widely by site. Trainer time and delayed release carry cost, but the training materials themselves are cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required tank installation or test data missing from the baseline file
rows.imp_rat: Missing installation or test data from a baseline file is a records completeness issue, marginal to daily storage function; low relevance with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Handover data packages are notoriously incomplete despite MC/turnover procedures, so missing install and test records are a chronic, recurring gap. Compiling the baseline/turnover file is a slow, document-heavy effort often squeezed at project end. Ensuring completeness requires document-control diligence rather than technical skill, and is easily under-resourced. Missing baseline data later forces costly re-tests or re-surveys to reconstruct records.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required tank usage entries omitted during operation
rows.imp_rat: Omitted usage entries during operation is a logging shortfall with minimal impact on the physical storage job; both proxies remain low.
rows.sat_rat: Where logging remains manual, entries are routinely skipped or delayed under workload, and only partial electronic-logging adoption keeps omission rates high. Manual logging of usage entries adds small but repetitive time burden across a shift. Completing entries needs little skill, only discipline and remembering to do it. Omitted entries cost little directly but create downstream reconciliation and compliance rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required final-condition or disposition details omitted from the retirement record
rows.imp_rat: Omitted final-condition details in a retirement record is a documentation gap with limited functional bearing on storage; both proxies remain low under the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Retirement records are commonly assembled ad hoc without enforced templates, so required final-condition and disposition fields are frequently omitted. Compiling final-condition details is a back-end task often rushed as crews demobilize. Knowing which disposition details matter requires familiarity with regulatory record requirements that field crews frequently lack. Incomplete records carry little immediate cost but create expensive liability and re-investigation later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required retired-tank records missing from the archive
rows.imp_rat: Missing retired-tank records from the archive is an archival gap with little effect on active storage; both proxies stay low under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Document control for retired assets is inconsistent, and required records frequently go missing across handoffs between contractors, owners and regulators. Filing records to the archive is quick but routinely deferred until after project close-out. Archiving demands little specialized skill, mostly discipline in knowing what must be retained. Missing archived records cost nothing upfront but drive costly reconstruction during audits or future site transactions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residue from label replacement contaminating the container exterior or controlled area
rows.imp_rat: Label-replacement residue on the container exterior is largely cosmetic/administrative (low relevance) with modest contamination consequences (low severity), sitting at the bottom of importance.
rows.sat_rat: Standard solvent-wipe practice removes most residue, though adhesive smears occasionally persist and can contaminate the exterior or controlled area. Label stripping and residue cleaning is a quick manual step within maintenance flow. Removing adhesive residue cleanly takes care but little specialised skill. Solvents and wipes for label residue are inexpensive and readily stocked.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of product-contact surfaces becoming contaminated during leak testing
rows.imp_rat: Product-contact surface contamination during leak testing is a narrow procedural risk; relevance to core job is low, and severity is capped by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Using clean, dry media and dedicated test connections is standard, so contamination during leak testing is fairly uncommon though not eliminated. Leak testing is a brief step that adds minor time. Using clean test media and fittings requires modest procedural knowledge. Clean test gas or water and dedicated fittings are inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of tearing or puncturing the replacement inner liner during installation
rows.imp_rat: Tearing a replacement inner liner during install is a maintenance defect (limited relevance to the core job) with easily-caught, low-severity consequences, keeping it at the low end.
rows.sat_rat: Tears and punctures during installation are a common recurring defect that current manual practice only partly prevents. Liner installation is fairly quick but a torn liner forces a full redo. Fitting a replacement liner without puncture is a manual-dexterity task where technician care directly determines success. Liners are relatively cheap, so a torn one is modest material waste plus rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of service-fitting surfaces obscured by residue or deposits during inspection
rows.imp_rat: Service-fitting surfaces obscured by residue during inspection is a narrow inspection-visibility nuance; relevance is low and severity trivial since surfaces can be recleaned and reinspected.
rows.sat_rat: Standard degreasing and CIP remove most residue, but tight geometries and dried deposits still obscure some fitting surfaces during inspection. Pre-inspection cleaning of fittings is routine and adds only modest time. Judging when a surface is clean enough to inspect requires operator experience but is well within trained ability. Cleaning solvents, brushes and CIP cycles are inexpensive and standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of product residue remaining in vent passages after cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Residue in vent passages after cleaning is a narrow cleaning-completeness detail (low relevance) with minor carryover consequence (low severity). Held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Vent passages are hard-to-reach dead spaces where residue routinely persists and is seldom verified clean, making this a recurring poorly-served gap. Extra vent-passage flushing and drying add time to an already lengthy cleaning cycle. Cleaning narrow vent passages effectively requires knowledgeable technique and the right tooling that many shops lack. Specialized vent-cleaning nozzles are cheap, but trapped residue causes cross-contamination costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that restored identification markings become illegible during routine handling and cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Illegible restored identification markings hamper traceability but are cosmetic to the containment job; ranked low, so both proxies are near the floor.
rows.sat_rat: Marking durability varies widely; adhesive labels and inks still degrade under repeated caustic washing and abrasion, so illegibility recurs. Applying durable labels or engraving is fast but re-marking after each reconditioning cycle adds steps. Producing durable, correctly-placed markings needs modest operator care rather than scarce expertise. Chemical-resistant labels, laser marking and RFID tags cost more than basic labels but are affordable.

rows.source_job: Drain and dry container before storage
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual liquid spilling into the surrounding fill-finish area during drainage
rows.imp_rat: Residual liquid spilling during drainage is a localized cleanup issue with limited consequence and modest centrality to the containment job.
rows.sat_rat: Established drainage setups contain most liquid, yet splash and overflow spills into the surrounding area still happen with viscous or foaming residues. Controlled drainage with catch trays and pumps adds handling time but is a routine step. Careful draining to avoid spills needs attentive operators but is not a scarce specialist skill. Drip trays, absorbents and drainage rigs are low-cost consumables already in place.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in acceptance criteria for IBC capacity, construction, dimensions, closures, pallet base, stacking rating, and discharge features
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in acceptance criteria is an administrative specification issue of low relevance to the functional job, with low direct severity, placing it near the bottom.
rows.sat_rat: Standard URS templates cover many parameters, but ambiguity in construction and discharge criteria still recurs, leaving meaningful gaps. Clarifying acceptance criteria upfront costs specification time but prevents later disputes. Writing unambiguous criteria across capacity, construction, closures, and stacking needs experienced specification authors. Ambiguity is cheap to introduce but costly when it drives disputes and rejections at acceptance.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of discovering conditioning accessory incompatibility after attachment work begins
rows.imp_rat: Discovering conditioning-accessory incompatibility after work begins is a workflow rework nuisance (low relevance) with modest lost-effort consequence (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-attachment fit checks exist but incomplete records mean incompatibilities still surface after work begins fairly often. Discovering accessory incompatibility mid-attachment forces stoppages and rework that consume time. Anticipating incompatibility requires deep familiarity with fittings and specifications that not all technicians hold. Late discovery wastes labor and sometimes damaged parts, adding avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of process material released through the configured vent during service
rows.imp_rat: Process material released through the configured vent during service is a modest defect; both centrality to the core containment job and consequence severity are held low under this batch's ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Standard vent designs limit carryover, but improperly matched vents still allow occasional material release. Configuring the vent to retain material adds no meaningful prep time. Balancing venting against material retention needs process understanding that varies by operator. Material lost through the vent is direct product waste plus cleanup cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of liner folding or twisting after seating inside the container
rows.imp_rat: Liner folding or twisting after seating can hamper filling but is a modest defect; both proxies stay low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual liner seating produces variable results, with folds and twists remaining a common recurring defect. Seating a liner smoothly can take extra time to work out wrinkles. Getting a liner to sit without folds or twists is a finicky manual task highly dependent on operator skill. A twisted liner may need to be pulled and replaced, wasting the liner and time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of purchase order specifications that conflict with the selected stainless steel IBC configuration
rows.imp_rat: Purchase order specifications conflicting with the selected IBC configuration is a procurement-admin mismatch, peripheral to the job and with trivial, easily-corrected consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Routine spec-reconciliation practice catches most PO conflicts, though a few slip through on multi-parameter stainless configurations. Cross-checking PO specifications against the selected stainless configuration is a quick reconciliation step. Spotting spec-versus-configuration conflicts is a bounded review most procurement engineers can perform. Catching conflicts before order avoids costly wrong-configuration deliveries.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed tank maintenance events lacking a corresponding record
rows.imp_rat: Missing maintenance records for completed events is a documentation defect with limited direct impact on tank function; consequences are recordkeeping only, kept under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS work-order closeout enforces some record capture, yet manual entry and after-the-fact logging mean a meaningful fraction of events still go unrecorded or under-documented. Logging completed work adds a documentation step that technicians often defer under time pressure. Recording an event is low-skill, but doing it completely and accurately depends on discipline more than expertise. Digital record capture is cheap, but missing records create downstream compliance and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that preventive maintenance is scheduled after the tank service deadline
rows.imp_rat: Scheduling preventive maintenance after the service deadline can shorten tank uptime, but it is a schedule-administrative error with modest consequences, ranked below the prior batch and capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Computerized maintenance management systems reliably generate due-date alerts and are widely deployed, though backlog, resource constraints and override still let some PMs slip past the deadline. CMMS scheduling triggers work orders automatically, saving planners the time of manual tracking. Setting up service intervals requires some planning judgement but day-to-day scheduling is largely automated. Scheduling software is a modest fixed cost, and catching PM before the deadline avoids costlier reactive repairs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the backup does not represent the current approved tank configuration
rows.imp_rat: A backup not matching the current approved configuration risks restore errors, but it is a data-management concern peripheral to daily processing; held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Backups are frequently taken on a schedule disconnected from approved configuration changes, so stale or non-current backups are a common, poorly mitigated pain. Refreshing a backup to match the current configuration is quick if triggered, but it is rarely tied to change events so it lags. Recognizing that a configuration change invalidates the last backup requires attentive change-management discipline that is often lacking. Storage for backups is cheap, so the cost barrier is low, but the failure cost of restoring a stale configuration is high.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of tank components routed to incompatible recycling streams
rows.imp_rat: Routing components to incompatible recycling streams (waste) is an end-of-life environmental error with minimal relevance to processing and low severity; well below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Scrap dealers and material markings help, but mixed materials and coatings are still routinely routed to the wrong stream. Segregating components into recycling streams adds sorting time at teardown. Identifying alloys, linings and coatings correctly requires material knowledge that varies by crew. Mis-routed components lose recovery value or contaminate recycling loads, a direct cost hit.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required final inspection findings left undocumented at end of service
rows.imp_rat: Undocumented final inspection findings at end of service is a low-relevance recordkeeping defect with limited direct consequence to processing; well below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and inspection forms are common, yet findings still get partially captured or lost under time pressure at retirement. Documenting inspection findings is a modest end-of-service administrative task. Recording findings needs an inspector's judgement but is procedurally straightforward. Poor documentation is cheap in the moment but costs later in disputes and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that archived tank records become unavailable during the required retention period
rows.imp_rat: Archived records becoming unavailable during retention risks compliance gaps, but it is an archival concern far from active processing; kept under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Retention policies exist, but format obsolescence, orphaned files and unindexed archives still make records unavailable when later needed. Archiving is a one-time action that does not slow disposal. Proper records management needs archival discipline more than technical skill. Digital archiving is inexpensive, though long retention and migration carry modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of reusable tank accessories damaged during removal
rows.imp_rat: Damaging reusable tank accessories during removal is peripheral to the core processing job and its consequences are minor cost/rework; capped by the batch-5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: When salvage is prioritized it works, but disposal-mode teardown often treats accessories as scrap and damages them. Careful removal of accessories slows teardown compared to demolition. Salvaging reusable fittings intact takes deliberate, experienced handling. Damaged accessories forfeit resale or reuse value, the core loss this need addresses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when an immediate transfer or hold judgment is required
rows.plain: Stay calm when you must immediately decide whether to move something forward or place it on hold. This helps you avoid mistakes during the work.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when an immediate judgment is required is an emotional error, essentially unrelated to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of anxiety while transfer or hold status confirmation is pending
rows.plain: The worker wants less worry while waiting to learn whether material was moved or placed on hold. This matters because they need to know its current status before taking the next step.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety while transfer/hold status confirmation is pending is an emotional-latency error, peripheral to containment with trivial product consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when asked to account for vessel status without a confident answer
rows.plain: The worker wants to avoid being caught off guard if asked what condition a vessel is in and cannot give a confident answer. This matters because people need accurate information to carry out the work safely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when unable to account for vessel status is an emotional-standing error, peripheral to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of uncertainty about whether discharge can begin while fill-finish personnel await handoff
rows.plain: Know sooner whether the finished material can be handed over while the sterile-filling team is waiting to receive it. This helps the handoff happen on time.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about discharge while personnel await handoff is a coordination-latency error, remote from containment integrity with minor consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of anxiety as the validated holding allowance narrows before discharge begins
rows.plain: Make people less worried as the allowed storage time gets shorter before the material must be moved onward. This helps them hand it over on time without unnecessary panic.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety as the holding allowance narrows is an emotional error peripheral to physical containment; consequences fall on the individual, not the product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when fill-finish personnel request a discharge decision before readiness is clear
rows.plain: Stay calm when the people finishing the product ask for a release decision before it is clear that everything is ready. This helps avoid handing over material too soon.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when asked for a discharge decision is an emotional error, peripheral to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when conflicting indications require an immediate aseptic-status judgment
rows.plain: Stay calm when different signs point to different answers about whether the work stayed free from contamination. This helps you make a prompt and careful safety decision.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure over conflicting indications is a personal-emotional error, essentially unrelated to physical containment with individual consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of lingering self-doubt after responding to an ambiguous aseptic-boundary challenge
rows.plain: Feel less uncertain after answering an unclear question about where sterile handling begins or ends. Clear boundaries help the person respond with confidence and protect product safety.
rows.imp_rat: Self-doubt after an ambiguous aseptic-boundary challenge is an emotional-residue error, essentially unrelated to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that leadership attributes an avoidable hold-batch deviation to the operator's execution
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that leaders blame the operator for an avoidable problem that causes a batch to be held instead of released. Careful work helps prevent delays and waste.
rows.imp_rat: Leadership attributing a deviation to the operator is a blame-attribution error, tangential to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility loss with quality leadership when hold records are incomplete or inconsistent
rows.plain: Keep quality leaders’ trust by making sure records about products or materials placed on hold are complete and consistent. Clear records help show what was held, when, and why.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss when hold records are incomplete is a documentation-reputational error, tangential to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of the operator being bypassed in hold-vessel execution decisions by quality or manufacturing leadership
rows.plain: The operator should not be left out of decisions about carrying out work with a vessel that holds material just because quality or production leaders become involved. This matters because the operator's hands-on knowledge is important for a successful outcome.
rows.imp_rat: Operator bypassed in execution decisions is an authority-standing error, tangential to the containment job with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of successful hold batches being credited to supervisory intervention rather than operator execution
rows.plain: When a batch is successfully held, the success should be recognized as the operator's work rather than mainly credited to a supervisor stepping in. This matters because accurate credit builds trust in the operator's ability.
rows.imp_rat: Successful batches credited to supervisors is a recognition-attribution error, essentially unrelated to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a suspected sterility compromise is attributed to the operator's bulk-hold handling
rows.plain: The worker handling product held in bulk wants to avoid being blamed if the product may no longer be clean or safe. This matters because their handling could be seen as the cause.
rows.imp_rat: Sterility compromise attributed to the operator's handling is a blame-attribution error, peripheral to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility loss with quality and microbiology personnel when vessel aseptic integrity is questioned
rows.plain: Maintain trust with quality and germ-control staff when people question whether a production container has stayed protected from contamination. There should be convincing evidence that it remained sterile.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss when vessel integrity is questioned is a reputational error, tangential to physical containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of the operator being bypassed in decisions about holding or discharging sterile bulk
rows.plain: The operator should continue to be involved in deciding whether to keep sterile material on hold or release it. This matters because the operator's direct knowledge is important when making this decision.
rows.imp_rat: Operator bypassed in hold/discharge decisions is a standing error, tangential to the containment job with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of occasions when the operator's aseptic judgment is publicly second-guessed during deviation review
rows.plain: Reduce situations where other people publicly question the operator's judgment about keeping the process free from contamination during a review of a problem. This supports fairer reviews of the operator's actions.
rows.imp_rat: Operator judgment publicly second-guessed in deviation review is a standing error, tangential to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety spikes while borderline vessel indications remain unresolved
rows.plain: They want fewer sudden worries while unclear signs from a container remain unresolved. This helps them know whether the process stayed within safe limits or may have been affected.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety spikes over borderline indications are an emotional-state error, peripheral to actually containing sterile bulk; consequences are personal, not to the product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of fearing blame before the source of a possible deviation is understood
rows.plain: Avoid fearing blame before the cause of a possible problem is known. This gives the person room to investigate the facts and respond fairly.
rows.imp_rat: Fearing blame before a deviation source is understood is a personal-emotional error, essentially unrelated to the containment job with minimal consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when pressed to confirm vessel status
rows.plain: Stay calm when asked to confirm the condition of a container. This helps the worker check the facts before saying that it is ready or safe to use.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when pressed on vessel status is a personal-emotional error, essentially unrelated to physical containment with minimal consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when unable to defend the current parameter assessment
rows.plain: The person wants to show that the current equipment settings and limits are understood and under control. This matters because the work should be carried out within safe, approved conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when unable to defend a parameter assessment is an emotional-standing error, essentially unrelated to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety spikes while unexplained aseptic-hold changes remain unresolved
rows.plain: They want fewer sudden worries while unexplained changes in how long sterile products are held are still being investigated. This matters because they need confidence that the products remain free from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety spikes over unresolved aseptic-hold changes are a personal-state error, only loosely tied to containment, with minor consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an aseptic hold deviation is attributed to the executor's actions by site leadership
rows.plain: The worker wants to avoid being blamed by site leaders if a problem occurs while sterile product is being held. This matters because their actions during that hold may be examined closely.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation attributed to the executor by leadership is a reputational error, peripheral to the containment job with individual consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility loss with quality leadership after apparent departures from approved vessel setup
rows.plain: Avoid losing quality leaders’ trust when the actual setup of a production container appears different from the approved setup. The work should match the agreed instructions.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss after apparent departures from setup is a reputational error, peripheral to the containment job with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which hold-monitoring judgments are second-guessed by manufacturing leadership
rows.plain: Manufacturing leaders should question the person monitoring held materials less often. This supports steady decisions about when those materials can move forward.
rows.imp_rat: Hold-monitoring judgments second-guessed by leadership is a reputational-authority error, tangential to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a contamination finding is attributed to the executor's vessel stewardship during batch review
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a contamination problem is blamed on the worker who cared for and handled the product container during review of the batch. The records should show whether the container was handled and protected properly.
rows.imp_rat: Attribution of a contamination finding to the executor is a reputational error, peripheral to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility loss with Quality Assurance when an aseptic-hold concern is evaluated
rows.plain: Protect trust with the quality team when checking whether a product can safely remain protected from germs while waiting. The evaluation should be clear and well supported.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss with QA when a hold concern is evaluated is a reputational error, peripheral to physical containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the executor is bypassed in aseptic-hold escalation decisions by quality and production leadership
rows.plain: The person doing the work wants quality and production leaders to include them when deciding whether germ-free product should remain on hold. This helps ensure important information is not overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Executor bypassed in escalation decisions is an authority-standing error, tangential to the containment job with individual consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of visible uncertainty while the release judgment is expected
rows.plain: Make it quick and easy to reach and explain the decision to release the product when others are waiting for it. This helps the release proceed calmly and without unnecessary delay.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty while a release judgment is expected is a perception-latency error, peripheral to containment with minor consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when sterility evidence supports conflicting release conclusions
rows.plain: Stay calm when test results about freedom from harmful germs point to different release decisions. This helps the team review the evidence carefully before deciding whether the product can move forward.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when evidence supports conflicting conclusions is an emotional error, tangential to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when challenged to explain the release judgment before stakeholders
rows.plain: They want to avoid feeling exposed when people ask them to explain why something was approved for release. They need to show that the decision was based on sound information.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when explaining a release judgment is an emotional-standing error, essentially unrelated to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of lingering doubt after forming a go or no-go judgment from borderline evidence
rows.plain: Quickly feel confident in a yes-or-no decision when the available proof is close to the line between acceptable and unacceptable. This makes the decision less stressful and easier to defend.
rows.imp_rat: Lingering doubt after a go/no-go judgment is a psychological-residue error, peripheral to containment with negligible direct consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when containment-integrity signals conflict during sterile bulk holding
rows.plain: Stay calm when the signs about whether the material is safely contained do not agree while it is being held in a sterile container. This matters because a steady response helps protect the material and people from possible contamination or escape.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when integrity signals conflict is an emotional error, tangential to the containment job with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when asked for a containment judgment without defensible evidence
rows.plain: The supervisor wants to avoid being caught off guard if asked to judge whether a problem was contained without having solid proof. This matters because the judgment should be based on evidence, not guesswork.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed without defensible containment evidence is a reputational error, peripheral to the containment job with personal consequence.

rows.source_job: Reassurance that nothing is slipping past
rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety episodes when sterile bulk hold vessel signals conflict
rows.plain: A supervisor wants conflicting signs from the container holding sterile material to be explained. This helps ensure that a developing problem is not missed.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety episodes when signals conflict is an emotional error, peripheral to containment with personal consequence.

rows.source_job: Reassurance that nothing is slipping past
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when stakeholders demand assurance that no sterile bulk hold signal was missed
rows.plain: The person wants confidence that no warning sign showing that germ-free product is being held back has been overlooked. This matters because a missed warning could allow a problem to continue unnoticed.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when asked whether a signal was missed is an emotional-standing error, tangential to containment with individual consequence.

rows.source_job: Reassurance that nothing is slipping past
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unease while awaiting current evidence about the sterile bulk hold vessel
rows.plain: Feel less uneasy while waiting for up-to-date proof about the tank that holds the germ-free product before the next step. This helps ensure that an important issue is not being missed.
rows.imp_rat: Unease while awaiting current evidence is an emotional-latency error, peripheral to the containment function with minor consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a held-bulk contamination finding is publicly attributed to weak governance before quality leadership
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a contamination problem in product being held before the next step is publicly blamed on weak oversight. The controls for keeping the product clean should be clearly managed and documented.
rows.imp_rat: A contamination finding publicly attributed to weak governance is a reputational error, peripheral to physical containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the job overseer's sterile-integrity judgments are second-guessed during KPI reviews
rows.plain: The person responsible for keeping the work free from germs wants managers to question their judgments less often during performance reviews. This matters because repeated challenges can make it harder to act confidently and show that the work is being controlled safely.
rows.imp_rat: Integrity judgments second-guessed during KPI reviews is a reputational error, peripheral to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the job overseer is bypassed in held-bulk disposition decisions
rows.plain: The person overseeing the work should remain involved when deciding what to do with stored material that is being held back. This helps ensure the material is handled safely and consistently.
rows.imp_rat: Overseer bypassed in disposition decisions is a standing error, peripheral to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the job overseer's control authority is second-guessed when holding records conflict
rows.plain: People should not repeatedly question the overseer’s authority when records about held material disagree. Clear control helps resolve the conflict and keeps decisions ready for review.
rows.imp_rat: Control authority second-guessed when records conflict is a reputational error, peripheral to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile bulk hold decisions in which the job overseer is bypassed because their authority is doubted
rows.plain: Make sure the person overseeing the job is included in decisions about holding germ-free material. Their authority should be trusted so important decisions are not made without them.
rows.imp_rat: Overseer bypassed because authority is doubted is a standing error, peripheral to the containment job with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of inspection commitments missed publicly before quality leadership or regulatory inspectors
rows.plain: Reduce the number of promises made during an inspection that are visibly missed before company quality leaders or government inspectors. This helps show that the organization does what it says it will do.
rows.imp_rat: Publicly missed inspection commitments are an administrative/reputational error; capped at 1 on both proxies by the batch ceiling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile bulk solution unrecovered after transfer from the hold vessel
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of sterile liquid product left behind when it is moved out of the storage vessel. This helps prevent usable product from being wasted.
rows.imp_rat: Unrecovered solution after transfer from the vessel is yield waste, secondary to the CFJ; held at 1 on both.
rows.sat_rat: Drainable designs are established but some holdup loss always remains. Drainability design adds engineering time secondarily. Low-holdup transfer design needs specialized skill. Unrecovered product after transfer is direct waste, the dominant cost loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile bulk solution lost through required sampling during the hold
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of sterile liquid product used up when samples must be taken during storage. This preserves more product for use or sale.
rows.imp_rat: Solution lost through required sampling is minor waste; relevance and severity capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Closed sampling systems reduce loss but sampling still consumes product. Sampling adds minor time but that is not the loss. Reducing sampling loss needs sampling-system design skill. Product consumed by sampling is direct waste, the primary loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of visible uncertainty while explaining a challenged specification choice
rows.plain: People need to quickly explain why a particular requirement was chosen when someone challenges it.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty explaining a spec choice is emotional; capped at 1 on both proxies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of composure loss during unanticipated reviewer challenges
rows.plain: Avoid losing calm and confidence when reviewers raise unexpected challenges. Staying composed helps the person explain the recommendation clearly and respond thoughtfully.
rows.imp_rat: Composure loss during challenges is emotional; both proxies held at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of self-doubt when aseptic requirements and reviewer interpretations conflict
rows.plain: Reduce uncertainty when cleanliness requirements and reviewers disagree about what they mean. This helps the buyer make a decision they can explain and defend.
rows.imp_rat: Self-doubt when requirements conflict is emotional; capped at 1 on both proxies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when unable to explain an evaluation criterion during review
rows.plain: The person wants to avoid being caught off guard when asked why something was used to judge the purchase and cannot explain it. This matters because they need to show that the decision was fair and well supported.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed on a criterion is emotional; capped at 1 on relevance and severity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unexpected production findings that call the vessel recommendation into question
rows.plain: The buyer wants unexpected findings during production not to make people question the recommended production tank or container. This helps the recommendation remain trustworthy after work begins.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected findings questioning the recommendation is reliability/reputational; capped at 1 on both.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while awaiting evidence that could undermine the vessel recommendation
rows.plain: The buyer wants to spend less time worrying while waiting for information that might show the recommended container is not suitable. This matters because they want confidence before choosing it.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting undermining evidence is emotional; both proxies held at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of self-doubt caused by conflicting assessments of the vessel recommendation
rows.plain: Reduce uncertainty when different people give conflicting opinions about which vessel should be chosen. A clear, well-supported recommendation helps the person make the decision with confidence.
rows.imp_rat: Self-doubt from conflicting assessments is emotional; both proxies held at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling personally exposed when a production integrity concern is linked to the vessel recommendation
rows.plain: They want to avoid feeling blamed when a concern about keeping production reliable is connected to their equipment recommendation. This matters because they need confidence that the recommendation will not create an unexpected production problem.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed on integrity concern is emotional; capped at 1 on both proxies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterility uncertainty caused by weak evidence for portions of the intended hold
rows.plain: The buyer wants less time spent unsure whether the product stayed sterile during parts of the planned waiting period when the supporting proof is weak. This helps people make a more confident safety decision.
rows.imp_rat: Sterility uncertainty from weak hold evidence relates to the CFJ but is capped at 1 on both proxies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of sterility doubt caused by conflicting containment evidence during evaluation
rows.plain: Reduce doubt that the product will remain free from harmful germs when different pieces of evidence about keeping it contained disagree. This helps the buyer feel confident that the equipment can protect the product.
rows.imp_rat: Sterility doubt from conflicting evidence touches the CFJ but ranking caps both proxies at 1.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety spikes caused by unresolved sterility questions before sign-off
rows.plain: The person helping choose the purchase wants to worry less while questions about whether the process is free from harmful living germs remain open before final approval. This matters because the process must be safe to approve.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety spikes over sterility questions is emotional; capped at 1 on relevance and severity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of feeling exposed while sterility questions remain unanswered during review
rows.plain: Spend less time feeling vulnerable while questions about keeping the product free from germs remain unanswered. This helps the recommendation feel safe and well supported.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed while sterility questions pend is emotional; both proxies held at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an inspection deficiency being attributed to the specifier's evaluation criteria
rows.plain: The person who defines the requirements wants to reduce the chance that an inspection problem is blamed on the rules they used to judge suppliers or equipment. This helps show that the selection process was sound.
rows.imp_rat: Deficiency attributed to evaluation criteria is reputational; capped at 1 on relevance and severity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost when qualification evidence conflicts with the recommendation rationale
rows.plain: They want people to keep trusting the recommendation when the proof that something works does not match the reasons given for recommending it. This matters because decision-makers need clear, consistent support for their choice.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when evidence conflicts with rationale is reputational; both proxies held at 1.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of exclusion from sterile bulk hold vessel decisions after evaluation criteria are challenged
rows.plain: Avoid being left out of decisions about which storage vessel to use for sterile liquid after people challenge the evaluation standards. Clear, well-supported standards help the person defend the choice.
rows.imp_rat: Exclusion after criteria challenged is reputational; both proxies held at 1 per ceiling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of reviewer second-guessing caused by inconsistent specification rationale
rows.plain: Avoid having reviewers question a specification because the reasons behind its requirements are inconsistent. A clear explanation helps show that the chosen requirements are deliberate and defensible.
rows.imp_rat: Reviewer second-guessing from inconsistent rationale is process friction; both proxies held at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of stakeholder blame for qualification rework tied to the recommendation
rows.plain: Avoid being blamed when the recommendation has to be changed or checked again to meet requirements. This protects the person who proposed it from appearing careless.
rows.imp_rat: Blame for qualification rework is reputational; capped at 1 on relevance and severity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a sterility-assurance failure being attributed to the bulk-hold recommendation before quality and site leadership
rows.plain: The person recommending a temporary hold wants to reduce the chance that a failure to keep a product sterile is blamed on that recommendation. This matters because quality leaders and site leaders may hold the recommender responsible.
rows.imp_rat: Sterility failure attributed to the recommendation is reputational; both proxies held at 1 per ceiling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with quality and microbiology reviewers when the containment rationale is challenged
rows.plain: The person influencing the purchase wants to avoid losing the trust of quality and germ-control reviewers if the explanation for containing contamination is questioned.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost with reviewers is reputational; capped at 1 on both proxies per ceiling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of exclusion from final sterile bulk-hold criteria decisions after stakeholders question the specifier's judgment
rows.plain: Avoid being left out of final decisions about how long a sterile liquid may be stored because others question the person's judgment. Clear support for the decision helps protect the product and maintain trust.
rows.imp_rat: Exclusion from criteria decisions is reputational/organizational; capped at 1 on both.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of auditor or regulator blame for criteria that inadequately address aseptic containment risk
rows.plain: The adviser wants to reduce the chance that an auditor or government inspector blames them for using requirements that did not adequately protect against contamination. This helps ensure the requirements address the main safety risks.
rows.imp_rat: Auditor blame for weak aseptic criteria is compliance/reputational; capped at 1 on both proxies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of reputational damage from perceived commercial bias in the containment recommendation
rows.plain: Avoid damage to the person's reputation caused by people thinking the recommendation for containing contamination was influenced by business interests. The recommendation should clearly be based on protecting product safety.
rows.imp_rat: Reputational damage from perceived bias is peripheral to the CFJ; both proxies held at 1.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile bulk solution temperature excursions at each required batch volume
rows.plain: The buyer wants the sterile liquid to spend less time outside its required temperature range at every planned batch size. This helps protect the product and shows that the equipment will work as production grows.
rows.imp_rat: Temperature excursion duration touches aseptic containment quality, but ranking caps it: relevance and severity held at 1 as it sits below batch 4.
rows.sat_rat: Temperature control is mature but volume-specific excursion behavior is less fully characterized. Volume-dependent thermal response adds characterization time. Thermal modeling across volumes needs engineering skill. Excursions at certain volumes risk degradation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile bulk solution unrecovered after transfer at each required batch volume
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of sterile liquid product left behind after it is moved, for every required batch size. This helps more of each batch reach the next step of production.
rows.imp_rat: Unrecovered solution after transfer is a yield-waste error, secondary to containment; capped at 1 on both.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer designs reduce holdup but residual unrecovered volume persists across volumes. Recovery optimization adds process-design time secondarily. Maximizing recovery needs transfer-design expertise. Unrecovered bulk at each volume is direct product waste, the primary loss this need targets.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of containment footprint, loaded mass, or utility demand exceeding facility constraints at required batch volumes
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment and supporting services will fit the building when the required batch sizes are made. This includes having enough room, weight capacity, and power or other services.
rows.imp_rat: Footprint/mass/utility exceeding facility constraints could block deployment, but ranking caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Facility-fit reviews are routine, so footprint/utility overruns are usually caught in design. Facility-fit checks add planning time secondarily. Footprint/utility fit assessment needs facilities-engineering skill. Exceeding facility constraints forces costly facility changes, the primary cost concern.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of containment turnaround between batches at each required batch volume
rows.plain: Spend less time cleaning and preparing the enclosed work area between batches of different sizes. This helps the next batch start sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Containment turnaround between batches is a throughput-delay error, secondary to the CFJ; held at 1 on both.
rows.sat_rat: Turnaround processes are defined but cycle times remain a recurring throughput bottleneck. Turnaround duration between batches is the direct loss this need targets, and cleaning/sterilization cycles remain a real throughput constraint. Turnaround optimization needs process and cleaning expertise. Long turnaround reduces asset utilization, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of assessment handling introducing contamination unrelated to the containment candidate
rows.plain: The adviser wants to reduce the chance that the way a product or system is evaluated adds contamination that was not caused by the equipment being considered. This helps make the evaluation fair and reliable.
rows.imp_rat: Assessment handling introducing unrelated contamination is a test-integrity error, tangential to the containment job with study-validity consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic handling controls reduce but do not eliminate assessment-introduced contamination. Careful handling to avoid extraneous contamination adds procedural time. Avoiding assessment-induced contamination depends on skilled aseptic technique, the core dependence. False contamination causes wasted investigations, cost impact.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of bias toward a containment option independent of technical fit
rows.plain: Avoid favoring one way to contain the material for reasons unrelated to how well it meets the technical need. Fair evaluation should focus on how suitable each option is.
rows.imp_rat: Bias toward an option independent of fit is a selection defect; capped at 1 on relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Structured objective evaluation methods exist but bias mitigation is inconsistently enforced, leaving real residual bias. Debiasing decisions adds review time secondarily. Avoiding option bias independent of technical fit demands disciplined, experienced facilitation, the core dependence. Biased selection leads to costlier suboptimal choices.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required containment evidence remains unavailable before the purchase recommendation
rows.plain: Make it faster to obtain proof that the proposed protection can contain dangerous medicines before recommending a purchase. This helps the buyer judge the safety risk.
rows.imp_rat: Containment evidence unavailable before purchase recommendation is a procurement-timing error, peripheral to containment with schedule consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Late evidence stalls decisions but does not itself degrade containment assurance. Evidence unavailability directly delays the purchase recommendation. Assembling evidence promptly needs organized documentation skill. Delay extends procurement cycles with resource cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time criteria approval is delayed by unresolved reviewer disagreements
rows.plain: Agreement on how success will be judged should be reached sooner when reviewers disagree. This prevents disagreements from delaying the work.
rows.imp_rat: Criteria-approval delay from reviewer disagreements is a governance-timing error, remote from containment with schedule-only consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Disagreements introduce some inconsistency but mainly stall timelines. Reviewer disagreements directly delay criteria approval, the core loss here. Resolving expert disagreements needs senior authority and negotiation skill. Delay ties up resources but the primary loss is schedule.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time scheduled qualification execution waits for approved prerequisites
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for required approvals, materials, or other preparations before scheduled testing can begin.
rows.imp_rat: Qualification execution waiting for prerequisites is a scheduling delay, remote from the containment job with recoverable consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Prerequisite delays are common but do not affect containment outcome quality. Waiting for approved prerequisites is a direct scheduling delay. Sequencing prerequisites requires planning competence. Idle vessels and staff during waits waste resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while unresolved vessel-qualification findings await disposition
rows.plain: The buyer wants unresolved findings about whether the container was properly tested and shown to work to be settled. This matters so possible contamination problems can be addressed before they cause trouble.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety while qualification findings await disposition is emotional; capped at 1 on both proxies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of visible uncertainty when decision-makers challenge the vessel-containment evidence
rows.plain: The buyer wants to remain confident when decision-makers question the proof that a production container keeps its contents safely contained. Clear proof helps prevent concerns about contamination or escape.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty when challenged on containment evidence is peripheral to the CFJ; both proxies capped at 1 per ranking.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of feeling exposed when a containment-evidence gap cannot be explained under scrutiny
rows.plain: Spend less time feeling vulnerable when a missing piece of proof about keeping harmful material contained cannot be explained. This helps the recommendation remain credible when people ask difficult questions.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of feeling exposed on an evidence gap is emotional/peripheral; both proxies held at 1 per ranking.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when tracing a qualification conclusion to its supporting evidence
rows.plain: Stay calm when asked to connect an approval decision to the evidence that supports it. This helps show that the decision was based on facts and can withstand questioning.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure tracing a conclusion is emotional; both proxies held at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt that unexplained containment-evidence variation will withstand quality and regulatory scrutiny
rows.plain: Make sure unexplained differences in the evidence about keeping harmful material contained can be explained and defended during formal review. This helps the recommendation hold up to quality and government inspection.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that evidence variation withstands scrutiny is an assurance-confidence error, peripheral to containment with review-level consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while conflicting interpretations of vessel-containment evidence remain unresolved
rows.plain: The buyer wants conflicting views about whether the evidence shows the container keeps its contents safely contained to be settled. This matters so the choice can withstand careful questioning.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety over unresolved evidence interpretation is emotional/peripheral; both proxies held at 1.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while cross-functional confirmation of protocol readiness remains pending
rows.plain: The buyer wants all involved groups to confirm that the work instructions are ready. This matters because they want to avoid starting studies that are likely to fail.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting protocol-readiness confirmation is emotional; capped at 1 on both proxies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of lost calm when deviation and invalid-run triggers are interpreted inconsistently
rows.plain: People should interpret unusual events and failed production runs the same way, so they can stay confident that a study or run will not be wrongly judged unsuccessful.
rows.imp_rat: Lost calm over inconsistent deviation triggers is emotional; both proxies held at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling unprepared for a foreseeable qualification-study failure
rows.plain: They want to avoid being caught unprepared if a planned study to prove the equipment works fails. This matters because they need to know how they would respond to a failure that could reasonably happen.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling unprepared for a qualification failure is emotional; both proxies capped at 1.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that quality and validation leaders attribute an unsuccessful containment qualification to the evaluator's recommendation
rows.plain: Avoid having quality and testing leaders blame the evaluator’s advice if testing shows that the containment method did not work. This helps make clear that the conclusion reflects the evidence rather than personal blame.
rows.imp_rat: Attribution of a failed qualification to the evaluator is a reputational error, peripheral to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost when containment-repeatability claims fail under defined or worst-case conditions
rows.plain: Protect trust by making sure claims that the equipment repeatedly keeps harmful material contained remain true during normal and especially difficult tests. The results should demonstrate dependable protection.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when repeatability claims fail is a reputational error, peripheral to containment execution with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of being second-guessed by quality and validation leaders after evaluation criteria omit consequential containment challenges
rows.plain: Avoid having quality leaders repeatedly question the decision because important containment problems were left out of the evaluation. This helps show that the recommendation considered the problems that could have serious consequences.
rows.imp_rat: Being second-guessed after criteria omit challenges is a reputational error, peripheral to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of being bypassed in containment decisions after quality and validation leaders judge the evidence ambiguous
rows.plain: The person wants others to rely on them when deciding how to prevent harmful material from spreading, even when the available proof is unclear. This matters because unclear decisions can put product quality and safety at risk.
rows.imp_rat: Being bypassed after evidence judged ambiguous is an authority-standing error, peripheral to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost when unresolved vessel-evidence inconsistencies are exposed during regulatory scrutiny
rows.plain: They want reviewers to keep trusting them when they uncover conflicting evidence about a container during an official inspection. This matters because unresolved contradictions can make the whole evaluation seem unreliable.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when evidence inconsistencies surface in scrutiny is a reputational error, peripheral to containment with review consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of being bypassed in the vessel recommendation after purchasing and quality stakeholders question the evaluator's evidence standards
rows.plain: The person wants buyers and quality specialists to trust the proof used to recommend a container, so they are not bypassed. Strong, clearly explained proof helps others believe the recommendation is sound.
rows.imp_rat: Being bypassed after evidence standards questioned is a reputational-authority error, peripheral to containment with personal consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of stakeholder challenges caused by ambiguous vessel-qualification criteria
rows.plain: People involved in the decision should raise fewer challenges because the standards for proving that a container is suitable are clear. This matters because unclear standards make evaluations harder to trust.
rows.imp_rat: Stakeholder challenges from ambiguous qualification criteria is a documentation-clarity error, peripheral to containment with review-friction consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the inspection-defensible vessel evaluation misses the governance-review deadline
rows.plain: The specialist wants the vessel assessment, including the evidence needed to defend it during an inspection, finished before the review deadline. Missing the deadline could delay the decision.
rows.imp_rat: Vessel evaluation missing a governance deadline is a scheduling-defect, remote from containment with review-timing consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when reviewers request traceable support for the contact-material recommendation
rows.plain: The person wants records that clearly support the recommendation for the material that touches the product. This matters because reviewers may need to follow the reasoning and verify that the material is appropriate.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when reviewers request traceable support is a personal-standing error, peripheral to containment with individual consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about unresolved material-suitability evidence after recommendation sign-off
rows.plain: The buyer should quickly know what proof is still missing that the material is suitable after the recommendation is approved. This helps prevent late surprises.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about material-suitability evidence after sign-off is an administrative-ambiguity error, peripheral to real-time containment with minor consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of self-doubt when late-stage findings challenge assumptions behind the contact-material recommendation
rows.plain: Make sure the choice of materials that touch the product will still make sense if new problems are found late in the project. This prevents last-minute surprises and loss of confidence.
rows.imp_rat: Self-doubt when late findings challenge a recommendation is an emotional error, peripheral to containment with personal consequence.
