Forecasting product and regulatory shifts over payback requires specialized foresight. An unsuitable investment before payback destroys expected value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of capital authorization revisions caused by post-approval changes to aseptic holding requirements
rows.plain: Fewer approved investment plans should need to be changed because the requirements for keeping products sterile change afterward. This helps prevent unexpected increases in cost or scope.
rows.imp_rat: Authorization revisions from changed holding requirements is rework; both proxies held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Post-approval requirement changes recur when URS is not fully settled at the gate. Post-approval requirement changes trigger revision cycles that lengthen timelines. Locking aseptic holding requirements before approval requires thorough requirements engineering. Each revision adds engineering and administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of facility modification, utility connection, and production-disruption cost understated
rows.plain: Avoid underestimating the costs of changing the building, connecting services such as power and water, and interrupting production. This gives a more truthful picture of what the purchase will really cost.
rows.imp_rat: Understated facility-modification cost is a budgeting-completeness defect, remote from containment with financial-only consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Understatement here is a persistent, recurring pattern in vessel capital cases. Facility modification and utility costs surface late in scoping, delaying complete outlay figures. Estimating utility connection and disruption costs requires facilities engineering knowledge the executor must source externally. Facility modification, utility connection and production-disruption costs are commonly understated in capital outlays.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of commissioning, qualification, validation, and regulatory documentation cost understated
rows.plain: Avoid understating the cost of starting the equipment, proving that it works properly, checking that it continues to work, and preparing required official records. These activities can add substantial cost to the project.
rows.imp_rat: Understated commissioning/regulatory documentation cost is a budgeting-accuracy defect, remote from containment with financial-only consequence.
rows.sat_rat: The understatement is a recurring, predictable pattern given weak historical cost baselines for documentation. Documentation cost is typically estimated near approval, adding late revisions when understated. Forecasting CQV and regulatory documentation effort requires regulatory-affairs experience rarely held by the purchaser. Validation and regulatory documentation costs are consistently understated because their effort is hard to predict up front.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of cleaning, sterilization, utility, maintenance, consumable, and spare-parts cost understated
rows.plain: Avoid understating the costs of cleaning, making equipment safe from germs, utilities, upkeep, supplies, and replacement parts. Including these costs gives a more realistic picture of what the equipment will cost over time.
rows.imp_rat: Understated cleaning/utility/spares cost is a budgeting-completeness defect, remote from containment with financial-only consequence.
rows.sat_rat: The status quo repeatably underestimates these operating costs due to reliance on optimistic supplier data. Cleaning, sterilization and consumable costs are usually appended late, delaying a complete cost view. Estimating utility, maintenance and spare-parts spend requires operations expertise the executor lacks directly. Recurring cleaning, sterilization, utility and consumable costs are frequently understated in capital models.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency among scope, warranty, service, and ownership assumptions used to compare acquisition options
rows.plain: Make sure the options are compared using consistent assumptions about what is included, how long it is covered for repairs, what support is provided, and who owns which responsibilities. This helps reveal the true long-term cost of each option.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent scope/warranty comparison assumptions is variability; both proxies held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Acquisition-option comparisons are inconsistently normalized in status-quo evaluation. Normalizing scope and warranty assumptions across bids takes extra evaluation time. Comparing warranty, service and ownership terms consistently needs procurement expertise. Inconsistent ownership assumptions can hide true cost differences between options.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that vessel service-life, replacement, and end-of-life assumptions prove inaccurate
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that estimates about how long the vessel will last, when it will need replacing, and how it will be retired turn out to be wrong. Better estimates help predict the equipment’s full cost over time.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate service-life assumptions is a reliability/forecast error; both proxies held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Service-life projections are commonly optimistic and inconsistently validated. Wrong service-life assumptions surface late, forcing unplanned timing changes. Estimating service life and end-of-life needs engineering-reliability expertise. Inaccurate service-life assumptions distort replacement budgeting.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of lifecycle expenditure counted more than once across acquisition, facility, compliance, and operating estimates
rows.plain: Avoid counting the same cost more than once in estimates for buying the equipment, changing the facility, meeting requirements, and running the operation. Removing duplicates makes the total cost more accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Double-counting lifecycle expenditure is a financial-estimation defect, remote from containment with budgetary-accuracy consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Duplication occurs irregularly and is hard to catch without a consolidated model, so results are inconsistent. Reconciling overlapping estimates takes extra cycles that delay the gate. Detecting double-counting across acquisition, facility and operating estimates needs disciplined cost-model auditing skill. Lifecycle expenditure is sometimes counted more than once when separate estimators overlap scope, distorting totals.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required tank history records missing at ownership transfer
rows.imp_rat: Missing tank history records at ownership transfer is an administrative handover gap with minimal bearing on the core processing job; lowest tier, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Tank history is often fragmented across paper and systems, so required records are commonly missing at ownership transfer. Gathering scattered history records at transfer is a slow, manual assembly task. Locating decades-old vessel records requires familiarity with legacy filing that few retain. Missing records force reconstruction or revaluation, an avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable vessel records remaining active after deregistration
rows.imp_rat: Vessel records remaining active after deregistration is a registry-cleanup defect with negligible functional consequence; lowest importance tier.
rows.sat_rat: Without a linked asset register, vessel records frequently stay active in some systems long after the tank is gone. Deregistration filings are quick but often deferred after physical disposal. Knowing which registries and jurisdictions apply needs compliance-specific expertise. Lingering active records incur ongoing fees and inspection obligations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required stakeholders omitted from the tank retirement notification
rows.imp_rat: Omitting stakeholders from a tank retirement notification is a purely administrative communication gap with trivial functional consequences; ranked lowest tier and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Notification lists exist but are frequently incomplete, so required stakeholders are routinely omitted. Issuing retirement notifications is quick once the distribution list is known. Knowing every required stakeholder demands institutional knowledge that is often held by few people. Missing a notification is nearly free to send but costly if it triggers compliance gaps.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the monitoring gap before tank retirement activities are complete
rows.imp_rat: A monitoring gap before retirement completion is an end-of-life administrative timing issue with trivial consequence; lowest tier, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled decommissioning plans keep most gaps bounded, but sequencing delays still leave intervals where monitoring lapses before retirement is truly done. The window between monitoring cessation and completed retirement is the very delay this need targets, and it is often longer than planned. Closing out monitoring cleanly needs coordination rather than deep expertise. A prolonged monitoring gap ties up resources and extends oversight cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of candidate reconditioned IBCs with undocumented prior contents, cleaning history, or repairs
rows.plain: Fewer used large reusable containers should have an unknown history of what they held, how they were cleaned, or what repairs they received. This helps people judge whether the containers are safe and suitable to use.
rows.imp_rat: Undocumented prior contents, cleaning history, or repairs on candidate reconditioned IBCs is a contamination-provenance concern, but ranked below the prior batch so it stays low relevance and low severity per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Reconditioned IBC provenance is chronically incomplete and inconsistent, making this one of the worst-served needs at acquisition. Establishing prior contents, cleaning, and repair history for reconditioned IBCs requires slow investigation with often-unresponsive sources. Assessing whether an undocumented reconditioned IBC is fit for pharma use requires deep contamination-risk expertise. Undocumented reconditioned units are frequently scrapped or downgraded, wasting acquisition spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of ownership cost omitted from the new-versus-reconditioned IBC comparison
rows.plain: Include all costs of owning and using the container when comparing a new one with a restored one. This shows which choice is less expensive over time, not just at the beginning.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting ownership cost from the new-versus-reconditioned comparison is a cost-analysis waste of peripheral relevance and low severity; held at 1 under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: New-versus-reconditioned comparisons routinely omit cost elements, making outcomes inconsistent. Compiling ownership costs for new versus reconditioned options is time-consuming. Estimating reconditioning ownership costs requires niche expertise. Ignored ownership cost is the exact waste at stake and is commonly underestimated.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the chosen IBC option cannot meet the project readiness date
rows.plain: The chosen large container should be available and ready in time for the project. This prevents the project from being delayed by choosing an option that cannot meet the planned date.
rows.imp_rat: The chosen IBC option missing the project readiness date is a delay/selection issue, peripheral and capped at 1 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Lead-time estimates from suppliers are reasonably established but variability means readiness dates are sometimes missed. Whether an IBC option meets the project readiness date is fundamentally a lead-time question that frequently constrains schedules. Matching option lead times to project milestones requires planning judgement that experienced buyers apply. A late option can force expensive expediting or alternate sourcing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood the supplier rejects the order because the selected IBC quantity is unavailable
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the supplier refuses the order because the requested number of large containers is not available. This helps the purchase go through as planned.
rows.imp_rat: A supplier rejecting an order for unavailable IBC quantity is a procurement-availability error, peripheral to the container's core job (low relevance) with reschedulable impact (low severity); limited to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard PO confirmation practice catches most availability shortfalls, though surprises still occur for stainless IBCs. Availability is typically confirmed during quoting, avoiding late-stage delay. Checking quantity availability is routine buyer work needing little special skill. Rejected orders waste some rework effort but rarely large money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the purchase transaction waits for required internal approvals
rows.plain: The purchase order should not sit waiting for internal approval. Faster approval helps the order move forward sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Transaction waiting for internal approvals is a purely administrative delay peripheral to the IBC's core job, so both relevance and severity are held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic approval workflows make the process reasonably consistent, with residual delays from escalations and out-of-office approvers. Internal approval routing via workflow tools has cut waiting substantially, though sign-offs still queue behind absent approvers. Submitting for approval requires no special skill under established procurement policy. Approval delays rarely incur direct cost beyond occasional missed pricing windows.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required billing, delivery, or commercial terms missing from the order
rows.plain: Include all needed payment, delivery, and business terms in the order. This helps the buyer and seller agree on what will be provided and under what conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Missing billing or commercial terms on the order cause ambiguity, but the mandatory cap keeps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory-field enforcement in purchasing systems reliably captures most required terms, with occasional gaps on non-standard orders. ERP order templates auto-populate standard terms, minimizing time spent completing them. Ensuring all required terms are on the order relies on buyers following the system's mandatory fields. Missing commercial terms cause invoice disputes and rework, a contained cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which the final transaction total exceeds the approved purchase value
rows.plain: Keep the final amount charged on the purchase order as close as possible to, and no higher than, the approved purchase amount.
rows.imp_rat: The error of the final transaction total exceeding the approved purchase value is peripheral to the physical function of intermediate bulk containers and, per the batch-5 ceiling, ranks below prior items; both relevance and severity are held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Three-way match catches many overruns, but unbudgeted landed-cost variances still frequently inflate the final transaction total. Final invoice reconciliation against approved value happens after the fact, adding no upfront time but delayed detection. Three-way matching to catch final-total overruns is standard AP practice requiring modest skill. Freight surcharges, currency shifts, and add-ons still push final totals above approved value despite matching.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the technician is injured by unstable units, sharp edges, or shifted components during inspection
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment is stable and free of dangerous sharp edges or loose parts before the technician checks it. This helps prevent injuries during the inspection.
rows.imp_rat: Technician injury from unstable units or sharp edges is a real safety concern, but the mandatory batch ceiling caps both proxies at 1 despite the harm potential.
rows.sat_rat: Mature workplace-safety practice, PPE, and mechanical handling make inspection injuries uncommon where procedures are enforced. Safety protocols add minimal time to inspection when followed correctly. Safe handling of unstable or sharp units is covered by standard warehouse safety training. Injuries are costly but PPE and handling equipment that prevent them are inexpensive and routine.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required IBC surfaces and fittings that cannot be accessed for inspection on arrival
rows.plain: Make sure every required part of the large reusable container can be reached and checked when it arrives, so damage is not missed.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccessible surfaces and fittings on arrival impede inspection, yet the importance ceiling forces relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Palletized, uniform IBC stacking usually exposes required surfaces, but nested or shrink-wrapped loads still block some fittings on arrival. When fittings are blocked, repositioning units for surface access adds inspection time. Knowing which IBC surfaces and fittings must be inspected is standard trained-inspector knowledge. Inaccessible surfaces force partial re-handling, a limited added cost per receipt.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in scope and usage assumptions across the IBC acquisition arrangements
rows.plain: Make sure all purchase options for the large reusable containers are based on the same description of what is included and how they will be used. This makes the options easier to compare.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent scope and usage assumptions across acquisition arrangements is a procurement-ambiguity error, tangential to the CFJ (low relevance) with correctable impact (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Scope assumptions are seldom documented uniformly, so inconsistency across acquisition arrangements is common. Aligning scope and usage assumptions across arrangements requires slow clarification rounds. Interpreting differing scope assumptions leans on procurement judgement. Hidden scope inconsistencies surface later as unbudgeted cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of lifecycle cost omitted when comparing the IBC acquisition arrangements
rows.plain: Make sure all costs over the equipment’s entire useful life are included when comparing different ways to obtain it. This helps avoid choosing an option that only looks cheaper at first.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting lifecycle cost when comparing acquisition arrangements is a financial-analysis error with low relevance to physical containment and modest severity; held at 1 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without a standard TCO model, comparisons omit cost categories inconsistently across arrangements. Assembling a full lifecycle cost view across arrangements is slow and rarely done well. Proper lifecycle costing needs specialized financial-technical skill many teams lack. Omitted lifecycle cost elements are precisely the overspend this need targets, and they are frequently left out.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in responsibility for cleaning, validation, maintenance, damage, and contamination under each IBC acquisition arrangement
rows.plain: Clearly assign who is responsible for cleaning, checking that cleaning worked, upkeep, damage, and contamination under each way of obtaining the equipment. This prevents disagreements about who must act or pay.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in responsibility for cleaning/maintenance/contamination under acquisition arrangements is a contractual clarity gap of modest relevance here; held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Responsibility for cleaning, validation, maintenance and contamination is frequently left ambiguous across arrangements. Negotiating clear responsibility splits for cleaning and validation extends contracting time. Defining liability for contamination and validation requires legal and quality expertise. Unclear responsibility surfaces as disputed charges and unbudgeted liability.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of IBC availability commitments lacking credible support across the acquisition arrangements
rows.plain: Claims that large reusable containers will be available should be backed by reliable evidence for every buying or leasing option. This makes it easier to choose an option that can actually supply the containers.
rows.imp_rat: Unsupported IBC availability commitments touch acquisition reliability, but per the batch ceiling this ranks below prior items; relevance and severity are both capped at 1 despite potential procurement disruption.
rows.sat_rat: Buyers take commitments largely at face value, so uncredible availability claims often go unchallenged. Vetting the credibility of availability commitments adds due-diligence time. Assessing whether a delivery commitment is credible needs supplier-evaluation experience. Unsupported commitments cause costly schedule breakdowns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of return and exchange obligations that remain unspecified across the IBC acquisition arrangements
rows.plain: Make sure every purchase deal clearly says who is responsible when items are returned or exchanged. This prevents disagreements and unexpected costs.
rows.imp_rat: Unspecified return/exchange obligations across acquisition arrangements is a commercial-terms ambiguity of peripheral relevance and low severity; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Return and exchange obligations are often left partly unspecified, though standard terms cover common cases. Pinning down return and exchange terms adds contract negotiation time. Drafting complete return obligations needs contracting know-how. Unspecified return terms create disputed costs later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required traceability links that cannot be verified through supplier records
rows.plain: Make sure supplier records can show where important materials or products came from and where they went. This matters so problems can be traced back to their source.
rows.imp_rat: Unverifiable traceability links through supplier records is a documentation-completeness concern only loosely tied to the core containment job (low relevance), and the ceiling bounds its severity (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Supplier record systems are fragmented and often paper-based, so a meaningful fraction of traceability links simply cannot be confirmed. Verifying traceability links through supplier records is a laborious back-and-forth that stretches qualification timelines. Reconstructing and validating traceability chains demands experienced quality auditors who understand supply-chain provenance. Unverifiable traceability can force rejection of otherwise usable stock, wasting purchase value.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the supplier's documented production capacity and capacity available for the project
rows.plain: Make sure the supplier’s stated production ability matches the amount of production capacity it can actually provide for this project.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between supplier documented and available production capacity is a supply-planning error of limited relevance to the containment job; held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Capacity is discussed but rarely rigorously verified, so mismatches remain a real risk. Confirming project-available capacity against documented capacity takes supplier dialogue time. Judging whether stated capacity is truly available for the project needs procurement acumen. Capacity mismatch can force costly expediting or resourcing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of supplier commercial terms that conflict with approved purchasing requirements
rows.plain: Avoid agreeing to supplier business terms that conflict with the organization’s approved purchasing rules. This helps prevent problems with the purchase agreement.
rows.imp_rat: Supplier terms conflicting with approved purchasing requirements is a procurement defect, capped at 1 on relevance and severity by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard terms-and-conditions review and approved supplier lists catch most conflicts, though non-standard clauses still slip through. Comparing supplier terms against purchasing policy is a defined review step that adds modest time. Detecting conflicts between supplier terms and approved requirements needs experienced procurement judgement. Conflicting terms slipping through can incur unbudgeted liabilities and disputes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of supplier qualification delays caused by late responses and corrective evidence
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for a supplier to finish its approval because it replied late or provided needed proof late. This helps the supplier become approved sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Supplier qualification delays from late responses are a procurement-process error peripheral to the container function, capped at relevance 1 and severity 1 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Responses are erratic across suppliers, making qualification timelines unpredictable despite standardized checklists. Supplier qualification routinely stalls waiting on late questionnaire responses and corrective evidence, with cycles dragging over weeks. Chasing and evaluating corrective evidence relies on experienced quality/procurement staff, and that judgement is scarce. The delay ties up staff effort and can push procurement to costlier expedited alternatives.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that scheduled transport capacity is unsuitable for the ordered stainless steel IBCs
rows.plain: Make sure the planned delivery vehicle and equipment can safely carry the ordered large stainless-steel containers.
rows.imp_rat: Transport capacity unsuitable for ordered IBCs could disrupt delivery, but this batch ranks below prior items; relevance and severity fixed at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Established freight practices handle most transport suitability, with occasional oversized or configuration surprises. Arranging suitable transport is a routine logistics step done in normal lead time. Matching transport to IBC dimensions is standard freight-planning knowledge. Unsuitable transport causes reschedule fees but these are usually avoidable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the delivery vehicle is unable to access the designated unloading area upon arrival
rows.plain: Make sure the delivery vehicle can reach the planned unloading place when it arrives, so the container can be unloaded safely and on time.
rows.imp_rat: A vehicle unable to access the unloading area delays receipt, but ranked below prior items; relevance and severity capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Delivery access is usually confirmed in advance, though occasional site-access surprises still occur. Verifying dock and access ahead of delivery is a fast routine check. Assessing vehicle access to the unloading area is basic logistics coordination. Access failures cause failed-delivery and reattempt charges but are infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that receiving space is unavailable when the stainless steel IBCs arrive
rows.plain: Make sure there is enough receiving space when the stainless-steel containers arrive. This prevents unloading and delivery problems.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable receiving space on arrival can slow intake, yet it ranks low here; relevance and severity held at 1 under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard inbound scheduling generally secures receiving space, so failures are relatively rare. Checking receiving space availability is a quick pre-arrival coordination step. Confirming warehouse space needs only basic warehouse coordination. Lack of space causes demurrage or rehandling costs occasionally.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of disruption to scheduled sterile fill-finish activities caused by container delivery
rows.plain: Deliver the container without interrupting scheduled work that makes sterile products and puts them into their final containers. This helps the delivery happen while planned production continues.
rows.imp_rat: Disruption to sterile fill-finish from container delivery has real consequences, but the batch ranking caps relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Safety-stock and staged deliveries mitigate some disruption, but delivery variability against rigid fill windows still causes recurring interruptions. Sterile fill-finish lines are highly time-sensitive and container delivery slips still cause measurable downtime despite buffer stock practices. Coordinating delivery timing against a validated fill schedule requires scarce planning expertise spanning logistics and GMP operations. Disrupted sterile fill runs are extremely costly in lost batch value and idle cleanroom time, poorly contained today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that conflicting delivery details cause a missed receiving appointment
rows.plain: Make sure everyone has the same delivery date, time, and location. Conflicting details could cause the delivery to miss its scheduled appointment.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting delivery details causing a missed receiving appointment is a scheduling ambiguity of modest impact; relevance and severity capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Dock-scheduling systems reduce conflicts but data entered from multiple parties still diverges often enough that missed appointments remain common. Appointment scheduling portals confirm slots quickly, but reconciling conflicting details still consumes back-and-forth calls before the delivery window. Spotting a discrepancy between PO, ASN, and booking data before it causes a miss depends on a diligent receiving clerk cross-checking manually. A missed appointment triggers re-delivery fees and detention charges, a recurring avoidable expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of relevant stainless steel IBC suppliers omitted from the market research
rows.plain: Include all important suppliers of large stainless-steel containers in the market research. This gives the buyer more suitable suppliers to consider.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting relevant suppliers from market research is a completeness defect of limited centrality to the container's function, so both proxies remain at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Ad hoc searches and familiar-vendor bias mean relevant suppliers are routinely omitted from the shortlist. Broader supplier discovery takes more search time, which buyers under deadline often cut short. Comprehensive market scanning for stainless IBC suppliers depends on researcher skill and networks. Missing suppliers forgoes competitive pricing leverage, a real cost impact.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in specification data across stainless steel IBC supplier product lines
rows.plain: Make sure the product information is consistent across the different lines of large reusable containers offered by stainless-steel suppliers. This helps the buyer compare products accurately.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent specification data across supplier product lines complicates comparison, a modest concern that, being below the prior batch, is capped at 1 on both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: No industry-wide spec template exists for stainless IBCs, so inconsistency across supplier product lines is a chronic, largely unmitigated source of comparison error. Reconciling divergent spec formats across stainless steel IBC suppliers is slow manual work with no time-saving standard tool. It takes an experienced engineer to spot that two datasheets describe the same attribute under different labels, so the need leans heavily on scarce judgement. Inconsistency itself wastes little material but drives rework hours and occasional wrong buys.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of gathered stainless steel IBC supplier and product-line information that is outdated
rows.plain: Use up-to-date information about suppliers and product ranges for stainless-steel bulk containers, so purchasing decisions are based on current facts.
rows.imp_rat: Outdated supplier and product-line information gathered in research is a minor data-currency error, held at relevance 1 and severity 1 by the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Web catalogs, datasheets and directories are frequently stale, so a meaningful share of gathered IBC info is out of date. Refreshing supplier and product-line data means re-collecting from scattered sources, adding delay. Spotting outdated specs and discontinued product lines takes an experienced researcher's eye. Gathering data is cheap, but acting on outdated info causes rework and mis-buys.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to obtain technical and commercial information from stainless steel IBC suppliers
rows.plain: Get product and pricing information from suppliers of large stainless-steel containers more quickly. This helps the buyer compare suppliers and make a decision sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Time to obtain technical and commercial information from suppliers is a procurement-speed delay of low centrality, with both proxies held at 1 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Response completeness and speed vary by supplier, but established RFI practice makes this manageable. Obtaining technical and commercial data from IBC suppliers still relies on email/RFI cycles that take days, though responsive vendors are quicker. Framing the right technical questions needs some product knowledge but is routine for procurement. Information gathering itself is low cost, mostly staff time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required IBC specification fields omitted from supplier datasheets
rows.plain: Make sure supplier information includes every required detail about the large reusable container, so different containers can be compared fairly.
rows.imp_rat: Required specification fields omitted from supplier datasheets slows evaluation, a lower-priority gap the ranking caps at 1 for relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and RFQ templates partly mitigate omissions, yet supplier datasheets still routinely leave required fields blank. Chasing suppliers for missing datasheet fields lengthens the acquisition timeline. A knowledgeable buyer using a checklist can usually detect which required fields are absent. Omitted fields occasionally cause rebuys but the direct cost impact is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of IBC specifications that cannot be directly compared because units or reference conditions differ
rows.plain: The details for different containers should use the same units and measurement conditions so they can be compared directly. This makes it easier to choose between containers fairly.
rows.imp_rat: IBC specifications not directly comparable due to differing units or reference conditions hampers selection, a peripheral concern capped at 1 under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: There is no enforced common reference basis, so incomparable specs slip through routinely despite careful reviewers. Manually normalizing units and reference conditions across quotes adds delay to every comparison cycle. Recognizing that a capacity or pressure figure is stated under different reference conditions requires domain expertise that many buyers lack. Undetected unit or reference mismatches can lead to costly wrong specifications being purchased.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of relying on conflicting specifications for the same IBC configuration
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of using different or conflicting details for the same large reusable container. This helps people choose and use the correct information.
rows.imp_rat: Relying on conflicting specs for one IBC configuration is a spec-management error, marginal to the immediate CFJ (low relevance) and usually caught downstream (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without a single authoritative spec source per configuration, conflicting specifications persist as a recurring hazard. Resolving conflicting figures for one configuration forces back-and-forth that delays decisions. Detecting that two documents contradict each other for the same IBC requires careful cross-checking expertise. Acting on a conflicting spec risks costly procurement errors.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity when mapping supplier terminology to sterile fill-finish requirements
rows.plain: Make the supplier's terms clear and show how they match the requirements for filling products into clean containers. This helps people compare container options and avoid choosing ones that do not fit the process.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity mapping supplier terminology to fill-finish requirements is a specification-clarity error, peripheral to the core IBC job (low relevance) and resolvable (low severity); held to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: No shared terminology bridge exists between suppliers and sterile fill-finish specs, leaving ambiguity largely unresolved. Clarifying supplier terminology against sterile fill-finish needs requires slow expert consultation. Mapping vendor terms to GMP sterile requirements demands rare combined procurement and pharma-quality expertise. Misinterpretation can cause acquisition of IBCs unsuitable for sterile use, a very costly error.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of including a superseded or configuration-mismatched datasheet in the IBC comparison
rows.plain: Use the correct, current specification sheet that matches the container being compared. This helps ensure the comparison is based on accurate information.
rows.imp_rat: Including a superseded datasheet in a comparison is a pre-acquisition documentation slip, tangential to the CFJ (low relevance) and correctable before harm (low severity); capped at 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Version control for supplier documents is informal, so stale or mismatched datasheets frequently enter comparisons undetected. Verifying that each datasheet is the current, correctly configured revision adds review time. Catching a superseded or configuration-mismatched sheet demands attentive version control that is easy to overlook. Buying against an outdated spec can cause expensive returns or non-conforming units.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required certification artifacts missing from the supplier documentation
rows.plain: Collect every required document showing that the supplier meets official safety or legal standards. This helps confirm that the supplier's claims are properly supported.
rows.imp_rat: Missing certification artifacts in supplier documentation is a procurement paperwork defect; the ranking forces both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists exist but supplier packets frequently arrive incomplete, and incompleteness is often caught inconsistently rather than by a systematic gate. Chasing missing certification artifacts from suppliers introduces multi-day delays before a container package can be released. Knowing exactly which artifacts are mandatory for a given IBC grade requires regulatory literacy that many procurement staff lack. Missing certificates mean paying for containers that cannot be used until documentation is completed, tying up capital.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that certification evidence applies to a different IBC model or configuration
rows.plain: Make sure the safety and approval documents are for the exact large container model and setup being considered. Documents for a different version may not prove that this one is acceptable.
rows.imp_rat: Certification evidence applying to a different IBC model is a documentation-matching error; per the batch-4 ceiling it ranks below all prior items, so both relevance and severity are held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Document-control practice usually links certificates to models, though evidence occasionally applies to a different configuration and is not always caught. Matching certification evidence to the exact IBC model and configuration is a quick documentary check. Confirming model/configuration correspondence requires careful attention but limited specialized skill. Mismatched certification evidence is cheap to catch but costly if it invalidates acceptance later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of certifications that expire before the planned service period
rows.plain: Make sure required approvals remain valid for the entire planned period of use. An approval that expires too soon could stop the work or require an early renewal.
rows.imp_rat: Certifications expiring before the planned service period undermine reliability, yet the batch-5 cap forces relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Asset systems with expiry alerts reliably flag lapses, though occasional untracked certs still expire prematurely. Certification tracking is automated, giving advance notice well before service periods begin. Aligning certification validity with planned service windows requires basic compliance-tracking competence. An expired certification pulling a unit from service wastes capital, though tracking prevents most cases.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies among certification markings, test evidence, and approval documents
rows.plain: Make sure the certification labels, testing proof, and approval papers all tell the same story. This helps show that the equipment meets the required rules.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies among certification markings and approval documents are a paperwork-consistency error; the ranking places it below prior batches, so both scores stay at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Today's practice relies on paper or PDF packets manually compared against physical markings, so contradictions routinely slip through and surface only later as deviations. Cross-checking certification markings against test evidence and approval documents is a slow manual reconciliation that inflates receipt-verification cycle time. Spotting genuine discrepancies among certificates, test reports, and approvals demands trained quality-assurance judgement that is scarce and unevenly applied at acquisition. Undetected documentation conflicts trigger costly rework, holds, or rejected lots but the checking itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of quotations based on inconsistent stainless steel IBC specifications or scope assumptions
rows.plain: Base fewer price offers on conflicting descriptions or assumptions about the stainless-steel container and its cover. This makes the prices easier to compare fairly.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent stainless steel IBC specs in quotations create quoting ambiguity, but ranked lower here; relevance and severity capped at 1 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Quotations are frequently built on divergent specs and scope, undermining like-for-like comparison. Normalizing quotes onto a consistent spec basis before comparison is slow manual effort. Detecting that quotes rest on differing specs or scope needs experienced review. Inconsistent quote bases lead to selecting the wrong-priced option.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between a tank shipment milestone and its status update
rows.imp_rat: Delay between a shipment milestone and its status update is a logistics-visibility issue peripheral to storage, and the ceiling keeps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Some carriers offer tracking portals, but for oversized tank freight status flows are inconsistent and often delayed. Shipment status updates often lag milestones because they rely on manual carrier communication. Logging and relaying shipment status requires minimal skill. Slow status updates add expediting and idle-crew costs but are not individually large.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of tank warranty registration missing the activation deadline
rows.imp_rat: Missing a warranty registration activation deadline is administrative and peripheral to the containment job; both the low stakes and the ceiling keep proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: No systematic reminder is standard, so warranty activation depends on individual diligence and is missed with some regularity. Warranty registration is a brief task easily deferred and forgotten past its deadline. Registering a warranty requires almost no specialized skill. A missed activation deadline can void coverage and expose the buyer to full repair costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of tank lifecycle costs omitted from the estimate
rows.imp_rat: Omitted lifecycle costs from an estimate is purely financial-planning administration, essentially peripheral to storing contents; lowest relevance and severity in the batch.
rows.sat_rat: TCO frameworks exist but are inconsistently used, so acquisition decisions frequently rely on incomplete cost pictures. Building a full lifecycle cost estimate is a slow analytical exercise often skipped under time pressure. Total-cost-of-ownership modeling for tanks requires specialized financial and engineering skill rarely applied. Omitted lifecycle costs like maintenance, cleaning and decommissioning routinely blow later budgets.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial or particulate contamination entering the material during filling
rows.imp_rat: Microbial or particulate contamination during filling directly degrades stored material quality, yet the mandatory ceiling forces relevance and severity to remain at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Sanitary design standards and validation exist but residual contamination risk during fill remains a persistent, hard-to-eliminate concern. Specifying and validating contamination controls extends the acquisition timeline for sterile tanks. Preventing microbial and particulate ingress during filling demands deep aseptic-process and CIP/SIP expertise. A contamination event destroys product batches at enormous cost, so controls are worth heavy investment.

rows.source_job: Collect and file tank documentation package
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required tank certifications, drawings, or manuals missing from the filed package
rows.imp_rat: Missing certifications, drawings, or manuals from the filed package is a documentation-completeness defect of limited functional stakes, capped at 1 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Document control systems retain most records, yet legacy drawings, vendor certs, and revised manuals routinely go missing from filed packages. Assembling the certification and drawing package is a clerical task done at project close-out without major delay. Compiling documents needs organizational diligence rather than technical skill. Document management costs little beyond administrative time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed tank service activities omitted from maintenance history
rows.imp_rat: Completed service activities omitted from maintenance history is a record-keeping defect with minor stakes for the core job, held at the floor by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS work-order closeout captures most activities, but manual and field-completed tasks are still frequently omitted from the record. Logging completed activities takes little time when done at task close-out. Recording history needs discipline rather than expertise, so skill is not the barrier. Record-keeping via CMMS is a low-cost administrative activity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of preventive service being scheduled after a compliance deadline
rows.imp_rat: Preventive service scheduled after a compliance deadline touches regulatory standing, but per the batch ceiling this error ranks lower than all prior items, so both proxies are held at the floor.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS and compliance calendars make on-time preventive service highly dependable, so missed deadlines are now the exception rather than the rule. The whole need is about not slipping past a scheduled deadline, and CMMS scheduling directly addresses timely execution. Little specialized skill is required; it depends on planners and reliable reminders more than expertise. Scheduling software is a low, already-absorbed overhead cost.

rows.source_job: Verify tank labeling and identification markings
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a tank marking does not match the assigned tank identity
rows.imp_rat: A tank marking mismatched to identity is a traceability defect with modest consequences, and the batch ceiling holds both proxies at the minimum.
rows.sat_rat: Manual cross-checks work most of the time, but transposed IDs, faded stencils, and unrecorded re-designations still create occasional mismatches. Checking or re-stenciling a tank marking is a trivially quick task. Verifying marking against identity requires only basic attention, not specialized skill. Marking materials and labor are negligible in cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of retained tank operating records omitted from the export
rows.imp_rat: Operating records omitted from an export is a data-completeness defect with limited functional consequence, and the mandatory ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Records handover at disposal is typically informal, so portions of retained operating history are routinely left out of the export. Exporting retained operating records is a low-effort transfer once the records are located. The task needs administrative rather than technical skill, mostly knowing which records must be retained. Omitted operating records cost little at export but create costly gaps for future owners or regulators.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of damage to serviceable tank components during removal
rows.imp_rat: Damage to serviceable components during removal is a servicing waste concern with modest consequence, and the batch ceiling holds relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: When salvage is planned there are standard dismantling practices, but disposal usually prioritizes speed so serviceable parts are frequently damaged during removal. Careful removal of serviceable components takes longer than cutting a tank straight to scrap. Salvaging valves, fittings and instruments intact requires skilled riggers and dismantlers who understand component value. Damaged serviceable components are lost salvage value, but careful removal labor also costs money, creating a trade-off.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of tank material assigned to incompatible recycling streams
rows.imp_rat: Assigning tank material to incompatible recycling streams is an end-of-life waste concern peripheral to the storage job; low relevance and the ceiling both keep proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Established scrap-metal grading and coating-removal practices handle common tank materials well, though lined, clad or contaminated sections are sometimes misrouted. Sorting materials into recycling streams adds sorting time but is a familiar scrap-yard workflow. Distinguishing alloys, coatings and lined materials needs some material-identification knowledge that scrap handlers sometimes lack. Misassigned material lowers scrap recovery value and can incur contamination penalties.

rows.source_job: Assess residual value of retired tank
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of internal corrosion or structural degradation remaining undetected during valuation
rows.imp_rat: Undetected internal corrosion or structural degradation is fairly central to a tank's containment job and can cause catastrophic failure, but the batch ceiling caps importance at 1.0, so relevance and severity are held minimal.
rows.sat_rat: UT, visual and NDT methods exist but coverage of internal and hidden surfaces at disposal is often partial, so degradation regularly escapes detection during valuation. Inspecting for internal corrosion during valuation adds inspection time but is a bounded activity. Detecting hidden corrosion and structural degradation depends heavily on skilled NDT inspectors and interpretation, which is the binding constraint. Missed degradation leads to overvaluation and salvage disputes, and thorough inspection itself carries measurable cost.

rows.source_job: Learn container preparation and handling procedures
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that operator learning gaps remain undetected before container preparation begins
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that people’s missing knowledge or skills go unnoticed before they start preparing and handling containers. This helps prevent mistakes and unsafe work.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected operator learning gaps before preparation are only indirectly tied to the container's core function (low relevance) and the immediate consequences are contained by downstream checks (low severity). Held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Learning gaps commonly remain undetected because sign-off is often attendance-based rather than proficiency-verified. Competency assessment before preparation adds scheduling time that pressured operations frequently compress. Surfacing hidden learning gaps requires skilled assessors and structured testing that most sites apply superficially. Undetected gaps cause downstream deviations whose cost is diffuse and rarely fed back to training budgets.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required current content and stainless steel IBC specifications unavailable during procedure development
rows.plain: Have all required information about the material being filled and the large steel container available when designing the filling steps, so the procedure fits the actual materials and container.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable content and stainless steel IBC specifications during procedure development is an informational gap peripheral to core function (low relevance), bounded in consequence by the ceiling (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Specification availability during development is inconsistent, with outdated or missing documents a chronic complaint. Hunting for current content and stainless IBC specifications during procedure development wastes time. Locating and validating the right specs requires familiarity with document systems and standards. Missing specs cause rework of procedures, an indirect cost.

rows.source_job: Set fill-level indicator or measurement reference
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the technician selects an incorrect reference specification when container records conflict
rows.plain: Make sure the technician uses the correct written measurement requirement when container records disagree. This helps ensure the fill level is set accurately.
rows.imp_rat: Selecting an incorrect reference specification when records conflict is a data-ambiguity error tangential to the CFJ (low relevance), with consequences held low per the batch ceiling (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting records occur regularly and there is little systemic aid, so correctness hinges on scarce expert judgement. Deciding between conflicting reference specs adds deliberation time. Selecting the correct reference when records conflict rests almost entirely on the technician's expertise and judgement. A wrong selection propagates errors that are costly to unwind.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of apparent fill-level fluctuations that do not correspond to net content changes
rows.plain: Make sure the reported fill level does not appear to change when the actual amount inside has stayed the same. This helps people trust the reading and avoid unnecessary adjustments.
rows.imp_rat: Apparent fill-level fluctuations that don't reflect real changes are a minor measurement artifact — peripheral relevance and trivial severity.
rows.sat_rat: Level indication on IBCs is easily fooled by agitation, temperature, and geometry, so spurious fluctuations are a common and poorly mitigated nuisance. Chasing apparent level swings that aren't real content changes wastes verification time. Recognizing that sloshing, foaming, or sensor drift is causing false readings takes interpretive experience. False fluctuations rarely cost material directly but trigger unnecessary investigation effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time a loaded IBC waits for an available stacking location
rows.plain: A filled large container should spend less time waiting for an open place where it can be stacked. This keeps containers moving and avoids delays in storage.
rows.imp_rat: Wait time for an available stacking location is a throughput/logistics delay peripheral to the container's function; capped at 1 on both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Stacking availability is fairly predictable in a managed warehouse, so the main issue is queueing time rather than uncertainty. Loaded IBCs frequently wait for a free stacking bay when warehouse space is tight, directly delaying flow. Deciding where to stage a full IBC needs little skill, just knowledge of the layout. Waiting IBCs tie up floor space and forklift labor, an indirect but real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time filling is delayed by unavailable material, connections, or release authorization
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to fill a container because the material, connections, or permission to release it is not ready. This helps the filling work begin and finish sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Filling delayed by unavailable material or authorization is a scheduling bottleneck peripheral to the CFJ; both relevance and severity held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduling and ERP systems help sequence fills, but material, connection and sign-off bottlenecks still routinely stall the start of filling. Filling is often held up waiting for material availability, a free connection, or release authorization, causing direct idle time. Coordinating readiness requires scheduling awareness but no specialized skill. Delayed filling idles equipment and operators, a recurring efficiency cost.

rows.source_job: Label container with contents and status
rows.stmt: Reduce the time the container retains an obsolete fill date or operational status after a change
rows.plain: Update the container’s date and status promptly after a change, so its label does not give outdated information.
rows.imp_rat: Retaining an obsolete fill date after a change is a labeling-currency lapse; the ceiling constrains both relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Manual, paper or ad hoc label updates lag behind actual changes, so containers routinely carry obsolete fill dates or operational status. Updating a fill date or status label is quick when done, but the update itself is frequently deferred or forgotten. Changing a status tag needs no skill, only the discipline to remember it during busy operations. Stale status data causes misuse of expired or wrongly-classified contents, an indirect cost, while the update is nearly free.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between a fill or dispensing event and completion of its record
rows.plain: Shorten the time between filling or dispensing something and finishing its record. This helps keep usage and production records accurate and up to date.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between a fill/dispensing event and record completion is administrative, marginal to the containment job with trivial consequences, so both proxies are low.
rows.sat_rat: Manual record-keeping introduces delays and transcription gaps between the event and its documented completion. Completing the record after a fill or dispense event often lags because it depends on manual logging or batch-record entry. Accurate record completion needs a trained operator familiar with documentation requirements. Automated data capture reduces the lag but the systems to do so are a notable investment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of transport departure delay caused by required attachment points being unusable at loading
rows.plain: Shorten the delay before transport can leave when the points used to secure the container cannot be used during loading. This helps the container be loaded and secured sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Transport departure delay from unusable attachment points is an administrative/logistics timing issue with minimal severity and low job centrality.
rows.sat_rat: Standard IBC designs have redundant lift points and pre-load inspection, so total unusability is relatively infrequent. When forklift pockets or lifting lugs are damaged or blocked at loading, departure stalls until an alternate handling method is arranged, adding meaningful delay. Rigging around unusable attachment points requires some handler experience but no scarce expertise. Delays tie up trucks and dock labor, a modest but real recurring cost.

rows.source_job: Position container at point of use
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of positioning delay caused by an obstructed access route or point-of-use location
rows.plain: Move the container to its working location without delays caused by blocked paths or blocked access. This helps the task begin sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Positioning delay from an obstructed access route is a logistics inconvenience, largely peripheral with trivial consequence to containment.
rows.sat_rat: Layout and pre-move checks reduce but do not eliminate blocked routes, so the delay recurs unpredictably. Positioning delays from obstructed routes are common and typically resolved by manual rerouting or forklift repositioning, which eats real time on congested plant floors. Clearing and choosing an alternate access route relies on operator judgement and site knowledge rather than any specialized skill. The main cost is idle labor and equipment time during the delay, which is moderate but recurring.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of container components assigned to the wrong material category
rows.plain: Sort every part of a container into the correct material group. This helps the materials be handled or recycled properly.
rows.imp_rat: Components assigned to the wrong material category is a classification/records error tangential to the core job (low relevance) with low practical severity. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: IBC construction is fairly standardized, so with established teardown practice miscategorization is limited but still occurs with composite or mixed parts. Sorting components into material categories adds hands-on time proportional to disassembly effort. Correctly categorizing HDPE bottles, steel cages, and pallet materials requires basic material-ID training that most recyclers have. Miscategorized components lower recovered material value and can contaminate recycling streams.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recycling shipments rejected because required material or decontamination records are missing
rows.plain: Fewer recycling shipments should be turned away because required records about the materials or their cleaning are missing. Complete records show that the shipment can be safely accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Recycling shipments rejected for missing records is an end-of-life documentation issue distant from the core job; held at 1 on relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Record completeness is inconsistent across generators, so rejections remain a recurring occurrence despite standard manifest practice. Chasing missing material or decontamination records to resolve a rejected shipment adds significant turnaround delay. Assembling defensible decon records requires cleaning-validation knowledge that generators frequently lack for prior IBC contents. Rejected recycling shipments incur return freight, re-processing, and lost material value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of identification, hazard, shipment, and tracking markings that remain legible after processing
rows.plain: Make sure old identification, safety, shipping, and tracking marks are no longer readable after processing, so outdated information cannot cause confusion.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of legibility of identification/hazard/tracking markings after processing has traceability relevance, but the batch ceiling forces both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Stripping and grinding usually work, but faint ghost markings sometimes remain legible after processing. Ensuring markings are obliterated adds a verification pass to processing. Knowing which markings must be defaced and confirming illegibility takes procedural attention. Residual legible markings risk misuse liability but little direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of stripping markings from an IBC not approved for transfer, disposal, or recycling
rows.plain: Avoid removing labels from a large storage container unless it has been approved for transfer, disposal, or recycling. The labels help ensure the correct container is handled in the approved way.
rows.imp_rat: Stripping markings from an IBC not approved for transfer/disposal/recycling is a labeling-control error marginal to the container's core job (low relevance), with severity held low by the batch ranking (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Practice depends on operator attention and clear labeling, so mistakes recur where identification is ambiguous. Verifying an IBC's approval status before stripping markings is a brief check that rarely slows the workflow. Correctly deciding whether markings may be removed requires knowing UN certification and transfer rules, and errors trace directly to operator judgement. Improperly stripping markings can render a still-usable container unsaleable, a modest but avoidable loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of adhesive, ink, or label debris introduced into the IBC or surrounding sterile area during removal
rows.plain: Prevent glue, ink, or label pieces from getting into the large container or nearby sterile area while old markings are removed. This helps protect the cleanliness of the area and its contents.
rows.imp_rat: Adhesive or label debris entering the IBC or sterile area during removal is a contamination-risk error, but under this batch's mandatory ceiling both proxies stay at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Adhesive and label debris routinely finds its way into the container or clean area during removal. Containing and cleaning adhesive or debris adds time to the removal step. Preventing debris from entering the IBC or sterile area demands disciplined technique and containment know-how. Contamination cleanup and rejected batches carry meaningful cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of damage to the stainless steel surface or finish during marking removal
rows.plain: Remove old labels or markings without harming the metal surface or its finish. This keeps the equipment looking and working as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Damage to stainless steel finish during marking removal is a cosmetic/durability defect with limited relevance to core containment and low severity; held at 1 under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Outcomes vary widely with operator technique, so finish damage during marking removal is inconsistent. Careful marking removal that spares the finish slows the operation versus aggressive stripping. Removing marks without scratching or etching stainless requires a skilled hand and the right solvents or media. Surface damage lowers scrap and reuse value of the stainless.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of retiring the record for the wrong intermediate bulk container
rows.plain: Avoid taking the wrong large storage container out of the records. This keeps the asset list accurate and prevents work from being done on the wrong container.
rows.imp_rat: Retiring the wrong container's record is a data-accuracy defect, yet ranked lower here; both proxies fixed at 1 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Serialized or barcoded IBCs make correct-record retirement fairly reliable, though worn or missing labels on end-of-life containers still cause occasional misidentification. Identifying the correct container to retire is quick when unique IDs or barcodes are present and legible. Matching a physical IBC to its record needs only basic scanning skill where labels survive. Retiring the wrong record wastes little material but can misstate inventory value and force corrective effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between physical disposal of the intermediate bulk container and its recorded retirement
rows.plain: There should be less time between physically discarding a large container and marking it as retired in the records. This keeps the records matched with what is actually onsite.
rows.imp_rat: The delay between physical disposal and recorded retirement of an IBC is a purely administrative record-keeping lag with low relevance to the core containment job and minor severity when it occurs; capped at 1.0 per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: The delay itself is the error and remains common where recording is not tied to the physical event. Recorded retirement frequently lags physical disposal by days or weeks because updates are batched or deferred. Prompt recording needs no special skill, just disciplined process. The lag causes continued costs on already-disposed assets.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of linked asset and inventory systems that still list the disposed intermediate bulk container as active
rows.plain: After the large container is thrown away, make sure every connected record system shows that it is no longer in use. This prevents people from treating a discarded container as available.
rows.imp_rat: Linked systems still listing a disposed IBC as active is a data-sync error, marginal to the container's function (low relevance) with correctable consequences (low severity); capped at 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Disconnected asset and inventory systems mean disposed IBCs commonly remain flagged active somewhere. Updating every linked system lags because status changes propagate manually across platforms. Knowing all downstream systems that list the asset requires cross-functional familiarity. Stale active listings cause phantom maintenance and inventory-carrying costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required disposition fields missing from the intermediate bulk container asset record
rows.plain: Fill in all required information about what happened to the large reusable container in its equipment record. This makes it clear whether the container is in use, stored, transferred, or disposed of.
rows.imp_rat: Missing disposition fields in the asset record is a data-completeness defect of modest impact; both proxies capped at 1 by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Manual end-of-life data entry into asset systems is notoriously incomplete for low-value bulk containers, so required disposition fields are routinely left blank. Filling disposition fields is quick when someone does it, but records languish incomplete for months because disposal is nobody's priority. Knowing which disposition fields matter requires asset-management familiarity that warehouse and disposal staff often lack. Missing fields cost little directly but drive downstream reconciliation and audit rework that consumes staff hours.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of incorrect disposition details entered in the intermediate bulk container asset record
rows.plain: Make sure the container’s asset record correctly states what happened to it, such as whether it was stored, moved, or discarded.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect disposition details in the asset record is a data-entry defect, tangential to the CFJ (low relevance) and correctable (low severity); the batch cap holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual entry of disposition details still yields recurring errors despite validation fields. Correct data entry is fast when done, but corrections after the fact take longer. Accurate disposition entry needs attentive clerical work more than specialized skill. Incorrect details cause downstream rework rather than direct material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required container history and transfer records that are missing or conflicting
rows.plain: Keep a complete and consistent record of the container’s past use and transfers, so its condition and history can be trusted before it is donated.
rows.imp_rat: Missing or conflicting history/transfer records is a documentation completeness error, tangential to the CFJ (low relevance) with low direct severity. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Fragmented ownership and handling histories mean a meaningful percentage of records are missing or contradictory, a chronic data-quality problem. Reconciling missing or conflicting history and transfer records is a slow investigative task. Resolving conflicting records demands careful cross-checking judgement across disparate systems. Poor records depress resale value and can force conservative disposal, wasting asset value.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the container is damaged during loading or transport to the recipient
rows.plain: Keep the container from being damaged while it is loaded and transported to the person receiving it, so it arrives usable.
rows.imp_rat: Damage during loading or transport to recipient is a defect concern, but it is held at 1.0 because this batch ranks below the prior one.
rows.sat_rat: Established palletizing and load-securing practice keeps damage uncommon, though rough handling still occasionally harms units. Proper loading and securing for transport to a recipient adds routine handling time only. Safe loading of IBCs is well-understood forklift and dunnage work needing standard training. Transit damage can total a reusable container, a cost that good handling largely prevents.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the container waits for donation approval and ownership-release documents
rows.plain: Get approval to donate the usable container and complete the ownership paperwork without delay.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting on donation approval and ownership-release documents is a slow-moving administrative delay held at 1 on both proxies by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Donation and ownership-release processes are ad hoc and infrequent, so their unpredictable duration is a recurring unresolved delay. Donation approval and ownership-release paperwork often takes weeks, leaving the container idle in the interim. Preparing release documents needs administrative and legal attention that is not always readily available. The waiting container consumes storage and delays freeing value, adding holding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of responsible stakeholders omitted from the intermediate bulk container retirement notification
rows.plain: Reduce the number of people or groups affected by retiring the large container who are left out of the notice. This helps everyone who needs to act or prepare receive the information.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted stakeholders in retirement notifications is a notification-completeness defect of low centrality; both proxies set to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Notification recipient lists are typically maintained informally, so responsible parties are regularly left off the retirement notice. Building the notification list is fast once a distribution template exists, but assembling it ad hoc delays it. Knowing every responsible stakeholder relies on institutional memory that is easily lost with staff turnover. Omitted stakeholders cause missed obligations and rework rather than direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that responsible stakeholders receive the intermediate bulk container retirement notification after retirement activities begin
rows.plain: Make sure the people responsible are told that the large storage container is being retired before the retirement work starts.
rows.imp_rat: Late delivery of retirement notification after activities begin is a timing issue of low centrality; both proxies fixed at 1 per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: The mismatch between physical disposal and record notification means stakeholders are often informed only after the fact. Notifications frequently lag behind physical retirement activity because disposal proceeds on operational schedules independent of paperwork. Sequencing notice before action is a coordination habit rather than a skill, but it is inconsistently practiced. Late notice causes surprise, rework and occasional wrongful disposal that carry real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting intermediate bulk container retirement details communicated to internal and external stakeholders
rows.plain: Make sure everyone receives the same information about when and how the large storage container will be retired. Consistent details help both internal staff and outside parties plan accordingly.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting retirement details to stakeholders create communication ambiguity but rank low here; both proxies held at 1 per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without a single source of truth, different stakeholders often receive divergent retirement details pulled from separate emails and spreadsheets. Reconciling retirement details before communicating takes time that rushed disposal processes rarely allow. Keeping stakeholder messages consistent requires coordination discipline more than technical skill. Conflicting details cause rework, disputes and duplicated handling that add avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that receipt of the intermediate bulk container retirement notification remains unconfirmed for a required stakeholder
rows.plain: Make sure every person who needs to know about retiring the large reusable container confirms that they received the notice. This matters so no one misses the change.
rows.imp_rat: Unconfirmed receipt of retirement notification is an acknowledgment-reliability gap of modest impact; both proxies held at 1 under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Email-based notifications rarely enforce read receipts, so whether a required stakeholder actually saw the notice is often genuinely unknown. Chasing confirmations manually stretches the retirement timeline unpredictably. Tracking who has acknowledged needs only persistence, not specialized expertise. Unconfirmed receipt causes duplicated follow-up effort and occasional disputed retirements.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the intermediate bulk container retirement notification is issued before required retirement authorizations are final
rows.plain: Do not tell affected people that the large reusable container is being retired until all required approvals are complete. This prevents an announcement based on an unfinished decision.
rows.imp_rat: Premature retirement notification before authorizations are final causes rework, yet under the batch-5 ceiling both relevance and severity must stay at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Notifications are sometimes issued in anticipation of approval rather than after it, so premature notices occur but are not the dominant failure. Waiting for authorizations to finalize before notifying adds delay that pressured disposal teams sometimes skip. Confirming authorization status requires knowing the approval workflow, which is not always transparent to notifiers. Premature notification can trigger reversals and wasted handling if authorization later fails.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of disabling the wrong IBC because condemnation identifiers are unclear
rows.plain: Make sure the correct large container is made unusable when it must be taken out of service. Clear identifying marks help prevent the wrong container from being disabled.
rows.imp_rat: Disabling the wrong IBC due to unclear condemnation identifiers is an identification-error risk marginal to the core job here (low relevance), with severity constrained by the ranking (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Unclear or faded condemnation identifiers make wrong-unit errors a genuine recurring risk without a robust marking standard. Confirming condemnation identifiers before disabling adds minor time when identifiers are legible. Reading and matching condemnation marks is low-skill but depends on consistent marking conventions being followed. Disabling the wrong IBC destroys a serviceable asset, a direct avoidable loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recovered product-contact components lacking verifiable identity or service history
rows.plain: Make sure parts that touched the product have a known identity and a checkable record of where they were used and serviced. This helps people decide whether the parts are safe to reuse.
rows.imp_rat: Recovered components lacking verifiable identity or service history is a traceability gap peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with modest severity here. Held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: At disposal, identity marks are often worn, painted over, or missing, so a large share of recovered components enter the stream with unverifiable history despite serialization efforts.
