Tracing identity or service history of recovered product-contact parts adds significant time when records are scattered across paper and disconnected systems. Verifying provenance demands experienced staff who know what markings and documentation to look for, and that expertise is scarce at disposal. Unverifiable parts are usually scrapped rather than reused, wasting recoverable value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of components with undetected wear or defects entering spare-parts inventory
rows.plain: Make sure worn or damaged parts are found before usable parts are put into storage. This prevents faulty parts from being used later.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected worn spare-part components entering inventory is relevant to IBC maintenance readiness and could cause a later failure, but the mandatory ceiling caps this below the higher-ranked batch, so relevance and severity stay at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Visual-only checks at disposal miss internal and incipient defects routinely, so worn components regularly slip into spare-parts stock. Thorough wear inspection lengthens processing, so under throughput pressure it is frequently abbreviated. Detecting subtle fatigue, corrosion, or micro-defects requires trained inspectors and often NDT that few disposal operations staff. Defective parts reaching inventory later cause failures and rework whose cost dwarfs the inspection saved.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of reusable components damaged during extraction from the retired container
rows.plain: Reduce the number of reusable parts damaged while they are removed from the retired container. This helps keep usable parts available for future use.
rows.imp_rat: Damage to reusable components during extraction from a retired container is a salvage-efficiency defect with low relevance to core containment; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Damage rates during extraction vary heavily with tooling and operator care. Gentle extraction to avoid damage is slower than forceful removal. Extracting reusable components intact from a retired container hinges on the technician's disassembly skill. Components damaged in extraction lose their reuse value entirely.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to extract reusable components from seized or inaccessible connections
rows.plain: Spend less time removing reusable parts from connections that are stuck or cannot be reached easily. Recovering these parts can avoid throwing away items that can still be used.
rows.imp_rat: Time to extract components from seized connections is a dismantling-efficiency delay of low centrality to containment; held at 1 under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Success is unpredictable, and seized connections regularly cause long, uncertain extraction efforts. Freeing seized or inaccessible connections can consume disproportionate labor time with heat, penetrants, and repeated attempts. Working seized joints loose without destroying parts takes considerable mechanical skill. Extended labor on stuck connections drives up disposal cost.

rows.stk: Waste Management Technician or Environmental Health and Safety Officer
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of IBC remnant loads rejected for failing receiving-facility acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Fewer loads of leftover material from large reusable containers should be turned away because the receiving facility will not accept them. This helps ensure the leftover material can be disposed of as planned.
rows.imp_rat: Remnant loads rejected at receiving is a rework/disposal acceptance issue distant from core function; capped at 1 on both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance criteria vary by facility and are not always communicated up front, so rejections recur even with experienced shippers. A remnant load bounced at receiving must be requeued and reprocessed, adding meaningful delay. Meeting receiving acceptance criteria requires knowing the facility's specific residue and cleanliness thresholds, which is uneven across senders. Rejected loads generate return transport and repeat-handling costs that are avoidable but common.

rows.stk: Waste Management Technician or Environmental Health and Safety Officer
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of disposal delays caused by incomplete waste characterization or transfer documentation
rows.plain: Complete the waste description and transfer paperwork before disposal. This prevents leftover material from being held up or rejected.
rows.imp_rat: Disposal delays from incomplete waste characterization are an end-of-life administrative concern peripheral to the container's core containment job; capped at 1 on both proxies by the ranking ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documentation gaps are common enough that hold-ups are a predictable recurring problem rather than a rare surprise. Incomplete waste characterization or transfer paperwork is a classic cause of loads sitting on hold, and reconstructing missing data routinely stretches disposal by days. Proper characterization demands hazmat and analytical knowledge that many disposal sites do not have in-house, forcing waits on outside expertise. Delays mean demurrage, storage, and repeat sampling charges that accumulate quickly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recurring contracts tied to the retired intermediate bulk container that remain active after decommissioning
rows.plain: Fewer ongoing service agreements connected to the retired large container should remain active after it has been taken out of service. This helps avoid paying for services that are no longer needed.
rows.imp_rat: Recurring contracts staying active after IBC decommissioning is a financial-tracking error, marginal to the container function (low relevance) with recoverable cost consequences (low severity); ranking limits it to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without integrated triggers, some recurring contracts predictably survive decommissioning unnoticed. Cancelling recurring contracts takes time and often lags decommissioning by billing cycles. Someone must know every contract tied to the unit, which demands institutional knowledge often lost. Recurring charges on decommissioned IBCs continue to bleed money until someone catches them.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an associated lease or service contract renewing after the intermediate bulk container is retired
rows.plain: Make sure related rental or service agreements are canceled when the large container is retired. Otherwise, the organization could keep paying for services it no longer needs.
rows.imp_rat: A lease renewing after IBC retirement is a financial-waste error only loosely tied to the container job (low relevance) with recoverable monetary consequences (low severity); ceiling limits it to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Weak linkage between asset-retirement events and contract systems means retired containers still get renewed with some regularity. Auto-renewals fire on schedule regardless of whether retirement was recorded in time. Preventing erroneous renewal requires someone who understands both the contract terms and the asset's retired status. A renewed lease on a retired IBC means paying for an asset no longer in service, a direct financial leak.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of terminating a contract for an active intermediate bulk container instead of the retired container
rows.plain: Avoid ending the contract for a container still in use instead of the one that was retired. This prevents service from being stopped for the wrong container.
rows.imp_rat: Terminating a contract for an active rather than retired IBC is an administrative mix-up, peripheral to the container's core job (low relevance) with reversible consequences (low severity); limited to 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Confirmation steps usually catch this, but identifier confusion occasionally leads to terminating the wrong contract. Verifying which container a contract covers before termination adds a brief check. Distinguishing the retired unit from an active sibling requires careful matching of identifiers. Terminating the wrong container's contract disrupts an active asset and incurs reinstatement costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of contract closure delay caused by disputed return, inspection, or payment obligations
rows.plain: Close related service agreements quickly when people disagree about what must be returned, checked, or paid. This helps prevent the cancellation from remaining unfinished.
rows.imp_rat: Contract closure delay from disputed return or payment obligations is administrative; the batch-5 ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Disputes over return condition and payment are a recurring, poorly-mitigated cause of stuck contract closures. Contract closure routinely drags when return, inspection or payment obligations are disputed at IBC end of life. Untangling disputed obligations demands contract and commercial expertise that is scarce at the disposal stage. Closure delays extend liability, tie up deposits and accrue avoidable holding and dispute costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of cancellation requests returned because required authorization is missing
rows.plain: Prevent cancellation requests from being sent back because the required permission is missing. This allows unwanted services to be cancelled without delay.
rows.imp_rat: Cancellation requests bounced for missing authorization create rework, but the ranking cap holds relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Missing-authorization rejections happen regularly but are usually caught and corrected through predictable resubmission. Returned cancellation requests force a full re-submission cycle that adds days to the process. Knowing which authorization is required upfront depends on process familiarity that is unevenly held. Rejected requests waste administrative effort but little material.

rows.stk: Compliance Administrator or Environmental Health and Safety Officer
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required disposal compliance documentation is not issued after disposal of the intermediate bulk container
rows.plain: Make sure the required proof that the container was disposed of properly is issued afterward. This matters because people may need it to show the disposal followed the rules.
rows.imp_rat: Failure to issue disposal compliance documentation is a downstream administrative record error largely peripheral to the container's core containment job (low relevance), and the ranking ceiling caps its consequences here (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Licensed disposal vendors routinely issue certificates as part of their service, making non-issuance uncommon though not impossible with informal handlers. Certificates of disposal or destruction are typically issued on a predictable cycle after processing. Producing compliance documentation is a clerical function backed by established templates requiring little specialized judgement. Failure to issue documentation is cheap to avoid but expensive if it surfaces in an audit, so most facilities invest in it.

rows.stk: Compliance Administrator or Environmental Health and Safety Officer
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required regulatory details missing from the intermediate bulk container disposal documentation
rows.plain: Make sure the paperwork for getting rid of the large container includes all legally required details. Complete records help show that it was disposed of properly.
rows.imp_rat: Missing regulatory details in disposal documentation is an end-of-life paperwork defect peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1 on both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Manifest systems and EHS review catch most omissions, yet edge-case waste codes and jurisdictional fields still slip through occasionally. Completing regulatory disposal fields is routine paperwork that adds modest time when templates are used. Knowing every required regulatory detail for a given IBC's prior contents still demands compliance expertise that smaller disposers may outsource. Missing details trigger fines or rework, but standardized manifest forms keep most costs contained.

rows.stk: Compliance Administrator or Environmental Health and Safety Officer
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency between the disposal documentation and the intermediate bulk container transfer and disposal records
rows.plain: Make sure the disposal paperwork matches the records showing how the large reusable containers were moved and disposed of. This helps prove that disposal rules were followed.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency between disposal docs and transfer/disposal records is a reconciliation issue of low centrality; both proxies held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: With disposal paperwork and asset transfer records kept in separate, unlinked systems, inconsistency between them is a persistent unresolved problem. Cross-checking disposal documents against transfer and disposal records is slow when done manually across disconnected systems. Reconciling two record sets requires an attentive person who understands both, a skill scarcely applied to container disposal. Inconsistencies surface late in audits and force expensive investigation and re-documentation.

rows.stk: Compliance Administrator or Environmental Health and Safety Officer
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay in receiving the intermediate bulk container disposal documentation after disposal
rows.plain: Receive the required proof that a large storage container was disposed of soon after disposal. This shows that the disposal was completed properly.
rows.imp_rat: Delayed disposal documentation is a downstream administrative lag with limited bearing on the core container job; both relevance and severity held at 1 under the ranking ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: The delay is a persistent friction dependent on external parties' turnaround. Disposal documentation from third-party recyclers or processors often arrives days to weeks after the event. Chasing documentation needs only administrative follow-up, not special skill. Delayed paperwork holds open records but incurs little direct cost.

rows.stk: Compliance Administrator or Environmental Health and Safety Officer
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that previously issued intermediate bulk container disposal documentation cannot be produced during an audit
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that records showing how previously used large containers were disposed of cannot be found during an inspection. This helps prove that disposal rules were followed.
rows.imp_rat: Inability to produce prior disposal documentation during an audit is a recordkeeping/audit-readiness error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance), with severity constrained by the batch ranking (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Digital manifest and EDMS retention has improved retrievability, yet inconsistent archiving practices still leave audit-time gaps for older disposals. Retrieving old documentation during an audit can be slow when records are paper or scattered across systems. Locating archived disposal records is a records-management task needing organization more than specialized skill. An inability to produce documents risks penalties, driving investment in retention but often on legacy filing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the processing-tank purchase request waits for required approver decisions
rows.imp_rat: A purchase request waiting on approver decisions is a procurement workflow delay with minor, correctable impact; lowest importance tier.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic approval workflows with routing and reminders are widely deployed and make cycle time fairly predictable, though bottlenecks at senior approvers persist. Approval waits are the core error here and remain a common source of delay despite workflow tools. Little specialized skill is needed; the delay is about approver availability and process routing. Approval delays chiefly cost schedule float rather than direct money, though they can push into price-escalation windows.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of quotations received from suppliers lacking required pharmaceutical quality documentation or manufacturing qualifications
rows.plain: Receive fewer price offers from suppliers that lack the required medicine-industry quality records or manufacturing qualifications. This helps ensure the chosen supplier can meet quality expectations.
rows.imp_rat: Quotations lacking pharmaceutical quality documentation is a supplier-vetting defect at the sourcing stage; capped at 1 on both proxies by the ranking ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Approved-supplier lists and prequalification help, but quotes lacking full pharma documentation still routinely enter the pipeline. Screening quotations for missing pharmaceutical quality documentation adds review time before suppliers can be shortlisted. Judging whether a quote's manufacturing qualifications meet pharma standards requires specialist supplier-qualification knowledge. Pursuing under-qualified quotes wastes sourcing effort and risks costly late disqualification.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of qualified IBC suppliers that fail to submit quotations by the budget-review deadline
rows.plain: Fewer approved suppliers of large product containers should fail to send prices by the budget-review deadline. This gives decision-makers the information they need on time.
rows.imp_rat: Qualified suppliers missing the quotation deadline is a process-timing error peripheral to the container function, capped at relevance 1 and severity 1.
rows.sat_rat: Even qualified suppliers routinely miss budget-review deadlines, so bid coverage is inconsistent despite reminders. Quote turnaround from qualified suppliers is bounded by RFQ deadlines but slippage still happens. Running an RFQ and chasing bids is standard procurement work needing modest skill. Missing quotes reduce competitive tension and can raise the final price paid.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of quotations that omit required delivery commitments, commercial terms, or landed-cost components
rows.plain: Receive fewer price offers that leave out required delivery promises, business terms, or parts of the total delivered cost. This helps prevent unexpected charges and delays.
rows.imp_rat: Quotations omitting delivery commitments or landed-cost components hinder comparison, yet the ceiling limits relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: RFQ templates request required elements, but supplier quotes still routinely omit delivery commitments or landed-cost lines. Chasing quotes for missing delivery or landed-cost components adds cycle time to sourcing. Knowing which landed-cost components a complete quotation must include requires procurement expertise. Omitted landed-cost elements lead to budget surprises later, a real cost-control gap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that quoted pricing or commercial terms change or expire before selection and budget approval
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that the quoted price or other sale terms change or end before a supplier is chosen and the budget is approved. This helps support a clear purchasing decision.
rows.imp_rat: Quoted pricing or terms expiring before approval creates instability in procurement, but the ranking ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Validity windows are stated on quotes, but slow budget approval routinely lets terms lapse before selection. Quotes carry validity dates, but approval cycles frequently outrun them, forcing re-quotes that add time. Managing quote validity against internal approval timelines needs an attentive buyer's coordination. Expired quotes mean losing negotiated pricing and re-pricing at higher rates, a direct cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that supplier references contain claims or identities that cannot be independently verified
rows.plain: Make sure supplier references and the people or organizations named in them are real and can be checked independently.
rows.imp_rat: Unverifiable supplier reference claims is a due-diligence ambiguity that is peripheral to the IBC's core job, so both proxies stay at 1 under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: There is no dependable, standardized way to confirm supplier reference authenticity, so unverifiable claims regularly slip through. Verifying reference claims and identities adds days of outreach with no fast standard channel. Independently verifying references demands investigative diligence and know-how many buyers lack. Verification effort is modest in cost but the risk of relying on false claims can be expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical supplier qualification concerns unsupported by references from comparable sterile fill-finish users
rows.plain: Make sure concerns about a supplier are backed by examples from similar customers that use the supplier for sterile product work. This helps the buyer judge whether the supplier is reliable.
rows.imp_rat: Missing supplier-qualification references from comparable sterile users is a peripheral procurement concern for the core job and, being below the prior batch, is capped at 1 for both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Comparable sterile fill-finish references are hard to obtain and rarely systematically captured, leaving qualification concerns often unsupported. Obtaining references from comparable sterile fill-finish users is a slow, relationship-dependent outreach effort. Judging and sourcing credible peer references for supplier qualification demands specialized industry network and evaluation expertise that is scarce. Proceeding on unsupported qualification concerns risks costly supplier failure but reference gathering itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of undisclosed commercial influence on recommendations about stainless steel IBC suppliers
rows.plain: Be more certain that supplier recommendations are not secretly shaped by business interests. This helps make supplier choices based on trustworthy information.
rows.imp_rat: Undisclosed commercial influence on recommendations is an integrity concern that is peripheral to the core job here and capped at relevance 1 and severity 1.
rows.sat_rat: Distributor and consultant recommendations rarely disclose commercial interest, so undisclosed influence commonly distorts source choices. Detecting hidden commercial influence is not a time issue but a diligence gap. Recognizing undisclosed bias in recommendations requires savvy that many buyers lack. Biased recommendations can steer spend toward costlier or inferior suppliers.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that references reflect outdated supplier or stainless steel IBC performance
rows.plain: Make sure the customer feedback reflects the supplier's current performance and the recent performance of its stainless-steel containers. This prevents decisions based on old information.
rows.imp_rat: Outdated reference performance data is a low-centrality reliability concern for sourcing, and the batch-5 cap keeps relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: References are often historical and rarely date-stamped, so outdated performance frequently informs decisions. Confirming that references reflect current performance requires extra time-consuming outreach. Judging whether a reference is stale relative to current stainless IBC performance takes domain experience. Stale references cost little to obtain but risk costly poor supplier selection.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for peer and reference contacts to provide input for source qualification
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for other people and past customers to share their opinions about a potential supplier. This helps the buyer decide sooner whether the supplier is suitable.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for peer and reference contacts to provide input is a minor qualification delay, held at relevance 1 and severity 1 under the batch-5 ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Reference input arrives unpredictably and incompletely, with no reliable mechanism to compel timely responses. Peer and reference contacts respond on their own schedule, often taking weeks and stalling source qualification. Knowing whom to ask and how to frame reference queries relies on the buyer's network and experience. The waiting costs little cash but delays decisions and consumes follow-up effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a delivered IBC specification mismatch remains undetected during receipt verification
rows.plain: Make sure the delivered large container matches the ordered size and design before the delivery is accepted. This helps catch the wrong item before it is put into use.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected spec mismatch at receipt is a verification defect, yet the batch ceiling forces relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Incoming inspection is a mature standard practice, so most spec mismatches are caught, though subtle ones still slip through. Receipt verification against specification is a defined step but adds inspection time on delivery. Comparing delivered specs to the PO requires moderate technical familiarity that receiving staff usually have. Undetected spec mismatches cause expensive downstream failures, though the check itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of delivered IBC units lacking complete documentation required by the purchase order
rows.plain: Make sure every delivered large container has all the documents required by the purchase order, so the delivery can be properly checked.
rows.imp_rat: Delivered units lacking complete PO documentation is a paperwork-completeness issue peripheral to containment (low relevance) and administratively fixable (low severity). Bounded by ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documentation checklists exist but per-unit completeness gaps still routinely appear at receipt. Verifying that each delivered unit carries complete PO-required documentation slows acceptance. Confirming documentation completeness requires knowing the full required set, a modest specialist knowledge. Incomplete documentation delays usability and ties up purchased assets.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of delivered IBC units with identifiers that conflict across IBC markings, delivery documents, and the purchase order
rows.plain: Make sure each delivered large container has the same identifying information on the container, delivery paperwork, and order. This helps confirm that the correct units were received.
rows.imp_rat: Identifier conflicts across markings, documents, and PO are a traceability-mismatch issue peripheral to containment (low relevance) and administratively correctable (low severity). Bounded by ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual three-way identifier matching is error-prone and conflicts recur across similar units. Cross-matching identifiers across markings, delivery docs, and PO adds handling time at receipt. Identifier reconciliation is a vigilance task needing accuracy more than expertise. Identifier conflicts cause misallocation and rework but are moderate in cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting an incorrect count of delivered IBC units against the purchase order
rows.plain: Make sure the number of large containers delivered matches the number ordered before accepting them. An incorrect count can leave the organization short or cause payment problems.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting an incorrect delivered unit count is a receiving defect; administrative in nature and capped at 1 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard goods-receipt counting practice reliably catches count discrepancies, so this is well served. Counting delivered units against the PO is quick and routine at receiving. Verifying counts requires minimal skill, making this among the least demanding acceptance tasks. Count errors are cheap to detect and correct at receipt before invoice reconciliation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of receipt verification delay caused by delivered IBC units being inaccessible for inspection
rows.plain: Make the delivered large liquid containers easy to reach and inspect as soon as they arrive. This helps workers confirm the order without unnecessary delay.
rows.imp_rat: Receipt verification delay from inaccessible units slows inspection, but ranking below the prior batch limits relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Established receiving workflows make units accessible in most cases, with occasional layout or handling issues causing verification delay. Staging and dock layout practices usually keep delivered IBC units accessible, but congested yards still delay inspection. Positioning units for efficient inspection access relies on warehouse handlers' routine competence rather than scarce skill. Inspection delays extend detention windows and labor idle time, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of received IBC units registered under an asset identifier that does not match the physical unit
rows.plain: Register each received large reusable container with the identification number that is actually printed or attached to that container, so records match the physical item.
rows.imp_rat: Asset identifier mismatch at receipt is a registration/traceability error tangential to containment (low relevance) and correctable (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Asset management systems and barcode/label practices help, so mismatches occur but are increasingly controlled. Confirming asset-identifier-to-physical-unit match adds registration time but is bounded. Asset registration matching requires care rather than scarce expertise. Mismatched asset records cause tracking errors that are moderately costly to correct.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of received IBC asset records missing required identification, specification, or acceptance attributes
rows.plain: Fewer records for received large portable containers should be missing required identification, description, or approval details. Complete records make it possible to know what each container is and whether it was accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Asset records missing required identification or acceptance attributes is a records-completeness error tangential to containment (low relevance) and recoverable (low severity). Held at 1.0 per ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Asset registration templates drive most fields, yet required attributes are still frequently left blank or wrong. Populating full asset records with identification, spec, and acceptance attributes adds data-entry time. Completing asset attributes correctly requires familiarity with the required record schema. Incomplete asset records create downstream tracking waste but modest direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that delivery and acceptance records contain conflicting information for an IBC unit
rows.plain: Make sure the delivery details and acceptance records agree about the large container being received.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting delivery/acceptance records for an IBC are a documentation reconciliation issue peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance), with low practical severity. Bounded by the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Separate delivery and acceptance documentation is often manually keyed, so conflicts arise and are inconsistently reconciled. Reconciling conflicting delivery and acceptance records for a unit adds investigation time. Resolving record conflicts requires attentive cross-checking rather than deep expertise. Conflicting records can delay payment and cause disputes but rarely destroy container value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of received IBC units represented by duplicate asset records
rows.plain: Fewer received containers should be listed more than once in the records. Each physical container should have one clear record.
rows.imp_rat: Duplicate asset records for received IBCs cause data hygiene rework, but the ceiling limits relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Asset-management systems with unique-ID scanning reduce duplicates well, though manual entries and re-receipts still spawn some. Barcode and RFID scanning at receipt makes asset registration fast, but reconciling duplicates later takes cleanup time. Correct asset-record creation depends on staff following naming and scanning conventions consistently. Duplicate records inflate inventory and maintenance-planning cost modestly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between delivery acceptance and completed asset registration for each received IBC unit
rows.plain: Register each received container as soon as its delivery is accepted. This makes sure every container is identified and tracked.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between acceptance and asset registration is an administrative recordkeeping lag of low centrality, with both proxies capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: ERP goods-receipt and fixed-asset workflows are mature, so registration is fairly consistent when procedures are followed. Asset registration after delivery acceptance is often batched, so a lag of days persists before units are fully recorded. Registering received IBCs into an asset system is a clerical task requiring little expertise. Delay ties up minor labor and can briefly obscure asset visibility, but little direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required IBC attributes omitted from the requirements definition
rows.plain: State every important requirement for the large reusable containers, so the right containers can be selected and supplied.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required IBC attributes from the requirements definition is an upstream specification gap of modest centrality here; both proxies capped at 1 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement templates exist but attributes are still routinely omitted from the definition. Building a complete requirements definition takes iterative effort but is bounded. Ensuring all required IBC attributes are captured relies on an experienced specifier. Omitted requirements drive costly change orders downstream.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required prior-use records missing from the container history
rows.plain: Keep a complete record of what the container was used for before, so people can judge whether it is suitable for its next use.
rows.imp_rat: Missing prior-use records in the container history is a recordkeeping-completeness defect loosely tied to the CFJ (low relevance), and the ceiling bounds its consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Container history records are commonly incomplete and poorly maintained, so gaps persist and are difficult to close. Locating missing prior-use records means prolonged supplier follow-up that delays container acceptance. Interpreting fragmentary container histories to judge suitability requires seasoned quality judgement. Absent history records often render containers unusable, wasting their purchase cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that service-history records are linked to the wrong container
rows.plain: Make sure records about past use and servicing are attached to the correct container.
rows.imp_rat: Service-history records linked to the wrong container is a data-association error tangential to the core job at this rank (low relevance), with severity capped by the batch ceiling (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Manual identifier matching is error-prone and mislinks occur, especially across fleets of similar-looking containers. Confirming that service records match the correct physical container adds identification-checking time at receipt. Detecting mislinked records requires attentive verification of identifiers against physical units, a vigilance-heavy task. Wrong-container record linkage can lead to using unsuitable units, risking expensive product loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of prior-content entries that lack a specific material identity
rows.plain: Identify exactly what each material was in the equipment before it was used again, rather than leaving its identity unclear.
rows.imp_rat: Prior-content entries lacking material identity is an ambiguity in records; peripheral to core containment and constrained to 1 by the ranking ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Prior-content entries are frequently generic or blank, so the specific identity needed for a fit-for-use decision is often missing. Chasing specific material identity for vague prior-content entries slows acceptance decisions. Judging contamination risk from generic prior-content descriptions requires chemical and cleaning-validation expertise. Unidentified prior contents often force rejection, wasting purchase value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved contradictions among cleaning, repair, and inspection records
rows.plain: Resolve contradictions among records of cleaning, repairs, and inspections. This helps establish an accurate history of how the equipment was used and cared for.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved contradictions among cleaning, repair, and inspection records is a record-consistency issue only loosely central to core function (low relevance), and severity is capped by the ranking (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: These records are typically siloed and manually kept, so contradictions are common and often left unresolved. Resolving contradictions among cleaning, repair, and inspection records requires time-consuming cross-referencing and clarification. Reconciling conflicting maintenance records demands experienced reviewers who can judge which record is authoritative. Unresolved contradictions can force conservative rejection, wasting container value.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time spent waiting for requested service-history records before the purchase decision
rows.plain: Receive records showing how the equipment or service was used and maintained as quickly as possible before deciding to buy. This helps the buyer make a better-informed choice.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for service-history records before purchase is a pre-decision delay of minor centrality, with both relevance and severity held at 1 per the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Availability and completeness of service records vary widely by supplier, so buyers often decide on partial data. Service-history records are frequently obtained only after manual requests and repeated follow-ups, stalling the purchase decision for days to weeks. Interpreting service history for stainless IBCs needs technical judgement that not every buyer has on hand. Waiting rarely costs money directly but delays commitment and can force rushed decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between the ordered IBC quantity and the approved project demand
rows.plain: Make sure the number of large reusable containers ordered matches the amount approved for the project.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between ordered IBC quantity and approved demand is a procurement-accuracy error, peripheral to the core job (low relevance) with adjustable consequences (low severity); ranking limits it to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Requisition-to-approval controls make quantity mismatch fairly well contained, with only occasional slips. Matching order quantity to approved demand is a quick reconciliation step. Comparing an order against an approved demand figure is basic clerical work. Over- or under-ordering wastes capital, but approvals usually cap this.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required quality and compliance documentation deliverables omitted from the purchase order
rows.plain: Make sure the order includes all required records about quality and meeting rules, so the buyer receives the promised documentation with the container.
rows.imp_rat: Compliance deliverables omitted from the purchase order is a procurement documentation defect; held at 1 on both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Standard PO templates with documentation clauses are common practice, so this is better controlled than field-level discrepancies but template gaps still recur. Correcting a purchase order that omitted required documentation deliverables forces a re-issue cycle that delays fulfillment. Drafting complete PO documentation clauses depends on quality and regulatory expertise being embedded in the buying process. Omitted deliverables can require re-procurement or costly retroactive documentation gathering.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the supplier cannot meet the delivery location and delivery date specified in the purchase order
rows.plain: Make sure the supplier can deliver the order to the right place on the date stated in the order. This helps prevent delays and delivery problems.
rows.imp_rat: Supplier missing delivery location/date is a delay issue peripheral to container function and constrained to 1 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Contractual delivery terms and supplier scheduling are standard, so misses are moderate and generally manageable. Delivery-date commitments are captured in the PO but on-time performance still causes schedule waiting when missed. Assessing supplier delivery capability requires some sourcing judgement but is a familiar procurement competency. Missed delivery can trigger expedited sourcing costs and downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount by which the purchase order total exceeds the approved budget
rows.plain: Keep the full order cost within the approved budget. This helps ensure the order can be placed without overspending.
rows.imp_rat: A purchase order exceeding approved budget is a financial waste concern, but the batch-5 ranking caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: ERP budget-gate approvals reliably block over-budget POs, though manual overrides and off-system buys still cause occasional overruns. Budget checks at PO creation are near-instant in the ERP. Comparing PO totals to approved budgets is a routine, low-skill system check. Budget-control workflows directly prevent most PO totals from exceeding approved amounts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration that the ordered IBC shipment location and delivery status remain unavailable after dispatch
rows.plain: Keep the time without the location and delivery update for the ordered large shipping container as short as possible after it leaves the sender. This helps the team know where it is and when to expect it.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable location and delivery status after dispatch is a tracking-reliability gap that is not job-critical here; the ceiling holds both relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: First-mile visibility is inconsistent, so location and delivery status can stay dark for a day or more depending on carrier. Most carriers post a tracking link at dispatch, but a real gap exists before the first location scan appears for many IBC freight movements. Accessing dispatch confirmation and tracking is a routine buyer task requiring no special skill. Blind windows after dispatch cost mainly follow-up labor rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of the lag between actual IBC shipment movement and reported status
rows.plain: Keep the delay between the container shipment actually moving and its reported status as short as possible. This gives people a more accurate view of where the shipment is.
rows.imp_rat: Lag between actual IBC movement and reported status is a visibility delay of low centrality to the core job, and the batch-5 ranking forces both relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Status updates are generally consistent on major lanes but degrade with regional carriers and manual milestone entry. Modern carrier scans and telematics report movement within hours, but end-to-end status lag still occurs at handoffs and cross-border legs. Reading status feeds requires little expertise once a portal or track-and-trace tool is in place. The lag itself costs little directly, mostly chasing effort by buyers.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an IBC shipment delivery exception remains unreported until it threatens the receiving schedule
rows.plain: Make sure any delivery problem with the large container shipment is reported quickly. Early notice gives the receiving site time to protect its schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Unreported delivery exceptions threatening the receiving schedule matter operationally, but the ranking caps relevance and severity at 1 for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Automated visibility platforms catch routine delays reliably, yet silent failures and non-integrated carriers leave recurring gaps where exceptions surface too late. Carrier tracking and EDI status feeds surface many delays automatically, but exception escalation to the receiving team still lags on off-hours and edge cases. Interpreting a raw tracking event as a schedule-threatening exception still relies on an experienced logistics coordinator's judgement. Unreported exceptions carry real downstream cost from idled receiving crews, but the monitoring tooling itself is cheap and widely available.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting delivery updates from the carrier and supplier for the ordered IBC shipment
rows.plain: Make sure the delivery company and supplier give matching updates about the ordered large container shipment. This helps staff know where the shipment is and when to expect it.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting delivery updates from carrier and supplier is an administrative coordination annoyance rather than central to the IBC's core job, and the ceiling caps both relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Carrier and supplier systems are rarely integrated, so conflicting delivery updates remain a frequent, poorly-resolved problem. Reconciling conflicting carrier and supplier updates consumes coordinator time chasing the true status. Judging which conflicting update to trust requires experienced logistics interpretation. Acting on wrong delivery info causes wasted crew scheduling and expediting costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of tracking references that do not correspond to the ordered IBC shipment
rows.plain: Avoid using tracking numbers or references that belong to a different shipment. This helps workers follow the correct large container shipment.
rows.imp_rat: Tracking references that do not correspond to the ordered shipment is a logistics data defect with limited centrality to the container's function; both proxies are held at 1 under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mature carrier tracking and EDI/ERP order linkage make wrong-shipment references uncommon, though manual entry and split loads still cause occasional mismatches. Tracking references are typically generated instantly at booking, so time-to-availability is not the pain here. Matching a tracking number to an order is a low-skill clerical check that carriers and ERP integrations largely automate. Occasional mismatched references cost some clarification calls but rarely material money on IBC shipments.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to receive all mandatory documentation for the cross-border IBC shipment
rows.plain: Receive all required documents for sending or receiving the large container across a national border more quickly. This helps prevent the shipment from being delayed.
rows.imp_rat: Delay in receiving mandatory cross-border documentation affects timing, but the batch cap requires relevance and severity of 1.
rows.sat_rat: Standard checklists ensure the right documents are eventually collected, but timeliness varies with third-party responsiveness. Gathering all mandatory documents from suppliers, testing bodies, and authorities is a serial process that routinely runs long. Chasing and validating each document requires an experienced coordinator who knows who issues what. Delayed documentation ties up shipments and working capital, an indirect but real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of IBC shipment documentation rejected for missing, inaccurate, or inconsistent entries
rows.plain: Have fewer shipping forms for large reusable containers rejected because information is missing, wrong, or does not match across the forms. This helps shipments pass review without delays.
rows.imp_rat: Rejected shipment documentation from bad entries can slow clearance, yet ranked below prior items; relevance and severity capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized forms and validation checks catch many errors, yet missing or inconsistent entries still slip through often enough to be a routine problem. Templated documentation and shipping software speed preparation, but rework on rejected entries still adds delay. Accurate completion of shipment paperwork demands trained trade-compliance staff who know each field's requirements. Documentation rejections cause demurrage and rework labor costs that recur across shipments.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty about the documentation required by origin, transit, and destination jurisdictions
rows.plain: Find out exactly which papers are needed in the country where the goods start, the countries they pass through, and the country where they arrive.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about jurisdictional documentation requirements matters for compliant shipping, but ranking below the prior batch forces relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Broker guidance reduces uncertainty but shifting regulations and inconsistent guidance leave real recurring ambiguity. Researching jurisdiction-specific requirements takes considerable lead time before each new cross-border lane. Knowing exactly what origin, transit, and destination jurisdictions require is deep specialist knowledge concentrated in brokers and compliance experts. Customs brokers and consultants are engaged to close this gap, an ongoing cost that only partly removes uncertainty.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that approved IBC shipment documentation is unavailable to the responsible filing party at the required submission point
rows.plain: Make sure the approved paperwork for the large container shipment reaches the person who must submit it on time. This prevents the shipment from being held up by missing documents.
rows.imp_rat: Missing approved shipment documentation at the filing point can stall a cross-border IBC move, but the batch ceiling caps this below the higher-ranked items, so relevance and severity are held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Document management systems make approved files widely accessible, yet version-control errors and access gaps still occasionally leave the filer without the right document. Shared document repositories and e-filing make approved paperwork retrievable quickly at the submission point. Ensuring the right approved version reaches the filer still depends on disciplined document control by staff. Missing documents at filing cause clearance delays and storage costs, though cloud repositories have reduced this.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an IBC shipment certificate or declaration becomes invalid before border clearance
rows.plain: Make sure the certificate or declaration for the large container shipment is still valid when the shipment reaches the border. This prevents customs clearance from being delayed.
rows.imp_rat: An invalid shipment certificate before border clearance could halt movement, but the ranking caps relevance and severity at 1 for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Expiry-date tracking tools help, but the timing of border clearance versus certificate validity remains unpredictable enough that lapses still occur. Certificate validity windows are tracked, but expedited re-issuance when documents lapse before clearance still costs days. Judging which certificates expire and when relative to transit time requires specialized regulatory knowledge. An invalid declaration at the border triggers costly holds, storage, and re-certification fees.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the technician waits for the required compatible valve, closure cap, or seals
rows.plain: The technician should quickly receive the correct valve, cap, or sealing parts needed for the job. This prevents the fitting work from stopping while parts are located.
rows.imp_rat: Technician waiting for a compatible valve or seal is a parts-availability delay peripheral to the job; both proxies held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Stockouts and mismatched parts are common enough that waiting is a routine experience, only partly buffered by kitting. Waiting for the correct compatible valve, cap or seals is a frequent hold that stalls preparation. The delay is a supply/logistics issue rather than a skill gap. Held-up labor and expedited parts orders add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the supporting surface load rating is unavailable or unclear before placement
rows.plain: Make sure it is clear how much weight the supporting surface can safely hold before placing the system on it. This helps prevent the surface or equipment from failing.
rows.imp_rat: An unclear supporting-surface load rating before placement is a staging-information gap; the batch-4 ceiling forces both proxies to 1 despite any stacking-safety implication.
rows.sat_rat: Load ratings are often poorly documented at point of use, so availability is inconsistent and gaps recur. Chasing an unclear surface load rating before placement delays setup. Interpreting or locating load-rating data requires some engineering literacy the technician may lack. An overloaded surface risks failure and damage, but the check itself is cheap when data exists.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree the container remains out of level after positioning
rows.plain: Make sure the container is level after it is placed, so it sits properly and is less likely to shift or work incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: A container remaining out of level after positioning is a minor placement error (low relevance) with modest consequences (low severity); the batch cap holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Level pads, adjustable feet and spirit-level checks handle this well in most installations, with residual issues on uneven floors. Leveling a positioned container with shims or adjustable feet adds a short setup step. Achieving level uses a simple bubble level and basic adjustment, requiring little specialized skill. Out-of-level containers risk incomplete discharge and spill loss, a modest material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the technician waits for approved pressure limits or acceptance criteria at test start
rows.plain: The technician should have the allowed pressure range or pass-and-fail rules before testing begins. This makes it clear how to carry out the test and judge the result.
rows.imp_rat: Technician waiting for approved pressure limits at test start is a test-readiness delay; both proxies capped at 1 by the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Missing criteria can also push testing to proceed on assumptions, but the primary felt loss is delay. Technicians routinely wait for approved pressure limits or acceptance criteria at test start, a common bottleneck. The wait reflects approval-chain dependencies rather than technician skill. Idle technician and equipment time during the wait is a direct cost drain.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict between training guidance and approved sterile fill-finish procedures for stainless steel IBCs
rows.plain: Make sure the training instructions match the approved steps for safely handling large stainless-steel containers used with sterile products. This helps staff work safely and consistently.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict between training guidance and approved sterile procedures is a documentation-alignment issue peripheral to the container job (low relevance) with low direct severity here. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Training materials frequently drift out of sync with current approved procedures because change control does not reliably propagate to training content. Reconciling training guidance against approved sterile fill-finish SOPs takes document-control effort that lags procedure updates. Spotting conflicts requires people fluent in both training material and validated aseptic procedures, a scarce overlap. Conflicts drive deviations and revalidation costs that are significant but attributed elsewhere.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of trainees who complete training with undetected errors in filling, handling, stacking, or discharging stainless steel IBCs
rows.plain: Make sure fewer trainees finish their instruction while still making unnoticed mistakes when filling, moving, stacking, or emptying the large steel containers. This helps prevent handling errors in real work.
rows.imp_rat: Trainees finishing with undetected handling errors is a training-quality gap only indirectly linked to container function (low relevance), with severity constrained here by the batch ordering. Held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: A meaningful fraction of trainees complete with undetected errors because assessment rarely stresses all four operations under realistic conditions. Thoroughly assessing filling, handling, stacking and discharging competence lengthens qualification, often shortcut for speed. Catching undetected trainee errors demands expert observation across multiple operations that few evaluators fully provide. Errors passing through training cause costly product loss and deviations later, indirectly borne downstream.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required personnel who miss scheduled stainless steel IBC training
rows.plain: Make sure all required staff complete their scheduled training on safely handling large stainless-steel containers. This helps prevent damage, mistakes, and injuries during handling.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel missing scheduled training is a scheduling/administrative delay peripheral to the container's function (low relevance) with low direct severity. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Learning management systems and matrices reasonably flag who missed scheduled training, though enforcement of completion before work varies. Scheduling and rescheduling missed sessions consumes coordinator time but is a routine LMS task. Tracking attendance requires little specialized skill once a training system is in place. Missed training carries low direct cost, mainly rescheduling overhead.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of the conditioned container surface affected by gaps or folds after attachment
rows.plain: Make sure the attached conditioning equipment does not leave gaps or folds across the container surface. This helps the equipment treat the surface evenly.
rows.imp_rat: Gaps or folds on the conditioned container surface after attachment is a cosmetic/prep defect with low centrality and minor consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Fitted accessories and templates limit gaps, but folds and imperfect coverage still recur with manual application. Smoothing the conditioned surface adds a little handling time. Achieving gap- and fold-free coverage depends on operator care and manual dexterity. Gaps or folds may require reapplication, wasting material and labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood the conditioning accessory shifts or detaches during subsequent container handling
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the attached equipment moves or comes loose while the container is handled later. This helps prevent damage or unsafe handling.
rows.imp_rat: A conditioning accessory shifting or detaching during handling is a minor accessory defect, peripheral to core containment with trivial consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Standard fasteners and straps hold accessories well, though rough handling still occasionally loosens them. Securing a conditioning accessory is a brief fastening step. Proper attachment relies on basic technique and does not need specialist skill. A detached accessory during handling can damage contents or require rehandling, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in the expected service-pressure behavior provided to the technician
rows.plain: Clearly state how much pressure the technician should expect while air or gas is released from a container. This helps the technician set up the equipment safely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in expected service-pressure behavior for the technician is a testing-instruction gap; capped at 1 on both proxies by the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure behavior is frequently under-specified or ambiguous in handed-over documents, leaving recurring uncertainty. Clarifying expected service-pressure behavior often means waiting for documentation, adding delay. Understanding pressure behavior demands engineering knowledge that must be supplied clearly to the technician. Ambiguity risks unsafe testing or rework, with modest direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent aligning the IBC and transfer-equipment endpoints before connection
rows.plain: Spend less time making sure the large container and the transfer equipment line up correctly before connecting them. This helps the connection happen smoothly and safely.
rows.imp_rat: Time spent aligning IBC and transfer-equipment endpoints is a minor efficiency delay (low relevance) with mild consequences (low severity); ranking constrains it to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Alignment quality varies with operator and fixturing, but standard guides and connectors keep it usually workable. Aligning IBC and transfer-equipment endpoints is a manual jockeying step that routinely consumes several minutes per connection. Getting endpoints to mate cleanly relies on operator feel and experience with the specific equipment. The time and occasional gasket damage from misalignment carry modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which the intended formulation and exposure conditions are incompletely specified
rows.plain: Make sure the planned mixture and the conditions it will be exposed to are described completely. This helps determine whether the materials that touch it will remain safe and suitable.
rows.imp_rat: Incompletely specified formulation and exposure conditions is a specification-clarity issue upstream of container function (low relevance), with consequences held low by the batch ranking (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Formulation/exposure details are often partially specified at handover, producing recurring ambiguity. Chasing incomplete formulation and exposure conditions delays procedure setup. Recognizing what specification is missing requires process knowledge the technician may not fully have. Proceeding on incomplete conditions risks wasted runs and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that applied content, hazard, or traceability information is incorrect for the container
rows.plain: Make sure the container has correct information about what it contains, its hazards, and how it can be traced. This helps people handle and identify it safely.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect content, hazard, or traceability information on the container is a labeling-accuracy defect, but ranked below the prior batch so it remains low relevance and low severity per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized labeling data flows and verification checks catch most errors, though transcription mistakes still slip through. Verifying applied content/hazard/traceability data adds a check step of modest duration. Correct application depends on operator attention and cross-checking against records. Incorrect traceability can trigger recalls or holds, a high-cost failure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required labels, placards, or markings missing before filling or dispatch
rows.plain: Make sure every needed label, warning sign, and marking is in place before something is filled or sent out, so people know what it contains and how to handle it safely.
rows.imp_rat: Missing labels or placards before dispatch is a marking defect; relevant to compliance but constrained to 1.0 by the ranking rule.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and dispatch controls make missing markings fairly uncommon, though occasional omissions still occur. Confirming all required labels and placards before dispatch is a quick checklist step. Completeness checking is routine and requires little specialized skill given standard checklists. Missing markings cause dispatch holds and returns, a contained cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of obsolete markings remaining and conflicting with current container information
rows.plain: Remove old labels so they do not conflict with the container’s current information. This helps people identify the container and its dangers correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Obsolete markings conflicting with current info is a labeling ambiguity; relevant to clarity but held at 1.0 under the ranking constraint.
rows.sat_rat: Residual obsolete markings conflicting with current status remain a common deviation source because manual label removal and verification are inconsistently enforced. Removing or reconciling old labels adds manual inspection time that crews often rush past between batches. Recognizing which markings are obsolete versus current depends on operator diligence and product knowledge that varies widely. Wasted material and rework from mislabeled containers are recurring but rarely tracked as a distinct cost line.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required markings become detached or illegible during handling, cleaning, or dispatch
rows.plain: Make sure the labels and warning signs stay attached and readable while the equipment is moved, cleaned, or sent away. This helps people identify it and understand possible dangers.
rows.imp_rat: Detached or illegible markings during handling is a labeling defect; while relevant to compliance it must stay at 1.0 under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Durable labels, plates and etching are mature and mostly survive handling and cleaning, with occasional failures on harsh cycles. Ensuring durable markings adds negligible time at application. Choosing robust marking methods requires some knowledge but is largely proceduralized. Detached or illegible markings cause rework and rejection, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contaminants to the container or controlled area while applying markings
rows.plain: Prevent dirt or other unwanted material from getting on the container or into the protected work area while labels are being added. This keeps the container and work area safe for use.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contaminants while applying markings has contamination risk, but the batch ranking caps relevance and severity at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established GMP marking practices reduce contamination risk substantially, though inadvertent particle or residue introduction still occurs during manual application. Cleanroom gowning, wipe-down and controlled-marking procedures add setup time but are budgeted into standard workflows. Avoiding contamination during marking demands trained aseptic technique and constant vigilance that not all operators sustain. Approved low-migration inks and dedicated marking supplies carry modest incremental cost within controlled areas.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of stainless steel IBC records containing an incorrect container identifier
rows.plain: Make sure each record for a stainless-steel storage container has the correct identifying number or name. This helps workers find and track the right container.
rows.imp_rat: An incorrect container identifier in stainless IBC records undermines traceability, but the ceiling for this lower-ranked batch keeps relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode or RFID scanning where deployed reduces wrong identifiers well, but manual sites still generate transcription errors. Manual identifier entry and verification add brief data-entry time at registration. Correctly recording an identifier is low-skill but transposition errors persist with manual keying. Incorrect identifiers cause traceability rework whose cost is occasional rather than chronic.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of stainless steel IBC records containing an incorrect tare weight
rows.plain: Make sure each record for a stainless-steel storage container has the correct weight of the empty container. This supports accurate weighing when material is added.
rows.imp_rat: An incorrect tare weight in IBC records can skew net-content calculations, a data error capped at 1 on both proxies because it ranks below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Tare weights are often recorded from nameplates or scales fairly reliably, but manual entry and stamped-value drift still introduce errors. Capturing tare weight requires a quick weigh step that is usually built into intake. Reading and recording a scale value needs little skill though wrong-container mix-ups happen. An incorrect tare skews net-content accounting, a moderate cost when it slips through.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of stainless steel IBC records containing an incorrect lifecycle status
rows.plain: Make sure each record for a stainless-steel storage container correctly shows whether it is available, in use, under repair, or in another condition. This helps people know what can be used.
rows.imp_rat: An incorrect lifecycle status in IBC records can misdirect use decisions, but this item ranks below the prior batch so both proxies stay at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Lifecycle status errors recur because status updates depend on timely manual entry that is inconsistently performed. Updating lifecycle status is a quick system action when done, but status changes are often deferred. Setting correct status requires understanding of container state, a modest but sometimes overlooked judgement. Incorrect status leads to misuse and idle assets, a moderate hidden cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of registration being blocked because required container attributes are unavailable or unreadable
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a container cannot be added to the inventory list because important details are missing or cannot be read. This helps the company track the container correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Registration blocked by unavailable or unreadable container attributes stalls onboarding, but the item sits lower than the previous batch so relevance and severity are held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Worn nameplates and incomplete attribute capture still block registration often enough that it remains a recurring interruption. Chasing missing or unreadable attributes to complete registration causes waiting that stalls preparation. Reading and interpreting container attributes needs modest skill but degraded nameplates require judgement. Blocked registrations tie up assets and labor with a modest but real carrying cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of duplicate inventory records created for stainless steel IBCs
rows.plain: Avoid creating more than one inventory entry for the same stainless-steel container. This keeps stock counts accurate and prevents confusion.
rows.imp_rat: Duplicate inventory records for stainless IBCs is a back-office data hygiene error, marginal to the container's core function (low relevance) with easily reconciled consequences (low severity); batch ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Modern inventory systems with unique-identifier constraints prevent most duplicates, though manual or multi-system setups still create some. Duplicate-record checks are near-instant when the system enforces unique keys at entry. Preventing duplicates needs little operator skill where the database enforces uniqueness. Duplicate records cause inventory reconciliation labor, a modest recurring cost.

rows.source_job: Learn container preparation and handling procedures
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required preparation steps omitted from learning
rows.plain: Learn every step needed to prepare and handle the container, so it can be used safely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Preparation steps omitted from learning is a training-coverage gap only loosely coupled to the container job (low relevance) with modest severity. Held at 1.0 per ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Step omissions in learning are inconsistently caught because checklists and curricula are not always cross-verified against the full procedure. Covering every required step in training extends session length that is often trimmed under production pressure. Ensuring no step is omitted from learning relies on trainer thoroughness and curriculum completeness that varies. Omitted steps produce rework and waste that is not directly costed against training.

rows.source_job: Learn container preparation and handling procedures
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the learned sequence and the required preparation sequence
rows.plain: Make the learned steps for preparing containers match the steps that are actually required. This helps the equipment handle containers correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between learned and required preparation sequence is a process defect held at 1.0 under the batch ranking constraint.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation between learned and required preparation sequence is only loosely controlled by SOP walkthroughs, so inconsistencies persist. Verifying that a trainee's learned sequence matches the required one takes supervisory time that is often abbreviated. Detecting sequence drift depends heavily on an experienced trainer's judgement rather than a codified check. Sequence errors surface later as scrap or reprocessing, an indirect cost seldom attributed to training.

rows.source_job: Learn container preparation and handling procedures
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of inconsistency among the container preparation instructions available to the operator
rows.plain: Make the container-preparation instructions as consistent as possible. This helps operators follow the right steps without confusion or conflicting guidance.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent container preparation instructions is an operator-guidance clarity issue peripheral to core containment (low relevance), with severity capped by the batch ordering (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Instruction sets from multiple sources frequently disagree, and version control gaps make inconsistency a recurring problem. Reconciling inconsistent preparation instructions costs the operator interpretation time. Resolving conflicting instructions demands experienced judgement about which to trust. Following the wrong instruction causes rework and scrap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of gaskets or seals left pinched, twisted, or unevenly seated after replacement
rows.plain: Make sure each new gasket or seal sits flat and correctly, without being pinched, twisted, or uneven.
rows.imp_rat: Pinched or unevenly seated gaskets after replacement matter to sealing, but this batch's ranking places the error low in relevance and severity here. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Correct seating is common but inconsistent, and improperly seated seals recur wherever assembly is manual and unverified. A pinched seal detected at test forces disassembly and reseating, adding rework time. Proper seating of gaskets without twisting or pinching is a hands-on manual-finesse task that depends directly on technician skill. A ruined gasket is cheap, but a bad seat that leaks in service is costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of intermediate bulk container downtime caused by unavailable replacement gaskets or seals
rows.plain: Spend less time with the liquid-storage container out of service because replacement seals are unavailable. This helps the container return to use sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Downtime from unavailable replacement gaskets or seals is a logistics/maintenance delay only tangential to the CFJ (low relevance), and per the batch ceiling its consequences are bounded (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Common elastomer seals are widely stocked so shortages are infrequent, though specialty pharma-grade gaskets occasionally cause delays. Downtime waiting on unavailable gaskets or seals is the direct error, and stocking common seals usually keeps it short. Sourcing and fitting standard seals requires little specialized skill. Keeping seal/gasket inventory is cheap relative to the container downtime avoided.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of replacement labels applied with incorrect identification or hazard information
rows.plain: Put the correct identifying and safety information on every replacement label. This helps people know what the container holds and how to handle it safely.
rows.imp_rat: Replacement labels with incorrect ID or hazard info can mislead handling, yet this item ranks lower than the prior batch and so both proxies are limited to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Label templates help, but incorrect identification or hazard content on replacement labels still slips through manual application. Correcting a mislabeled container is a quick reprint-and-reapply once noticed. Selecting the right hazard and ID data for a label requires regulatory knowledge and care. Label material is cheap; the exposure is compliance and misidentification risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of containers bearing obsolete labels that conflict with replacement labels after the job
rows.plain: Make sure old labels do not remain when new labels are put on containers. This prevents people from seeing conflicting information about what a container is or contains.
rows.imp_rat: Obsolete labels conflicting with replacements is a peripheral labeling housekeeping error (low relevance) with mostly minor confusion consequences (low severity). Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures call for removing superseded labels, but old markings are frequently left in place alongside new ones, creating conflicting identity. Stripping and replacing conflicting labels adds a small step but rarely delays release much. Removing obsolete labels is unskilled, though knowing which must go requires attention. Label material cost is trivial; the concern is misidentification, not spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of replacement labels that detach from stainless steel container surfaces during use
rows.plain: Keep replacement labels attached to stainless-steel containers while the containers are being used. Secure labels help people continue to identify the containers correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Replacement labels detaching from stainless steel surfaces is a labeling-reliability defect of limited centrality to containment and low severity; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Adhesion to stainless steel exposed to washing, chemicals and abrasion remains a persistent weak point, and detaching labels are a common recurring field complaint. Applying a replacement label is quick, so time is not the issue. Proper surface prep and adhesive selection require some know-how that is often skipped in practice. Labels are cheap but repeated re-labelling and lost identification add recurring waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of replacement labels positioned where required information is obscured
rows.plain: Place each replacement label so that all required information can be seen. This helps people read the label when they need to identify or handle the container.
rows.imp_rat: Replacement labels positioned to obscure required information reduces readability, a lower-priority error that the ranking caps at 1 for both relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Placement guidance exists but is inconsistently followed, so labels occasionally obscure required markings. Repositioning an obscuring label is a minor, quick fix. Correct placement needs basic awareness of required-info zones, not deep skill. Placement errors cost negligible material, only rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of attached instruments remaining outside specified tolerance after calibration
rows.plain: Fewer connected measuring devices should still give readings within the required range after they are adjusted. This helps ensure the equipment is working accurately.
rows.imp_rat: Attached instruments outside tolerance after calibration is a measurement-quality error that would otherwise rate higher, but the mandatory ceiling constrains both proxies to keep importance at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Mature calibration regimes with traceable standards and pass/fail limits keep most instruments within tolerance, though drift and occasional out-of-tolerance findings still occur between intervals. Instrument calibration follows scheduled intervals with established turnaround, so timing is rarely the bottleneck.
