Calibration is performed by trained metrology or instrument technicians using documented procedures, lowering reliance on ad hoc judgement. Calibration equipment and certified standards carry cost but are routine budgeted items.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of IBC unavailability to sterile fill-finish activities during calibration
rows.plain: Keep the large container unavailable for sterile production for as little time as possible while its attached measuring devices are checked and adjusted. This helps production resume sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailability to sterile fill-finish during calibration is a scheduling delay; the batch-4 ceiling forces both scores to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled calibration intervals make downtime predictable, but coordinating it with sterile-fill demand still causes avoidable idle time. Calibration windows remove the IBC from sterile fill-finish for scheduled periods that directly reduce availability. Calibration is performed by qualified metrology staff whose availability can constrain scheduling. Downtime during calibration carries opportunity cost plus metrology labor expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of sanitation cycles performed outside validated limits
rows.plain: Cleaning cycles should stay within the tested limits for how they must be performed. This helps ensure the inside of the container is properly cleaned.
rows.imp_rat: Sanitation cycles outside validated limits is a process defect; despite contamination implications it is held at 1 per the mandatory batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cycles with parameter monitoring are mature practice in regulated settings, keeping most cycles within limits though sensor drift and manual overrides cause occasional excursions. Validated sanitation cycles run on fixed automated timing, so staying in-limit rarely adds time. Automated CIP/wash systems reduce reliance on operator skill to hold parameters within validated bounds. Excursions force re-cleaning and reprocessing, but in-limit running is the efficient norm.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sanitation records with missing or conflicting release information
rows.plain: Make sure cleaning records clearly show whether the container was approved for use, with no missing or conflicting information. This helps prevent an improperly cleaned container from being used.
rows.imp_rat: Sanitation records with missing release info is a documentation-ambiguity error, peripheral to the core function here (low relevance) and typically remediable (low severity); held to 1.0 by the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Sanitation records are often paper or fragmented, so missing or conflicting release information recurs and undermines release certainty. Missing release info holds a container until records are chased down, adding delay. Completing sanitation release records needs trained QA judgement to interpret validity. Incomplete records mostly cost administrative rework and held assets.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required closure interfaces left untested before filling
rows.plain: Test every required opening and closing connection for leaks before filling the container. This helps prevent product from escaping or being contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Required closure interfaces left untested before filling risks leaks, but the mandatory ceiling for this lower-ranked batch holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-fill closure integrity testing is established practice and catches most untested interfaces, though a few can still bypass the check. Closure testing is a defined step that adds modest, planned time before filling. Leak/closure testing is procedural and only moderately skill-dependent. An untested interface that leaks in service is costly, but testing itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of leak tests conducted outside approved test conditions
rows.plain: Make sure container leak checks are performed under the required conditions. Otherwise, a test might miss a leak or give a misleading result.
rows.imp_rat: Leak tests run outside approved conditions could undermine containment validation, but under this batch's importance ceiling both relevance and severity are held at 1 as it ranks below prior items.
rows.sat_rat: UN/regulatory-mandated periodic test procedures specify approved conditions and most accredited facilities follow them, though condition deviations still slip through in less controlled settings. Leak tests have defined stabilization and hold times that are generally scheduled without major delay. Conducting a valid leak test requires trained operators who understand approved pressure, temperature and medium conditions. Test rigs and consumables have cost but are standard maintenance-shop equipment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of certification records or compliance marks rejected for inaccurate or incomplete information
rows.plain: Avoid having approval records or compliance labels rejected because they contain wrong or missing information. Complete and accurate information helps show that the container is allowed to keep being used.
rows.imp_rat: Rejected certification records for inaccurate information is a rework/compliance concern held at 1 per the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Templates and QA review catch many errors, but manual data entry still yields inaccurate or incomplete records that get rejected at a noticeable rate. Rejected certification records force resubmission cycles that delay return to service. Producing accurate compliance marks and certs requires knowledge of UN/regulatory formats, a real skill dependency. Rework of documentation is labor cost more than material, and moderate in scale.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of undetected defects in vessel walls, welds, seals, valves, or closures after inspection
rows.plain: Find any damage or flaws in container walls, joined parts, seals, valves, or covers after checking them. This helps prevent unsafe or unreliable containers from being used again.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected wall, weld, seal or valve defects would normally be severe, but the mandatory batch ceiling forces both relevance and severity to 1 so importance cannot exceed 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Visual and pressure inspection catch most defects, but subtle wall, weld, or seal flaws still escape at a rate that leaves genuine residual risk. Thorough inspection of walls, welds, seals, valves, and closures is time-consuming but the real problem is escapes. Detecting subtle defects demands highly skilled inspectors and, for welds, NDT competence that is scarce. An undetected defect reaching service can cause a costly failure or spill far exceeding inspection cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of repaired or replaced components failing integrity retesting
rows.plain: Fewer repaired or replaced parts should fail when they are checked again for proper condition. This helps ensure the returned container is safe and ready to use.
rows.imp_rat: Repaired components failing integrity retesting matters for reliability, but this batch is ranked below prior items so relevance and severity are held at 1 per the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory integrity retesting after repair is established practice and catches most failures before service, though a residual fraction still fails first-pass. When a repaired component fails retest, the rework loop consumes substantial extra turnaround time. Correct component selection and fitting depends on technician skill, but standard repair procedures moderate this dependence. Retest failures waste the replacement part, labor, and the retest itself, a meaningful but bounded cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of reconditioning delays caused by unavailable compliant replacement components
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for approved replacement parts before putting a returned container back into usable condition. This helps the container become available again sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Reconditioning delays from unavailable compliant components are a supply/maintenance timing issue marginal to the container's functional job (low relevance), and consequences are capped here (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Supply of validated, compliance-certified parts is less predictable than generic hardware, so component-availability delays recur regularly. Reconditioning stalls waiting for compliant replacement components are a frequent and sometimes lengthy source of delay. Identifying a compliant substitute component requires regulatory and materials knowledge, slowing procurement. Expedited sourcing of certified components and idle-container cost during waits add up.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of the containment-body surface obscured by residue or unrelated surface discolouration
rows.plain: Keep residue and unrelated color changes from covering the chamber's outer surface. This helps workers see its true condition during inspection.
rows.imp_rat: Containment-body surface obscured by residue or discolouration impairs inspection, but the mandatory batch ordering caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Standard pre-inspection cleaning clears most obscuration, but stubborn stains and discolouration still mask portions of the surface and cause missed or ambiguous findings. Cleaning surfaces enough for inspection is a routine bounded task. Distinguishing residue and discolouration from actual defects requires an experienced inspector's judgement. Additional cleaning agents and labour to clear obscuring residue add modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of the containment-body surface inaccessible to visual or tactile examination
rows.plain: Make as much of the sealed chamber's outer surface as possible easy to see or touch during inspection. This helps workers find wear or damage that might otherwise be missed.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccessible containment-body surface for inspection has integrity-verification relevance, but per the batch ceiling both proxies are capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: A meaningful fraction of the containment body remains structurally inaccessible to visual or tactile check, so latent defects in those zones routinely go undetected. Inspecting accessible surfaces is quick, but blind areas require slow disassembly or endoscopy. Examining concealed or confined areas demands skilled inspectors and specialized borescope technique. Accessing hidden surfaces can require costly teardown or equipment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of inlet caps, outlet adapters, or vent ports omitted from inspection
rows.plain: Make sure inspectors check every inlet cap, outlet adapter, and vent opening. Missing any of these checks could leave worn or unsafe fittings unnoticed.
rows.imp_rat: Caps, adapters, or vent ports omitted from inspection touches integrity but is ranked low in relevance and severity within this batch. Bounded by the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Inspection checklists covering inlet caps, outlet adapters, and vent ports are standard and reasonably effective, though manual sign-off still permits occasional omissions. Inspecting each port and adapter is fast, so omissions do not stem from time pressure. The inspection is low-skill but attention-dependent, and rushed checks skip components. An omitted inspection costs nothing directly until a defect escapes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting wear classifications for the same service-fitting condition
rows.plain: Make sure people give the same wear assessment when looking at the same connection fitting. This helps ensure worn parts are identified and replaced correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting wear classifications for the same fitting condition is a classification-ambiguity issue peripheral to core containment (low relevance), and consequences are held low by the batch ordering (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: The same condition being graded differently by different people is the core inconsistency, and status-quo subjective criteria leave it poorly controlled. Re-inspecting a fitting to resolve conflicting wear calls adds rework time whenever classifications disagree. Wear classification leans heavily on individual inspector judgement, and without shared reference standards different technicians reach different verdicts. Conflicting calls trigger either unnecessary replacements or missed ones, wasting parts or causing later failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of vent components installed in the wrong position or orientation during reassembly
rows.plain: Make sure the vent parts are put back in the correct place and facing the right way. This helps the venting system work properly.
rows.imp_rat: Vent components installed in wrong position during reassembly would matter operationally, but the batch ceiling forces both proxies to 1 so importance stays at or below 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice relies on visual checks and torque procedures, but wrong-orientation vent errors still recur because many designs are not fully poka-yoked, so outcomes vary by operator. Reassembly of vent components adds modest time but rushed jobs increase the chance of misorientation. Correct vent positioning depends heavily on technician attention and familiarity with the specific IBC model, and error-proofing keying is inconsistent across designs. A misinstalled vent is cheap to correct if caught but can cause costly overpressure or spill events if missed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration the container remains unavailable for sterile fill-finish use because repair and release records are incomplete
rows.plain: Make the container available for sterile medicine filling sooner by completing the repair and release records. This helps confirm the repaired container is ready and approved for use.
rows.imp_rat: Container unavailability for sterile fill-finish due to incomplete repair/release records is a documentation-driven delay peripheral to core function (low relevance), bounded in severity by the ceiling (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Record completeness is inconsistent across shifts and systems, so release timing is unpredictable. Sterile fill-finish release hinges on complete repair and release records, and paper or fragmented digital systems routinely leave containers quarantined for days awaiting documentation reconciliation. Assembling a compliant release dossier demands trained QA personnel whose judgement bottlenecks throughput. Held-back sterile IBCs represent idle high-value assets and lost production windows, a significant recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of identification markings restored with incorrect UN performance, capacity, or hazard information
rows.plain: Make sure restored labels show the correct transport rating, container capacity, and danger information.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect UN performance or hazard markings on restored identification could matter safety-wise, but the batch ranking fixes it low in relevance and severity here. Ceiling applies at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Reference to original certification helps, but manual re-stamping still produces incorrect performance or hazard data at a rate that undermines confidence. Getting UN markings right first time avoids reissue, but corrections are quick when caught. Restoring correct UN performance, capacity, and hazard data demands specific regulatory knowledge that not all technicians hold. Wrong markings mostly cost rework and compliance risk rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required identification markings omitted when the container is returned to service
rows.plain: Put back every required label or marking when the container is returned to use. This helps people identify it and handle it correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted identification markings on returned containers is a peripheral traceability issue relative to the core IBC job (low relevance) and its consequences are administratively correctable (low severity); ranking caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Return-to-service checklists cover required markings well, but occasional omissions still occur under manual verification. Adding a missing marking before return is quick once identified. Knowing the full required marking set needs some regulatory familiarity but is checklist-driven. Omissions cost rework and potential recall, not much material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of restored identification markings that remain illegible when the container is returned to service
rows.plain: Make sure the restored labels and markings can still be read when the container is used again. This matters so people can identify the container correctly and handle it safely.
rows.imp_rat: Restored markings remaining illegible on return to service is a marking-quality defect; despite traceability implications it is capped at 1 by the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Restored markings via stamping or durable engraving hold up reasonably, but painted or stencilled restorations frequently degrade and become illegible again in service. Re-marking or stamping identification is a fairly quick operation when done. Legible restoration depends on operator technique and choice of durable marking method. Marking tools are low cost, but illegible IDs cause costly traceability failures.

rows.source_job: Store idle containers under protective conditions
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of idle IBCs dented, punctured, or deformed by impacts during storage
rows.plain: Make sure unused large containers are protected from impacts that could dent, puncture, or deform them while they are stored.
rows.imp_rat: Idle IBCs dented or punctured during storage affects integrity, but the batch ranking places this error low in relevance and severity here. Held at 1.0 per ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Racking and handling rules exist but crowded storage and forklift traffic still routinely dent and deform idle units. Impact damage during storage is not a time issue but a physical-outcome one. Careful handling with forklifts needs operator competence, and impacts trace to handling discipline more than expertise. Dented or punctured IBCs mean scrap or costly repair, a direct material loss.

rows.source_job: Store idle containers under protective conditions
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that idle IBCs remain outside designated protected storage because suitable space is unavailable
rows.plain: Move unused large containers into protected storage sooner when suitable space becomes available. This helps protect the containers from unsuitable conditions while they are idle.
rows.imp_rat: Idle IBCs remaining outside protected storage for lack of space is a storage-logistics delay only tangential to the CFJ (low relevance), and consequences are bounded by the ceiling (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Exposure outdoors is inconsistent and driven by fluctuating inventory, giving no dependable guarantee that idle units stay protected. Idle IBCs routinely accumulate in yards and aisles because designated protected storage fills up, and today's practice offers little to shorten how long they wait outside. Deciding where to stage idle units takes little expertise, so the delay is not skill-limited but space-limited. Warehouse space is a real cost constraint and expanding protected storage is expensive, so operators tolerate exposure rather than pay for more racking.

rows.source_job: Record maintenance actions in service history
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed maintenance actions omitted from each container's service history
rows.plain: Fewer completed repair or upkeep tasks should be missing from the service history of each container. This helps workers know what has been done to each one.
rows.imp_rat: Maintenance actions omitted from service history is a record-completeness error, marginal to the immediate container function (low relevance) with recoverable consequences (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Where CMMS or barcode systems are deployed omissions are low, but many maintenance shops still rely on manual entry where actions routinely go unrecorded. Logging a completed action takes little time when digital systems are used but manual paper trails slow closeout. Recording requires diligence rather than expertise, yet completeness depends on operator discipline. The recording itself is inexpensive, though omitted history creates downstream compliance and rework costs.

rows.source_job: Record maintenance actions in service history
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of maintenance entries assigned to the wrong container unit
rows.plain: Make sure each maintenance action is recorded under the correct container or machine unit. This prevents work from being confused with work done on another unit.
rows.imp_rat: Maintenance entries assigned to the wrong container unit corrupt service history, yet this recordkeeping error ranks below the prior batch, forcing both relevance and severity to 1 under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode or serial-number scanning where deployed strongly reduces misassignment, but manual keying still ties records to the wrong container at times. Misassigned entries are usually caught and reassigned without significant delay. Correctly matching an entry to a unit needs care but little expertise. Wrong assignment wastes reconciliation effort rather than material.

rows.source_job: Record maintenance actions in service history
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of maintenance entries containing inaccurate action details
rows.plain: Make sure each maintenance record correctly describes what work was done. Accurate details help others understand the equipment's repair history.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate maintenance-entry action details are a documentation-quality error of peripheral relevance and low severity relative to the CFJ; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Free-text manual entries frequently carry transcription and description errors, and dropdown or structured-form systems that would constrain this are unevenly adopted. Entering accurate details takes marginal extra time compared with a cursory note. Accuracy depends on the technician's care and understanding rather than scarce expertise. Inaccurate entries are cheap to make but generate costly confusion and rework later.

rows.source_job: Record maintenance actions in service history
rows.stmt: Minimize the time between completing a maintenance action and recording it in the container's service history
rows.plain: Record a completed repair or upkeep task in the container's service record as soon as possible. This keeps an accurate history of the container's care.
rows.imp_rat: The delay between completing maintenance and recording it in service history is a pure administrative timing lag, peripheral to the container's function with trivial consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Delayed entry risks lost or reconstructed-from-memory records, though barcode/tablet logging is increasingly closing the gap. The lag between completing a maintenance action and logging it is the core issue, and paper/manual entry still introduces delays despite growing digital tools. Recording actions needs little skill, just discipline to do it promptly. Digital service-history systems cost little to run and reduce downstream rework cost.

rows.source_job: Record maintenance actions in service history
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting duplicate entries for the same maintenance action
rows.plain: Make sure the same maintenance action is recorded only once and with consistent details. This prevents confusion about what work was actually done.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting duplicate maintenance entries is a record-keeping ambiguity, minor and capped at 1 by the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS systems reduce duplicates via unique work-order IDs, but hybrid paper/digital workflows still generate conflicting entries for the same action. Duplicate entries are usually noticed and reconciled without major delay. Avoiding duplicates needs little skill, just disciplined single-point data entry. Duplicates waste minor administrative effort to untangle rather than material.

rows.source_job: Drain and dry container before storage
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty when the operator confirms that the IBC interior is fully dry
rows.plain: Be more certain that the inside of the container is completely dry when the operator says it is dry. This helps prevent moisture-related problems during storage.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty in confirming the interior is fully dry is a reconditioning verification detail; the batch ceiling constrains both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Visual/tactile dryness checks are subjective and hidden moisture in seams or dead legs commonly escapes confirmation. Confirming full dryness can require extended forced-air or heated drying that prolongs the cycle. Judging that a container interior is truly dry rests largely on operator inspection and experience, the limiting factor without instrumentation. Moisture meters or drying validation add modest cost that many operations skip.

rows.source_job: Drain and dry container before storage
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of IBC unavailability caused by draining and drying
rows.plain: Make the large container available again as soon as possible after it is emptied and dried. This reduces the time it cannot be used for work.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailability from draining and drying is a turnaround delay peripheral to containment; batch ceiling holds both scores at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Drying-time predictability improves with active drying systems, but variable residue and ambient conditions still cause inconsistent turnaround. Draining and especially thorough drying keep the IBC out of service for meaningful durations, the core loss here. Speeding drainage/drying depends more on equipment than on operator skill. Forced-air and heated drying add energy cost but reduce idle-container cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of caps, bungs, or valve lock-rings omitted from the torque check
rows.plain: Make sure every cap, plug, and valve ring is included when checking how tightly the closure parts are fastened. This helps prevent leaks or loose parts.
rows.imp_rat: Closures omitted from the torque check is a reliability gap, but it must remain at 1 given this batch ranks lowest per the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Torque wrenches and checklists exist and cover most closures, but manual tracking still lets individual caps, bungs, or lock-rings slip through unrecorded. Torque-checking every closure is quick when done, so omissions are not a time problem. The task is low-skill but relies on vigilance, and a distracted technician can skip a fitting. A missed torque check costs little directly unless it causes a leak downstream.

rows.source_job: Flush contaminated container with neutralising agent
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of the IBC interior that retains reactive residue after flushing
rows.plain: Leave as little reactive residue as possible inside the large container after rinsing it with a neutralizing liquid. This helps prevent the remaining material from causing a harmful reaction.
rows.imp_rat: Reactive residue retained in the IBC interior after flushing is a real contamination risk, but the mandatory batch ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Established CIP and wash protocols remove most residue, but hard-to-reach geometry and reactive product films still leave residual contamination that verification does not always catch. Flushing and rinse cycles are time-bounded but reactive residues may require repeat cycles that extend duration. Selecting the correct cleaning agents and verifying neutralization demands chemical-cleaning knowledge. Cleaning solvents, water and energy are significant recurring cost drivers.

rows.stk: Operations or EHS personnel responsible for end-of-life container processing.
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty in information identifying the container's former contents and associated hazards
rows.plain: Be more certain about what the container held before and what dangers those contents may have left behind. This helps people clean and handle the empty container safely.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about former contents matters for downstream handling but is not central to the CFJ; held at the mandated 1 on both relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Former-content identification relies on labels and paperwork that are frequently missing, ambiguous or unverified, leaving recurring hazard uncertainty. Resolving uncertainty about former contents often means waiting on upstream documentation, delaying work. Inferring hazards from incomplete labeling demands experienced hazmat judgement. Uncertainty forces conservative handling and testing that waste resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of dismantling stoppages caused by seized or deformed container connections
rows.plain: Prevent work from stopping because container connections are stuck or misshapen. This allows the container to be taken apart more quickly and safely.
rows.imp_rat: Dismantling stoppages from seized/deformed connections are an end-of-life process delay of low relevance and modest severity; capped at 1 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Stoppage duration is erratic and largely unmitigated by standard practice. Seized or deformed connections cause dismantling to stall while workarounds are attempted. Overcoming stubborn connections quickly needs an experienced hand. Stoppages idle labor and equipment, raising cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recalled containers omitted from the return shipment
rows.plain: Fewer containers that have been called back should be missed when the return shipment is prepared. This helps ensure all affected containers are sent back.
rows.imp_rat: Recalled containers omitted from a return shipment is a recall-fulfillment defect, but constrained to 1.0 as this batch sits below prior items.
rows.sat_rat: Without strong asset tracking, some recalled IBCs are routinely missed from return batches. Locating and consolidating every recalled unit for a shipment can take time when fleet tracking is weak. Identifying which units fall under a recall requires cross-referencing lot and serial data, a task prone to oversight. Omitted recalled containers leave liability and may require a costly second shipment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the manufacturer rejects the return because required recall or warranty records are incomplete
rows.plain: Make sure all records needed for the recall or warranty return are complete. Otherwise, the manufacturer may refuse to accept the machine.
rows.imp_rat: Rejected returns due to incomplete recall/warranty records is an administrative ambiguity; peripheral and capped at 1.0 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Incomplete recall paperwork is a frequent reason returns are refused, and record-keeping quality varies widely across owners. Gathering recall or warranty records to satisfy a manufacturer's return conditions can delay the shipment. Knowing which records the manufacturer requires demands familiarity with warranty terms that field staff often lack. A rejected return means the disposer eats the full replacement or disposal cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time a return-ready container remains onsite awaiting collection
rows.plain: A container that is ready to be returned should spend less time waiting at the site for pickup. This frees space and helps the return happen promptly.
rows.imp_rat: A return-ready container awaiting collection ties up assets mildly; the batch cap keeps relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Established pool and carrier collection routines make pickup reasonably dependable, though timing gaps persist. Return-ready containers can sit onsite awaiting collection while carrier or pool pickups are scheduled on their own cadence. Arranging collection is a routine logistics task requiring little specialized skill. Waiting containers occupy yard space and may accrue demurrage or extended rental charges.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a container sustains additional damage during return handling or transit that obscures the recalled defect
rows.plain: Prevent a container from being damaged further while it is returned, so the original recalled problem can still be seen. This helps people identify and handle the defect correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Additional damage obscuring a recalled defect during return is a defect-handling concern, but it is capped at 1.0 as this batch ranks below the prior one.
rows.sat_rat: Standard freight handling is not tuned to preserve specific defects, so incidental damage during return remains a common uncontrolled variable. Careful return handling to preserve a recalled defect can add packing and inspection time. Handling a defective unit so its fault stays visible requires awareness of what evidence the manufacturer needs. Transit damage that obscures the defect can void warranty recovery, a real cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of final disposition records archived without required evidence
rows.plain: Keep disposal records from being stored without the required proof, so there is clear evidence of what was done.
rows.imp_rat: Final disposition records archived without required evidence weakens compliance defensibility, but the mandatory ceiling for this batch holds relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and templates catch most requirements, but records archived with incomplete evidence remain a recurring gap. Assembling required evidence adds handling time that is sometimes skipped at closeout. Knowing exactly which certificates and photos constitute complete evidence requires procedural knowledge that varies by operator. Missing-evidence rework and audit findings impose costs that proper capture would avoid.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of final disposition records associated with the wrong intermediate bulk container
rows.plain: Make sure each disposal record is linked to the correct large container.
rows.imp_rat: Final disposition records tied to the wrong IBC corrupts traceability, but this item sits beneath the previous batch, forcing both proxies to 1 under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode or serial scanning helps, but manual keying and near-identical asset numbers still produce records tied to the wrong container. Correctly matching a record to the right IBC is quick when serial capture is disciplined. Accurate association leans on careful clerical attention rather than deep expertise. Mis-association wastes effort correcting records but little material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of archived record sets containing conflicting disposition details
rows.plain: Make sure stored groups of disposal records do not give conflicting instructions about what should happen to them. This helps ensure records are handled consistently and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Archived record sets with conflicting disposition details create audit ambiguity, yet ranking below the prior batch limits relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Multiple parallel record systems and manual re-entry regularly create conflicting disposition details that go undetected until audit. Reconciling conflicting entries across record sets consumes review time when discrepancies surface. Spotting and resolving conflicting disposition details takes an experienced records reviewer. Conflicts trigger re-investigation costs but rarely direct material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of archived final disposition records being inaccessible during a compliance review
rows.plain: Keep the final records showing what happened to the equipment easy to find during a formal review. Inaccessible records could make it difficult to prove that the equipment was handled properly.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccessible archived disposition records during a compliance review carries real regulatory risk, but the ranking places it beneath the previous batch, holding relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Established document-management practice keeps most disposition records accessible, but inconsistent indexing and orphaned files still cause occasional inaccessibility during compliance reviews. Digital archives allow near-instant retrieval, though legacy paper records slow some pulls during audits. Retrieving archived records needs only basic system familiarity where an EDMS exists. Storage and archival systems are inexpensive relative to the fines avoided by having records available.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay between final disposition and required archival completion
rows.plain: Finish storing records after a decision is made about them without unnecessary waiting. This helps keep required records complete and available on time.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between disposition and archival completion is a records-timing issue of low centrality; both proxies held at 1 per the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard closeout workflows usually complete archival, but backlogs create recurring delays. The gap between final disposition and archival completion depends on how promptly closeout is processed and often lags. Archiving is routine clerical work requiring little skill. Archival delay carries minimal direct cost beyond compliance exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of retiring the wrong IBC because its identity does not match service records
rows.plain: Avoid taking the wrong large storage container out of service because its identity does not match the service history. This helps ensure the correct container is retired.
rows.imp_rat: Retiring the wrong IBC due to identity mismatch with service records is an identity-control defect marginal to the CFJ at this rank (low relevance), with severity bounded by the ceiling (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Serial-number matching works when records are current, but stale or mismatched service records still cause occasional wrong retirements. Cross-checking identity against service records before retirement is quick when records are accessible. Matching an IBC to its history is largely a lookup task requiring modest training. Retiring the wrong container wastes remaining service life and incurs replacement cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required retirement details omitted from service records
rows.plain: Make sure the service records include all important details about retiring and removing the container. This provides a clear record of what happened and how it was completed.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted retirement details in service records is an administrative completeness gap; both proxies set to 1 per the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Retirement details are chronically under-recorded because IBCs are treated as disposable packaging rather than tracked assets, so omissions are the norm not the exception. Service-record retirement entries are fast to make but are frequently deferred until the container is already gone. Determining which retirement details are mandatory demands process knowledge that frontline disposal handlers seldom have. Omitted retirement details rarely trigger immediate spend but create compliance exposure and later correction costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the IBC remains in active service inventory while retirement authorization is unresolved
rows.plain: Finish retiring the container as soon as the decision to retire it is settled, so it does not remain needlessly listed as in use.
rows.imp_rat: Time an IBC sits in active inventory pending retirement authorization is a mild inefficiency; the ceiling keeps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Delays are common because authorization workflows are slow, though the eventual outcome is not usually in doubt. Containers sit in active-service inventory drawing space and system attention while retirement authorization stalls. Moving an item out of service inventory is procedurally simple once approval clears, needing little skill. Idle authorization holds tie up warehouse space and overstate active inventory, adding carrying cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that unclear legal title or transfer authority blocks the ownership transfer
rows.plain: Make sure there is clear proof of who legally owns the container and who is allowed to transfer it. This helps prevent the ownership change from being blocked.
rows.imp_rat: Unclear legal title blocking ownership transfer is peripheral to the IBC's core containment job (low relevance), and while a stalled transfer is inconvenient the consequences are administrative rather than catastrophic (low severity). Capped at 1.0 per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Ownership documentation for pooled or leased IBCs is often ambiguous, making title disputes a recurring obstacle. Resolving unclear title or transfer authority can stall an ownership transfer while approvals are sought. Establishing legal title and transfer authority may require contract or legal review beyond routine operations. Blocked transfers tie up assets and can force default disposal, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the stainless steel IBC identity does not match the transfer records
rows.plain: The large stainless-steel container should be the same one named in the transfer records. This prevents ownership records from being attached to the wrong container.
rows.imp_rat: IBC identity not matching transfer records is a traceability administrative error; capped by batch ceiling at 1.0, and it is peripheral to the container physically doing its bulk-containment job.
rows.sat_rat: Durable engraved identity on stainless IBCs gives strong consistency, though record databases can still lag or contain transcription errors. Stainless steel IBCs usually carry durable permanent identifiers, making transfer-record matching fast. Verifying a stamped ID against records is straightforward and needs little expertise. A mismatch can strand a high-value stainless unit, but permanent marking limits how often this happens.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required cleaning, material-history, or prior-use records missing at transfer
rows.plain: Have all required cleaning, usage-history, and previous-owner records ready when the container changes hands. This helps the new owner understand how the container was used and cared for.
rows.imp_rat: Missing cleaning or prior-use records at transfer is a records-completeness gap, tangential to containment performance (low relevance) and typically recoverable (low severity). Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning and prior-use records are frequently incomplete at end of life, so gaps at transfer are common. Reconstructing missing cleaning or prior-use records at transfer delays handover. Compiling material-history records requires knowledge of prior contents and cleaning validation that is often unavailable. Missing histories can force re-cleaning or block reuse, adding avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of uncertainty about which party owns the container and bears operational responsibility
rows.plain: Shorten the time when no one is sure who owns the container or is responsible for running and caring for it. Clear responsibility helps prevent missed actions and disputes.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity over container ownership and operational responsibility can slow accountability, though the ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Ownership ambiguity for pooled and leased IBCs is a well-known chronic friction with no reliable resolution mechanism. Ownership and operational-responsibility questions can linger for weeks when leased or shared IBCs reach end of life. Resolving ownership requires contract-reading judgement that frontline disposal staff lack. Prolonged ambiguity means someone pays storage, handling or fees for a container nobody claims.
